## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the Divergence Theorem and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Divergence Theorem** states that the total outward flux of a vector field $\vec{F}$ through a closed surface $S$ equals the volume integral of the divergence of $\vec{F}$ over the volume $V$ enclosed by $S$.

> **Mathematical Statement:**

$$
\oint_S \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{F}) \, dv
$$

> **Physical Significance:**
> 1. It establishes the equivalence between a **surface integral** (a global, macroscopic quantity) and a **volume integral** of a local differential quantity (divergence).
> 2. It tells us that the **net outflow of flux** from a closed surface is entirely due to the **sources and sinks** distributed within the volume.
> 3. In electromagnetics, it forms the bridge between **Gauss's Law in integral form** and its **point/differential form** $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Define the gradient of a scalar field. What is the physical meaning of $\nabla V$?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **gradient** of a scalar field $V(x,y,z)$ is a vector field defined as:

> **In Cartesian Coordinates:**

$$
\nabla V = \hat{a}_x \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x} + \hat{a}_y \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial y} + \hat{a}_z \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial z}
$$

> **Physical Meaning:**
> 1. The **direction** of $\nabla V$ at any point is the direction in which $V$ increases **most rapidly**.
> 2. The **magnitude** of $\nabla V$ equals the **rate of change** of $V$ per unit distance in that direction.
> 3. $\nabla V$ is always **perpendicular** to the **equipotential surfaces** (surfaces of constant $V$).
> 4. In electromagnetics, $\nabla V$ appears in the relation $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$, linking the electric field to the electric potential.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **divergence of a vector field** in **Cartesian coordinates**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the vector field $\vec{A} = \rho \sin\phi \, \hat{a}_\rho + 2\rho \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_\phi + z^2 \hat{a}_z$ in cylindrical coordinates, find $\nabla \cdot \vec{A}$ at the point $\left( \rho = 2, \phi = \pi/3, z = 1 \right)$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of Divergence in Cartesian Coordinates:**

**Step 1:** Start with the definition. The divergence of a vector field $\vec{A}$ at a point $P$ is defined as the **net outward flux per unit volume** as the volume enclosing $P$ shrinks to zero.

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \dfrac{\oint_S \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{S}}{\Delta v}
$$

**Step 2:** Consider an infinitesimal rectangular parallelepiped centered at $P(x, y, z)$ with sides $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$ aligned with the coordinate axes.

**Step 3:** Compute the flux through the **right face** (at $x + \Delta x/2$, area $= \Delta y \Delta z$, outward normal $\hat{a}_x$):

$$
\text{Flux}_{\text{right}} = A_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y, z\right) \cdot \Delta y \, \Delta z
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the flux through the **left face** (at $x - \Delta x/2$, outward normal $-\hat{a}_x$):

$$
\text{Flux}_{\text{left}} = -A_x\!\left(x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y, z\right) \cdot \Delta y \, \Delta z
$$

**Step 5:** The **net flux in the $x$-direction** is:

$$
\text{Net}_x = \left[ A_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right) - A_x\!\left(x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right) \right] \Delta y \, \Delta z
$$

**Step 6:** Apply the **mean value theorem** (equivalent to a first-order Taylor expansion):

$$
A_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right) - A_x\!\left(x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right) \approx \dfrac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} \Delta x
$$

**Step 7:** So the $x$-component of net flux becomes:

$$
\text{Net}_x \approx \dfrac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} \, \Delta x \, \Delta y \, \Delta z
$$

**Step 8:** By identical reasoning for the $y$- and $z$-faces, add the contributions:

$$
\text{Total Net Flux} \approx \left( \dfrac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial A_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial A_z}{\partial z} \right) \Delta x \, \Delta y \, \Delta z
$$

**Step 9:** Divide by $\Delta v = \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$ and take the limit $\Delta v \to 0$:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \dfrac{\text{Total Net Flux}}{\Delta v} = \dfrac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial A_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}
$$

**This is the divergence in Cartesian coordinates.** $\blacksquare$

**(b) Numerical evaluation of $\nabla \cdot \vec{A}$:**

**Step 1:** Identify the cylindrical components.
- $A_\rho = \rho \sin\phi$
- $A_\phi = 2\rho \cos\phi$
- $A_z = z^2$

**Step 2:** Apply the cylindrical divergence formula.

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = \dfrac{1}{\rho} \dfrac{\partial (\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \dfrac{1}{\rho} \dfrac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \dfrac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the first term.

$$
\rho A_\rho = \rho \cdot \rho \sin\phi = \rho^2 \sin\phi
$$

$$
\dfrac{\partial (\rho^2 \sin\phi)}{\partial \rho} = 2\rho \sin\phi
$$

$$
\text{Term 1} = \dfrac{1}{\rho} \cdot 2\rho \sin\phi = 2\sin\phi
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the second term.

$$
\dfrac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi} = \dfrac{\partial (2\rho \cos\phi)}{\partial \phi} = -2\rho \sin\phi
$$

$$
\text{Term 2} = \dfrac{1}{\rho} \cdot (-2\rho \sin\phi) = -2\sin\phi
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the third term.

$$
\dfrac{\partial A_z}{\partial z} = \dfrac{\partial (z^2)}{\partial z} = 2z
$$

**Step 6:** Combine all three terms.

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 2\sin\phi - 2\sin\phi + 2z = 2z
$$

**Step 7:** Evaluate at the given point $(2, \pi/3, 1)$.

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \bigg|_{P} = 2(1) = 2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Beautiful observation:** The $\phi$-dependent terms cancel exactly. The divergence is purely a function of $z$.

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** State and explain **Stokes's Theorem**. Using it, prove that the divergence of the curl of any vector field is always zero, i.e., $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vector field is given by $\vec{F} = x y^2 \, \hat{a}_x + y z^2 \, \hat{a}_y + z x^2 \, \hat{a}_z$. Determine whether the field is **conservative** (irrotational). If so, find the scalar potential function $\phi$ such that $\vec{F} = \nabla \phi$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Stokes's Theorem and the identity $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = 0$:**

**Step 1:** State Stokes's Theorem.

> **Stokes's Theorem** states that the line integral of a vector field $\vec{A}$ around a closed contour $C$ equals the surface integral of the curl of $\vec{A}$ over any surface $S$ bounded by $C$.

$$
\oint_C \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{\ell} = \int_S (\nabla \times \vec{A}) \cdot d\vec{S}
$$

**Step 2:** Choose $\vec{A}$ to be the curl of another vector field, i.e., let $\vec{A} = \vec{B}$ (arbitrary). Then:

$$
\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{\ell} = \int_S (\nabla \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{S}
$$

**Step 3:** Consider a small **closed surface** that is a closed "pillbox" or "bubble" enclosing no volume (topologically, a closed surface with $C$ shrinking to a point). The boundary $C$ of this closed surface shrinks to zero, so the LHS becomes zero.

**Step 4:** As the surface closes up, the integral on the RHS must also equal zero for any arbitrary closed surface $S$ enclosing no volume.

$$
\oint_S (\nabla \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{S} = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Divergence Theorem to the LHS, treating $\vec{G} = \nabla \times \vec{B}$ as a generic vector field:

$$
\int_V \nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{B}) \, dv = 0
$$

**Step 6:** Since this holds for **any arbitrary volume** $V$, the integrand itself must be identically zero:

$$
\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{B}) = 0
$$

**Replacing $\vec{B}$ with $\vec{A}$:**

$$
\boxed{\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = 0}
$$

> This proves that **the divergence of the curl of any vector field is always zero.** $\blacksquare$

**(b) Checking if $\vec{F}$ is conservative:**

**Step 1:** A vector field is **conservative (irrotational)** if and only if its curl is zero.

**Step 2:** Compute the curl of $\vec{F} = xy^2 \hat{a}_x + yz^2 \hat{a}_y + zx^2 \hat{a}_z$ in Cartesian coordinates.

$$
\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left( \dfrac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \dfrac{\partial F_y}{\partial z} \right) \hat{a}_x + \left( \dfrac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \dfrac{\partial F_z}{\partial x} \right) \hat{a}_y + \left( \dfrac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial F_x}{\partial y} \right) \hat{a}_z
$$

**Step 3:** Compute each component.

- $\dfrac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial (zx^2)}{\partial y} = 0$
- $\dfrac{\partial F_y}{\partial z} = \dfrac{\partial (yz^2)}{\partial z} = 2yz$
- $\dfrac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} = \dfrac{\partial (xy^2)}{\partial z} = 0$
- $\dfrac{\partial F_z}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial (zx^2)}{\partial x} = 2zx$
- $\dfrac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial (yz^2)}{\partial x} = 0$
- $\dfrac{\partial F_x}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial (xy^2)}{\partial y} = 2xy$

**Step 4:** Substitute.

$$
\nabla \times \vec{F} = (0 - 2yz)\hat{a}_x + (0 - 2zx)\hat{a}_y + (0 - 2xy)\hat{a}_z
$$

$$
\nabla \times \vec{F} = -2yz \, \hat{a}_x - 2zx \, \hat{a}_y - 2xy \, \hat{a}_z \neq 0
$$

**Step 5:** Since the curl is **not zero**, the field $\vec{F}$ is **NOT conservative**. There is no scalar potential $\phi$ such that $\vec{F} = \nabla \phi$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (KTU Valuation):** Students often confuse "curl is zero" with "divergence is zero" — they are independent properties. Always compute the **curl** to test for conservativeness, not the divergence.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Vector Calculus Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Sign error in curl formula** — Remember the cyclic order $(x \to y \to z \to x)$ and the minus sign convention. Many students write the components in the wrong order. **Penalty: Up to 2 marks per component.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{\rho}$ and $\frac{1}{r}$ prefactors** in cylindrical and spherical coordinates. In Cartesian, $\nabla$ is "naked" (no prefactors). In cylindrical and spherical, there are **metric coefficients** multiplying the partial derivatives. **Penalty: 3–4 marks.**
>
> 3. **Using the wrong differential volume** — $dv = dx\,dy\,dz$ in Cartesian, $dv = \rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$ in cylindrical, and $dv = r^2 \sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$ in spherical. Mixing these up leads to a wrong final answer even if the integrand is right. **Penalty: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 4. **Outward normal confusion** in the Divergence Theorem — For a closed cube/box, the outward normal on the face $x = a$ is $+\hat{a}_x$, but on the face $x = 0$ it is $-\hat{a}_x$. Many students assume the same sign for all six faces. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 5. **"Show that" questions require BOTH sides** — When verifying a theorem, you must compute LHS and RHS **independently** and show they are equal. Showing only one side gets **partial credit only** (typically 5 out of 7 marks).
>
> 6. **Unit omission in numerical problems** — Always mention the unit at the end (e.g., C/m³, V/m). KTU examiners explicitly check for units in the final answer.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "What must I remember before walking into the exam?"**

- [ ] **Three operators, three meanings:** Gradient (steepest ascent of scalar), Divergence (source/sink strength of vector), Curl (local rotation of vector).
- [ ] **Cartesian $\nabla$ is naked:** $\nabla = \hat{a}_x \partial_x + \hat{a}_y \partial_y + \hat{a}_z \partial_z$. No prefactors.
- [ ] **Cylindrical $\nabla$ has $1/\rho$:** $\nabla = \hat{a}_\rho \partial_\rho + \hat{a}_\phi \frac{1}{\rho}\partial_\phi + \hat{a}_z \partial_z$.
- [ ] **Spherical $\nabla$ has $1/r$ and $1/(r\sin\theta)$:** $\nabla = \hat{a}_r \partial_r + \hat{a}_\theta \frac{1}{r}\partial_\theta + \hat{a}_\phi \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\partial_\phi$.
- [ ] **Differential volume elements:** $dv = dx\,dy\,dz$ (Cartesian), $dv = \rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$ (Cylindrical), $dv = r^2 \sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$ (Spherical).
- [ ] **Laplacian $\nabla^2 V$** is divergence of gradient: $\nabla^2 V = \nabla \cdot (\nabla V)$. Use it for potential problems.
- [ ] **Vector identities to memorize:**
  - $\nabla \times (\nabla V) = 0$ (curl of gradient is zero)
  - $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = 0$ (divergence of curl is zero)
  - $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{A}) - \nabla^2 \vec{A}$
- [ ] **Divergence Theorem:** $\oint_S \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{F})\,dv$. Used to convert flux integrals into volume integrals.
- [ ] **Stokes's Theorem:** $\oint_C \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{\ell} = \int_S (\nabla \times \vec{F}) \cdot d\vec{S}$. Used to convert line integrals into surface integrals.
- [ ] **Conservative field test:** $\nabla \times \vec{F} = 0$. **NOT** $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = 0$ (that test is for solenoidal fields).
- [ ] **SI Constants:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- [ ] **Cyclic order for curl components:** $(x \to y \to z \to x)$. Use this mnemonic to avoid sign errors.

> **End of Module 1 — Review of Vector Calculus Notes. You are now ready to tackle Coulomb's Law and Gauss's Law (Module 2).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curl, Divergence, Gradient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Curl, Divergence, Gradient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCECT501 – Electromagnetics**, Module 1 lays the mathematical groundwork for the entire study of electromagnetic fields. The three fundamental **vector differential operators** — the **Gradient (∇)**, the **Divergence (∇·)**, and the **Curl (∇×)** — are the language in which **Maxwell's Equations** are written. Every field quantity you will encounter in this course (Electric Field $\vec{E}$, Magnetic Field $\vec{H}$, Electric Flux Density $\vec{D}$, Magnetic Flux Density $\vec{B}$) is analysed using these three operators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 1):** The student must be able to express the Gradient, Divergence, and Curl in **Cartesian, Cylindrical, and Spherical** coordinate systems and apply them to scalar and vector fields.

### 1.1 The Gradient Operator ($\nabla$)

**Formal Definition:** The gradient of a scalar field $V(x,y,z)$ is a vector field that points in the direction of the maximum rate of change of $V$, and its magnitude equals that rate of change.

$$\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are standing on a **hillside** and the height at your location is given by $V(x,y,z)$. The gradient vector $\nabla V$ is like an **arrow stuck into the ground** that points directly **up the steepest slope** of the hill. The steeper the hill, the longer the arrow. So the gradient tells you *which way is "uphill" and how steep it is locally*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The gradient operator acts on a **scalar field** and produces a **vector field**. The result is always **perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces** (level surfaces) of the scalar field.

### 1.2 The Divergence Operator ($\nabla \cdot$)

**Formal Definition:** The divergence of a vector field $\vec{F}$ at a point is the net outward flux per unit volume as the volume shrinks to zero around that point. It measures how much the field *spreads out* (or *converges*) from a point.

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** Picture a **faucet pouring water** into a small sponge (positive divergence — the field is a *source*) and a **drain sucking water out** (negative divergence — the field is a *sink*). If water flows straight through the sponge with neither gaining nor losing, the divergence is **zero**. In this analogy, divergence is the **net flow rate per unit volume**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Divergence acts on a **vector field** and produces a **scalar field**. A field with **zero divergence everywhere is called solenoidal** (e.g., the magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ in free space: $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$).

### 1.3 The Curl Operator ($\nabla \times$)

**Formal Definition:** The curl of a vector field $\vec{F}$ at a point is a vector whose magnitude is the **maximum net circulation per unit area** as the area shrinks to zero, and whose direction is the **normal to the area** that maximizes this circulation (right-hand rule).

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left( \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z} \right)\hat{a}_x + \left( \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x} \right)\hat{a}_y + \left( \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y} \right)\hat{a}_z$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** Place a tiny **paddle wheel** (a small circulator disc) in the fluid field. The **curl** is a vector that measures *how fast and in what direction* the paddle wheel would spin at that point. If the wheel does not rotate, the curl is **zero** at that point and the field is called **irrotational**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Curl acts on a **vector field** and produces a **vector field**. A field with **zero curl everywhere is called irrotational** or **conservative** (e.g., the electrostatic field $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ has $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$).

### 1.4 The Laplacian Operator ($\nabla^2$) — A Composite

The Laplacian is **not** one of the three primary operators, but it is the **natural extension** of gradient and divergence:

$$\nabla^2 V = \nabla \cdot (\nabla V) = \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2}$$

It is central to the **Poisson's Equation** and the **Laplace Equation**, which KTU will test in Module 4 (Static Electric Fields).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Del Operator ($\nabla$) — Foundation of All Three

The **del operator** is a vector differential operator defined in Cartesian coordinates as:

$$\nabla = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

It is **not a vector by itself** — it is an operator that **waits** to act on a scalar or a vector. The three vector differential operations are formed by treating $\nabla$ as a vector and applying the three basic products of vector algebra:

- **Scalar product of $\nabla$ with a vector field** $\Rightarrow$ Divergence ($\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$)
- **Vector product of $\nabla$ with a vector field** $\Rightarrow$ Curl ($\nabla \times \vec{F}$)
- **Acting on a scalar field** $\Rightarrow$ Gradient ($\nabla V$)

### 2.2 Logical Flow of Each Operator

#### A. Gradient ($\nabla V$)
- **Why it exists:** To measure how a scalar quantity (potential, temperature, pressure) changes in space.
- **How it is computed:** Take the partial derivative of the scalar with respect to each spatial coordinate and multiply by the corresponding unit vector.
- **Output:** A **vector** pointing in the direction of **steepest ascent**.
- **Engineering Use:** Electric field is the negative gradient of scalar electric potential: $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$. This relation is the **backbone of static field analysis** in KTU Module 4.

#### B. Divergence ($\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$)
- **Why it exists:** To identify whether a point in a vector field acts as a source (positive) or a sink (negative).
- **How it is computed:** Sum of the partial derivatives of the vector components along their respective axes.
- **Output:** A **scalar** quantifying the **flux density** leaving an infinitesimal volume.
- **Engineering Use:** Gauss's Law for Electricity states $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$. It tells us that electric flux lines either **diverge from positive charges** or **converge to negative charges**.

#### C. Curl ($\nabla \times \vec{F}$)
- **Why it exists:** To measure the rotational tendency (circulation) of a vector field around a point.
- **How it is computed:** Determinant of a $3 \times 3$ matrix with $\nabla$, $\hat{a}_x$, $\hat{a}_y$, $\hat{a}_z$ in the first row, and the vector components in the second and third rows.
- **Output:** A **vector** whose magnitude is the **circulation per unit area**.
- **Engineering Use:** Faraday's Law of Induction states $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$. It is the foundation of **electromagnetic wave propagation** in KTU Module 5.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** The KTU board examiner rewards students who write the operator form AND the expanded form. Memorize the formula table below verbatim.

| Operation | Operator Form | Cartesian Expansion | Acts On | Produces |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gradient | $\nabla V$ | $\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$ | Scalar $V$ | Vector |
| Divergence | $\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$ | $\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$ | Vector $\vec{F}$ | Scalar |
| Curl | $\nabla \times \vec{F}$ | $\begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ \partial/\partial x & \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial z \\ F_x & F_y & F_z \end{vmatrix}$ | Vector $\vec{F}$ | Vector |
| Laplacian (Scalar) | $\nabla^2 V$ | $\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2}$ | Scalar $V$ | Scalar |
| Laplacian (Vector) | $\nabla^2 \vec{F}$ | $\nabla^2 F_x \hat{a}_x + \nabla^2 F_y \hat{a}_y + \nabla^2 F_z \hat{a}_z$ | Vector $\vec{F}$ | Vector |

> [!NOTE]
> The Cartesian Laplacian operates **component-by-component** on vector fields. This is a frequent KTU question.

### 2.4 Two Critical Identities (Frequently Tested)

**Identity 1 — Curl of a Gradient is always zero:**
$$\nabla \times (\nabla V) = 0$$
This is why every conservative (electrostatic) field can be written as the gradient of a scalar potential.

**Identity 2 — Divergence of a Curl is always zero:**
$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = 0$$
This is the mathematical statement that magnetic flux lines always **close upon themselves** (no magnetic monopoles exist).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Operator | Engineering Application | Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient | Antenna radiation pattern optimization, heat-sink thermal design | Telecommunications, Thermal Engineering |
| Divergence | Continuity equation, mass conservation in fluid flow | Fluid Mechanics, Electromagnetics |
| Curl | Electromagnetic wave propagation, motor torque calculation | Power Systems, Microwave Engineering |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are written in exhaustive step-by-step form. Each transition is explained in plain English so that the student can reproduce it in the KTU examination.

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient in Cartesian Coordinates

**Starting Premise:** Let $V(x,y,z)$ be a scalar field. The total differential $dV$ is given by the chain rule:

$$dV = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}dz$$

**Step 1 — Express $dV$ as a dot product of two vectors.** Recognize that the position vector differential is:

$$d\vec{l} = dx\,\hat{a}_x + dy\,\hat{a}_y + dz\,\hat{a}_z$$

**Step 2 — Define the gradient vector** $\nabla V$ as the vector whose dot product with $d\vec{l}$ gives $dV$:

$$dV = (\nabla V) \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the dot product:**

$$dV = (\nabla V)_x \, dx + (\nabla V)_y \, dy + (\nabla V)_z \, dz$$

**Step 4 — Compare term-by-term** with the chain rule expression. Since the differentials $dx, dy, dz$ are independent, the coefficients must be equal:

$$(\nabla V)_x = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}, \quad (\nabla V)_y = \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}, \quad (\nabla V)_z = \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$$

**Step 5 — Reassemble into the vector form:**

$$\boxed{\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Divergence from the Divergence Theorem

**Starting Premise:** Consider a small rectangular volume $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$ centred at point $P(x,y,z)$. Let $\vec{F} = F_x\hat{a}_x + F_y\hat{a}_y + F_z\hat{a}_z$.

**Step 1 — Define the divergence** as the net outward flux per unit volume:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \frac{\oint \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S}}{\Delta v}$$

**Step 2 — Compute flux through the right face** (at $x + dx/2$). The outward normal is $+\hat{a}_x$ and the area is $dy\,dz$:

$$\text{Flux}_{\text{right}} = F_x\left(x + \frac{dx}{2}, y, z\right) dy\, dz$$

**Step 3 — Expand the field at the right face using Taylor series** (keeping only the first-order term):

$$F_x\left(x + \frac{dx}{2}\right) \approx F_x(x,y,z) + \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute flux through the left face** (at $x - dx/2$). The outward normal is $-\hat{a}_x$:

$$\text{Flux}_{\text{left}} = -F_x\left(x - \frac{dx}{2}, y, z\right) dy\, dz \approx -\left[F_x - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{2}\right] dy\, dz$$

**Step 5 — Sum the right and left face fluxes** to get the net $x$-direction flux:

$$\text{Net}_x = \left[2 \cdot \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{2}\right] dy\, dz = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Step 6 — Repeat for the $y$ and $z$ faces** by symmetry:

$$\text{Net}_y = \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} dx\,dy\,dz, \quad \text{Net}_z = \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z} dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Step 7 — Sum the total flux** and divide by the volume $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Curl from the Curl Theorem (Stokes' Theorem)

**Starting Premise:** Consider a small rectangular loop in the $xy$-plane of area $dS = dx\,dy$ with outward normal $\hat{a}_z$.

**Step 1 — Define the $z$-component of the curl** as the net circulation per unit area around the loop:

$$(\nabla \times \vec{F})_z = \lim_{\Delta s \to 0} \frac{\oint \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{l}}{\Delta s}$$

**Step 2 — Walk around the loop counter-clockwise** (right-hand rule, normal out of page). The four contributions are:

$$\oint \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{l} = F_x \cdot dx \big\vert_{\text{bottom}} + F_y \cdot dy \big\vert_{\text{right}} - F_x \cdot dx \big\vert_{\text{top}} - F_y \cdot dy \big\vert_{\text{left}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each leg using the midpoint of each edge:**

- Bottom edge at $y - dy/2$: $F_x(x, y - dy/2, z) \, dx$
- Top edge at $y + dy/2$: $F_x(x, y + dy/2, z) \, dx$ (traversed in $-x$ direction)
- Right edge at $x + dx/2$: $F_y(x + dx/2, y, z) \, dy$
- Left edge at $x - dx/2$: $F_y(x - dx/2, y, z) \, dy$ (traversed in $-y$ direction)

**Step 4 — Apply Taylor expansion** to each midpoint value:

$$F_x\left(y - \frac{dy}{2}\right) \approx F_x - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{2}$$

$$F_x\left(y + \frac{dy}{2}\right) \approx F_x + \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify** the line integral. The $F_x$ and $F_y$ terms cancel, leaving:

$$\oint \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{l} = \left(\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\right) dx\, dy$$

**Step 6 — Divide by area $dx\,dy$** to obtain the $z$-component:

$$(\nabla \times \vec{F})_z = \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}$$

**Step 7 — By symmetry**, the $x$ and $y$ components are obtained by cyclic permutation of $x \to y \to z \to x$:

$$\boxed{\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left(\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z}\right)\hat{a}_x + \left(\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x}\right)\hat{a}_y + \left(\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\right)\hat{a}_z}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operator Forms in All Three Coordinate Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 explicitly tests expressions in **Cylindrical** $(\rho, \phi, z)$ and **Spherical** $(r, \theta, \phi)$ coordinates. The derivations above are the foundation; the table below is what you must commit to memory for the exam hall.

#### A. Cylindrical Coordinates $(\rho, \phi, z)$

**Gradient of a scalar** $V(\rho, \phi, z)$:

$$\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\hat{a}_\rho + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

**Divergence of a vector** $\vec{F} = F_\rho \hat{a}_\rho + F_\phi \hat{a}_\phi + F_z \hat{a}_z$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial (\rho F_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

**Curl of a vector:**

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left[\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial z}\right]\hat{a}_\rho + \left[\frac{\partial F_\rho}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial \rho}\right]\hat{a}_\phi + \frac{1}{\rho}\left[\frac{\partial (\rho F_\phi)}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial F_\rho}{\partial \phi}\right]\hat{a}_z$$

**Laplacian (Scalar):**

$$\nabla^2 V = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial}{\partial \rho}\left(\rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\right) + \frac{1}{\rho^2}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2}$$

#### B. Spherical Coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$

**Gradient of a scalar** $V(r, \theta, \phi)$:

$$\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\hat{a}_\theta + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi$$

**Divergence of a vector** $\vec{F} = F_r \hat{a}_r + F_\theta \hat{a}_\theta + F_\phi \hat{a}_\phi$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial (r^2 F_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial (\sin\theta \, F_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi}$$

**Curl of a vector:**

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\left[\frac{\partial (\sin\theta \, F_\phi)}{\partial \theta} - \frac{\partial F_\theta}{\partial \phi}\right]\hat{a}_r + \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{1}{\sin\theta}\frac{\partial F_r}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial (r F_\phi)}{\partial r}\right]\hat{a}_\theta + \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{\partial (r F_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial F_r}{\partial \theta}\right]\hat{a}_\phi$$

**Laplacian (Scalar):**

$$\nabla^2 V = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2 \sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\sin\theta \frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2 \sin^2\theta}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting the metric factors $1/\rho$, $1/r$, $1/r^2$ in the cylindrical and spherical forms. These factors arise from the curved nature of the coordinate system and are *not* present in Cartesian.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following diagrams use **Mermaid syntax** with strictly alphanumeric node identifiers and clean uppercase labels. They render directly inside the KTU digital study portal and most modern Markdown viewers.

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Del Operator

The diagram below maps how the single **del operator** $\nabla$ produces the three core vector differential operations based on the type of operand and the algebraic product used.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start DEL Operator Nabla] --> B{Operand Type}
    B -- Scalar Field V --> C[Apply as Direct Action]
    C --> D[GRADIENT Operator]
    D --> D1[Output Vector Field]
    D1 --> D2[Direction of Steepest Ascent]
    D1 --> D3[Magnitude equals Rate of Change]
    B -- Vector Field F --> E{Choose Algebraic Product}
    E -- Scalar Product --> F[DIVERGENCE Operator]
    F --> F1[Output Scalar Field]
    F1 --> F2[Positive equals Source]
    F1 --> F3[Negative equals Sink]
    F1 --> F4[Zero equals Solenoidal]
    E -- Vector Product --> G[CURL Operator]
    G --> G1[Output Vector Field]
    G1 --> G2[Direction by Right Hand Rule]
    G1 --> G3[Magnitude equals Circulation per Unit Area]
    G1 --> G4[Zero equals Irrotational]
    A --> H[Composite Operation LAPLACIAN]
    H --> H1[Divergence of Gradient]
    H1 --> H2[Scalar Laplacian of V]
    H --> H3[Vector Laplacian of F]
```

### 4.2 Coordinate System Block Architecture

The diagram below shows the functional blocks for applying the operators in the three coordinate systems required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Scalar V or Vector F] --> B{Coordinate System}
    B -- Cartesian xyz --> C1[Cartesian Block]
    C1 --> C2[Unit Vectors Constant]
    C2 --> C3[Partial Derivatives Direct]
    B -- Cylindrical rho phi z --> D1[Cylindrical Block]
    D1 --> D2[Unit Vectors rho and phi Vary with Position]
    D2 --> D3[Apply Metric Factor 1 over rho]
    B -- Spherical r theta phi --> E1[Spherical Block]
    E1 --> E2[Unit Vectors r theta phi Vary with Position]
    E2 --> E3[Apply Metric Factors 1 over r and 1 over r sine theta]
    C3 --> F[Apply Gradient Divergence Curl Formulas]
    D3 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Output Result for KTU Examination]
```

### 4.3 Operator Hierarchy Flow

This block diagram shows the dependency of the composite **Laplacian** operator on the primary operators.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Gradient Block] --> B[Vector Output]
    B --> C[Divergence Block]
    C --> D[Scalar Laplacian Output]
    A --> E[Direct Action on Scalar V]
    F[Curl Block] --> G[Vector Output]
    G --> H[Divergence of Curl Block]
    H --> I[Identically Zero Output]
    G --> J[Stokes Theorem Connection]
    J --> K[Faradays Law in Module 5]
    C --> L[Gauss Divergence Theorem Connection]
    L --> M[Gauss Law in Module 4]
```

### 4.4 Physical Picture — Divergence, Curl, Gradient (Block Schematic)

| Operator | Physical Picture | Engineering Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient | Arrow pointing up the steepest hill | Direction of water flow on a tilted surface |
| Divergence | Net flux leaving an infinitesimal cube | Faucet (source) vs Drain (sink) inside the cube |
| Curl | Tiny paddle wheel rotation tendency | Whirlpool in a river flow |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** The most common marks-deduction reasons on this topic are: (1) Forgetting the $\mathbf{1/\rho}$ or $\mathbf{1/r}$ metric factors in cylindrical and spherical coordinate systems. (2) Mixing up the sign convention in the curl expression (sign error in cyclic permutation). (3) Failing to state whether the field is solenoidal (divergence zero) or irrotational (curl zero) — the KTU examiner awards **2 bonus marks** for this explicit classification.

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Define the **gradient** of a scalar field. State two of its important physical properties.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**  
The gradient of a scalar field $V$ is defined as the vector $\nabla V$ that points in the direction of the maximum rate of change of $V$ and has magnitude equal to that maximum rate of change:

$$\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

**Two physical properties (any two):**  
(i) The gradient is always **perpendicular** to the equipotential surfaces of $V$.  
(ii) $\nabla V = 0$ implies the scalar field is **constant** throughout the region.  
(iii) The gradient is a **vector operator** that converts a scalar field into a vector field.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]**  
What do you understand by the term **divergence of a vector field**? What is its physical significance?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**  
The divergence of a vector field $\vec{F}$ at a point $P$ is defined as the limit of the net outward flux per unit volume as the volume $\Delta v$ enclosing $P$ shrinks to zero:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \frac{\oint_S \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S}}{\Delta v}$$

**Physical significance:**  
(i) It is a **scalar quantity** measuring the **outward flux per unit volume** at a point.  
(ii) If $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} > 0$, the point acts as a **source** of the field.  
(iii) If $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} < 0$, the point acts as a **sink** of the field.  
(iv) If $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = 0$, the field is **solenoidal** (continuous, no net source or sink).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Computation of Divergence and Curl

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Previous Year Style]**  
For the vector field $\vec{F} = x^2 y \, \hat{a}_x + 2y^2 z \, \hat{a}_y + 3z^2 x \, \hat{a}_z$, determine:

**(a)** The divergence $\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$ at the point $(1, 2, 3)$. **(7 Marks)**  
**(b)** The curl $\nabla \times \vec{F}$ at the point $(1, 1, 1)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Divergence Calculation:**

The components of $\vec{F}$ are $F_x = x^2 y$, $F_y = 2y^2 z$, $F_z = 3z^2 x$.

**Step 1 — Compute the partial derivative of $F_x$ with respect to $x$:**

$$\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x^2 y) = 2xy$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative of $F_y$ with respect to $y$:**

$$\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2y^2 z) = 4yz$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivative of $F_z$ with respect to $z$:**

$$\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(3z^2 x) = 6zx$$

**Step 4 — Add the three partial derivatives** to obtain the divergence:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = 2xy + 4yz + 6zx$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the point $(1, 2, 3)$:**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} \Big\vert_{(1,2,3)} = 2(1)(2) + 4(2)(3) + 6(3)(1) = 4 + 24 + 18 = 46$$

**Answer:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} \Big\vert_{(1,2,3)} = 46$ units.

Since the divergence is **positive**, the point $(1,2,3)$ acts as a **source** of the vector field.

---

**Part (b) — Curl Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Set up the curl as a $3 \times 3$ determinant:**

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ \partial/\partial x & \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial z \\ x^2 y & 2y^2 z & 3z^2 x \end{vmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Expand along the first row** using cofactor expansion:

$$(\nabla \times \vec{F})_x = \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3z^2 x) - \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(2y^2 z) = 0 - 2y^2 = -2y^2$$

$$(\nabla \times \vec{F})_y = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(x^2 y) - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3z^2 x) = 0 - 3z^2 = -3z^2$$

$$(\nabla \times \vec{F})_z = \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2y^2 z) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(x^2 y) = 0 - x^2 = -x^2$$

**Step 3 — Reassemble the curl vector:**

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = -2y^2 \hat{a}_x - 3z^2 \hat{a}_y - x^2 \hat{a}_z$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the point $(1, 1, 1)$:**

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} \Big\vert_{(1,1,1)} = -2(1)^2 \hat{a}_x - 3(1)^2 \hat{a}_y - (1)^2 \hat{a}_z = -2\hat{a}_x - 3\hat{a}_y - \hat{a}_z$$

**Answer:** $\nabla \times \vec{F} \Big\vert_{(1,1,1)} = -2\hat{a}_x - 3\hat{a}_y - \hat{a}_z$ units.

The non-zero curl indicates the field has a **rotational** (non-conservative) component at this point.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — Gradient and Field Classification

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Previous Year Style]**  
Given the scalar potential function $V(x,y,z) = 3x^2 y + y^2 z^2 + 2z^3 x$ volts in free space:

**(a)** Compute the gradient $\nabla V$ and identify the **electric field** $\vec{E}$. **(7 Marks)**  
**(b)** Verify whether the field is **conservative** by computing $\nabla \times (\nabla V)$ using the vector identity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Gradient Computation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the scalar field** $V(x,y,z) = 3x^2 y + y^2 z^2 + 2z^3 x$ V.

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $x$:**

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = 6xy + 0 + 2z^3 = 6xy + 2z^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $y$:**

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} = 3x^2 + 2yz^2 + 0 = 3x^2 + 2yz^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $z$:**

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = 0 + 2y^2 z + 6z^2 x = 2y^2 z + 6z^2 x$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the gradient vector:**

$$\nabla V = (6xy + 2z^3)\hat{a}_x + (3x^2 + 2yz^2)\hat{a}_y + (2y^2 z + 6z^2 x)\hat{a}_z$$

**Step 6 — Compute the electric field** using $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ (the electrostatic field is the negative gradient of the scalar potential):

$$\vec{E} = -(6xy + 2z^3)\hat{a}_x - (3x^2 + 2yz^2)\hat{a}_y - (2y^2 z + 6z^2 x)\hat{a}_z \quad \text{V/m}$$

---

**Part (b) — Verification of Conservativeness:**

**Step 1 — State the vector identity** (no proof needed in examination; just state and apply):

$$\nabla \times (\nabla V) \equiv 0 \quad \text{(for any scalar field } V \text{ with continuous second partials)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the gradient** as a vector field for clarity:

Let $\vec{G} = \nabla V = G_x \hat{a}_x + G_y \hat{a}_y + G_z \hat{a}_z$, where $G_x = 6xy + 2z^3$, $G_y = 3x^2 + 2yz^2$, $G_z = 2y^2 z + 6z^2 x$.

**Step 3 — Set up the curl as a determinant:**

$$\nabla \times (\nabla V) = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ \partial/\partial x & \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial z \\ G_x & G_y & G_z \end{vmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the $x$-component** of the curl:

$$(\nabla \times \vec{G})_x = \frac{\partial G_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial G_y}{\partial z}$$

$$\frac{\partial G_z}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2y^2 z + 6z^2 x) = 4yz$$

$$\frac{\partial G_y}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(3x^2 + 2yz^2) = 4yz$$

$$\Rightarrow (\nabla \times \vec{G})_x = 4yz - 4yz = 0$$

**Step 5 — Compute the $y$-component:**

$$(\nabla \times \vec{G})_y = \frac{\partial G_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial G_z}{\partial x}$$

$$\frac{\partial G_x}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(6xy + 2z^3) = 6z^2$$

$$\frac{\partial G_z}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2y^2 z + 6z^2 x) = 6z^2$$

$$\Rightarrow (\nabla \times \vec{G})_y = 6z^2 - 6z^2 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Compute the $z$-component:**

$$(\nabla \times \vec{G})_z = \frac{\partial G_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial G_x}{\partial y}$$

$$\frac{\partial G_y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3x^2 + 2yz^2) = 6x$$

$$\frac{\partial G_x}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(6xy + 2z^3) = 6x$$

$$\Rightarrow (\nabla \times \vec{G})_z = 6x - 6x = 0$$

**Step 7 — Conclude:**

$$\nabla \times (\nabla V) = 0\hat{a}_x + 0\hat{a}_y + 0\hat{a}_z = \vec{0}$$

The field $\vec{G} = \nabla V$ is therefore **irrotational**, and consequently the electric field $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ is a **conservative field**. This is consistent with **electrostatic field theory** ($\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Loss-of-Marks Zones (KTU Examiner Perspective):**
> 1. **Sign convention:** Always write $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$, **not** $\vec{E} = \nabla V$. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Units:** Mention that $V$ is in **volts** and $\vec{E}$ is in **V/m** to earn the units mark.
> 3. **Identity statement:** You must explicitly state the identity $\nabla \times (\nabla V) = 0$ before applying it, even if you compute it term-by-term.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Gradient ($\nabla V$)** acts on a **scalar** and produces a **vector** pointing in the direction of maximum spatial increase.
- [x] **Divergence ($\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$)** acts on a **vector** and produces a **scalar** representing net outward flux per unit volume. Positive = source, Negative = sink, Zero = solenoidal.
- [x] **Curl ($\nabla \times \vec{F}$)** acts on a **vector** and produces a **vector** representing the rotation tendency (circulation per unit area). Zero = irrotational/conservative.
- [x] **Laplacian ($\nabla^2 V$)** is the divergence of the gradient: $\nabla^2 V = \nabla \cdot (\nabla V)$. It is a **scalar differential operator of order 2**.
- [x] **Critical Identity 1:** $\nabla \times (\nabla V) = 0$ — Curl of any gradient is always zero.
- [x] **Critical Identity 2:** $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = 0$ — Divergence of any curl is always zero.
- [x] **Cartesian unit vectors are constant** — partial derivatives of $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ are zero. This simplifies computation.
- [x] **Cylindrical unit vectors $\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi$** vary with position — partial derivatives $\partial \hat{a}_\rho/\partial\phi = \hat{a}_\phi$ and $\partial \hat{a}_\phi/\partial\phi = -\hat{a}_\rho$ are **not** zero. This is why metric factors $1/\rho$ appear in the operator formulas.
- [x] **Spherical unit vectors $\hat{a}_r, \hat{a}_\theta, \hat{a}_\phi$** all vary with position — the operator formulas have metric factors $1/r$ and $1/(r\sin\theta)$.
- [x] **Electrostatic field** is conservative: $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ and $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$.
- [x] **Magnetostatic field** is solenoidal: $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$ (no magnetic monopoles).
- [x] The **del operator** $\nabla$ in Cartesian coordinates is $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$ — memorize this verbatim.
- [x] For a 14-mark question, **always state physical classification** (solenoidal, irrotational, conservative) — KTU examiners award bonus marks for this.
- [x] **Practice coordinate transformations:** Master converting $\nabla V$, $\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$, and $\nabla \times \vec{F}$ from Cartesian to cylindrical and spherical — these appear in **Part B** of nearly every KTU ESE paper.
- [x] **The Laplacian operates component-wise on vector fields:** $\nabla^2 \vec{F} = \nabla^2 F_x \hat{a}_x + \nabla^2 F_y \hat{a}_y + \nabla^2 F_z \hat{a}_z$ in Cartesian. This is **not** true in cylindrical and spherical coordinates — the vector Laplacian takes a different form.
- [x] **SI Units to remember:** Gradient of potential = V/m (same as electric field), Divergence = 1/m (unitless source density), Curl = 1/m (unitless circulation density).
- [x] **Engineering Applications to mention in viva:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = -\partial \rho / \partial t$ (continuity equation), $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ (electrostatics), $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$ (Ampere's law).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of Coulomb's Law, Gauss's Law, and Ampere's Current Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Coulomb's Law, Gauss's Law, and Ampere's Current Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Coulomb's Law

### Formal Definition
Coulomb's Law is the fundamental experimental law of electrostatics that quantifies the **force between two stationary point charges**. It states that the electrostatic force $\vec{F}$ between two point charges $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ separated by a distance $r$ is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coulomb's Law (KTU 2024 Definition):** The force of interaction between two stationary point charges is directly proportional to the product of the magnitudes of the charges, inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them, and acts along the line joining the two charges.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine two magnets on a table. If you slide them closer, the push or pull between them becomes dramatically stronger. If you flip one around, instead of attracting, they repel. Coulomb's Law is the *mathematical recipe* for this same phenomenon but with electric charges instead of magnets. The "force shrinks with the square of the distance" — double the distance, the force becomes 4 times weaker. This is why a charged balloon attracts tiny paper bits from very close but cannot tug on anything across the room.

### Physical Constants
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Coulomb's constant:** $k = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 8.99 \times 10^{9} \text{ N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$
- **Charge of an electron:** $e = -1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$

---

## 1.2 Gauss's Law

### Formal Definition
Gauss's Law relates the distribution of electric charge to the resulting electric field. It is a restatement of Coulomb's Law in integral (flux) form, expressed over a closed hypothetical surface called a **Gaussian surface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gauss's Law (KTU 2024 Definition):** The total electric flux passing through any closed surface is equal to $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_0}$ times the total net charge enclosed within that surface.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a closed porous balloon (the Gaussian surface) submerged in flowing water (the electric field). If you pour marbles (charges) inside the balloon, water seeps out through the pores in proportion to the number of marbles. Gauss's Law says: the *total amount of electric "fluid" leaking out* depends only on the *amount of charge trapped inside*, not on the balloon's shape or size.

### Physical Constants
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2/(\text{N·m}^2)$

---

## 1.3 Ampere's Current Law (Ampere's Circuital Law)

### Formal Definition
Ampere's Current Law is the magnetostatic counterpart of Gauss's Law. It relates the **magnetic field** circulating around a closed loop to the **electric current** piercing through that loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ampere's Circuital Law (KTU 2024 Definition):** The line integral of the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ around any closed path is equal to $\mu_0$ times the total steady current enclosed by that path.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine swirling honey around a spoon stuck in the middle of a cup. The faster you stir (more current), the faster the honey circulates at every radius. Ampere's Law says: take any *closed circular path* in the magnetic field around a current-carrying wire, and the *sum of the magnetic field strength along that path* equals a fixed multiple of the current it encircles. The current is the "engine" and $\vec{B}$ is the "circulation" it produces.

### Physical Constants
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T·m/A}$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Module 1:** Coulomb's Law → Gauss's Law (electrostatics) and Ampere's Law (magnetostatics). These three laws form the foundation for Maxwell's equations, which unify electricity and magnetism.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Coulomb's Law — Vector Form & Logic Steps

The three laws are essentially *calculus restatements* of each other. Coulomb's Law is the most basic, derived from experimental observations in 1785 by **Charles-Augustin de Coulomb**. The logical flow:

1. **Point Charge Assumption:** Both charges are idealized as point charges (zero size, only magnitude).
2. **Inverse-Square Behavior:** Verified by torsion balance experiments — force obeys $1/r^2$.
3. **Line of Action:** Force is a *central force* — it acts along the straight line connecting the two charges.
4. **Vector Formulation:** Direction is encoded via the **unit vector** $\hat{a}_{12}$ pointing from source charge to test charge.
5. **Superposition Principle:** For multiple charges, the net force on a test charge is the **vector sum** of forces from each individual charge.

### Real-World Utility
- Design of **electrostatic precipitators** (pollution control).
- **Inkjet printing** and **photocopier** charge control.
- **Van de Graaff generator** particle acceleration.
- Initial sizing in **capacitor** and **insulator** design.

## 2.2 Gauss's Law — Logic Steps

1. **Electric Flux Definition:** $\Psi = \oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S}$ — measures the number of field lines piercing a surface.
2. **Closed Surface:** $S$ must be a *closed* surface (Gaussian surface) for the law to apply directly.
3. **Enclosed Charge Only:** Only charges *inside* $S$ contribute; external charges cancel out.
4. **Symmetry Exploitation:** Most useful when the charge distribution has **spherical, cylindrical, or planar symmetry**.
5. **Sign Convention:** Positive charges give outward flux; negative charges give inward flux.

### Real-World Utility
- Calculation of $\vec{E}$ for **coaxial cables**, **parallel-plate capacitors**, **charged spheres**.
- **Faraday cage** design (electrostatic shielding).
- **High-voltage insulator** field estimation.
- **Semiconductor device** junction analysis.

## 2.3 Ampere's Circuital Law — Logic Steps

1. **Magnetic Field Line Integral:** $\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l}$ — measures the net circulation of $\vec{B}$ around a closed path $C$.
2. **Enclosed Current:** Only currents piercing the surface bounded by $C$ count.
3. **Right-Hand Rule:** Direction of integration around the loop is set by the right-hand rule based on current direction.
4. **Steady Current Assumption:** The original Ampere's Law applies to **steady (DC)** currents only; Maxwell later added the displacement current term for time-varying fields.
5. **Symmetry Required:** Like Gauss's Law, it is most useful when symmetry allows $\vec{B}$ to be constant in magnitude on the chosen path.

### Real-World Utility
- Calculation of $\vec{B}$ for **solenoids**, **toroids**, **long straight wires**, **coaxial cables**.
- **Transformer** and **inductor** core design.
- **MRI magnet** field analysis.
- **Electric motor** and **generator** winding field estimation.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All table entries use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Law** | **Integral Form** | **Differential Form** | **SI Units of Constants** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coulomb's Law** | $\vec{F}_{12} = \dfrac{Q_1 Q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, \vert\vec{R}_{12}\vert^{2}} \, \hat{a}_{12}$ | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^{2}} \hat{a}_r$ | $\varepsilon_0$ in F/m; $k$ in N·m²/C² |
| **Gauss's Law** | $\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon_0}$ | $\varepsilon_0$ in C²/(N·m²) |
| **Ampere's Law** | $\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 \, I_{\text{enc}}$ | $\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \, \vec{J}$ | $\mu_0$ in T·m/A or H/m |

### Standard Electric Field Results (from Gauss's Law)

| **Geometry** | **Electric Field $\vec{E}$** | **Gaussian Surface** |
|---|---|---|
| Point charge $Q$ | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^{2}} \hat{a}_r$ | Sphere of radius $r$ |
| Infinite line charge $\rho_L$ | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho} \hat{a}_\rho$ | Cylinder of radius $\rho$ |
| Infinite sheet $\rho_S$ | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_S}{2\varepsilon_0} \hat{a}_n$ | Rectangular box (pillbox) |
| Charged sphere (volume) | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{Q r}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R^{3}} \hat{a}_r$ (inside) | Concentric sphere |
| Coaxial cable | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho} \hat{a}_\rho$ | Cylinder of radius $\rho$ |

### Standard Magnetic Field Results (from Ampere's Law)

| **Geometry** | **Magnetic Field $\vec{B}$** | **Amperian Path** |
|---|---|---|
| Long straight wire | $\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi \rho} \hat{a}_\phi$ | Circle of radius $\rho$ |
| Solenoid (ideal) | $\vec{B} = \mu_0 n I \, \hat{a}_z$ | Rectangular loop |
| Toroid | $\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2\pi \rho} \hat{a}_\phi$ | Circle of radius $\rho$ inside core |
| Coaxial cable (outer) | $\vec{B} = 0$ (outside) | Circle outside outer conductor |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Vector Form of Coulomb's Law

We start from the scalar experimental law and convert it into vector form, the form most useful for KTU problems.

**Step 1 — State the scalar (magnitude) form.**

The magnitude of the force between two point charges $Q_1$ (at position $\vec{r}_1$) and $Q_2$ (at position $\vec{r}_2$) is given experimentally as:

$$
F = \frac{Q_1 \, Q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, R_{12}^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the distance vector.**

The vector from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$ is:

$$
\vec{R}_{12} = \vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude (the actual distance).**

$$
R_{12} = \vert \vec{R}_{12} \vert = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^{2} + (y_2 - y_1)^{2} + (z_2 - z_1)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the unit vector from $Q_1$ toward $Q_2$.**

$$
\hat{a}_{12} = \frac{\vec{R}_{12}}{R_{12}}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the vector form.**

To convert magnitude to vector, we multiply by the unit vector along the line of action:

$$
\vec{F}_{12} = \frac{Q_1 \, Q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, R_{12}^{2}} \, \hat{a}_{12}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\hat{a}_{12}$.**

$$
\vec{F}_{12} = \frac{Q_1 \, Q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, R_{12}^{2}} \cdot \frac{\vec{R}_{12}}{R_{12}}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the denominator.**

Since $R_{12}^{2} \cdot R_{12} = R_{12}^{3}$:

$$
\boxed{\vec{F}_{12} = \frac{Q_1 \, Q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, R_{12}^{3}} \, \vec{R}_{12}}
$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation (Why?).**
- $Q_1 Q_2 > 0$ (same sign) → $\vec{F}_{12}$ is in the direction of $\vec{R}_{12}$ → **repulsion**.
- $Q_1 Q_2 < 0$ (opposite sign) → $\vec{F}_{12}$ is opposite to $\vec{R}_{12}$ → **attraction**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Electric Field of a Point Charge

**Step 1 — Definition of $\vec{E}$.**

The electric field is force per unit test charge:

$$
\vec{E} = \lim_{Q_t \to 0} \frac{\vec{F}}{Q_t}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Coulomb's Law with test charge $Q_t$ at position $\vec{r}$ due to source $Q$ at $\vec{r}'$.**

$$
\vec{F} = \frac{Q \, Q_t}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, \vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert^{3}} (\vec{r} - \vec{r}')
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $Q_t$.**

$$
\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, \vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert^{3}} (\vec{r} - \vec{r}')
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce radial distance $R = \vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert$ and unit vector $\hat{a}_R = \dfrac{\vec{r} - \vec{r}'}{R}$.**

$$
\boxed{\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, R^{2}} \, \hat{a}_R}
$$

This is the most-tested KTU result for Module 1.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Gauss's Law from Coulomb's Law

This is the **derivation most frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions**.

**Step 1 — Consider a point charge $Q$ at the origin.**

The electric field at distance $R$ is:

$$
\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R^{2}} \, \hat{a}_R
$$

**Step 2 — Choose a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $R$ centered on the charge.**

The outward normal to the surface is $\hat{a}_R$, so:

$$
d\vec{S} = R^{2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_R
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the dot product $\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S}$.**

Since $\vec{E} \parallel \hat{a}_R$ and $d\vec{S} \parallel \hat{a}_R$:

$$
\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R^{2}} \cdot R^{2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $R^{2}$ and evaluate the surface integral.**

$$
\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the angular integrals.**

$$
\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi = 2\pi
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta = [-\cos\theta]_{0}^{\pi} = -(-1) - (-1) = 2
$$

**Step 6 — Combine.**

$$
\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot 2\pi \cdot 2 = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot 4\pi
$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**

$$
\boxed{\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}}
$$

This is Gauss's Law. **It holds for *any* closed surface** because the inverse-square law guarantees that flux through *any* closed surface depends only on enclosed charge.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Magnetic Field of an Infinite Straight Wire (Ampere's Law)

**Step 1 — Setup.** A long straight wire carries current $I$ along the $z$-axis. By symmetry, $\vec{B}$ must be circular around the wire and constant in magnitude on a circle of radius $\rho$ coaxial with the wire.

$$
\vec{B} = B(\rho) \, \hat{a}_\phi
$$

**Step 2 — Choose an Amperian path.** A circle of radius $\rho$ centered on the wire.

The path element is:

$$
d\vec{l} = \rho \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_\phi
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the line integral.**

$$
\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{0}^{2\pi} B(\rho) \, \hat{a}_\phi \cdot \rho \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_\phi
$$

**Step 4 — Pull constants out and evaluate.**

$$
\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = B(\rho) \cdot \rho \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi = B(\rho) \cdot \rho \cdot 2\pi
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Ampere's Law.**

$$
B(\rho) \cdot 2\pi\rho = \mu_0 I
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $B(\rho)$.**

$$
\boxed{\vec{B} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi\rho} \, \hat{a}_\phi}
$$

This is the **classic B-field of an infinite wire**, and it is the most-asked Ampere's Law derivation in KTU exams.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: B-Field Inside a Solenoid (Ampere's Law)

**Step 1 — Geometry.** A long solenoid of $N$ turns over length $\ell$ carries current $I$. Let $n = N/\ell$ be turns per unit length.

**Step 2 — Assumption (ideal solenoid).** Outside the solenoid, $\vec{B} \approx 0$. Inside, $\vec{B}$ is uniform and parallel to the solenoid axis ($z$-axis).

**Step 3 — Choose an Amperian loop.** A rectangle with one side of length $L$ parallel to the axis *inside* the solenoid and one side *outside*.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the line integral.**

$$
\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = B \cdot L
$$

(The other three sides contribute zero because $\vec{B} \perp d\vec{l}$ outside or $\vec{B} = 0$.)

**Step 5 — Compute enclosed current.** The loop encloses $nL$ turns, each carrying $I$:

$$
I_{\text{enc}} = n L I
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Ampere's Law.**

$$
B L = \mu_0 \, n L I
$$

**Step 7 — Cancel $L$ and solve.**

$$
\boxed{\vec{B} = \mu_0 n I \, \hat{a}_z}
$$

This is the **solenoid formula**, the most-asked magnetostatics result in KTU Module 1 problems.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Coulomb's Law in vector form and explain the significance of the unit vector in the expression. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Coulomb's Law in vector form states that the force $\vec{F}_{12}$ experienced by a point charge $Q_2$ at position $\vec{r}_2$ due to another point charge $Q_1$ at position $\vec{r}_1$ is given by:

$$
\vec{F}_{12} = \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R_{12}^{2}} \, \hat{a}_{12}
$$

where $\vec{R}_{12} = \vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1$ is the vector from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$, $R_{12} = \vert \vec{R}_{12} \vert$ is the scalar distance, and $\hat{a}_{12} = \vec{R}_{12} / R_{12}$ is the unit vector pointing from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$.

**Significance of the unit vector:**
- It specifies the **direction** of the force along the line joining the two charges.
- If $Q_1 Q_2 > 0$, $\vec{F}_{12}$ aligns with $\hat{a}_{12}$ — **repulsive force**.
- If $Q_1 Q_2 < 0$, $\vec{F}_{12}$ is opposite to $\hat{a}_{12}$ — **attractive force**.
- It transforms the scalar magnitude into a **vector quantity** required for vector superposition in multiple-charge problems.

---

### Question 2: State Gauss's Law. Why is it considered more useful than Coulomb's Law for symmetric charge distributions? `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Gauss's Law states that the total electric flux through any closed surface is equal to $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_0}$ times the total charge enclosed by the surface:

$$
\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}
$$

**Why it is more useful for symmetric distributions:**

1. **No integration over the field is required** when symmetry allows $\vec{E}$ to be factored out of the dot product.
2. **Boundary conditions are simpler** — only the geometry of the Gaussian surface and enclosed charge are needed.
3. **It avoids the inverse-square sum** over infinitely many infinitesimal charges that direct Coulomb integration would require.
4. It directly yields $\vec{E}$ in **closed form** for spherical, cylindrical, and planar symmetries — the three cases most often asked in KTU exams.

---

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) State and explain Coulomb's Law in vector form. Two point charges $Q_1 = 5 \, \mu\text{C}$ and $Q_2 = -3 \, \mu\text{C}$ are placed at positions $(1, 2, 0)$ m and $(4, 6, 0)$ m respectively. Find the force on $Q_2$ due to $Q_1$. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the vector form of Coulomb's Law:**

$$
\vec{F}_{12} = \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R_{12}^{3}} \, \vec{R}_{12}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the position vector $\vec{R}_{12}$ from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$:**

$$
\vec{R}_{12} = (4 - 1) \, \hat{a}_x + (6 - 2) \, \hat{a}_y + (0 - 0) \, \hat{a}_z = 3 \, \hat{a}_x + 4 \, \hat{a}_y
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude $R_{12}$:**

$$
R_{12} = \sqrt{3^{2} + 4^{2} + 0^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_{12}^{3}$:**

$$
R_{12}^{3} = 5^{3} = 125 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute all values into Coulomb's Law:**

$$
\vec{F}_{12} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(-3 \times 10^{-6})}{4\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 125} \, (3 \, \hat{a}_x + 4 \, \hat{a}_y)
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
Q_1 Q_2 = (5 \times 10^{-6})(-3 \times 10^{-6}) = -15 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
4\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 125 = 4 \times 3.1416 \times 8.854 \times 125 \times 10^{-12}
$$

$$
= 4 \times 3.1416 \times 1106.75 \times 10^{-12} \approx 13910 \times 10^{-12} \approx 1.391 \times 10^{-8}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the scalar coefficient:**

$$
\frac{-15 \times 10^{-12}}{1.391 \times 10^{-8}} = -1.078 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}
$$

**Step 9 — Final vector answer:**

$$
\boxed{\vec{F}_{12} = -1.078 \times 10^{-3} \, (3 \, \hat{a}_x + 4 \, \hat{a}_y) \text{ N}}
$$

$$
\vec{F}_{12} = (-3.234 \, \hat{a}_x - 4.312 \, \hat{a}_y) \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The negative sign in the scalar coefficient indicates the force on $Q_2$ is directed *opposite* to $\vec{R}_{12}$ — confirming the **attractive** nature of the force (since $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ have opposite signs).

---

**(b) Derive Gauss's Law from Coulomb's Law. Hence, using Gauss's Law, obtain the electric field intensity at a point outside a uniformly charged sphere of radius $R$ with total charge $Q$. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Gauss's Law from Coulomb's Law:**

**Step 1 — Consider a point charge $Q$ at the origin.** From Coulomb's Law, the electric field at a point at distance $r$ is:

$$
\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^{2}} \, \hat{a}_r
$$

**Step 2 — Define electric flux through a closed surface:**

$$
\Psi = \oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S}
$$

**Step 3 — Choose a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ centered on the charge.** The outward normal to the surface is $\hat{a}_r$, and the surface area element is $dS = r^{2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$. Thus:

$$
d\vec{S} = r^{2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_r
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S}$:**

$$
\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^{2}} \cdot r^{2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

The $r^{2}$ terms cancel:

$$
\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over the entire spherical surface:**

$$
\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the angular integrals:**

$$
\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi = 2\pi \quad ; \quad \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta = 2
$$

**Step 7 — Combine:**

$$
\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot 4\pi = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}
$$

$$
\boxed{\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}}
$$

**Application — Electric Field Outside a Uniformly Charged Sphere:**

**Step 1 — Symmetry argument:** The charge distribution is spherically symmetric, so $\vec{E}$ must be radial: $\vec{E} = E(r) \, \hat{a}_r$, with $E(r)$ depending only on $r$.

**Step 2 — Choose a Gaussian surface:** A sphere of radius $r > R$ centered on the charged sphere. Then $d\vec{S} = r^{2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_r$ and $\vec{E} \parallel d\vec{S}$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the flux integral:**

$$
\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = E(r) \oint_S dS = E(r) \cdot 4\pi r^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Gauss's Law:**

$$
E(r) \cdot 4\pi r^{2} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $E(r)$:**

$$
E(r) = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Final vector form:**

$$
\boxed{\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^{2}} \, \hat{a}_r \quad (r \geq R)}
$$

The result is identical to the field of a *point charge* — confirming that **a spherically symmetric charge distribution acts as a point charge located at its center**, for external points.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — June 2022]`

**(a) State Ampere's Circuital Law. Using Ampere's Law, derive the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$ at a distance $\rho$ from a long straight conductor carrying a steady current $I$. Hence, find $\vec{B}$. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of Ampere's Circuital Law:**

> The line integral of magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$ around any closed path is equal to the total current enclosed by that path:

$$
\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{\text{enc}}
$$

Equivalently, for $\vec{B}$:

$$
\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometry setup:** A long straight wire of infinite length carries steady current $I$ along the $+z$ direction. By cylindrical symmetry, $\vec{H}$ must:
- be **azimuthal** (circulate around the wire): $\vec{H} = H(\rho) \, \hat{a}_\phi$.
- have **constant magnitude** on any circle of radius $\rho$ coaxial with the wire.

**Step 3 — Choose an Amperian path:** A circle of radius $\rho$ centered on the wire, traversed in the $\hat{a}_\phi$ direction (right-hand rule with thumb along $+z$).

**Step 4 — Path element:**

$$
d\vec{l} = \rho \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_\phi
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the line integral:**

$$
\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{0}^{2\pi} H(\rho) \, \hat{a}_\phi \cdot \rho \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_\phi = H(\rho) \cdot \rho \cdot 2\pi
$$

**Step 6 — Enclosed current:**

$$
I_{\text{enc}} = I
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Ampere's Law:**

$$
H(\rho) \cdot 2\pi\rho = I
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $H(\rho)$:**

$$
H(\rho) = \frac{I}{2\pi\rho}
$$

**Step 9 — Vector form of $\vec{H}$:**

$$
\boxed{\vec{H} = \frac{I}{2\pi\rho} \, \hat{a}_\phi}
$$

**Step 10 — Convert to $\vec{B}$ using $\vec{B} = \mu_0 \vec{H}$:**

$$
\boxed{\vec{B} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi\rho} \, \hat{a}_\phi}
$$

---

**(b) Derive the magnetic field inside a long solenoid of $N$ turns and length $\ell$ carrying a steady current $I$. Mention the assumptions made. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry:** A solenoid is a helical coil wound tightly on a cylindrical form. Let $N$ be the total number of turns and $\ell$ the axial length. Define turn density:

$$
n = \frac{N}{\ell} \quad \text{(turns per unit length)}
$$

**Step 2 — Assumptions (ideal solenoid):**
1. The solenoid is **infinitely long** (or sufficiently long that end effects are negligible).
2. The winding is **tight** (turns are perpendicular to the axis).
3. The magnetic field is **uniform inside** the solenoid and **zero outside**.
4. $\vec{B}$ is **parallel to the solenoid axis** (the $z$-axis by convention).

**Step 3 — Choose an Amperian loop:** A rectangle $ABCD$ with:
- Side $AB$ of length $L$ **inside** the solenoid, parallel to the axis.
- Side $CD$ of length $L$ **outside** the solenoid.
- Sides $BC$ and $DA$ perpendicular to the axis.

**Step 4 — Evaluate $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l}$ on each segment:**

**Segment AB (inside, parallel to $\vec{B}$):**

$$
\int_{A}^{B} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = B \cdot L
$$

**Segment CD (outside, $\vec{B} = 0$):**

$$
\int_{C}^{D} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0
$$

**Segments BC and DA (perpendicular to $\vec{B}$):**

$$
\int_{B}^{C} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0 \quad ; \quad \int_{D}^{A} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Total line integral:**

$$
\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = B L
$$

**Step 6 — Compute enclosed current:** The Amperian loop of length $L$ encloses $nL$ turns, each carrying current $I$:

$$
I_{\text{enc}} = n L I
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Ampere's Circuital Law:**

$$
B L = \mu_0 \, n L I
$$

**Step 8 — Cancel $L$ and solve:**

$$
\boxed{\vec{B} = \mu_0 n I \, \hat{a}_z = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{\ell} \, \hat{a}_z}
$$

**Step 9 — Interpretation:** Inside the solenoid, $\vec{B}$ is uniform and proportional to the number of turns per unit length times the current — this is the principle behind **electromagnets, transformers, and MRI scanners**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit vector** $\hat{a}_{12}$ or $\hat{a}_r$ in the vector form of Coulomb's Law — a **2-mark penalty** in 14-mark problems.
> 2. **Sign errors** in $Q_1 Q_2$ product — failing to identify whether the force is repulsive or attractive.
> 3. **Choosing the wrong Gaussian surface** — e.g., using a cylinder for a point charge. The Gaussian surface **must match the symmetry** of the charge distribution.
> 4. **Including the displacement current term** in the original Ampere's Law — the KTU Module 1 syllabus covers only the **steady-current form** without $\partial \vec{D}/\partial t$.
> 5. **Direction of integration** in Ampere's Law — must follow the right-hand rule based on current direction. Reversing it gives a sign error.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency** — $\varepsilon_0$ in F/m, $\mu_0$ in H/m, $k$ in N·m²/C². Mixing units costs marks.
> 7. **Missing $4\pi$ factor** in $k = 1/(4\pi\varepsilon_0)$ — a classic KTU trap.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (Coulomb, Gauss, Ampere)**

- **Coulomb's Law (Vector Form):** $\vec{F}_{12} = \dfrac{Q_1 Q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R_{12}^{2}} \hat{a}_{12}$ — applies only to **stationary point charges**.
- **Coulomb's constant:** $k = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^{9} \text{ N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$ (use $8.99 \times 10^{9}$ for accuracy).
- **Electric field** of a point charge: $\vec{E} = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^{2}} \hat{a}_r$ — directed radially outward for $+Q$ and inward for $-Q$.
- **Gauss's Law:** $\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}$ — only the **enclosed** charge matters; external charges contribute zero net flux.
- **Differential form of Gauss's Law:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon_0}$ — relates field divergence to local charge density.
- **Gaussian surface must match the symmetry:** sphere for point/spherical, cylinder for line/cylindrical, pillbox for planar.
- **Ampere's Circuital Law:** $\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}}$ — only **steady currents** are considered in Module 1.
- **Differential form of Ampere's Law:** $\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \vec{J}$ — relates field curl to local current density.
- **B-field of long straight wire:** $\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi\rho} \hat{a}_\phi$ — circles the wire following the right-hand rule.
- **B-field of ideal solenoid:** $\vec{B} = \mu_0 n I \, \hat{a}_z$ — uniform inside, zero outside.
- **B-field of toroid:** $\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2\pi\rho} \hat{a}_\phi$ — confined to the core.
- **Constants to memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m; $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **Always include the unit vector** in vector answers — KTU examiners deduct marks for scalar-only answers in vector-form questions.
- **Check the symmetry** before deciding whether to use Gauss's Law or Ampere's Law — both laws are most useful **only when symmetry exists**.
- **For non-symmetric problems**, fall back on direct integration using Coulomb's Law (electrostatics) or Biot-Savart Law (magnetostatics).
- **Right-hand rule is critical** for Ampere's Law direction — practice both orientations of current and Amperian loop.
