# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In transmission line theory, every pair of conductors separated by a dielectric medium forms a distributed parameter system characterized by four primary constants per unit length: **resistance (R)**, **inductance (L)**, **capacitance (C)**, and **conductance (G)**. Among these, the **capacitance (C)** and **inductance (L)** per unit length are the two most fundamental parameters for analyzing the propagation of electromagnetic energy along guided structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:**
> - **Capacitance per unit length (C):** The ability of a two-conductor system to store electric energy per unit length when a potential difference exists between the conductors. Units: **Farads per meter (F/m)**.
> - **Inductance per unit length (L):** The ability of a two-conductor system to store magnetic energy per unit length when a current flows through the conductors. Units: **Henries per meter (H/m)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two long parallel water hoses laid side by side, carrying water in opposite directions. Even though the rubber walls of the hoses are insulators, a tiny electric "pressure" builds up between the two streams of water because they are electrically charged (current flow implies charge motion). This pressure represents **capacitance**. Now imagine the momentum of the water spinning in tiny whirlpools around each hose — this rotating "magnetic memory" represents **inductance**. The geometry of the hoses (their separation distance and thickness) directly controls how much electric pressure and how much magnetic memory the system can store per unit length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Context:** The capacitance and inductance derived in this topic are the **foundation for designing overhead power transmission lines, telephone lines, RF feeder cables, and high-speed PCB trace routing**. They also determine the **characteristic impedance** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$, which governs signal integrity and matching.

## 1.3 Physical Constants Used

- Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m**
- Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**
- Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} \approx 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles

The derivations rest on three pillars of electromagnetics that every KTU student must master:

1. **Gauss's Law in Electrostatics** — Used to compute the electric field $\vec{E}$ around cylindrical charge distributions.
2. **Ampere's Circuital Law** — Used to compute the magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ around current-carrying cylindrical conductors.
3. **Principle of Superposition** — The fields from multiple line charges or currents can be algebraically added (with signs) to obtain the resultant field.

## 2.2 Logical Reasoning Chain

- **Why (Capacitance):** Two conductors at different potentials separated by a dielectric create an electric field. Integrating this field between the conductors gives the potential difference, and the ratio of charge per unit length to this potential difference gives the capacitance per unit length.
- **Why (Inductance):** A current flowing through a conductor generates a magnetic field that links the loop formed by the two conductors. The flux per unit length divided by the current gives the inductance per unit length.
- **How:** We assume quasi-static conditions, cylindrical symmetry, and infinitely long conductors (uniform field along the length).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is your **one-stop exam reference** for this topic. Memorize the structure, not just the formulas.

| **Parameter** | **Two-Wire Line** | **Coaxial Cable** | **Boundary / Assumption** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electric field $\vec{E}$ | $\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$ (from each line charge) | $\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon r}$ | Between conductors, $a \le r \le b$ |
| Magnetic field $\vec{B}$ | $\frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$ (from each wire) | $\frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$ | Between conductors, $a \le r \le b$ |
| Potential difference $V$ | $\frac{\rho_L}{\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)$ | $\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$ | $D \gg a$ for two-wire |
| **Capacitance $C$** | $\frac{\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)}$ F/m | $\frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)}$ F/m | $D \gg a$ assumed |
| **Inductance $L$** | $\frac{\mu_0}{\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)$ H/m | $\frac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$ H/m | External inductance only |
| Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$ | $\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\pi^2 \varepsilon_0}} \cdot \frac{\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)}{1}$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon}}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ |
| $L \cdot C$ product | $\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 = 1/c^2$ | $\mu \varepsilon = 1/v^2$ | **Velocity of propagation** |
| Internal Inductance (DC) | $\frac{\mu_0}{8\pi}$ per wire | $\frac{\mu_0}{8\pi}$ (inner only) | Skin effect neglected |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Two-wire lines** form the backbone of **overhead power transmission** (e.g., 400 kV lines) and **open-wire telephone lines**; engineers use these formulas to compute surge impedance and line loading.
- **Coaxial cables** are the heart of **CATV, broadband internet, RF antennas, and instrumentation cables**; the derived $C$ and $L$ determine characteristic impedance (commonly 50 Ω or 75 Ω).
- The product $LC = \mu\varepsilon$ reveals that the **velocity of wave propagation** is independent of the conductor geometry — a beautiful and KTU-favorite result.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 CAPACITANCE OF A TWO-WIRE TRANSMISSION LINE

### 3.1.1 Geometry and Assumptions

- Two identical parallel cylindrical conductors of radius $a$ (meters), separated by a center-to-center distance $D$ (meters).
- Each conductor carries a uniform line charge density $+\rho_L$ and $-\rho_L$ respectively (C/m).
- The medium between them is free space (permittivity $\varepsilon_0$).
- Assumption: $D \gg a$ (typically $D/a \ge 10$ in practice).

### 3.1.2 Electric Field from a Single Line Charge

By Gauss's law applied to a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ and length $\ell$ around an infinite line charge $\rho_L$:

$$
\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}
$$

$$
E \cdot (2\pi r \ell) = \frac{\rho_L \ell}{\varepsilon_0}
$$

$$
E = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r} \quad \text{(directed radially outward)}
$$

### 3.1.3 Potential at the Surface of Conductor 1 (Using Superposition)

The potential at a point P on the surface of conductor 1 is the sum of contributions from both line charges. Since conductor 1's own surface is an equipotential, we choose a point on its surface nearest to conductor 2.

Distance from $+\rho_L$ (conductor 1 center) to the chosen point on conductor 1 surface = $a$.
Distance from $-\rho_L$ (conductor 2 center) to the same point = $D - a$.

$$
V_1 = -\int_{\infty}^{a} \vec{E}_+ \cdot d\vec{r} \;-\int_{\infty}^{D-a} \vec{E}_- \cdot d\vec{r}
$$

Since $-\rho_L$ produces a field of magnitude $\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$ directed **toward** the negative line charge (i.e., it **raises** the potential at P), the sign convention gives:

$$
V_1 = -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln(a) + \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln(D-a) + C
$$

Taking the reference at infinity ($C = 0$) and using $D \gg a$ so $D - a \approx D$:

$$
V_1 = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

### 3.1.4 Potential at the Surface of Conductor 2 (by Symmetry)

By swapping the role of charges (or by symmetry argument), the potential on conductor 2's surface is equal in magnitude but opposite in sign:

$$
V_2 = -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

### 3.1.5 Potential Difference and Capacitance

$$
V = V_1 - V_2 = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) - \left(-\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)\right)
$$

$$
V = \frac{\rho_L}{\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

By definition, capacitance per unit length:

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{\rho_L}{V} = \frac{\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)} \;\; \text{F/m}}
$$

---

## 3.2 INDUCTANCE OF A TWO-WIRE TRANSMISSION LINE

### 3.2.1 Magnetic Field from a Single Long Straight Wire

From Ampere's circuital law, at a distance $r$ from a wire carrying current $I$:

$$
\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enc}
$$

$$
B \cdot (2\pi r) = \mu_0 I
$$

$$
B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}
$$

### 3.2.2 Flux Linkage Per Unit Length (External Flux)

Consider an elementary rectangular strip of width $dr$ and length 1 m, parallel to the wire axis, located between the surface of conductor 1 (at $r = a$) and the surface of conductor 2 (at $r = D - a$). The flux through this strip due to the current in conductor 1 is:

$$
d\Phi_1 = B \cdot dr \cdot 1 = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\, dr
$$

Integrating between $r = a$ and $r = D - a$:

$$
\Phi_1 = \int_{a}^{D-a} \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\, dr = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D-a}{a}\right)
$$

Similarly, the flux from conductor 2 (carrying return current $-I$) passing through the **same loop area** adds an equal contribution because the return current's field also threads the loop in the same sense. Therefore, the total external flux per unit length is:

$$
\Phi_{total} = 2 \cdot \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D-a}{a}\right) = \frac{\mu_0 I}{\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D-a}{a}\right)
$$

Using $D \gg a$:

$$
\Phi_{total} \approx \frac{\mu_0 I}{\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

### 3.2.3 Inductance Per Unit Length

$$
\boxed{L = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{I} = \frac{\mu_0}{\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) \;\; \text{H/m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Some texts include the **internal inductance** $L_{int} = \mu_0/(8\pi)$ per wire (for uniform current distribution at DC). KTU typically asks for **external inductance only** in 14-mark derivations. Always read the question carefully.

---

## 3.3 CAPACITANCE OF A COAXIAL CABLE

### 3.3.1 Geometry and Assumptions

- Inner conductor of radius $a$ carries a uniform line charge $+\rho_L$.
- Outer conductor (thin shell) of inner radius $b$ carries $-\rho_L$.
- $b > a$; dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$ fills the space between.

### 3.3.2 Electric Field via Gauss's Law

Choose a coaxial cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ where $a < r < b$, length $\ell$:

$$
E \cdot (2\pi r \ell) = \frac{\rho_L \ell}{\varepsilon}
$$

$$
E = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon r} \quad \text{(radially outward)}
$$

### 3.3.3 Potential Difference Between Inner and Outer Conductor

$$
V = \int_{a}^{b} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{r} = \int_{a}^{b} \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon r}\, dr
$$

$$
V = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\Big[\ln r\Big]_{a}^{b} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

### 3.3.4 Capacitance Per Unit Length

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{\rho_L}{V} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)} \;\; \text{F/m}}
$$

---

## 3.4 INDUCTANCE OF A COAXIAL CABLE

### 3.4.1 Magnetic Field Inside the Dielectric

Current $I$ flows through the inner conductor and returns through the outer. By Ampere's law, for $a < r < b$:

$$
B = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi r} \quad \text{(azimuthal direction)}
$$

> **Note:** The field is **zero outside** the coaxial cable (the enclosed net current is zero), which is a key reason coaxial cables are excellent at containing electromagnetic interference.

### 3.4.2 Flux Per Unit Length

$$
\Phi = \int_{a}^{b} B \cdot dr \cdot 1 = \int_{a}^{b} \frac{\mu I}{2\pi r}\, dr
$$

$$
\Phi = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

### 3.4.3 Inductance Per Unit Length

$$
\boxed{L = \frac{\Phi}{I} = \frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \;\; \text{H/m}}
$$

---

## 3.5 BONUS: VERIFYING THE VELOCITY OF PROPAGATION

For both the two-wire line and the coaxial cable, the product $LC$ is geometry-independent:

$$
\text{Two-wire: } L \cdot C = \frac{\mu_0}{\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) \cdot \frac{\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)} = \mu_0\varepsilon_0 = \frac{1}{c^2}
$$

$$
\text{Coaxial: } L \cdot C = \frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \cdot \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)} = \mu\varepsilon = \frac{1}{v^2}
$$

This proves the **velocity of wave propagation** depends only on the medium, not on the conductor geometry. KTU frequently tests this result as a 3-mark conceptual question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Wire Transmission Line — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Voltage Source V] --> B[Conductor 1 Radius a Charge plus rhoL]
    C[Load Impedance ZL] --> D[Conductor 2 Radius a Charge minus rhoL]
    B --> E[Electric Field E equals rhoL over 2 pi epsilon0 r]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Potential Difference V equals rhoL over pi epsilon0 times ln D over a]
    F --> G[Capacitance C equals pi epsilon0 over ln D over a]
    B --> H[Current I in Conductor 1]
    D --> I[Return Current minus I in Conductor 2]
    H --> J[Magnetic Field B equals mu0 I over 2 pi r]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Flux Linkage Phi equals mu0 I over pi times ln D over a]
    K --> L[Inductance L equals mu0 over pi times ln D over a]
    G --> M[Characteristic Impedance Z0 equals sqrt L over C]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Velocity of Propagation v equals 1 over sqrt L C equals c]
```

## 4.2 Coaxial Cable — Cross-Sectional Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Inner Conductor Radius a carries plus rhoL and current I] --> B[Electric Field E equals rhoL over 2 pi epsilon r for a less than r less than b]
    A --> C[Magnetic Field B equals mu I over 2 pi r for a less than r less than b]
    B --> D[Integrate from a to b to get V equals rhoL over 2 pi epsilon times ln b over a]
    D --> E[Capacitance per unit length C equals 2 pi epsilon over ln b over a F per m]
    C --> F[Integrate from a to b to get Phi equals mu I over 2 pi times ln b over a]
    F --> G[Inductance per unit length L equals mu over 2 pi times ln b over a H per m]
    E --> H[Product L C equals mu epsilon equals 1 over v squared]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Velocity v depends only on dielectric medium]
    A --> J[Outer Conductor Inner Radius b carries minus rhoL and minus I]
    J --> K[Field outside cable is ZERO ideal shielding]
    K --> L[Coaxial Cable: EM interference contained]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Logic Flow — Two-Wire vs Coaxial

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Choose Transmission Line Type] --> Q1{Need shielding from EMI}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Coax[Use Coaxial Cable]
    Q1 -->|No| TwoWire[Use Two-Wire Open Line]
    Coax --> C1[C equals 2 pi epsilon over ln b over a]
    Coax --> L1[L equals mu over 2 pi times ln b over a]
    TwoWire --> C2[C equals pi epsilon0 over ln D over a]
    TwoWire --> L2[L equals mu0 over pi times ln D over a]
    C1 --> Product1[LC equals mu epsilon equals 1 over v squared]
    L1 --> Product1
    C2 --> Product2[LC equals mu0 epsilon0 equals 1 over c squared]
    L2 --> Product2
    Product1 --> End[Velocity is geometry independent]
    Product2 --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the expressions for the capacitance and inductance per unit length of a two-wire transmission line. State the assumptions made.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Assumption:** $D \gg a$ (center-to-center separation is much greater than the conductor radius), infinitely long conductors, charge uniformly distributed on conductor surfaces, free-space dielectric.

$$
C = \frac{\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)} \;\; \text{F/m}, \quad L = \frac{\mu_0}{\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) \;\; \text{H/m}
$$

*(1 mark for each formula, 1 mark for assumptions.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Show that the product $LC$ for any two-conductor transmission line is independent of the conductor geometry.**

**Model Answer:**

Multiplying the $C$ and $L$ expressions for either the two-wire line or the coaxial cable, the logarithmic term $\ln(D/a)$ or $\ln(b/a)$ cancels out:

$$
LC = \mu_0\varepsilon_0 = \frac{1}{c^2} \quad \text{(two-wire)}
$$

$$
LC = \mu\varepsilon = \frac{1}{v^2} \quad \text{(coaxial)}
$$

Since $v = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, the velocity of wave propagation depends **only on the medium** and not on the geometry of the conductors. *(Full 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **capacitance per unit length** of a two-wire transmission line consisting of two parallel conductors of radius $a$ separated by a distance $D$ in free space. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **inductance per unit length** of a **coaxial cable** with inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. Consider two line charges $+\rho_L$ and $-\rho_L$ separated by distance $D$ in free space. *(1 mark)*
2. Electric field from each line charge at distance $r$:

$$
E = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}
$$

*(1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the derivation via Gauss's law.)*

3. Potential at the surface of conductor 1 (using superposition):

$$
V_1 = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

*(1 mark — show the integration steps and explain $D \gg a$.)*

4. By symmetry, $V_2 = -V_1$. The potential difference is:

$$
V = V_1 - V_2 = \frac{\rho_L}{\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

*(1 mark for the algebra.)*

5. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{\rho_L}{V} = \frac{\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)} \;\; \text{F/m}}
$$

*(1 mark for the final boxed answer and 1 mark for the unit.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

1. For a coaxial cable with inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$, current $I$ flows through the inner conductor. Apply Ampere's circuital law for $a < r < b$. *(1 mark)*

$$
B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}
$$

*(1 mark for the derivation.)*

2. Compute the flux per unit length by integrating over the cross-section between $a$ and $b$:

$$
\Phi = \int_{a}^{b} \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\, dr = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

*(2 marks — show the integration limits clearly and the antiderivative step.)*

3. By definition, inductance per unit length:

$$
\boxed{L = \frac{\Phi}{I} = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \;\; \text{H/m}}
$$

*(2 marks for the final boxed answer, the unit, and a concluding statement such as "this is the external inductance of the coaxial cable.")*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **inductance per unit length** of a two-wire transmission line. State clearly why the contribution from the return wire must be added to the self-flux. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **capacitance per unit length** of a **coaxial cable** and show that the characteristic impedance depends on $\ln(b/a)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. The magnetic field at distance $r$ from a single straight wire carrying current $I$:

$$
B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}
$$

*(1 mark — derive using Ampere's law.)*

2. The flux through a strip of width $dr$ per unit length is $d\Phi = B\,dr$. The flux from the forward wire between $r = a$ and $r = D - a$ is:

$$
\Phi_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D-a}{a}\right)
$$

*(2 marks — show the integration step and limits.)*

3. **The return wire carries $-I$ and its magnetic field also threads the same loop area in the same sense (the loop encloses both conductors).** Therefore, the total flux linkage is **twice** the single-wire contribution. *(2 marks — this is the key conceptual point.)*

4. With $D \gg a$:

$$
\boxed{L = \frac{\mu_0}{\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) \;\; \text{H/m}}
$$

*(2 marks — final expression with unit and the $D \gg a$ simplification.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

1. Apply Gauss's law to a cylindrical surface of radius $r$ ($a < r < b$) inside the coaxial cable:

$$
E = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon r}
$$

*(1 mark.)*

2. Integrate to find the potential difference:

$$
V = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

*(2 marks.)*

3. Capacitance per unit length:

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)} \;\; \text{F/m}}
$$

*(1 mark.)*

4. Inductance per unit length (from previous derivation or stated):

$$
L = \frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

*(1 mark.)*

5. Characteristic impedance:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \cdot \frac{\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)}{2\pi}\cdot 2\pi}
$$

Wait, let me write it cleanly:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

*(2 marks — clearly show that $Z_0$ is directly proportional to $\ln(b/a)$. This is the standard 50 Ω or 75 Ω design equation used in practice.)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in inductance:** Inductance derivations for two-wire and coaxial cables require the contribution from **both** conductors. The total flux linkage is $2\times$ the single-conductor contribution. Marks deducted: typically **2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign error in capacitance derivation:** The potential due to the **negative** line charge must **raise** the potential of the opposite conductor (or its sign must be carefully tracked). A common error is assigning both potentials the same sign, which gives $V = 0$. Marks deducted: **2-3 marks**.
> 3. **Units missing in the final answer:** Always write **F/m** and **H/m** explicitly. Marks deducted: **1 mark** per sub-part.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption $D \gg a$:** The simplification $\ln[(D-a)/a] \approx \ln(D/a)$ is only valid under this assumption. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing the two geometries:** Mixing up the formulas between two-wire and coaxial cable is a fatal error. Memorize the **structure**: two-wire has $\pi\varepsilon_0$ in numerator, coaxial has $2\pi\varepsilon$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two-wire line** capacitance: $C = \dfrac{\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/a)}$ F/m
- **Two-wire line** inductance: $L = \dfrac{\mu_0}{\pi}\ln(D/a)$ H/m
- **Coaxial cable** capacitance: $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$ F/m
- **Coaxial cable** inductance: $L = \dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)$ H/m
- The product $LC = \mu\varepsilon = 1/v^2$ — **geometry-independent propagation velocity** (KTU favorite).
- The ratio $L/C$ gives the **characteristic impedance** $Z_0$ — critical for signal matching.
- Gauss's law $\to$ capacitance; Ampere's law $\to$ inductance. **Master these two laws cold.**
- The assumption $D \gg a$ (for two-wire) must be **explicitly stated** in the exam.
- **External inductance** is what KTU typically asks; internal inductance is $L_{int} = \mu_0/(8\pi)$ per wire and is often neglected at high frequencies due to skin effect.
- Coaxial cables are **self-shielding** because the magnetic field outside is zero — a common 3-mark question.
- Always **box** the final answer and **mention units** to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Scalar and Vector Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Magnetic Vector Potential $\vec{A}$

In KTU 2024 Scheme Electromagnetics (PCECT501), the **magnetic vector potential** $\vec{A}$ is formally defined as a vector field whose curl produces the magnetic flux density:

$$\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$$

This definition arises directly from one of Maxwell's equations for magnetostatics, namely the solenoidal (divergence-free) nature of $\vec{B}$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$$

Because the divergence of any curl is identically zero ($\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = 0$), the existence of $\vec{A}$ is mathematically guaranteed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The vector potential $\vec{A}$ is **not unique**. If $\vec{A}$ is a valid potential, then so is $\vec{A}' = \vec{A} + \nabla \psi$ for any scalar field $\psi$. This freedom is known as **gauge freedom**, and fixing it is called choosing a **gauge condition**.

**Units of $\vec{A}$:** From $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$, the unit of $\vec{A}$ is **weber per meter (Wb/m)** or equivalently **tesla-meter (T·m)**.

### 1.2 The Magnetic Scalar Potential $V_m$

In current-free regions ($\vec{J} = 0$), Ampère's law reduces to:

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = 0$$

Since any irrotational vector can be written as the gradient of a scalar, we can define the **magnetic scalar potential** $V_m$ as:

$$\vec{H} = -\nabla V_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Pressure Field of a River:** Imagine $\vec{B}$ as the visible flow of water in a river (its vortices and eddies). The vector potential $\vec{A}$ is like the *stream function* describing the entire flow pattern — knowing $\vec{A}$ tells you everything about the circulation. The magnetic scalar potential $V_m$, on the other hand, is like a *pressure map* of the riverbed in a region with no eddies — useful only where the water flows smoothly without rotation.

### 1.3 Why Two Potentials?

| Potential | Domain of Validity | Source It Encodes |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| $\vec{A}$ (Vector) | All space, both inside and outside currents | Current distribution $\vec{J}$ |
| $V_m$ (Scalar) | Only in current-free regions ($\vec{J} = 0$) | Magnetic charges (conceptual) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\vec{A}$ is the **fundamental** potential; $V_m$ is a **specialized tool** used only for boundary-value problems where currents vanish. The KTU 2024 syllabus strongly emphasizes $\vec{A}$ for circuit and antenna problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need $\vec{A}$? The Operational Logic

**Step 1 — The Problem with $\vec{B}$ Alone:**
Magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ is a physical observable (measured by a compass or Hall probe). However, computing $\vec{B}$ directly from current distributions requires the Biot–Savart law, a difficult vector integral. For complex geometries, this becomes intractable.

**Step 2 — The Helmholtz Decomposition Insight:**
Any well-behaved vector field can be decomposed into a curl part and a gradient part. Since $\vec{B}$ is divergence-free, it is *purely* a curl — and therefore describable by a single vector field $\vec{A}$.

**Step 3 — The Poisson Equation Path:**
Substituting $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$ into Ampère's law ($\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$, with $\vec{H} = \vec{B}/\mu$) leads to a vector Poisson equation. This is a *linear second-order differential equation* with well-known solution techniques — vastly easier than the Biot–Savart integral.

**Step 4 — The Coulomb Gauge:**
The curl alone does not uniquely specify $\vec{A}$. We impose the **Coulomb gauge condition**:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$$

This decouples the three Cartesian components of $\vec{A}$, making each one satisfy a scalar Poisson equation.

### 2.2 Deriving the Equation for $\vec{A}$ — The Logical Flow

Starting from $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$ and $\vec{H} = \vec{B}/\mu$:

1. Apply Ampère's law: $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$
2. Substitute: $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = \mu \vec{J}$
3. Use the vector identity: $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{A}) - \nabla^2 \vec{A}$
4. Apply Coulomb gauge ($\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$):

$$\nabla^2 \vec{A} = -\mu \vec{J}$$

This is the **vector Poisson equation** for $\vec{A}$, the cornerstone of magnetostatic potential theory.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------------|------------|--------------|
| 1 | Definition of $\vec{A}$ | $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$ | $\vec{A}$ in **Wb/m** |
| 2 | Coulomb gauge | $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$ | Standard choice in KTU problems |
| 3 | Vector Poisson equation | $\nabla^2 \vec{A} = -\mu \vec{J}$ | $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ in **H/m** |
| 4 | Solution for $\vec{A}$ | $\vec{A} = \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi} \int \dfrac{\vec{J}}{\mathcal{R}} \, dv$ | Volume integral form |
| 5 | Line current form | $\vec{A} = \dfrac{\mu I}{4\pi} \oint \dfrac{d\vec{l}}{\mathcal{R}}$ | $\mathcal{R}$ is source-to-field distance |
| 6 | Scalar potential definition | $\vec{H} = -\nabla V_m$ | Valid only if $\vec{J} = 0$ |
| 7 | Laplace equation for $V_m$ | $\nabla^2 V_m = 0$ | In source-free region |
| 8 | Gauge transformation | $\vec{A}' = \vec{A} + \nabla \psi$ | $\psi$ any scalar field |
| 9 | $\vec{B}$ from $V_m$ | $\vec{B} = -\mu \nabla V_m$ | In linear media, current-free region |
| 10 | Permeability of free space | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | **H/m** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **antenna theory**, $\vec{A}$ is the bridge between the current on the antenna and the radiated electric field. In **transformer and inductor design**, $\vec{A}$ provides a direct path from winding currents to stored magnetic energy. In **plasma physics and particle accelerators**, $V_m$ is used to design magnetic confinement fields.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Existence of $\vec{A}$ from $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$

**Given:** Maxwell's equation for magnetostatics $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$.

**Goal:** Show that there exists a vector field $\vec{A}$ such that $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$.

**Step 1 — Recall the vector identity:**
For *any* sufficiently smooth vector field $\vec{F}$:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) \equiv 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the identity with $\vec{F} = \vec{A}$:**

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the given condition:**
If we set $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$, then $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = \nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = 0$, which exactly matches the magnetostatic condition.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
Therefore, the representation $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$ is **always valid** for any magnetostatic field. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Vector Poisson Equation for $\vec{A}$

**Given:**
- $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$
- $\vec{H} = \vec{B} / \mu$ (linear, homogeneous, isotropic medium)
- $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$ (Ampère's law, magnetostatic)

**Goal:** Derive $\nabla^2 \vec{A} = -\mu \vec{J}$.

**Step 1 — Substitute $\vec{H} = \vec{B}/\mu$ into Ampère's law:**

$$\nabla \times \left(\frac{\vec{B}}{\mu}\right) = \vec{J}$$

**Step 2 — Take $\mu$ outside the curl (homogeneous medium):**

$$\frac{1}{\mu}(\nabla \times \vec{B}) = \vec{J}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$:**

$$\frac{1}{\mu}\left[\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{A})\right] = \vec{J}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the BAC–CAB vector identity:**

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{A}) - \nabla^2 \vec{A}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**

$$\frac{1}{\mu}\left[\nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{A}) - \nabla^2 \vec{A}\right] = \vec{J}$$

**Step 6 — Impose the Coulomb gauge $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$:**

$$\frac{1}{\mu}\left[\nabla(0) - \nabla^2 \vec{A}\right] = \vec{J}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify and rearrange:**

$$-\frac{1}{\mu}\nabla^2 \vec{A} = \vec{J}$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \vec{A} = -\mu \vec{J}} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Integral Solution (Biot–Savart Form) for $\vec{A}$

**Given:** The vector Poisson equation $\nabla^2 \vec{A} = -\mu \vec{J}$ in free space, with $\vec{A} \to 0$ at infinity.

**Step 1 — Compare with the scalar Poisson equation:**
The scalar Poisson equation $\nabla^2 V = -\rho/\varepsilon_0$ has the well-known solution:

$$V = \frac{1}{4\pi \varepsilon_0}\int \frac{\rho}{\mathcal{R}} \, dv$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the structural analogy:**
Vector Poisson's equation in Cartesian coordinates decouples into three scalar Poisson equations (one per component, courtesy of the Coulomb gauge):

$$\nabla^2 A_x = -\mu J_x, \quad \nabla^2 A_y = -\mu J_y, \quad \nabla^2 A_z = -\mu J_z$$

**Step 3 — Apply the scalar Poisson solution to each component:**

$$A_x = \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\int \frac{J_x}{\mathcal{R}} \, dv, \quad A_y = \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\int \frac{J_y}{\mathcal{R}} \, dv, \quad A_z = \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\int \frac{J_z}{\mathcal{R}} \, dv$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a single vector equation:**

$$\boxed{\vec{A} = \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\int \frac{\vec{J}(\vec{r'})}{\mathcal{R}} \, dv'}$$

where $\mathcal{R} = \vert \vec{r} - \vec{r'} \vert$ is the distance from the source point to the field point. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The prime on the source variable $dv'$ and $\vec{r'}$ is essential in the integrand. Students frequently lose marks for omitting the prime notation, which leads to ambiguity between source and field coordinates.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Laplacian of the Scalar Magnetic Potential

**Given:** Current-free region where $\nabla \times \vec{H} = 0$ and $\vec{H} = -\nabla V_m$.

**Step 1 — Start with the no-monopole condition (in linear media):**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0 \implies \nabla \cdot (\mu \vec{H}) = 0$$

**Step 2 — For homogeneous $\mu$, take it outside the divergence:**

$$\mu \, \nabla \cdot \vec{H} = 0 \implies \nabla \cdot \vec{H} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\vec{H} = -\nabla V_m$:**

$$\nabla \cdot (-\nabla V_m) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using the identity $\nabla \cdot (\nabla f) = \nabla^2 f$:**

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 V_m = 0}$$

This is **Laplace's equation** for $V_m$, valid only in source-free regions. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: From Current to Magnetic Field via Potentials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Current Distribution J_r_prime] --> A1[Source Region]
    A1 --> A2[Apply Vector Poisson Equation]
    A2 --> A3[Integrate J over Volume]
    A3 --> A4[Vector Potential A at Field Point r]
    A4 --> A5[Apply Curl Operator]
    A5 --> A6[Magnetic Flux Density B at r]
    A4 --> A7[Impose Coulomb Gauge div A equals 0]
    A7 --> A8[Decoupled Scalar Poisson Equations]
    A8 --> A3
    A6 --> A9[Apply Constitutive Relation H equals B over mu]
    A9 --> A10[Magnetic Field Intensity H]
    A10 --> A11{Current Free Region?}
    A11 -->|Yes| A12[Define Scalar Potential Vm]
    A12 --> A13[Apply Negative Gradient]
    A13 --> A14[Recover H from Vm]
    A11 -->|No| A15[Use H directly from Ampere Law]
    style A0 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style A6 fill:#FFE5CC,stroke:#FF7F0E
    style A14 fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#2CA02C
    style A11 fill:#FFF5CC,stroke:#BCBD22
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Map: Gauge Freedom and Unique $\vec{B}$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Field B] --> B2{Decomposition Theorem}
    B2 --> B3[Curl Part equals B]
    B2 --> B4[Gradient Part equals 0]
    B3 --> B5[Choose A such that curl A equals B]
    B5 --> B6{Gauge Condition}
    B6 -->|Coulomb| B7[div A equals 0]
    B6 -->|Lorentz| B8[Time varying only]
    B6 -->|No Gauge| B9[Ambiguous A]
    B7 --> C1[Unique A in Coulomb Gauge]
    B8 --> C2[Unique A in Lorentz Gauge]
    B9 --> C3[Many Possible A]
    C1 --> D1[Compute B via curl A]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Same B always]
    style B1 fill:#FFE5CC,stroke:#FF7F0E
    style D2 fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#2CA02C
    style B6 fill:#FFF5CC,stroke:#BCBD22
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The top diagram shows the *computational pipeline* — how $\vec{J}$ produces $\vec{A}$, and how $\vec{A}$ produces $\vec{B}$. The bottom diagram illustrates *gauge freedom* — the same $\vec{B}$ can be generated by infinitely many $\vec{A}$'s, but imposing a gauge (Coulomb or Lorentz) selects one specific $\vec{A}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Pattern]**

**Q1.** Define magnetic vector potential $\vec{A}$. Show that $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$ is consistent with the Maxwell equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$.

**Model Answer:**
The magnetic vector potential $\vec{A}$ is a vector field such that the magnetic flux density is its curl: $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$. Taking the divergence of both sides:
$$\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = \nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = 0$$
since the divergence of any curl is identically zero. This matches Maxwell's equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$, confirming the consistency of the definition. The unit of $\vec{A}$ is **Wb/m**. *(3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

**Q2.** State and explain the Coulomb gauge condition. Why is it imposed when solving for $\vec{A}$?

**Model Answer:**
The Coulomb gauge condition is $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$. It is imposed because the curl alone does not uniquely specify $\vec{A}$ — adding any irrotational field $\nabla \psi$ gives the same $\vec{B}$. Imposing $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$ decouples the vector Poisson equation $\nabla^2 \vec{A} = -\mu \vec{J}$ into three independent scalar Poisson equations, one for each Cartesian component, enabling straightforward integration. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model ESE Pattern, Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Starting from $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$ and Ampère's law, derive the vector Poisson equation for $\vec{A}$ in free space. State clearly the gauge condition used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A long straight wire of infinite length carries a steady current $I$ along the $z$-axis. Using the integral definition of $\vec{A}$, find the magnetic vector potential at a point located at perpendicular distance $\rho$ from the wire. Hence compute the magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1:** Write $\vec{H} = \vec{B}/\mu_0$ for free space. Ampère's law (magnetostatic): $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute $\vec{H}$:
$$\nabla \times \left(\frac{\vec{B}}{\mu_0}\right) = \vec{J}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$:
$$\nabla \times \left[\nabla \times \vec{A}\right] = \mu_0 \vec{J}$$

**Step 4:** Use the BAC–CAB identity:
$$\nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{A}) - \nabla^2 \vec{A} = \mu_0 \vec{J}$$

**Step 5:** Impose the **Coulomb gauge** $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$:
$$-\nabla^2 \vec{A} = \mu_0 \vec{J}$$

**Step 6:** Final result:
$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \vec{A} = -\mu_0 \vec{J}}$$

**Gauge used:** Coulomb gauge, $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Set up the line integral form:**
For a line current:
$$\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{d\vec{l}'}{\mathcal{R}}$$

**Step 2 — Geometry:** Let the field point be at perpendicular distance $\rho$ in the $xy$-plane. Take the wire along the $z$-axis, so $d\vec{l}' = dz' \, \hat{a}_z$. The source-to-field distance is:
$$\mathcal{R} = \sqrt{\rho^2 + z'^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$$\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi}\hat{a}_z \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{dz'}{\sqrt{\rho^2 + z'^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the standard integral:**
$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{dz'}{\sqrt{\rho^2 + z'^2}} = \left[\ln\left(z' + \sqrt{\rho^2 + z'^2}\right)\right]_{-\infty}^{+\infty}$$

**Step 5 — Apply limits:** The integrand diverges logarithmically, reflecting the fact that $\vec{A}$ for an infinite wire is defined only up to a constant. We obtain:
$$\vec{A} = -\frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi}\ln(\rho) \, \hat{a}_z + \text{const.}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\vec{B}$ via the curl in cylindrical coordinates:**
$$\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A} = -\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial \rho}\hat{a}_\phi = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi \rho}\hat{a}_\phi$$

This matches Ampère's law result. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the integral expression for the magnetic vector potential:
$$\vec{A} = \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\int \frac{\vec{J}}{\mathcal{R}} \, dv$$
starting from the vector Poisson equation $\nabla^2 \vec{A} = -\mu \vec{J}$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A finite straight conductor of length $L$ lies along the $z$-axis from $z = -L/2$ to $z = +L/2$ and carries a current $I$. Find $\vec{A}$ at a point on the perpendicular bisector (i.e., on the $x$-axis at distance $\rho$). Hence find the limiting value of $\vec{B}$ as $L \to \infty$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Decouple the vector equation:**
Under the Coulomb gauge, the three Cartesian components of $\vec{A}$ satisfy:
$$\nabla^2 A_i = -\mu J_i, \quad i = x, y, z$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the scalar Poisson equation** $\nabla^2 V = -\rho/\varepsilon_0$, whose well-known solution is:
$$V(\vec{r}) = \frac{1}{4\pi \varepsilon_0}\int \frac{\rho(\vec{r'})}{\mathcal{R}} \, dv'$$
where $\mathcal{R} = \vert \vec{r} - \vec{r'} \vert$.

**Step 3 — Apply the analogy to each component:**
$$A_i(\vec{r}) = \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\int \frac{J_i(\vec{r'})}{\mathcal{R}} \, dv'$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a vector equation:**
$$\boxed{\vec{A}(\vec{r}) = \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\int \frac{\vec{J}(\vec{r'})}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r'} \vert} \, dv'}$$

**Physical meaning:** Each current element $\vec{J} \, dv'$ contributes a vector potential inversely proportional to the distance $\mathcal{R}$ from source to field point. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Use the line current form:**
$$\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi}\hat{a}_z \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} \frac{dz'}{\sqrt{\rho^2 + z'^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**
Using $\int \frac{dz'}{\sqrt{\rho^2 + z'^2}} = \sinh^{-1}\left(\frac{z'}{\rho}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{z' + \sqrt{\rho^2 + z'^2}}{\rho}\right)$:

$$\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi}\hat{a}_z \left[\ln\left(\frac{L/2 + \sqrt{\rho^2 + L^2/4}}{\rho}\right) - \ln\left(\frac{-L/2 + \sqrt{\rho^2 + L^2/4}}{\rho}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify (using symmetry arguments):**
By symmetry, the result can be written as:
$$\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi}\hat{a}_z \ln\left(\frac{\sqrt{\rho^2 + L^2/4} + L/2}{\sqrt{\rho^2 + L^2/4} - L/2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit $L \to \infty$:**
For $L \gg \rho$, $\sqrt{\rho^2 + L^2/4} \approx L/2$, giving:
$$\vec{A} \approx -\frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi}\ln(\rho) \, \hat{a}_z$$

This matches the infinite-wire result from Q.A(b). *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Missing gauge condition (–2 marks):** When deriving the vector Poisson equation, students often omit the statement "using the Coulomb gauge $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$." Without it, the derivation is incomplete.
> 2. **Confusing source and field coordinates (–1 mark):** Always use $\vec{r'}$ for the source (integration) variable and $\vec{r}$ for the field (observation) point. Writing $\vec{r}$ inside the integrand is a fatal error.
> 3. **Units not stated (–1 mark):** KTU 2024 examiners expect $\vec{A}$ in **Wb/m** and $\mu_0$ quoted explicitly as $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{4\pi}$ factor in the Biot–Savart-like form (–1 mark):** A classic slip-up that loses an easy mark.
> 5. **Applying $V_m$ where $\vec{J} \neq 0$ (–2 marks):** The scalar magnetic potential is **only valid** in current-free regions. Using it inside a current-carrying wire yields nonsense.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Magnetic Scalar and Vector Potential**

- **Definition of $\vec{A}$:** $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$; units **Wb/m**; ensures $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$ automatically.
- **Gauge freedom:** $\vec{A}$ is not unique. Choose **Coulomb gauge** $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$ for magnetostatics.
- **Vector Poisson equation:** $\nabla^2 \vec{A} = -\mu \vec{J}$ — the central differential equation of the topic.
- **Integral solution (Biot–Savart form):** $\vec{A} = \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}\int \dfrac{\vec{J}(\vec{r'})}{\mathcal{R}} \, dv'$; use $\mathcal{R} = \vert \vec{r} - \vec{r'} \vert$.
- **Line current form:** $\vec{A} = \dfrac{\mu I}{4\pi}\oint \dfrac{d\vec{l}'}{\mathcal{R}}$; useful for thin wires.
- **Magnetic scalar potential $V_m$:** Defined only in current-free regions via $\vec{H} = -\nabla V_m$.
- **Laplace's equation for $V_m$:** $\nabla^2 V_m = 0$ in source-free, homogeneous, linear media.
- **Always quote $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m** in numerical problems.
- **Engineering applications:** Antenna radiation, inductor and transformer design, magnetic recording, plasma confinement, MRI imaging.
- **Common KTU 2024 marks traps:** Missing gauge condition, swapped $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{r'}$, wrong unit, and misapplying $V_m$ inside current-carrying regions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisson and Laplace Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Poisson and Laplace Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Poisson's Equation** is a second-order partial differential equation that relates the Laplacian of the electric scalar potential $V$ to the local volume charge density $\rho_v$ in a region of space. In its most general vector form, it is expressed as:
> $$\nabla^2 V = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$
> where $\varepsilon$ is the **permittivity** of the medium (in free space, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \approx 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Laplace's Equation** is the special, homogeneous case of Poisson's equation, valid in regions of space that are entirely free of charge ($\rho_v = 0$). It is expressed as:
> $$\nabla^2 V = 0$$

The operator $\nabla^2$ (read as "del-squared" or "Laplacian") is a **second-order scalar differential operator** defined as the divergence of the gradient:
$$\nabla^2 V = \nabla \cdot (\nabla V)$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]  
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Heat Map" of the Electromagnetic Field:**  
> Imagine an unevenly heated metal plate. Wherever a heat source (a flame) is placed, the temperature rises sharply; in regions far from any source, the temperature is smooth and settles into a gentle curve. **Poisson's equation** is the mathematical "blueprint" describing the temperature everywhere — it accounts for *internal sources* (the flames, analogous to charges). **Laplace's equation** is the same blueprint *but in source-free regions* — it only describes how the potential gracefully curves and settles in the empty space between charges, with no internal heat added.

In electromagnetic terms, **Poisson's equation** is the master equation that tells us the entire shape of the electric potential $V$ in any region, given the charge distribution. Wherever there are no charges, the equation gracefully reduces to **Laplace's equation**, telling us only that the potential must be smooth, continuous, and have no local maxima or minima inside the region.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 \approx 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Relative permittivity:** $\varepsilon_r$ (dimensionless, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$)
- **Units of $V$:** Volts (V)
- **Units of $\rho_v$:** Coulombs per cubic meter ($\text{C/m}^3$)
- **Units of $\nabla^2 V$:** Volts per square meter ($\text{V/m}^2$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Foundation — The "Why" Behind the Equation

The Poisson/Laplace formulation is built on **three foundational pillars** of vector calculus and electrostatics:

1. **Gauss's Law in Differential (Point) Form:**
$$\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$

2. **The Definition of Electric Potential (Gradient Relationship):**
$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V$$

3. **The Vector Identity (Divergence of a Gradient gives the Laplacian):**
$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla V) = \nabla^2 V$$

### 2.2 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

- **Step 1:** Start with Gauss's law in point form: $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_v / \varepsilon$
- **Step 2:** Substitute the conservative field identity $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ to get: $\nabla \cdot (-\nabla V) = \rho_v / \varepsilon$
- **Step 3:** Pull the constant out: $-\nabla \cdot (\nabla V) = \rho_v / \varepsilon$
- **Step 4:** Recognize $\nabla \cdot (\nabla V) = \nabla^2 V$, yielding **Poisson's equation:** $\nabla^2 V = -\rho_v / \varepsilon$
- **Step 5:** If $\rho_v = 0$ (source-free region), we trivially obtain **Laplace's equation:** $\nabla^2 V = 0$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** The *negative* sign on the right-hand side arises because the electric field points from **high to low** potential, hence $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$. A positive charge $\rho_v$ creates a *negative curvature* in the potential landscape (a bowl shape pointing down), making the Laplacian negative.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation** | **Cartesian Form** | **When to Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Poisson | $\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = -\dfrac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$ | Regions containing volume charge density $\rho_v$ |
| Laplace | $\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = 0$ | Source-free regions ($\rho_v = 0$) |
| Cylindrical Poisson | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = -\dfrac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$ | Cylindrical symmetry (coaxial cables, line charges) |
| Spherical Poisson | $\dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^2 \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2 \sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2 \sin^2\theta}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} = -\dfrac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$ | Spherical symmetry (point charges, spheres) |
| Gradient-Field relation | $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ | Recover $\vec{E}$ from $V$ |
| Continuity of $V$ at boundary | $V_1 = V_2$ | Across any physical interface |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Boundary Conditions in the Cheat Sheet:**  
> - **Dirichlet (First kind):** Potential $V$ is specified on the boundary surface.  
> - **Neumann (Second kind):** Normal derivative $\partial V / \partial n$ is specified on the boundary.  
> - **Mixed (Third kind):** A linear combination of $V$ and $\partial V/\partial n$ is specified.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Capacitor & Transmission Line Design:** Engineers solve Laplace's equation in the dielectric region between capacitor plates to find fringing fields and breakdown voltages.
- **Semiconductor Device Physics:** Poisson's equation governs the built-in potential across p-n junctions in diodes and MOSFETs.
- **High-Voltage Insulator Design:** The potential distribution along ceramic insulators is solved using Laplace's equation with mixed boundary conditions.
- **Medical Imaging & Bioelectromagnetics:** Potential mapping around biological tissues (e.g., ECG/Electroencephalography inverse problems) is a Laplace-based problem.
- **Microwave & RF Engineering:** Field distribution inside waveguides and resonators is governed by the Laplacian operator.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Poisson's Equation from Gauss's Law

We begin with Gauss's law in **differential (point) form**:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$$

This is valid at every point in space. For a linear, isotropic, and homogeneous medium, the constitutive relation $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ holds, so we can substitute:

$$\nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \vec{E}) = \rho_v$$

Since $\varepsilon$ is a constant in a homogeneous medium, we can pull it outside the divergence operator:

$$\varepsilon \, \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_v$$

Dividing both sides by $\varepsilon$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$

Next, we invoke the **definition of electric potential**, which states that the electric field is the negative gradient of the scalar potential $V$:

$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V$$

Substituting this expression into the divergence equation gives:

$$\nabla \cdot (-\nabla V) = \frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$

Pulling the constant scalar $(-1)$ outside the divergence operator:

$$-\nabla \cdot (\nabla V) = \frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$

By the vector identity $\nabla \cdot (\nabla V) = \nabla^2 V$, the left-hand side becomes the **Laplacian of $V$**:

$$-\nabla^2 V = \frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$, we arrive at the celebrated **Poisson's equation**:

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 V = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Logical Narrative:** We moved from a *local flux balance* (Gauss's law) to a *differential equation in a single scalar function* $V$. This is a profound simplification because solving one scalar PDE replaces the problem of finding a full vector field $\vec{E}(x,y,z)$ with three components.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Uniqueness Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Theorem Statement (Uniqueness Theorem):**  
> The solution to Poisson's (or Laplace's) equation within a closed region $V$ is unique if the potential $V$ is specified on the boundary surface $S$ (Dirichlet condition), or its normal derivative $\partial V/\partial n$ is specified on $S$ (Neumann condition).

**Proof by Contradiction:**

Assume two distinct solutions $V_1$ and $V_2$ both satisfy Poisson's equation inside the region and the same Dirichlet boundary condition on the surface $S$. Define a difference function:

$$V_d = V_1 - V_2$$

Since both $V_1$ and $V_2$ individually satisfy Poisson's equation:

$$\nabla^2 V_1 = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{and} \quad \nabla^2 V_2 = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$

Subtracting, we get:

$$\nabla^2 (V_1 - V_2) = \nabla^2 V_d = 0$$

This means $V_d$ satisfies **Laplace's equation** inside the volume. Also, on the boundary $S$, since $V_1 = V_2$:

$$V_d \big|_S = 0$$

Now apply **Green's first identity** (a vector calculus identity):

$$\int_V \left( V_d \, \nabla^2 V_d + \vert \nabla V_d \vert^2 \right) dV = \oint_S V_d \, \frac{\partial V_d}{\partial n} \, dS$$

Since $\nabla^2 V_d = 0$ in the volume and $V_d = 0$ on the surface, both terms vanish:

$$\int_V \vert \nabla V_d \vert^2 \, dV = 0$$

The integrand $\vert \nabla V_d \vert^2 \geq 0$ everywhere. For the volume integral to equal zero, the integrand must be zero at *every* point in the volume:

$$\nabla V_d = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_d = \text{constant}$$

But since $V_d = 0$ on the boundary, this constant must be zero. Therefore:

$$V_d = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = V_2$$

The solution is **unique**. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Physical Interpretation:** The uniqueness theorem is the mathematical license that allows us to *guess* a solution and then verify that it satisfies both the differential equation and the boundary conditions. If it does, it is the *only* correct answer.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Solving Laplace's Equation in 1D

**Problem:** In a charge-free region $0 \le x \le a$, the potential satisfies Laplace's equation with boundary conditions $V(0) = 0$ and $V(a) = V_0$. Find $V(x)$.

**Solution:** Laplace's equation in 1D reduces to:

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = 0$$

Integrating once:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = A$$

Integrating a second time:

$$V(x) = A x + B$$

Apply boundary condition $V(0) = 0$:

$$V(0) = A(0) + B = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 0$$

Apply boundary condition $V(a) = V_0$:

$$V(a) = A a = V_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{V_0}{a}$$

Therefore, the unique potential distribution is:

$$\boxed{V(x) = \frac{V_0}{a} x}$$

The associated electric field:

$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\frac{dV}{dx} \hat{a}_x = -\frac{V_0}{a} \hat{a}_x$$

A uniform field, as expected between two parallel equipotential plates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Derivation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gauss Law Differential Form] --> B[nabla dot D equals rho sub v]
    B --> C[Constitutive Relation D equals epsilon E]
    C --> D[nabla dot E equals rho over epsilon]
    D --> E[Substitute E equals minus nabla V]
    E --> F[minus nabla squared V equals rho over epsilon]
    F --> G[Poisson Equation]
    G --> H{Special Case rho sub v equals 0}
    H -- Yes --> I[Laplace Equation nabla squared V equals 0]
    H -- No --> J[General Poisson Equation]
    I --> K[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Unique Solution V of x y z]
    L --> M[Recover E field using E equals minus nabla V]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Solution Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Problem Statement] --> Q[Identify Symmetry Cartesian Cylindrical Spherical]
    Q --> R[Write Laplacian in Chosen Coordinate System]
    R --> S[Determine Boundary Conditions Dirichlet Neumann]
    S --> T[Solve ODE or PDE]
    T --> U[Apply BCs to Find Constants]
    U --> V[Final Expression for V]
    V --> W[Take Gradient to Find E]
```

### 4.3 Coordinate System Selection Guide (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start] --> Sys{Type of Geometry}
    Sys -- Parallel plates, rectangular --> Cart[Use Cartesian Coordinates]
    Sys -- Coaxial cable, infinite line --> Cyl[Use Cylindrical Coordinates]
    Sys -- Point charge, sphere --> Sph[Use Spherical Coordinates]
    Cart --> Out[Write Laplacian and Solve]
    Cyl --> Out
    Sph --> Out
    Out --> Done[Apply Boundary Conditions]
```

> [!TIP]  
> **Diagram Note:** Since this topic is mathematical, the diagrams above are **block-level functional architecture flows** mapping the conceptual and operational relationships between the governing equations, the choice of coordinate system, and the solution procedure — as mandated for non-physical-drawing topics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**State and explain Poisson's and Laplace's equations. Mention their physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Poisson's Equation:** $\nabla^2 V = -\dfrac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$ — relates the Laplacian of the electric potential $V$ to the volume charge density $\rho_v$ at every point in space. **(1 mark)**

- **Laplace's Equation:** $\nabla^2 V = 0$ — special case of Poisson's equation valid in charge-free regions ($\rho_v = 0$). **(1 mark)**

- **Physical Significance:** Poisson's equation enables the determination of the electric potential $V$ in regions containing distributed charges, while Laplace's equation governs the potential in charge-free regions. Together they form the foundation for solving all electrostatic boundary-value problems. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
**State the uniqueness theorem for Poisson's equation. Why is it important in electrostatics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Statement:** A solution to Poisson's equation in a closed region is *unique* if the potential $V$ is specified on the entire boundary surface (Dirichlet condition) or if the normal derivative $\partial V / \partial n$ is specified on the boundary (Neumann condition). **(1.5 marks)**

- **Importance:** The uniqueness theorem justifies the method of *educated guessing*. An engineer can propose a candidate solution, verify that it satisfies both the differential equation and the boundary conditions, and thereby be assured that the proposed solution is the only physically correct answer — no further work is needed. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> 1. **Sign Error:** Students frequently write $\nabla^2 V = +\rho_v/\varepsilon$ (forgetting the negative). The negative sign comes from $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ and is non-negotiable. Lose 1 mark if reversed.  
> 2. **Forgetting the 1D case reduction:** When $\partial^2 V/\partial y^2 = \partial^2 V/\partial z^2 = 0$, Laplace reduces to $d^2V/dx^2 = 0$ — write it out explicitly, do not skip steps.  
> 3. **Boundary condition substitution:** Apply the boundary conditions at the correct *limits*; mixing up $V(0)$ and $V(a)$ is a common 1-mark deduction.  
> 4. **Units:** Always carry $\varepsilon_0$ explicitly if no medium is mentioned; do not silently set it to 1.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) Derive Poisson's equation starting from Gauss's law in differential form. State Laplace's equation as a special case.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Start with Gauss's law in point form: $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ **(1 mark)**
2. Use the constitutive relation for a linear, isotropic, homogeneous medium: $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ **(1 mark)**
3. Substitute to get: $\varepsilon \, \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_v \Rightarrow \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_v / \varepsilon$ **(1 mark)**
4. Use the potential definition: $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ **(1 mark)**
5. Substitute: $\nabla \cdot (-\nabla V) = \rho_v / \varepsilon$ **(1 mark)**
6. Apply vector identity: $\nabla \cdot \nabla V = \nabla^2 V$, leading to: $-\nabla^2 V = \rho_v / \varepsilon$ **(1 mark)**
7. Final form: $\nabla^2 V = -\rho_v / \varepsilon$. For $\rho_v = 0$, we get $\nabla^2 V = 0$ — Laplace's equation. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Given a region $0 \le x \le a$ in free space with $\rho_v = 0$ and boundary conditions $V(0) = V_0$ and $V(a) = 0$, find $V(x)$ and the corresponding electric field $\vec{E}$.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Since $\rho_v = 0$ and $V$ depends only on $x$, Laplace's equation reduces to: $\dfrac{d^2V}{dx^2} = 0$ **(1 mark)**
2. First integration: $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = A$ **(1 mark)**
3. Second integration: $V(x) = A x + B$ **(1 mark)**
4. Apply $V(0) = V_0$: $A(0) + B = V_0 \Rightarrow B = V_0$ **(1 mark)**
5. Apply $V(a) = 0$: $A a + V_0 = 0 \Rightarrow A = -V_0 / a$ **(1 mark)**
6. Final potential: $\boxed{V(x) = V_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{a}\right)}$ **(1 mark)**
7. Electric field: $\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\dfrac{dV}{dx}\hat{a}_x = \dfrac{V_0}{a}\hat{a}_x$ (uniform, pointing in $+x$ direction) **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a) Derive the Laplacian operator in cylindrical coordinates. Write Poisson's equation in this form.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Start with the scale factors: $h_1 = 1$, $h_2 = r$, $h_3 = 1$ for cylindrical $(r, \phi, z)$ **(1 mark)**
2. Gradient in cylindrical coordinates:
$\nabla V = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi + \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$ **(1 mark)**
3. Divergence of a vector in cylindrical coordinates:
$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r A_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \dfrac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}$ **(1 mark)**
4. Apply divergence to the gradient expression; for $V_r = \partial V/\partial r$, $V_\phi = (1/r)\partial V/\partial \phi$, $V_z = \partial V/\partial z$: **(1 mark)**
5. Combine and simplify:
$\nabla^2 V = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2}$ **(1 mark)**
6. Multiply by $-\varepsilon$ to obtain Poisson's equation in cylindrical form **(1 mark)**
7. Final form:
$\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = -\dfrac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$ **(1 mark)**

**(b) Two infinite parallel plates at $x = 0$ (potential $V_0$) and $x = d$ (potential $0$) enclose a charge-free region. Find $V(x)$ and $\vec{E}$. Justify your choice of coordinate system using the uniqueness theorem.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Symmetry Identification:** Plates are parallel, infinite, and perpendicular to the $x$-axis. Hence $V$ varies only with $x$ — Cartesian coordinates are optimal. **(1 mark)**
2. **Governing Equation:** $\rho_v = 0$ gives Laplace's equation: $\dfrac{d^2V}{dx^2} = 0$ **(1 mark)**
3. **First integration:** $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = A$ **(0.5 marks)**
4. **Second integration:** $V(x) = A x + B$ **(0.5 marks)**
5. **Apply $V(0) = V_0$:** $B = V_0$ **(0.5 marks)**
6. **Apply $V(d) = 0$:** $A d + V_0 = 0 \Rightarrow A = -V_0/d$ **(0.5 marks)**
7. **Final Potential:** $\boxed{V(x) = V_0\left(1 - \dfrac{x}{d}\right)}$ **(1 mark)**
8. **Electric Field:** $\vec{E} = -\dfrac{dV}{dx}\hat{a}_x = \dfrac{V_0}{d}\hat{a}_x$ (uniform field in $+x$ direction) **(1 mark)**
9. **Uniqueness Justification:** The proposed $V(x)$ satisfies both the differential equation (Laplace's equation) and the two Dirichlet boundary conditions. By the uniqueness theorem, this is the only physically valid solution. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Poisson's Equation:** $\nabla^2 V = -\rho_v/\varepsilon$ — applicable wherever charge density $\rho_v \neq 0$.
- ✅ **Laplace's Equation:** $\nabla^2 V = 0$ — applicable in source-free regions ($\rho_v = 0$).
- ✅ **Derivation Chain:** Gauss's Law $\rightarrow$ Constitutive Relation $\rightarrow$ $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ $\rightarrow$ Poisson/Laplace.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Always negative on the RHS of Poisson's equation (originates from $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$).
- ✅ **Cartesian Laplacian:** $\nabla^2 V = \partial^2 V/\partial x^2 + \partial^2 V/\partial y^2 + \partial^2 V/\partial z^2$.
- ✅ **Cylindrical Laplacian:** $\nabla^2 V = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2}$.
- ✅ **Spherical Laplacian:** $\nabla^2 V = \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^2 \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2 \sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2 \sin^2\theta}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2}$.
- ✅ **Uniqueness Theorem:** If $V$ (Dirichlet) or $\partial V/\partial n$ (Neumann) is specified on the entire boundary, the solution is unique.
- ✅ **Recovery of $\vec{E}$:** Always recover the electric field via $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ after finding the potential.
- ✅ **Coordinate Selection:** Choose Cartesian, cylindrical, or spherical based on the geometric symmetry of the problem.
- ✅ **Boundary Conditions in 1D:** Apply two conditions (typically $V(0)$ and $V(a)$) to determine the two constants of integration $A$ and $B$.
- ✅ **Permittivity:** Use $\varepsilon_0$ for free space; $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ for dielectric media.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Voltage and Electric Field Using Laplace and Poisson's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Determination of Voltage and Electric Field Using Laplace and Poisson's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **electrostatics**, the scalar electric potential $V$ (measured in **Volts**) is related to the volume charge density $\rho_v$ (in **C/m³**) through **Poisson's Equation**. In any region of space, Poisson's equation is the fundamental second-order partial differential equation that governs the spatial distribution of electric potential when free charges are present:

$$\nabla^2 V = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$

where $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ is the **permittivity of the medium**, $\varepsilon_0 \approx 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** is the permittivity of free space, and $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity (dimensionless).

In **source-free regions** where $\rho_v = 0$ (i.e., no free charge exists at the point of interest), Poisson's equation collapses to **Laplace's Equation**:

$$\nabla^2 V = 0$$

This elegant simplification is the most frequently applied form in KTU problems, as it allows us to find potential distributions in charge-free dielectric regions bounded by conductors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to: (1) state both equations in all three coordinate systems, (2) derive them from Gauss's law, (3) apply them to 1-D geometries (parallel plates, coaxial cables, spherical shells), and (4) extract the electric field via $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Temperature Field" Picture

Imagine a thin metal plate being heated unevenly from underneath. Once the temperature reaches a **steady state** (no time variation), the temperature at every point on the plate stops changing. This steady-state temperature distribution satisfies Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 T = 0$, because no heat is being generated or absorbed inside the plate itself.

Now replace the temperature field with an **electric potential field**:
- The **heated boundaries** (where temperature is fixed) → **conductors held at known voltages** (Dirichlet boundary condition).
- The **insulated boundaries** (no heat flux across them) → **surfaces where the normal derivative of V is zero** (Neumann boundary condition).
- A **heat source inside the plate** → **a region of volume charge density $\rho_v$**, which upgrades Laplace's equation to Poisson's equation.

Just as the steady-state heat equation maps the temperature, **Poisson's and Laplace's equations map the electric potential** in the steady (time-independent) electrostatic world. The Electric Field is then simply the **negative gradient** of this potential "landscape" — pointing from "high voltage hills" down to "low voltage valleys."

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize for KTU 2024:**
> - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** (permittivity of free space)
> - $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ (permittivity of the medium)
> - $1 \, \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **J**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Flow — From Gauss's Law to Poisson's Equation

The derivation pathway that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce is:

1. **Start with Gauss's Law in point (differential) form:** The divergence of the electric flux density equals the volume charge density.
2. **Substitute the constitutive relation** between $\mathbf{D}$ and $\mathbf{E}$.
3. **Replace $\mathbf{E}$** with the negative gradient of potential.
4. **Recognize** that the divergence of a gradient is the Laplacian operator $\nabla^2$.
5. **Arrive at Poisson's equation**, then specialize to Laplace's equation when $\rho_v = 0$.

## 2.2 Expansion in the Three Standard Coordinate Systems

KTU 2024 specifically tests whether students can write the Laplacian in all three systems. The expansion is:

- **Cartesian** $(x, y, z)$:
  $$\nabla^2 V = \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2}$$

- **Cylindrical** $(\rho, \phi, z)$:
  $$\nabla^2 V = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial}{\partial \rho}\left(\rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\right) + \frac{1}{\rho^2}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2}$$

- **Spherical** $(r, \theta, \phi)$:
  $$\nabla^2 V = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2 \sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\sin\theta \frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2 \sin^2\theta}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Symmetry Shortcut (very high KTU yield):** Before solving, always reduce the equation using the problem's symmetry.
> - Two infinite parallel plates → depends only on **one Cartesian variable** (say $x$).
> - Infinite coaxial cable → depends only on the radial cylindrical variable $\rho$.
> - Spherical shell → depends only on $r$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **SI Unit** | **When to Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Poisson's Equation | $\nabla^2 V = -\rho_v / \varepsilon$ | V/m² | Region containing volume charge |
| Laplace's Equation | $\nabla^2 V = 0$ | V/m² | Source-free region ($\rho_v = 0$) |
| Electric Field from V | $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$ | V/m | Always, after $V$ is found |
| Cartesian E-field | $\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_x \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} - \hat{a}_y \frac{\partial V}{\partial y} - \hat{a}_z \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$ | V/m | Cartesian problems |
| Cylindrical E-field | $\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_\rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho} - \hat{a}_\phi \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi} - \hat{a}_z \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$ | V/m | Coaxial / cylindrical problems |
| Spherical E-field | $\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_r \frac{\partial V}{\partial r} - \hat{a}_\theta \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta} - \hat{a}_\phi \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}$ | V/m | Spherical problems |
| Permittivity of medium | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ | F/m | All capacitor / dielectric problems |
| Dirichlet Boundary | $V = V_0$ (specified value) | V | Conducting surfaces at fixed potential |
| Neumann Boundary | $\partial V / \partial n = 0$ | V/m | Symmetry planes or insulated surfaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do NOT use the symbol** $\vert V \vert$ **inside markdown tables.** KTU valuation scripts will break the table rendering. Always use the form $V(x,y,z)$ or simply write "V" in tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coaxial cables and transmission lines:** Laplace's equation in cylindrical coordinates gives the potential and field inside the dielectric between the inner and outer conductors, enabling calculation of characteristic impedance.
- **MOSFET and semiconductor device design:** Poisson's equation with a space-charge region determines the depletion width and capacitance of p-n junctions.
- **High-voltage bushings and insulators:** Field maps in 2-D are obtained by solving $\nabla^2 V = 0$ numerically (Finite Element Method, FEM).
- **Vacuum tube electrodes and electron guns:** Laplace's equation gives the trajectory-shaping electric field between biased electrodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Poisson's Equation from Gauss's Law

This derivation is a **sure-shot 7-mark question** in KTU Module 1.

**Starting point — Gauss's Law in point form:**

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_v$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the linear, isotropic constitutive relation $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E}$ (valid for homogeneous, linear dielectrics):

$$\nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \mathbf{E}) = \rho_v$$

**Step 2:** Pull the constant $\varepsilon$ out of the divergence operator (since the medium is homogeneous):

$$\varepsilon \, (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) = \rho_v$$

**Step 3:** Express the electric field as the negative gradient of the scalar potential $V$:

$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the divergence equation:

$$\varepsilon \, (\nabla \cdot (-\nabla V)) = \rho_v$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that the divergence of a gradient is, by definition, the **Laplacian operator**:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla V) = \nabla^2 V$$

**Step 6:** Combine the signs and constants:

$$-\varepsilon \, \nabla^2 V = \rho_v$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 V = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}}$$

**Step 7 (Specialization):** In any region where $\rho_v = 0$, the right-hand side vanishes, giving **Laplace's Equation:**

$$\nabla^2 V = 0$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Potential Between Two Parallel Plates (Cartesian, Poisson)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Classic KTU type):** Two infinite parallel conducting plates are located at $x = 0$ and $x = d$. The plates are held at potentials $V = 0$ (at $x=0$) and $V = V_0$ (at $x = d$). The region between the plates contains a uniform volume charge density $\rho_v$. Find $V(x)$ and $\mathbf{E}(x)$.

**Step 1 — Reduce by symmetry.** Since the plates are infinite in $y$ and $z$, the potential cannot depend on $y$ or $z$. Also, no $\phi$ or $\theta$ dependence exists. Hence $V = V(x)$ only.

**Step 2 — Write Poisson's equation in 1-D Cartesian:**

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 3 — First integration with respect to x:**

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon} x + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Second integration with respect to x:**

$$V(x) = -\frac{\rho_v}{2\varepsilon} x^2 + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary condition 1:** At $x = 0$, $V = 0$:

$$V(0) = -\frac{\rho_v}{2\varepsilon}(0)^2 + C_1(0) + C_2 = C_2 = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Apply boundary condition 2:** At $x = d$, $V = V_0$:

$$V_0 = -\frac{\rho_v}{2\varepsilon} d^2 + C_1 d$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $C_1$:**

$$C_1 d = V_0 + \frac{\rho_v d^2}{2\varepsilon}$$

$$C_1 = \frac{V_0}{d} + \frac{\rho_v d}{2\varepsilon}$$

**Step 8 — Assemble the final potential:**

$$V(x) = -\frac{\rho_v}{2\varepsilon} x^2 + \left(\frac{V_0}{d} + \frac{\rho_v d}{2\varepsilon}\right) x$$

**Step 9 — Extract the Electric Field** using $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$ and noting $V = V(x)$ only:

$$\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_x \frac{dV}{dx}$$

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon} x + \frac{V_0}{d} + \frac{\rho_v d}{2\varepsilon}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{E}(x) = -\hat{a}_x \left[ -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon} x + \frac{V_0}{d} + \frac{\rho_v d}{2\varepsilon} \right]}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{E}(x) = \hat{a}_x \left[ \frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon} x - \frac{V_0}{d} - \frac{\rho_v d}{2\varepsilon} \right]}$$

**Step 10 — Sanity check (Limiting Case):** If $\rho_v \to 0$, the result collapses to $\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_x V_0/d$, which is the standard uniform field of a parallel-plate capacitor. **✓**

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Coaxial Cable (Cylindrical, Laplace)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (High KTU Yield):** An infinitely long coaxial cable has an inner conductor of radius $a$ at potential $V_a$ and an outer conductor of radius $b$ at potential $V_b$. The dielectric between them has permittivity $\varepsilon$. Find $V(\rho)$ and $\mathbf{E}(\rho)$.
