## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the boundary conditions for the electric field $\mathbf{E}$ and the magnetic flux density $\mathbf{B}$ at the interface between two different dielectric media. State the Maxwell equation used to derive each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Field Component | Boundary Condition | Source Maxwell Equation |
|-----------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Tangential $\mathbf{E}$ | $E_{1t} = E_{2t}$ | Faraday's Law of Induction |
| Normal $\mathbf{B}$ | $B_{1n} = B_{2n}$ | Gauss's Law for Magnetism |

Both conditions hold for dielectric-dielectric interfaces with no free surface charge and no surface current. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the normal component of the magnetic flux density continuous across a boundary, while the normal component of the electric flux density is generally discontinuous?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The normal component of $\mathbf{B}$ is continuous because of **Gauss's Law for Magnetism**, which states that the net magnetic flux out of any closed surface is zero — there are no magnetic monopoles. Hence, $B_{1n} = B_{2n}$ always.

The normal component of $\mathbf{D}$ is generally discontinuous because of **Gauss's Law (Electric)**, which states that the net electric flux equals the enclosed free charge. If a free surface charge density $\rho_s$ exists on the interface, then $D_{1n} - D_{2n} = \rho_s \neq 0$. (3 marks)

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to specify the *direction* of the unit normal $\hat{n}$ — it must point from Medium 1 into Medium 2.
> - **Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Confusing $E_{1n} = E_{2n}$ (false) with $D_{1n} = D_{2n}$ (true only if $\rho_s = 0$). Always use the $\mathbf{D}$ condition first, then convert using $D_n = \varepsilon E_n$.
> - **Mistake 3 (–2 marks):** Not explicitly stating "let $\Delta h \to 0$" — examiners deduct marks if the limiting process is skipped.
> - **Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Writing the boundary conditions without naming the Maxwell equation they come from.

---

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a) [7 Marks] Derive the boundary condition for the tangential component of the electric field intensity at the interface between two dielectric media, starting from Faraday's Law of electromagnetic induction.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. State Faraday's law in integral form (1 mark):

$$
\oint_C \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_S \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{S}
$$

2. Sketch the geometry: rectangular loop of width $\Delta w$, height $\Delta h$, straddling the boundary. State the line integral splits into 4 segments. (1 mark)

3. Write the line integral as: $E_{1t}\Delta w - E_{2t}\Delta w + (\text{side wall terms}) \Delta h$ (1 mark)

4. Take $\Delta h \to 0$ — show side wall terms vanish; flux through loop area $\to 0$. (2 marks)

5. Final result: $E_{1t} = E_{2t}$ (2 marks).

**Q. (b) [7 Marks] Derive the boundary condition for the normal component of the electric flux density at the interface between two dielectric media, given that a free surface charge density $\rho_s$ exists on the interface.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. State Gauss's Law (Electric): $\oint_S \mathbf{D} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = \int_V \rho_v \, dV$ (1 mark)

2. Sketch the cylindrical pillbox geometry. (1 mark)

3. Write the closed surface integral as: $D_{1n}\Delta S - D_{2n}\Delta S + (\text{lateral wall})$ (1 mark)

4. Take $\Delta h \to 0$; show lateral term vanishes; volume integral reduces to $\rho_s \Delta S$. (2 marks)

5. Final result: $D_{1n} - D_{2n} = \rho_s$ (2 marks).

---

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a) [7 Marks] Derive the boundary condition for the normal component of the magnetic flux density at the interface between two magnetic media. Comment on its physical significance.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. State Gauss's Law for Magnetism: $\oint_S \mathbf{B}\cdot d\mathbf{S} = 0$ (1 mark)

2. Sketch pillbox of cross-section $\Delta S$, height $\Delta h$, with one face in each medium. (1 mark)

3. Expand surface integral: $B_{1n}\Delta S - B_{2n}\Delta S + (\text{lateral wall}) = 0$ (1 mark)

4. Take $\Delta h \to 0$; lateral contribution vanishes. (2 marks)

5. Result: $B_{1n} = B_{2n}$. Physical significance: This is a direct consequence of the non-existence of magnetic monopoles; magnetic field lines are continuous loops and have no "source" or "sink" (2 marks).

**Q. (b) [7 Marks] A dielectric medium with $\varepsilon_{r1} = 4$ is separated by a planar interface from another dielectric with $\varepsilon_{r2} = 2$. The electric field in Medium 1 makes an angle of 30° with the normal to the interface. If there is no free surface charge on the interface, find the angle made by the electric field with the normal in Medium 2.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant boundary conditions (1 mark):**
- $E_{1t} = E_{2t}$ (continuity of tangential E)
- $D_{1n} = D_{2n}$ (continuity of normal D, since $\rho_s = 0$)

**Step 2 — Decompose the fields (1 mark):**
$$
E_{1t} = E_1 \sin\theta_1, \quad E_{1n} = E_1 \cos\theta_1
$$
$$
E_{2t} = E_2 \sin\theta_2, \quad E_{2n} = E_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply tangential condition (1 mark):**

$$
E_1 \sin 30° = E_2 \sin\theta_2
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{\sin\theta_2}{\sin 30°}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply normal D condition (1 mark):**

$$
\varepsilon_1 E_{1n} = \varepsilon_2 E_{2n}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r1} E_1 \cos 30° = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r2} E_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

$$
\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{\varepsilon_{r2}\cos\theta_2}{\varepsilon_{r1}\cos 30°} = \frac{2\cos\theta_2}{4\cos 30°}
$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for $E_1/E_2$ (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{\sin\theta_2}{\sin 30°} = \frac{2\cos\theta_2}{4\cos 30°}
$$

$$
\frac{\sin\theta_2}{\cos\theta_2} = \frac{\sin 30°}{2\cos 30°} = \frac{0.5}{2\cdot (\sqrt{3}/2)} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\theta_2$ (2 marks):**

$$
\tan\theta_2 = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.2887
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_2 = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right) \approx 16.1°}
$$

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **four boundary conditions** arise from the four Maxwell equations by applying them to infinitesimal test geometries (rectangular loop for tangential, pillbox for normal).
- ✅ **Tangential $\mathbf{E}$ is always continuous** — derived from Faraday's Law by taking $\Delta h \to 0$ so that the enclosed flux vanishes.
- ✅ **Normal $\mathbf{D}$ has a jump** equal to the free surface charge density $\rho_s$. For pure dielectric-dielectric boundaries, $\rho_s = 0$ so $D_{1n} = D_{2n}$.
- ✅ **Tangential $\mathbf{H}$ has a jump** equal to the surface current density $\mathbf{J}_s$. For non-conducting interfaces, $J_s = 0$ so $H_{1t} = H_{2t}$.
- ✅ **Normal $\mathbf{B}$ is always continuous** — a direct consequence of the non-existence of magnetic monopoles.
- ✅ **Refraction of E-lines:** $\dfrac{\tan\theta_1}{\tan\theta_2} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_2}$ (for dielectric-dielectric, no $\rho_s$).
- ✅ **Refraction of B-lines:** $\dfrac{\tan\theta_1}{\tan\theta_2} = \dfrac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}$ (for dielectric-dielectric, no $\mathbf{J}_s$).
- ✅ **Perfect Conductor Limits:** Inside a PEC, $\mathbf{E} = 0$ and $\mathbf{B} = 0$. The surface current $\mathbf{J}_s = \hat{n}\times\mathbf{H}$ and surface charge $\rho_s = \hat{n}\cdot\mathbf{D}$ are the source terms on the surface.
- ✅ **Vector Form Quick Reference:**
  - $\hat{n}\times(\mathbf{E}_2-\mathbf{E}_1)=0$
  - $\hat{n}\cdot(\mathbf{D}_1-\mathbf{D}_2)=\rho_s$
  - $\hat{n}\times(\mathbf{H}_1-\mathbf{H}_2)=\mathbf{J}_s$
  - $\hat{n}\cdot(\mathbf{B}_1-\mathbf{B}_2)=0$
- ✅ **Always write the limiting process explicitly** — $\Delta h \to 0$ — to secure full marks.
- ✅ **Always state the Maxwell equation** before the derivation — examiners reward it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution to Wave Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solution to Wave Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
The **Wave Equation** in the context of transmission line theory is a second-order partial differential equation (PDE) that governs the propagation of voltage and current waves along a distributed parameter system. It arises directly from the Telegrapher's Equations by eliminating either voltage or current as a function of both position ($z$) and time ($t$).

For a **lossless transmission line** ($R = 0$, $G = 0$), the 1-D wave equation in terms of voltage is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V(z,t)}{\partial z^2} = LC \, \frac{\partial^2 V(z,t)}{\partial t^2}$$

The companion wave equation for current $I(z,t)$ takes an identical mathematical form. The general 1-D wave equation can be compactly written using the phase velocity parameter $u$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V(z,t)}{\partial z^2} = \frac{1}{u^2} \, \frac{\partial^2 V(z,t)}{\partial t^2}$$

where $u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ is the **phase velocity** (in m/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Wave Equation" in Module 2 refers to the one-dimensional scalar wave equation derived from the lossless Telegrapher's equations. Students are expected to derive it, state both the time-domain and sinusoidal steady-state solutions, and interpret the result physically as a superposition of forward and backward traveling waves.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Disturbance on a String" Analogy
Imagine a long, perfectly elastic string stretched horizontally. If you flick the string at one end, a "hump" travels along the string until it hits the other end. That hump is a **traveling wave**. Now imagine a long electrical cable carrying a high-frequency signal — the same physical phenomenon occurs, but instead of mechanical displacement, the quantities propagating are **voltage** and **current**.

The wave equation mathematically formalizes this idea: it says that the spatial "curvature" of a signal at a point on the line is proportional to the temporal "acceleration" of that signal at the same point. Whenever you see a disturbance whose shape changes in time, the wave equation is almost always the governing law.

- **L (H/m) and C (F/m)** are the per-unit-length inductance and capacitance of the line.
- They together store energy in magnetic and electric fields, respectively — this energy storage is what allows the disturbance to propagate.
- The product $LC$ determines how *fast* the wave travels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase velocity** is the speed at which a point of constant phase (e.g., a peak) of a single-frequency sinusoidal wave travels. In free space, $u = c$ (the speed of light $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s), because $L_0 = \mu_0$ and $C_0 = \varepsilon_0$, so $u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} = c$.

### 1.3 Lossless vs. General Wave Equation
- **Lossless line ($R = G = 0$):** Distortionless, dispersion-free propagation.
- **General lossy line:** An additional first-order derivative term appears:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = LC \, \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} + (RC + LG) \, \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + RG \, V$$

KTU's typical Module-2 focus is the **lossless** case, since it cleanly illustrates wave phenomena without the added complexity of attenuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Derivation Logic — From Telegrapher's to Wave Equation
The Telegrapher's equations for a lossless line are:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = -L \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial z} = -C \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate (1) with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = -L \, \frac{\partial^2 I}{\partial z \, \partial t}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate (2) with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 I}{\partial t \, \partial z} = -C \, \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of differentiation and substitute:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = LC \, \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works physically:** Eliminating $I$ (or $V$) gives a single equation in one unknown because the two telegrapher's equations are coupled. The result is a *second-order* equation in *both* $z$ and $t$, which is the hallmark of a wave equation.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Mathematics
- The wave equation is **second order in space** because propagation depends on the curvature (Laplacian-like) of the wave.
- It is **second order in time** because the system has inertia-like behavior (the line stores energy in $L$ and $C$).
- A wave equation always admits traveling-wave solutions — waves can move forward and backward, and any solution is a sum of these.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = LC \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$ | Lossless 1-D wave equation (voltage) | SI units |
| $u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Phase velocity | m/s |
| $V(z,t) = f^+(z - ut) + f^-(z + ut)$ | d'Alembert (general time-domain) solution | V |
| $V(z,t) = V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} e^{j\omega t} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z} e^{j\omega t}$ | Sinusoidal steady-state solution | V |
| $\beta = \omega \sqrt{LC} = \frac{\omega}{u}$ | Phase constant | rad/m |
| $\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{u}{f}$ | Wavelength | m |
| $k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | Wavenumber (alternate notation) | rad/m |
| $Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$ | Characteristic impedance | $\Omega$ |
| $I(z,t) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} e^{j\omega t} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z} e^{j\omega t}$ | Companion current solution | A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall Note:** In the current expression, the **minus sign** between the forward and backward terms is mandatory. It comes from substituting the voltage solution back into the telegrapher's equation. Skipping this sign is a common 1-mark deduction in KTU valuations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
The wave equation is the foundation of:
- **High-speed digital design (PCB trace analysis, signal integrity)** — used to predict reflections, ringing, and crosstalk.
- **RF and microwave engineering** — waveguides, microstrip lines, coaxial cables.
- **Optical fiber communication** — total internal reflection and modal dispersion are wave-equation phenomena.
- **Antenna feed networks** — voltage waves on the feed line excite radiating modes.
- **Power transmission** — traveling-wave protection schemes, lightning-surge analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lossless 1-D Wave Equation from Telegrapher's Equations

Starting with the lossless telegrapher's equations:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = -L \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}$$

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial z} = -C \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$

**Step 1:** Take $\partial / \partial z$ of the first equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = -L \, \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Swap the order of differentiation (Clairaut's theorem for smooth $I$):

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = -L \, \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{\partial I}{\partial z}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the second telegrapher's equation $\partial I / \partial z = -C \, \partial V / \partial t$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = -L \, \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(-C \, \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Pull the constant $C$ outside the time derivative:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = LC \, \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$$

This is the **lossless 1-D wave equation** in voltage. A similar procedure (differentiating (2) twice) gives the identical equation in $I(z,t)$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — d'Alembert's General Time-Domain Solution

The d'Alembert solution is the *most general* solution to the 1-D wave equation. We propose a change of variables:

$$\xi = z - u t \quad \text{and} \quad \eta = z + u t$$

**Step 1:** Express partial derivatives using the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \xi} + \frac{\partial}{\partial \eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} = -u \, \frac{\partial}{\partial \xi} + u \, \frac{\partial}{\partial \eta}$$

**Step 2:** Apply $\partial^2 / \partial z^2$ to a generic $V(\xi, \eta)$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \xi^2} + 2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \xi \, \partial \eta} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \eta^2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply $\partial^2 / \partial t^2$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} = u^2 \left( \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \xi^2} - 2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \xi \, \partial \eta} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \eta^2} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the wave equation $\partial^2 V / \partial z^2 = (1/u^2) \partial^2 V / \partial t^2$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \xi^2} + 2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \xi \, \partial \eta} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \eta^2} = \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \xi^2} - 2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \xi \, \partial \eta} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \eta^2}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel the matching $\xi$ and $\eta$ second-derivative terms. Only the cross-term remains:

$$4 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \xi \, \partial \eta} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \xi \, \partial \eta} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Integration gives $V = f(\xi) + g(\eta)$. Reverting to $(z, t)$:

$$\boxed{V(z,t) = f^+(z - u t) + f^-(z + u t)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** $f^+(z - u t)$ is a *forward-traveling* wave (in $+z$ direction) because keeping $z - u t = \text{const}$ requires $z$ to increase as $t$ increases at rate $u$. Similarly, $f^-(z + u t)$ is a *backward-traveling* wave.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sinusoidal Steady-State (Phasor) Solution

For time-harmonic excitation $V(z,t) = \text{Re}\{\tilde{V}(z) e^{j\omega t}\}$, the time derivative becomes $\partial^2 / \partial t^2 \rightarrow -\omega^2$. The wave equation reduces to an **ordinary** differential equation (ODE):

$$\frac{d^2 \tilde{V}(z)}{dz^2} + \omega^2 LC \, \tilde{V}(z) = 0$$

Define the **phase constant** $\beta = \omega \sqrt{LC} = \omega / u$. Then:

$$\frac{d^2 \tilde{V}}{dz^2} + \beta^2 \tilde{V} = 0$$

This is a standard second-order linear ODE. The general solution is:

$$\tilde{V}(z) = V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$$

Multiplying by the time factor $e^{j\omega t}$ and taking the real part:

$$\boxed{V(z,t) = V_0^+ \cos(\omega t - \beta z) + V_0^- \cos(\omega t + \beta z)}$$

(where $V_0^\pm$ are now taken as real amplitudes after absorbing any phasor angles).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Companion Current Solution

To get the current from the voltage, substitute $\tilde{V}(z) = V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$ into the phasor telegrapher's equation $\partial \tilde{V}/\partial z = -j\omega L \tilde{I}$:

$$\tilde{I}(z) = -\frac{1}{j\omega L} \frac{d\tilde{V}}{dz}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the derivative:

$$\frac{d\tilde{V}}{dz} = -j\beta V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + j\beta V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute and simplify using $j\omega L \cdot j\beta = -\omega L \beta = -\omega L \cdot \omega \sqrt{LC} = -\omega^2 L \sqrt{LC}$:

$$\tilde{I}(z) = \frac{\beta}{\omega L} V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{\beta}{\omega L} V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$$

**Step 3:** Use $\beta / (\omega L) = \sqrt{LC}/L = 1/\sqrt{L/C} = 1/Z_0$ where $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ is the **characteristic impedance**:

$$\tilde{I}(z) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$$

**Step 4:** Convert back to time domain:

$$\boxed{I(z,t) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} \cos(\omega t - \beta z) - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} \cos(\omega t + \beta z)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the minus sign on the backward wave.** It arises naturally from the chain rule and is *not* a sign convention you can ignore. This sign is what gives rise to reflection coefficients with the correct polarity at loads.

### 3.5 Verification — Substitution Check

Plug $V = f^+(z - u t)$ into the wave equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 f^+}{\partial z^2} = f^{+''}(z - u t)$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 f^+}{\partial t^2} = u^2 f^{+''}(z - u t)$$

So $f^{+''}(z - u t) = LC \cdot u^2 f^{+''}(z - u t) = LC \cdot \frac{1}{LC} f^{+''}(z - u t) = f^{+''}(z - u t)$ ✓

This confirms that $u = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ is indeed the correct phase velocity and that the proposed form is a valid solution.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
State and explain the lossless one-dimensional wave equation for voltage on a transmission line. Define the phase velocity.

**Model Answer:**

The lossless 1-D wave equation for voltage is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V(z,t)}{\partial z^2} = LC \, \frac{\partial^2 V(z,t)}{\partial t^2}$$

It is obtained by differentiating the voltage telegrapher's equation $\partial V/\partial z = -L \, \partial I/\partial t$ with respect to $z$, the current telegrapher's equation $\partial I/\partial z = -C \, \partial V/\partial t$ with respect to $t$, and combining them. The **phase velocity** is the speed at which a point of constant phase propagates:

$$u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \;\; \text{(m/s)}$$

(2 marks for the equation, 1 mark for definition of phase velocity.)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
Write down the d'Alembert solution of the wave equation and identify the two physical components.

**Model Answer:**

$$V(z,t) = f^+(z - u t) + f^-(z + u t)$$

- $f^+(z - u t)$ — a **forward-traveling wave** moving in the $+z$ direction with speed $u$.
- $f^-(z + u t)$ — a **backward-traveling wave** moving in the $-z$ direction with speed $u$.

(1 mark each for statement and identification, 1 mark for direction description.)

---

### **Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern]`

**(a)** Starting from the lossless Telegrapher's equations, derive the 1-D wave equation for voltage on a transmission line. Clearly state the phase velocity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lossless transmission line has $L = 0.5 \, \mu\text{H/m}$ and $C = 200 \, \text{pF/m}$. A sinusoidal source of $V_s = 10 \cos(10^8 t)$ V drives the line. Find (i) phase velocity, (ii) phase constant, (iii) wavelength, (iv) the forward voltage wave $V^+(z,t)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Write the lossless telegrapher's equations:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = -L \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial z} = -C \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate (i) w.r.t. $z$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = -L \, \frac{\partial^2 I}{\partial z \, \partial t}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate (ii) w.r.t. $t$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 I}{\partial t \, \partial z} = -C \, \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 4:** Equate $\partial^2 I / \partial z \partial t = \partial^2 I / \partial t \partial z$ and substitute:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = -L \left(-C \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}\right) = LC \, \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = LC \, \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the phase velocity. Comparing with $\partial^2 V/\partial z^2 = (1/u^2) \partial^2 V/\partial t^2$:

$$u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $L = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ H/m, $C = 200 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\omega = 10^8$ rad/s.

**(i) Phase velocity:**

$$u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 200 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-16}}} = \frac{1}{10^{-8}} = 10^8 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Phase constant:**

$$\beta = \frac{\omega}{u} = \frac{10^8}{10^8} = 1 \;\; \text{rad/m}$$

**(iii) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi}{1} = 2\pi \approx 6.283 \;\; \text{m}$$

**(iv) Forward wave (assuming $V_0^+ = 10$ V at $z=0$):**

$$V^+(z,t) = 10 \cos(10^8 t - z) \;\; \text{V}$$

(Each sub-part: ~1.5–2 marks; showing the formula earns partial credit even if arithmetic is missed.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern]`

**(a)** Write down the d'Alembert solution of the lossless wave equation. Explain how this solution can be interpreted as a superposition of two oppositely-traveling waves. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a lossless line with $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$, the voltage at $z = 0$ is $V(0,t) = 100 \cos(\omega t)$ V, and the line is matched ($V_0^- = 0$). Compute (i) the forward wave $V^+(z,t)$ and (ii) the current $I(z,t)$ along the line, taking $\beta = 2$ rad/m and $\omega = 10^6$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Statement. The d'Alembert solution is:

$$V(z,t) = f^+(z - u t) + f^-(z + u t)$$

**Step 2:** Interpretation of $f^+(z - u t)$. Let $z - u t = k$ (a constant). Then $z = u t + k$, so a particular point of constant phase moves to the right (increasing $z$) as time increases. The speed of this motion is $dz/dt = u$. Hence $f^+$ is a **forward wave**.

**Step 3:** Interpretation of $f^-(z + u t)$. Let $z + u t = k$. Then $z = -u t + k$, so a constant-phase point moves in the $-z$ direction. Hence $f^-$ is a **backward wave**.

**Step 4:** Superposition. By the linearity of the wave equation, any sum of solutions is a solution. Therefore, the total voltage on the line is a linear superposition of the forward and backward waves, each propagating independently at speed $u$.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Forward wave.** Since $V_0^- = 0$ (matched line), and $V(0,t) = 100\cos(\omega t)$:

$$V^+(z,t) = 100 \cos(\omega t - \beta z) = 100 \cos(10^6 t - 2z) \;\; \text{V}$$

**(ii) Current along the line.** The general current phasor solution is:

$$\tilde{I}(z) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$$

With $V_0^- = 0$:

$$\tilde{I}(z) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z}$$

In time domain:

$$I(z,t) = \frac{100}{50} \cos(10^6 t - 2z) = 2 \cos(10^6 t - 2z) \;\; \text{A}$$

(1 mark for identification of forward wave; 1 mark for substitution; 1 mark for computing $\beta z$ product. 2 marks each for parts (i) and (ii) with proper substitution.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Sign errors:** Forgetting the minus sign on the backward current term is the single most common mistake. It will cost at least 1–2 marks and may lead to an incorrect reflection coefficient sign in later modules.
> - **Units of $\beta$:** $\beta$ is in rad/m, not rad/s. Confusing it with $\omega$ is a frequent error.
> - **Missing $LC$ factor:** Students sometimes write $\partial^2 V/\partial z^2 = \partial^2 V/\partial t^2$ without the $LC$ factor. Always include the $LC$ in the wave equation.
> - **Forgetting the time-domain $e^{j\omega t}$:** When writing sinusoidal solutions, multiply the phasor by $e^{j\omega t}$ and take the real part — don't just write the phasor as the final answer.
> - **Mis-identifying $u$:** The phase velocity is $u = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ for a transmission line, but for plane waves in free space it is $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$. Don't mix them up.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Lossless 1-D wave equation:** $\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = LC \, \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$ — derived from the telegrapher's equations.
- **Phase velocity:** $u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ — depends only on per-unit-length $L$ and $C$.
- **d'Alembert solution:** $V(z,t) = f^+(z - u t) + f^-(z + u t)$ — general time-domain form.
- **Sinusoidal steady-state:** $V(z,t) = V_0^+\cos(\omega t - \beta z) + V_0^-\cos(\omega t + \beta z)$ with $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$.
- **Characteristic impedance:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ — relates forward/backward voltage and current waves.
- **Current expression has a minus sign on the backward wave** — *do not forget*.
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta = u/f$.
- **Forward wave = $+z$ direction; backward wave = $-z$ direction.**
- **A matched line** has $V_0^- = 0$, meaning no reflection.
- **Lossy lines** introduce an additional attenuation term, but lossless is the KTU-Module-2 standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive the wave equation," always start by **writing both telegrapher's equations explicitly** before differentiating. Examiners give the first mark just for stating the starting equations correctly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propagation of Plane EM Wave in Perfect Dielectric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Propagation of Plane EM Wave in Perfect Dielectric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perfect Dielectric (Lossless Medium):** A medium characterized by zero electrical conductivity ($\sigma = 0$) and finite, positive permittivity ($\varepsilon$) and permeability ($\mu$). Because no conduction current can flow, an electromagnetic wave passing through it suffers **no attenuation** — only a constant-amplitude sinusoidal exchange of energy between the electric and magnetic fields.

A **plane electromagnetic wave** is a transverse wave in which both the electric field vector $\vec{E}$ and the magnetic field vector $\vec{H}$ lie in a plane perpendicular to the direction of propagation, and the wavefronts (surfaces of constant phase) are infinite parallel planes. Such a wave is the simplest exact solution of Maxwell's equations in a source-free, unbounded perfect dielectric.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Dropping a Pebble in a Perfectly Still Pond"

Imagine tossing a pebble into a glassy-smooth lake. The ripples spread outward in perfect circles without losing energy, because there is no friction (no "conductivity") in the water. Now imagine the wave in **3-D**: the ripples are infinite flat sheets, not circles. The electric field $\vec{E}$ is the vertical displacement of the water, the magnetic field $\vec{H}$ is the sideways sloshing that always accompanies it, and the lake itself is the **perfect dielectric**. Because the lake has no friction, the wave can travel forever without fading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in KTU 2024 Scheme:** This topic is the *foundation stone* for every higher module — waveguides, antennas, fibre optics, and microwave engineering all rely on the lossless propagation equations derived here.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m**
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**
- **Speed of light in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ **Ω**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCECT501 — Module 2):** Students must be able to (i) derive the lossless wave equation from Maxwell's equations, (ii) obtain the intrinsic impedance and phase velocity, and (iii) state the orthogonality relationship $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \vec{k}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Physics — "Why" the Wave Propagates

A plane EM wave exists because of a self-sustaining **handshake** between $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$:

1. A **time-varying $\vec{E}$** field produces (via Maxwell–Ampère's law) a curling $\vec{H}$ field around it.
2. That **time-varying $\vec{H}$** in turn produces (via Faraday's law) a curling $\vec{E}$ field around it.
3. The two curls continually *regenerate* each other, allowing the disturbance to ripple forward through space at a finite speed.
4. In a **perfect dielectric** ($\sigma = 0$), there is no Ohmic loss term $\vec{J}_c = \sigma \vec{E}$ to drain this energy, so the amplitude never decays.

### 2.2 Maxwell's Equations in a Source-Free Perfect Dielectric

| # | Differential Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\mu \dfrac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$ | Faraday's Law — changing $\vec{H}$ induces curling $\vec{E}$ |
| 2 | $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$ | Ampère–Maxwell Law — changing $\vec{E}$ produces curling $\vec{H}$ (no conduction term because $\sigma = 0$) |
| 3 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ | Gauss's Law — no free charges in the medium |
| 4 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{H} = 0$ | Gauss's Law for Magnetism — no magnetic monopoles |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations to Memorise for KTU ESE**

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wave equation | $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu\varepsilon \dfrac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$ | Helmholtz-type vector PDE for $\vec{E}$ | — |
| Wave equation (H) | $\nabla^2 \vec{H} = \mu\varepsilon \dfrac{\partial^2 \vec{H}}{\partial t^2}$ | Helmholtz-type vector PDE for $\vec{H}$ | — |
| Phase velocity | $v_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}} = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}}$ | Speed of constant-phase plane | m/s |
| Wavenumber | $k = \omega \sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\omega}{v_p} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | Spatial angular frequency | rad/m |
| Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_p}{f} = \dfrac{2\pi}{k}$ | Distance over which phase advances by $2\pi$ | m |
| Intrinsic impedance | $\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} = \eta_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_r}{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Ratio of $\vert E \vert$ to $\vert H \vert$ in the wave | Ω |
| Refractive index | $n = \sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r} = \dfrac{c}{v_p}$ | Optical density of the medium | dimensionless |
| Poynting vector (avg) | $\vec{P}_{avg} = \dfrac{\vert E_0 \vert^2}{2\eta}\, \hat{a}_k$ | Time-average power density | W/m² |
| Energy densities | $w_e = w_m = \dfrac{\varepsilon \vert E \vert^2}{4}$ | Equal electric and magnetic energy densities | J/m³ |
| Field relation | $\vec{H} = \dfrac{1}{\eta}\,\hat{a}_k \times \vec{E}$ | Cross-product orientation rule | A/m |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations govern **optical fibre design** (where silica is a near-perfect dielectric), **radar and satellite link budgeting**, **medical MRI RF coils**, and **microwave oven cavity tuning** — all of which KTU alumni work on in industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Wave Equation for $\vec{E}$ in a Perfect Dielectric

We start with the curl equations, with $\sigma = 0$ and source-free conditions.

**Step 1 — Take the curl of Faraday's Law:**

$$
\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla \times \left(-\mu \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the vector identity** $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{A}) - \nabla^2 \vec{A}$:

$$
\nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\mu \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{H})
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Gauss's law** $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ (no free charge), so the first term vanishes:

$$
- \nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\mu \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{H})
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Ampère–Maxwell's law** $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$:

$$
- \nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\mu \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\varepsilon \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}\right) = -\mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Final wave equation:**

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}
$$

By identical reasoning (curling the second equation and using $\nabla \cdot \vec{H} = 0$):

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{H} = \mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 \vec{H}}{\partial t^2}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** These are three-dimensional wave equations. The coefficient $\mu\varepsilon$ on the RHS has units of $1/v_p^2$, confirming that disturbances travel at the finite speed $v_p = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$.

### 3.2 Solution for a Plane Wave Travelling in the $+z$ Direction

Assume a uniform plane wave with $\vec{E}$ polarised along $\hat{a}_x$ and travelling along $\hat{a}_k = \hat{a}_z$:

$$
\vec{E}(z,t) = E_0 \cos(\omega t - k z)\, \hat{a}_x
$$

This is a valid solution of the 1-D wave equation. To find $\vec{H}$, substitute back into Faraday's law.

**Step 1 — Compute** $\nabla \times \vec{E}$. Since $E_x$ depends only on $z$ and $t$:

$$
\nabla \times \vec{E} = \frac{\partial E_x}{\partial z}\,\hat{a}_y
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the partial derivative:**

$$
\frac{\partial E_x}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left[E_0 \cos(\omega t - k z)\right] = E_0\,(k) \sin(\omega t - k z)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's law** $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\mu \dfrac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$:

$$
E_0\, k\, \sin(\omega t - k z)\,\hat{a}_y = -\mu \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate with respect to $t$** (the integration constant is a static field, which we drop):

$$
\vec{H}(z,t) = \frac{E_0\, k}{\mu \omega} \cos(\omega t - k z)\,\hat{a}_y = \frac{E_0}{\eta} \cos(\omega t - k z)\,\hat{a}_y
$$

where we have used $k = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{k}{\mu\omega} = \dfrac{1}{\eta}$.

**Final Solutions:**

$$
\boxed{\;\vec{E}(z,t) = E_0 \cos(\omega t - k z)\,\hat{a}_x\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\vec{H}(z,t) = \frac{E_0}{\eta} \cos(\omega t - k z)\,\hat{a}_y\;}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Intrinsic Impedance $\eta$

**Step 1 — From the curl relation between $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$** for the assumed plane wave:

$$
\frac{E_0}{H_0} = \frac{\omega \mu}{k} = \frac{\omega \mu}{\omega \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}
$$

**Step 2 — Therefore, the intrinsic impedance of the medium is:**

$$
\eta = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\;\; \text{(Ω)}
$$

**Step 3 — Free-space special case:**

$$
\eta_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12}}} \approx 376.73 \approx 120\pi \;\; \text{Ω}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Phase Velocity $v_p$

The phase of the cosine is $\phi = \omega t - k z$. For an observer moving with the wave crest, $\phi$ is constant, so:

$$
\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \omega - k \frac{dz}{dt} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{dz}{dt} = v_p = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}
$$

For free space, this reduces to $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0} = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Symbol | Value in Perfect Dielectric | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conductivity | $\sigma$ | **0** (by definition) | No Ohmic loss |
| Attenuation constant | $\alpha$ | **0 Np/m** | Wave does not decay |
| Phase constant | $\beta \equiv k$ | $\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ rad/m | Spatial rate of phase change |
| Refractive index | $n$ | $\sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}$ | Slows the wave vs. free space |
| Field ratio | $\vert E \vert / \vert H \vert$ | $\eta$ | Fixed by medium constants |
| Field orientation | $\vec{E} \cdot \vec{H}$ | 0 (orthogonal) | Transverse EM wave |
| Power flow | $\vec{P}$ | along $\hat{a}_k$ | Energy rides the wavefront |
| Skin depth | $\delta$ | $\infty$ | Field penetrates forever |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Plane EM Wave in a Perfect Dielectric

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Maxwell Curl E = -mu dH/dt] --> coreNode1
    srcB[Maxwell Curl H = eps dE/dt] --> coreNode1
    coreNode1{Wave Equation Source}
    coreNode1 --> waveE[Vector Wave Eqn for E]
    coreNode1 --> waveH[Vector Wave Eqn for H]
    waveE --> sol1[Plane Wave Solution: E0 cos wt-kz ax]
    waveH --> sol2[Plane Wave Solution: H0 cos wt-kz ay]
    sol1 --> rel1[Field Ratio E0 over H0 = eta]
    sol2 --> rel1
    rel1 --> zimp[Intrinsic Impedance sqrt mu over eps]
    sol1 --> vp1[Phase Velocity 1 over sqrt mu eps]
    sol2 --> vp1
    sol1 --> poy1[Instantaneous Poynting Vector E cross H]
    sol2 --> poy1
    poy1 --> pavg[Time Average Power E0 squared over 2 eta]
    zimp --> outA[Characteristic Property of Medium]
    vp1 --> outB[Speed of Wavefront]
    pavg --> outC[Energy Transported per m2 per s]
```

### 4.2 Spatial Field Sketch (ASCII Schematic)

```
         z (direction of propagation)
         ↑
         |
   · · · | · · · | · · · | · · · | · · · |   ← wavefronts (planes of constant phase)
         |
   ──────► E_x     H_y
   (·)    direction of travel  →  →
         |
   · · · | · · · | · · · | · · · | · · · |
         |
   ──────►  z = 0        z = λ/4      z = λ/2
                                            
   E(z,t) and H(z,t) are in phase
   E along +x, H along +y, propagation along +z
   (Right-hand rule: a_x × a_y = a_z)
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Exchange Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    enA[Electric Field Energy 1/2 eps E squared] -->|induced by changing flux| enB[Magnetic Field Energy 1/2 mu H squared]
    enB -->|induced by changing flux| enA
    enA --> poyOut[Poynting Vector E cross H] --> power[Power Flow along direction of k]
    power --> farField[Energy Radiated to Infinity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The three Mermaid charts above map out (i) the analytical chain from Maxwell's equations to all derived quantities, (ii) the spatial orientation of the three mutually perpendicular vectors, and (iii) the closed-loop energy exchange that lets a wave travel without any external source.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a perfect dielectric medium. Why is the conduction current density zero inside it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A perfect dielectric is a medium in which the electrical conductivity $\sigma = 0$, so the conduction current density $\vec{J}_c = \sigma \vec{E} = 0$. Because no real current can flow, the medium stores energy only in its electric and magnetic fields and is therefore lossless. In practice, materials such as dry air, fused silica, and high-purity Teflon approximate a perfect dielectric at radio and optical frequencies. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State any three properties of a uniform plane wave propagating in a lossless dielectric.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The fields $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ are mutually perpendicular and both are perpendicular to the direction of propagation (transverse EM wave).
2. $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ are in time-phase with each other, with the magnitude ratio $E/H = \eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$.
3. The wave travels with phase velocity $v_p = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ and suffers **no attenuation** ($\alpha = 0$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Starting from Maxwell's equations, derive the wave equation for the electric field in a source-free perfect dielectric. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a uniform plane wave with $\vec{E} = 10 \cos(2\pi \times 10^8\, t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_x$ V/m propagating in a lossless medium with $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $\mu_r = 1$, obtain **(i)** the phase constant $\beta$, **(ii)** the intrinsic impedance $\eta$, **(iii)** the magnetic field phasor $\vec{H}$, and **(iv)** the time-average Poynting vector. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q-A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- State the two curl equations with $\sigma = 0$ and $\rho_v = 0$. **(1 mark)**
- Take the curl of Faraday's law. **(1 mark)**
- Apply the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$. **(1 mark)**
- Use $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ to drop the gradient term. **(1 mark)**
- Substitute Ampère–Maxwell's law. **(1 mark)**
- Rearrange to the final form $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu\varepsilon\,\partial^2 \vec{E}/\partial t^2$. **(1 mark)**
- Comment that the identical derivation gives $\nabla^2 \vec{H} = \mu\varepsilon\,\partial^2 \vec{H}/\partial t^2$. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $E_0 = 10$ V/m, $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^8$ rad/s, $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $\mu_r = 1$.

**(i) Phase constant** (1.5 marks):

$$
\beta = \omega \sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = \omega \sqrt{\mu_0\mu_r \cdot \varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r} = \frac{\omega}{c}\sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{2\pi \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8}\sqrt{1 \times 4} = \frac{2\pi}{3} \times 2 = \frac{4\pi}{3} \;\; \text{rad/m} \approx 4.19\;\text{rad/m}
$$

**(ii) Intrinsic impedance** (1.5 marks):

$$
\eta = \eta_0 \sqrt{\frac{\mu_r}{\varepsilon_r}} = 120\pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}} = 60\pi \approx 188.5\;\Omega
$$

**(iii) Magnetic field** (2 marks): $\vec{H}$ is along $\hat{a}_y$ (since $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$):

$$
\vec{H}(z,t) = \frac{10}{60\pi}\cos(2\pi \times 10^8 t - \tfrac{4\pi}{3} z)\,\hat{a}_y \approx 0.053\,\cos(2\pi \times 10^8 t - \tfrac{4\pi}{3} z)\,\hat{a}_y\;\text{A/m}
$$

**(iv) Time-average Poynting vector** (2 marks):

$$
\vec{P}_{avg} = \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta}\,\hat{a}_z = \frac{(10)^2}{2 \times 60\pi}\,\hat{a}_z = \frac{100}{120\pi}\,\hat{a}_z \approx 0.265\,\hat{a}_z\;\text{W/m}^2
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the intrinsic impedance of a lossless dielectric and show that for free space it equals $120\pi\;\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EM wave of frequency 1 GHz propagates in a perfect dielectric with $\varepsilon_r = 2.1$ and $\mu_r = 1$ (typical of polyethylene). Calculate **(i)** the phase velocity, **(ii)** the wavelength, **(iii)** the wavenumber, and **(iv)** the time taken for the wave to travel 1 m inside the medium. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q-B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- Write the assumed plane wave solutions for $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$. **(1 mark)**
- Substitute $\vec{E} = E_0 \cos(\omega t - k z)\hat{a}_x$ into Faraday's law and compute the curl. **(2 marks)**
- Equate to $-\mu\,\partial \vec{H}/\partial t$ and integrate to obtain $H_0 = E_0/\eta$. **(2 marks)**
- Identify $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$, then substitute free-space values to obtain $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 120\pi\;\Omega$. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $f = 10^9$ Hz, $\varepsilon_r = 2.1$, $\mu_r = 1$.

**(i) Phase velocity** (2 marks):

$$
v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.1}} \approx 2.07 \times 10^8\;\text{m/s}
$$

**(ii) Wavelength** (2 marks):

$$
\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{2.07 \times 10^8}{10^9} \approx 0.207\;\text{m} = 20.7\;\text{cm}
$$

**(iii) Wavenumber** (1.5 marks):

$$
k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi}{0.207} \approx 30.36\;\text{rad/m}
$$

**(iv) Time to travel 1 m** (1.5 marks):

$$
t = \frac{d}{v_p} = \frac{1}{2.07 \times 10^8} \approx 4.83 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{s} = 4.83\;\text{ns}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in Faraday's law after taking the curl — it flips the sign of the magnetic field direction. (1 mark deduction)
> 2. **Mixing up $\beta$ and $\omega$** — KTU expects $\beta$ in rad/m, not Hz, and $\omega$ in rad/s, not Hz. (1 mark deduction)
> 3. **Using $\eta_0 = 377$ instead of $120\pi$** — KTU key marks prefer the *exact* symbolic form $120\pi\;\Omega$ for full credit. (0.5 mark deduction)
> 4. **Skipping the verification that $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$** when applying the vector identity in the wave-equation derivation — this is a routine KTU sub-step worth 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the final numerical answer (e.g., W/m², rad/m, ns). KTU examiners *do* deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Perfect dielectric** = $\sigma = 0$, $\alpha = 0$, no attenuation of the travelling wave.
- [x] **Vector wave equation** for $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ in source-free lossless medium: $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu\varepsilon\,\partial^2 \vec{E}/\partial t^2$.
- [x] **Plane-wave solution:** $\vec{E} = E_0 \cos(\omega t - k z)\,\hat{a}_x$ and $\vec{H} = (E_0/\eta)\cos(\omega t - k z)\,\hat{a}_y$.
- [x] **Three-way orthogonality:** $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \hat{a}_k$ (transverse EM).
- [x] **In-phase fields:** $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ reach maxima and minima at the *same* $z$ and $t$ in a lossless medium.
- [x] **Phase velocity:** $v_p = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = c/\sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}$.
- [x] **Wavenumber:** $k = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = 2\pi/\lambda$.
- [x] **Intrinsic impedance:** $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$; free-space value $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\;\Omega$.
- [x] **Poynting vector (time-average):** $\vec{P}_{avg} = (E_0^2/2\eta)\,\hat{a}_k$ W/m².
- [x] **Energy densities are equal:** $w_e = w_m = \tfrac{1}{4}\varepsilon \vert E \vert^2$ J/m³.
- [x] **Boundary at infinity:** No skin depth in a perfect dielectric — fields extend forever.
- [x] **Engineering uses:** Optical fibres, satellite comm links, RF/microwave circuits, MRI, radar.

> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Whenever a 14-mark problem asks you to "derive and calculate," always show the **derivation chain (5–6 marks)** *before* plugging in numbers (2–3 marks). KTU's ESE marking scheme explicitly awards separate marks for symbolic manipulation versus numerical substitution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lossy Medium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lossy Medium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Lossy Medium** (also called a **conducting medium** or **dissipative medium**) is a material region in which an electromagnetic wave suffers a continuous loss of energy as it propagates, due to the existence of finite electrical conductivity $\sigma \neq 0$. In such a medium, the conduction current density $\vec{J_c} = \sigma \vec{E}$ is comparable to (or dominates over) the displacement current density $\vec{J_d} = j\omega\varepsilon \vec{E}$, leading to irreversible ohmic heating (Joule losses) and therefore attenuation of the wave amplitude.

Mathematically, a lossy medium is one in which the **complex permittivity** is:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega}$$

The real part $\varepsilon$ stores energy reversibly, while the imaginary part $\frac{\sigma}{\omega}$ accounts for conduction losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorise:** A medium is termed "lossy" when the condition $\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$ is neither extremely large nor extremely small — i.e., conduction and displacement currents are of the same order. Pure conductors ($\sigma \to \infty$) and perfect dielectrics ($\sigma = 0$) are **limiting idealisations**, not lossy media.

### 1.2 The Real-World Analogy — "Muddy Pond vs. Clean Glass"

Imagine you shine a torchlight through two bodies of water:

| Medium Type | Real-Life Analogy | Wave Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Perfect Dielectric** ($\sigma = 0$) | Crystal-clear swimming pool | Beam travels long distances with **no loss** of intensity |
| **Lossy Medium** (finite $\sigma$) | A slightly muddy or salty lake | Beam **diminishes** with distance; energy heats the water |
| **Good Conductor** ($\sigma \to \infty$) | A thick slab of metal | Wave dies within a **few micrometres** at the surface — only the "skin" is penetrated |

The conductivity $\sigma$ acts like **friction** for the wave — every oscillation of the electric field drags free electrons, dissipating electromagnetic energy as heat (Joule heating, $P = \sigma E^2$ per unit volume).

### 1.3 Why Lossy Media Matter in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance (KTU CO Mapping):** Understanding lossy media is essential for the design of:
> - **Antenna radomes and radomes in aerospace**
> - **Underwater communication links (sonar / submarine cables)**
> - **Microwave ovens (food is a lossy dielectric)**
> - **PCB trace design (lossy substrates like FR-4)**
> - **EMI shielding enclosures (good conductor walls)**
> - **Bioelectromagnetic applications (human tissue is lossy at RF)**

### 1.4 Physical Constants You Must Remember

- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$
- Free-space permittivity: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$
- Free-space permeability: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$
- Intrinsic impedance of free space: $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377 \ \Omega$
- Copper conductivity: $\sigma_{Cu} = 5.8 \times 10^7 \ \text{S/m}$
- Seawater conductivity: $\sigma_{sw} \approx 4 \ \text{S/m}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does a Wave Attenuate? — The "Why & How" Breakdown

A plane wave $\vec{E} = E_0 e^{-\gamma z}\hat{a_x}$ travels in a lossy medium. Three things happen simultaneously:

1. **The medium conducts:** free charges accelerate under $\vec{E}$, converting EM energy into heat ($I^2R$ losses).
2. **The displacement current still exists:** but it is now **out of phase** with the conduction current by $90^\circ$. We capture this using **complex permittivity** $\varepsilon_c$.
3. **The propagation constant becomes complex:** $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$, where:
   - $\alpha$ = **attenuation constant** (Np/m) → amplitude decay
   - $\beta$ = **phase constant** (rad/m) → wave oscillation

### 2.2 Loss Tangent — The Master Classifier

The **loss tangent** is the dimensionless ratio that universally classifies a medium:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$$

**Why is it called "loss tangent"?** Because in the complex permittivity plane, $\tan\delta$ is literally the tangent of the loss angle $\delta$ between the real permittivity axis and the complex permittivity vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification Rule (Sadiku / KTU textbook standard):**
> - **Good Conductor:** $\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} \geq 100$  → conduction currents dominate (metals)
> - **Good Dielectric:** $\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} \leq 0.01$ → displacement currents dominate (air, glass, Teflon)
> - **Quasi-Conductor / Lossy Dielectric:** $0.01 < \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} < 100$ (seawater, soil, wet wood, biological tissue)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Lossy Medium

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Board Exam:** Always substitute $\sigma$ and $\omega\varepsilon$ numerically and check their ratio **first**, before picking the right approximation.

| # | Quantity | General Expression | Good Conductor ($\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$) | Good Dielectric ($\sigma \ll \omega\varepsilon$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Complex permittivity | $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon - j\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega}$ | $\approx -j\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega}$ | $\approx \varepsilon$ |
| 2 | Loss tangent | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$ | $\gg 1$ | $\ll 1$ |
| 3 | Propagation constant $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)}$ | $(1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$ | $j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$ |
| 4 | Attenuation constant $\alpha$ | $\text{Re}(\gamma)$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$ | $\dfrac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}$ |
| 5 | Phase constant $\beta$ | $\text{Im}(\gamma)$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$ | $\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ |
| 6 | Intrinsic impedance $\eta$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}$ | $(1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\angle 0^\circ$ |
| 7 | Skin depth $\delta_s$ | $\dfrac{1}{\alpha}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}$ | $\dfrac{2}{\sigma}\sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon}{\mu}}$ |
| 8 | Phase velocity $v_p$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{\beta}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2\omega}{\mu\sigma}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$ |
| 9 | Wavelength $\lambda$ | $\dfrac{2\pi}{\beta}$ | $2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi}{\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$ |
| 10 | Poynting vector | $\vec{S} = \dfrac{1}{2}\vert E\vert^2 e^{-2\alpha z}\,\text{Re}(\eta^*)$ | Decays as $e^{-2z/\delta_s}$ | Almost lossless |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Good Conductor formulas:** Everything comes from $\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$. Notice that $\alpha = \beta$ in a perfect conductor — they are **numerically identical**!

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Which Approximation? | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Waveguide wall losses | Good conductor | Copper walls at microwave frequencies: $\tan\delta \sim 10^{8}$ |
| Submarine communication | Quasi-conductor | Seawater $\sigma \approx 4$ S/m makes mid-range $\tan\delta$ |
| Microwave heating of food | Lossy dielectric | Water-rich food has $\sigma \sim 1$ S/m at 2.45 GHz |
| Underground EM geophysics | Quasi-conductor | Soil conductivity varies wildly |
| Optical fibres (core) | Good dielectric | Extremely pure SiO₂ has $\tan\delta < 10^{-11}$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Helmholtz Wave Equation in a Lossy Medium

**Starting Point — Maxwell's Equations in Phasor Form (for time-harmonic fields $e^{j\omega t}$):**

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu\vec{H} \quad \text{(Faraday's Law)}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J_c} + \vec{J_d} = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E} \quad \text{(Ampere's Law)}$$

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0, \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{H} = 0
$$

**Step 1 — Take the curl of Faraday's Law on both sides:**

$$
\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla \times (-j\omega\mu\vec{H})
$$

**Step 2 — Use the vector identity** $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$. Since $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ in a source-free lossy medium:

$$
-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu(\nabla \times \vec{H})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Ampere's Law** $(\nabla \times \vec{H}) = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}$:

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{E} = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the propagation constant** $\gamma$ such that $\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)$:

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \gamma^2 \vec{E}
$$

This is the **vector Helmholtz equation**. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: General Propagation Constant $\gamma$

$$
\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu\sigma + j^2\omega^2\mu\varepsilon = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon + j\omega\mu\sigma
$$

Write $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$. Then:

$$
\gamma^2 = (\alpha^2 - \beta^2) + j(2\alpha\beta)
$$

Equating real and imaginary parts:

$$
\alpha^2 - \beta^2 = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon \quad \cdots (1)
$$

$$
2\alpha\beta = \omega\mu\sigma \quad \cdots (2)
$$

**Solving (1) and (2)** (squaring and adding):

$$
(\alpha^2 + \beta^2)^2 = \omega^4\mu^2\varepsilon^2 + \omega^2\mu^2\sigma^2
$$

$$
\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = \omega\mu\sqrt{\omega^2\varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2}
$$

$$
\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} - 1\right]^{1/2}
$$

$$
\beta = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} + 1\right]^{1/2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two master expressions are the **most important formulas for any KTU numerical on lossy media**. Always reduce them to the good-conductor or good-dielectric limit before computing.

### 3.3 Derivation: Good-Conductor Limit ($\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$)

When $\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} \gg 1$, we have $\sqrt{1 + (\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2} \approx \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$. Substituting:

$$
\alpha \approx \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}
$$

$$
\beta \approx \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}
$$

So in a good conductor, $\boxed{\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}}$. ✓

### 3.4 Derivation: Skin Depth $\delta_s$ for a Good Conductor

The skin depth is defined as the distance at which the wave amplitude falls to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8%) of its surface value, i.e., $e^{-\alpha z} = e^{-1}$ when $z = 1/\alpha$.

$$
\delta_s = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}
$$

Using $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$
\boxed{\delta_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{2\pi f \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** All the current and EM energy is concentrated within a thin layer of thickness $\delta_s$ near the conductor's surface. This is why **AC flows on the "skin"** of a wire and why high-frequency circuits use **plated (gold/silver) surfaces**.

### 3.5 Derivation: Intrinsic Impedance $\eta$ for a Good Conductor

Starting from $\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}$. For a good conductor, $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$:

$$
\eta_c \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu}{\sigma}}\sqrt{j}
$$

Since $\sqrt{j} = \dfrac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$
\eta_c = (1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}}
$$

Magnitude and phase:

$$
\vert\eta_c\vert = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}}, \quad \angle \eta_c = 45^\circ
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The factor $(1+j)$ is the "signature" of a good conductor — it means $E$ and $H$ are **45° out of phase**, and the ratio $\vert\eta\vert$ is very small (milliohms). For copper at 1 MHz: $\vert\eta_c\vert \approx 0.36 \ \text{m}\Omega$!

### 3.6 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style

**Problem:** A 50 MHz plane wave travels in a medium with $\mu_r = 1$, $\varepsilon_r = 16$, $\sigma = 0.02$ S/m. Classify the medium and compute $\alpha$, $\beta$, $v_p$, $\lambda$, and $\eta$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $\omega\varepsilon$:

$$
\omega\varepsilon = 2\pi(50\times 10^6)(16)(8.854\times 10^{-12}) \approx 4.45\times 10^{-2} \ \text{S/m}
$$

Step 2 — Loss tangent:

$$
\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} = \frac{0.02}{4.45\times 10^{-2}} \approx 0.45
$$

Step 3 — Classification: $0.01 < 0.45 < 100$ → **Quasi-conductor / Lossy dielectric**.

Step 4 — Cannot use good-conductor or good-dielectric shortcut. Use master formulas directly:

$$
\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + (\tan\delta)^2} - 1\right]^{1/2}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1 + 0.45^2} = \sqrt{1.2025} = 1.0966
$$

$$
[\,1.0966 - 1\,]^{1/2} = \sqrt{0.0966} = 0.3109
$$

$$
\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon/2} = 2\pi(50\times 10^6)\sqrt{(4\pi\times 10^{-7})(16)(8.854\times 10^{-12})/2}
$$

$$
= 3.14\times 10^8 \cdot \sqrt{8.89\times 10^{-17}} \approx 3.14\times 10^8 \cdot 2.98\times 10^{-8.5}
$$

Numerically evaluating: $\alpha \approx 1.31$ Np/m, $\beta \approx 1.85$ rad/m.

Step 5 — Intrinsic impedance:

$$
\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{j(4.45\times 10^{-2})}{0.02 + j(4.45\times 10^{-2})}} \ \Omega
$$

$$
|\eta| \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{4.45\times 10^{-2}}{\sqrt{0.02^2 + (4.45\times 10^{-2})^2}}} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{4.45\times 10^{-2}}{0.0485}} \approx 0.958 \ \Omega \cdot j\text{-correction}
$$

Step 6 — Phase velocity and wavelength:

$$
v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi(50\times 10^6)}{1.85} \approx 1.70\times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi}{1.85} \approx 3.40 \ \text{m}
$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Media via Loss Tangent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start with material constants mu, epsilon, sigma and frequency f] --> B[Compute loss tangent tan delta equals sigma over omega epsilon]
    B --> C{Tan delta compared with 0.01 and 100}
    C -->|tan delta greater than 100| D[GOOD CONDUCTOR sigma dominates]
    C -->|tan delta less than 0.01| E[GOOD DIELECTRIC displacement current dominates]
    C -->|0.01 to 100| F[QUASI CONDUCTOR lossy dielectric]
    D --> G[Use alpha equals beta equals sqrt omega mu sigma by 2]
    E --> H[Use alpha approximately sigma by 2 sqrt mu over epsilon beta approximately omega sqrt mu epsilon]
    F --> I[Use full master formulas no shortcut]
    G --> J[Compute skin depth delta s equals sqrt 2 over omega mu sigma]
    H --> K[Compute intrinsic impedance eta equals sqrt mu over epsilon at angle 0]
    I --> L[Compute eta from general expression with complex value]
    J --> M[Final answer attenuation phase velocity wavelength impedance]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Physical Picture of Wave Attenuation in a Lossy Medium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Source EM wave at z equals 0] --> Q[Medium interface entering lossy region]
    Q --> R[Conduction current Jc equals sigma E dissipates heat]
    R --> S[Displacement current Jd equals j omega epsilon E stores and releases energy]
    S --> T[Result wave decays as exp minus alpha z]
    T --> U[Amplitude after one skin depth exp minus 1 equals 36.8 percent]
    U --> V[After two skin depths exp minus 2 equals 13.5 percent]
    V --> W[After three skin depths exp minus 3 equals 4.98 percent]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — Cascading Analysis of a Lossy-Medium Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Step 1 Identify sigma epsilon mu omega] --> s2[Step 2 Compute tan delta equals sigma over omega epsilon]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3 Decide approximation good conductor good dielectric or quasi]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4 Choose appropriate alpha beta formula]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5 Compute eta from relevant expression]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6 Derive vp equals omega over beta]
    s6 --> s7[Step 7 Derive lambda equals 2 pi over beta]
    s7 --> s8[Step 8 If asked derive skin depth delta s equals 1 over alpha]
    s8 --> s9[Step 9 Present final answer with units]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these block diagrams?** KTU examiners love flow-based answers. Reproducing this sequence of decisions in your answer script demonstrates **systematic problem-solving** and earns full marks even if an intermediate formula slips your memory.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a lossy medium. What is the physical significance of loss tangent in classifying a material?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **lossy medium** is a material region with finite electrical conductivity $\sigma \neq 0$ in which an electromagnetic wave propagating through it experiences attenuation due to ohmic (Joule) losses, i.e., continuous conversion of EM energy into heat by conduction currents.

The **loss tangent** is defined as:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$$

It represents the ratio of conduction current density to displacement current density inside the medium. Its physical significance lies in **classification of materials** for EM wave propagation:

- $\tan\delta \gg 1$ → **Good conductor** (e.g., copper, aluminium)
- $\tan\delta \ll 1$ → **Good dielectric** (e.g., air, Teflon, glass)
- $\tan\delta \approx 1$ → **Quasi-conductor / lossy dielectric** (e.g., seawater, wet soil, biological tissue)

It is frequency-dependent — a medium may behave as a conductor at low frequencies and as a dielectric at high frequencies. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define skin depth. Show that for a good conductor, the skin depth is $\delta_s = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Skin depth $\delta_s$ is defined as the distance into a conductor at which the amplitude of an EM wave has fallen to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8%) of its value at the surface.

For a plane wave $E = E_0 e^{-\alpha z}$, setting $E/E_0 = e^{-1}$ gives $z = 1/\alpha = \delta_s$.

For a good conductor, $\alpha = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}$ (derived from $\alpha = \beta$ in the good-conductor limit). Therefore:

$$\delta_s = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}$$

Substituting $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$\delta_s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`**

**(a)** Define a lossy medium. Starting from Maxwell's equations, derive the Helmholtz wave equation for the electric field in a conducting medium and obtain the propagation constant $\gamma$.

**(b)** For a good conductor, derive expressions for (i) attenuation constant $\alpha$, (ii) phase constant $\beta$, (iii) skin depth $\delta_s$, and (iv) intrinsic impedance $\eta_c$. Hence calculate the skin depth of copper at 10 MHz. Given: $\sigma_{Cu} = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu_{Cu} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write Maxwell's equations in phasor form** (assume $e^{j\omega t}$ time-dependence and source-free region):

$$
\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu\vec{H} \quad \cdots (1)
$$

$$
\nabla \times \vec{H} = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E} \quad \cdots (2)
$$

**Step 2 — Take curl of equation (1):**

$$
\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -j\omega\mu\,(\nabla \times \vec{H})
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the vector identity** $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$. For a homogeneous source-free region, $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$:

$$
-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the Helmholtz equation:**

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{E} = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E} = \gamma^2 \vec{E}
$$

where the **propagation constant** is:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)} = \alpha + j\beta
$$

For 1-D propagation along $z$ (assuming $\vec{E} = E_x \hat{a_x}$), the solution is:

$$
\vec{E} = E_0 e^{-\gamma z}\hat{a_x} = E_0 e^{-\alpha z}e^{-j\beta z}\hat{a_x}
$$

**(7 marks — full credit if all four steps are explicit)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Good-conductor condition:** $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$. Under this approximation:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}
$$

Using $\sqrt{j} = (1+j)/\sqrt{2}$:

$$
\gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} = \alpha + j\beta
$$

**Equating real and imaginary parts:**

$$
\boxed{\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}} \quad \cdots (3)
$$

**Skin depth:**

$$
\boxed{\delta_s = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}} \quad \cdots (4)
$$

**Intrinsic impedance:**

$$
\eta_c = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma}} = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\vert\eta_c\vert = \sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}}, \quad \angle\eta_c = 45^\circ} \quad \cdots (5)
$$

**Numerical computation for copper at 10 MHz:**

Given: $f = 10^7$ Hz, $\sigma = 5.8\times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = 4\pi\times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

Compute $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 10^7$ rad/s.

$$
\omega\mu\sigma = (2\pi \times 10^7)(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(5.8 \times 10^7)
$$

$$
= 8\pi^2 \times 5.8 \times 10^{7} = 8 \times 9.8696 \times 5.8 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
= 457.9 \times 10^{7} \approx 4.58 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\delta_s = \sqrt{\frac{2}{4.58\times 10^9}} = \sqrt{4.37 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m} = 20.9 \ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Answer:** Skin depth of copper at 10 MHz ≈ **20.9 μm** ✓ **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of loss tangent. Classify conducting media into good conductors, good dielectrics, and quasi-conductors. For each case, write the simplified expressions for $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\eta$.

**(b)** A 2 GHz plane wave propagates in a medium characterised by $\varepsilon_r = 9$, $\mu_r = 1$, $\sigma = 10^{-3}$ S/m. Find (i) the loss tangent, (ii) the classification of the medium, (iii) the attenuation constant $\alpha$, and (iv) the intrinsic impedance $\eta$. State whether the medium is good for long-distance communication.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

The **loss tangent** $\tan\delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)$ measures the relative dominance of conduction versus displacement currents inside a medium. **Classification:**

| Class | Condition | $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Good Conductor** | $\tan\delta \geq 100$ | $\sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}$ | $\sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}$ | $(1+j)\sqrt{\omega\mu/(2\sigma)}$ |
| **Good Dielectric** | $\tan\delta \leq 0.01$ | $\dfrac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ | $\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ | $\sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}\angle 0^\circ$ |
| **Quasi-Conductor** | $0.01 < \tan\delta < 100$ | Master formula required | Master formula required | General expression |

**Physical examples:**

- **Good conductor:** copper ($\sigma \approx 5.8\times 10^7$ S/m), silver, aluminium
- **Good dielectric:** vacuum, air, Teflon, glass
- **Quasi-conductor:** seawater ($\sigma \approx 4$ S/m), wet soil, biological tissue, FR-4 PCB substrate

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $f = 2 \times 10^9$ Hz, $\sigma = 10^{-3}$ S/m, $\varepsilon_r = 9$, $\mu_r = 1$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\omega\varepsilon$:**

$$
\omega\varepsilon = 2\pi(2\times 10^9)(9)(8.854\times 10^{-12}) = 2\pi \times 1.594 \times 10^{-1}
$$

$$
\approx 1.001 \times 10^{0} \approx 1.0 \ \text{S/m (approx.)}
$$

More precisely: $\omega\varepsilon = 2\pi \times 2\times 10^9 \times 9 \times 8.854\times 10^{-12} = 1.0016 \approx 1.0$ S/m.

**Step 2 — Loss tangent:**

$$
\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} = \frac{10^{-3}}{1.0} = 10^{-3} = 0.001
$$

**Step 3 — Classification:** Since $\tan\delta = 0.001 < 0.01$, this is a **good dielectric**. ✓

**Step 4 — Since it's a good dielectric, use simplified formulas:**

$$
\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = 2\pi(2\times 10^9)\sqrt{(4\pi\times 10^{-7})(9 \times 8.854\times 10^{-12})}
$$

$$
= 4\pi\times 10^9 \sqrt{3.998\times 10^{-17}} \approx 4\pi\times 10^9 \times 6.32\times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
\beta \approx 79.5 \ \text{rad/m}
$$

$$
\alpha \approx \frac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{2}\sqrt{\frac{4\pi\times 10^{-7}}{9 \times 8.854\times 10^{-12}}}
$$

$$
= 5\times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{\frac{1.257\times 10^{-6}}{7.969\times 10^{-11}}}
$$

$$
= 5\times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{1.577\times 10^{4}} = 5\times 10^{-4} \times 125.6
$$

$$
\alpha \approx 0.0628 \ \text{Np/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Intrinsic impedance:**

$$
\eta = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\frac{4\pi\times 10^{-7}}{9\times 8.854\times 10^{-12}}} \approx \frac{377}{3} \approx 125.7 \ \Omega
$$

(With a very small negative imaginary part due to finite $\sigma$, but for a good dielectric, $\eta \approx 125.7\angle 0^\circ\ \Omega$.)

**Step 6 — Communication suitability:**

Skin depth $\delta_s = 1/\alpha \approx 1/0.0628 \approx 15.9$ m.

Since attenuation is small (only ~0.06 Np/m) and the wave can travel tens of metres before significant loss, this medium is **acceptable for moderate-range communication** but inferior to free space or optical fibre. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Lossy Medium Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to classify first:** Many students directly plug into the good-conductor formula even when the medium is a quasi-conductor. Always compute $\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)$ first! (-2 marks typical penalty)
>
> 2. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in good conductor:** Both are equal numerically to $\sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}$, but their units are different (Np/m vs. rad/m). Write both! (-1 mark)
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $(1+j)$ in $\eta_c$:** The intrinsic impedance of a good conductor is **complex**, not real. The 45° phase lag between $E$ and $H$ is a frequently tested KTU concept. (-1 mark)
>
> 4. **Using $\mu_0$ for non-magnetic media:** If the problem states $\mu_r \neq 1$, you **must** use $\mu = \mu_r \mu_0$. Common slip-up! (-1 mark)
>
> 5. **Confusing skin depth with attenuation:** Skin depth $= 1/\alpha$, not $\alpha$ itself. Several students write "skin depth = 0.05 Np/m" — units betray the error. (-1 mark)
>
> 6. **Not stating the assumption $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$ explicitly:** Always declare the approximation before plugging numbers. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if the assumption is unstated.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision (read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall):**

- ☐ **Lossy medium = finite $\sigma$, conduction current comparable to displacement current.** Loss tangent $\tan\delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)$ is the master classifier.
- ☐ **Complex permittivity:** $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon - j\sigma/\omega$. The $-j\sigma/\omega$ term is the *loss* contribution.
- ☐ **General propagation constant:** $\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)} = \alpha + j\beta$.
- ☐ **Master formulas:**
  - $\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon/2}\left[\sqrt{1 + (\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2} - 1\right]^{1/2}$
  - $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon/2}\left[\sqrt{1 + (\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2} + 1\right]^{1/2}$
- ☐ **Good conductor shortcut:** $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}$. Use when $\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon) \gg 1$ (≥ 100).
- ☐ **Good dielectric shortcut:** $\beta \approx \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$, $\alpha \approx (\sigma/2)\sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$. Use when $\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon) \ll 1$ (≤ 0.01).
- ☐ **Skin depth (good conductor):** $\delta_s = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)} = 1/\alpha$. Distance for amplitude to drop to $1/e$.
- ☐ **Intrinsic impedance (good conductor):** $\eta_c = (1+j)\sqrt{\omega\mu/(2\sigma)}$ — magnitude $\sqrt{\omega\mu/(2\sigma)}$, phase $45^\circ$.
- ☐ **Phase velocity:** $v_p = \omega/\beta$. Wavelength: $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta$.
- ☐ **Poynting vector decays as $e^{-2\alpha z}$** — power fades twice as fast as field amplitude.
- ☐ **Engineering must-knows:** skin effect in AC wires, microwave oven heating, underwater EM links, PCB substrate losses, RF shielding by metal enclosures.
- ☐ **For copper at 1 MHz:** $\delta_s \approx 66 \ \mu$m; at 1 GHz: $\delta_s \approx 2.1 \ \mu$m. Use this for quick sanity checks.

> **End of Topic: Lossy Medium — Module 2, PCECT501 Electromagnetics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Good Conductor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Good Conductor # 1.
