# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define polarization of an electromagnetic wave. When is an EM wave said to be linearly polarized?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Polarization of an EM wave is the property that describes the **time-varying orientation of the electric field vector $\vec{E}$** in the plane transverse to the direction of propagation. It characterizes the locus traced by the tip of the $\vec{E}$-vector as the wave moves forward.

An EM wave is said to be **linearly polarized** when the electric field vector oscillates along a **single fixed direction** in the transverse plane. This occurs when the two orthogonal transverse components ($E_x$ and $E_y$) are either **in phase** ($\Delta\phi = 0$) or **in anti-phase** ($\Delta\phi = \pi$). The resulting locus is a straight line through the origin with slope equal to $E_{0y}/E_{0x}$.

> **Marking pattern:** Definition 1 mark, condition/equation 1 mark, example/diagram 1 mark.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. State and explain Brewster's law. Mention one engineering application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Brewster's Law** states that when an unpolarized light beam is incident on a dielectric interface at the **Brewster angle** $\theta_B$, the reflected beam becomes **completely linearly polarized** (pure s-polarization, with the electric field perpendicular to the plane of incidence). The mathematical statement is:

$$
\theta_B = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)
$$

where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the refractive indices of the two media.

**Physical reason:** At Brewster's angle, the reflected ray and the refracted ray are exactly at $90^\circ$ to each other. The induced dipoles in medium 2 cannot radiate along their own axis of oscillation, so the TM (p) component is fully transmitted.

**Application:** Used in **laser cavities** (Brewster windows) to couple out only one polarization, and in **polarizing beam-splitter cubes** for optical instrumentation.

> **Marking pattern:** Statement with formula 1 mark, physical reason 1 mark, application 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Starting from the general expression for a plane wave propagating in the $+z$ direction, derive the equation of the **polarization ellipse** in the $E_x$–$E_y$ plane. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A uniform plane wave in free space has electric field components $E_x = 3\cos(\omega t - \beta z)\;\mathrm{V/m}$ and $E_y = 4\cos(\omega t - \beta z + 60^\circ)\;\mathrm{V/m}$. Determine the polarization state, the tilt angle $\psi$, and the ellipticity angle $\chi$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of the Polarization Ellipse (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* State the general wave: $\vec{E} = \hat{x}E_{0x}\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_x) + \hat{y}E_{0y}\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_y)$.

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Set $z=0$ to analyze the trajectory at a fixed point. Introduce $\tau = \omega t + \phi_x$ and $\Delta\phi = \phi_y - \phi_x$. Write $E_x = E_{0x}\cos\tau$, $E_y = E_{0y}\cos(\tau + \Delta\phi)$.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Expand $E_y$ using the cosine addition formula, substitute $\cos\tau = E_x/E_{0x}$, isolate the $\sin\tau$ term via $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$, and square to remove the radical.

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Arrive at the final boxed equation:

$$
\dfrac{E_x^2}{E_{0x}^2} + \dfrac{E_y^2}{E_{0y}^2} - \dfrac{2 E_x E_y \cos\Delta\phi}{E_{0x} E_{0y}} = \sin^2\Delta\phi
$$

and identify it as the equation of an ellipse in the $E_x$–$E_y$ plane.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Identify the parameters: $E_{0x} = 3\;\mathrm{V/m}$, $E_{0y} = 4\;\mathrm{V/m}$, $\Delta\phi = +60^\circ = +\pi/3\;\mathrm{rad}$.

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Since $E_{0x} \neq E_{0y}$ ($3 \neq 4$) and $\Delta\phi \neq 0, \pi, \pm\pi/2$, the wave is **elliptically polarized**.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Compute the tilt angle using:

$$
\tan 2\psi = \dfrac{2 E_{0x} E_{0y} \cos\Delta\phi}{E_{0x}^2 - E_{0y}^2}
$$

$$
\tan 2\psi = \dfrac{2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 \cdot \cos 60^\circ}{3^2 - 4^2} = \dfrac{24 \cdot 0.5}{9 - 16} = \dfrac{12}{-7} = -\dfrac{12}{7}
$$

$$
2\psi = \arctan(-12/7) = -59.74^\circ \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \psi \approx -29.87^\circ \approx -29.9^\circ
$$

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Compute the ellipticity angle:

$$
\sin 2\chi = \dfrac{2 E_{0x} E_{0y} \sin\Delta\phi}{E_{0x}^2 + E_{0y}^2} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 \cdot \sin 60^\circ}{9 + 16} = \dfrac{24 \cdot (\sqrt{3}/2)}{25} = \dfrac{12\sqrt{3}}{25} \approx 0.8314
$$

$$
2\chi = \arcsin(0.8314) \approx 56.25^\circ \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \chi \approx 28.13^\circ
$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* The **positive** $\Delta\phi$ and **positive** $\sin 2\chi$ indicate the rotation sense: this is a **right-handed elliptically polarized** wave (in physics convention). Final answer block: elliptical, $\psi \approx -29.9^\circ$, $\chi \approx 28.1^\circ$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Derive the conditions under which a plane EM wave is (i) linearly polarized, (ii) circularly polarized, and (iii) elliptically polarized. Support your derivation with sketches of the $\vec{E}$-vector trajectory in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An antenna radiates a uniform plane wave in free space. The far-zone electric field is given by $\vec{E} = (\hat{x} + j\hat{y})\,E_0 \, e^{j(\omega t - kz)}$. Identify the polarization state, the sense of rotation, and the axial ratio of the polarization ellipse. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conditions for the Three Polarization States (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* Begin with the general polarization ellipse:

$$
\dfrac{E_x^2}{E_{0x}^2} + \dfrac{E_y^2}{E_{0y}^2} - \dfrac{2 E_x E_y \cos\Delta\phi}{E_{0x} E_{0y}} = \sin^2\Delta\phi
$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* **Linear condition:** $\Delta\phi = 0$ or $\pi$. Then $\cos\Delta\phi = \pm 1$, $\sin\Delta\phi = 0$, and the equation reduces to $(E_x/E_{0x} \mp E_y/E_{0y})^2 = 0$ — a straight line through the origin. *Sketch:* a single line segment back and forth.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* **Circular condition:** $E_{0x} = E_{0y} = E_0$ **and** $\Delta\phi = \pm\pi/2$. The general equation collapses to $E_x^2 + E_y^2 = E_0^2$ — a circle of radius $E_0$. *Sketch:* a circle, with rotation sense determined by the sign of $\Delta\phi$.

*Step 4 (2 marks):* **Elliptical condition:** The general case where $E_{0x} \neq E_{0y}$ and $\Delta\phi$ is neither $0$ nor $\pi$ nor $\pm\pi/2$. The trajectory is an ellipse with semi-axes determined by the eigenvalues of the conic matrix. *Sketch:* a tilted ellipse.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem Using Phasor Form (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Write the real form of the given field. The phasor $\vec{E}_s = (\hat{x} + j\hat{y}) E_0$ implies:

$$
\vec{E}(z,t) = \text{Re}\!\left[(\hat{x} + j\hat{y})E_0 \, e^{j(\omega t - kz)}\right]
$$

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Separate into real components. The $x$-component is in phase with $\omega t$ (coefficient $1$), the $y$-component is $90^\circ$ ahead (coefficient $j$):

$$
E_x = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz), \quad E_y = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz + 90^\circ) = -E_0 \sin(\omega t - kz)
$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* Compare with the canonical form. We have $E_{0x} = E_{0y} = E_0$ and $\Delta\phi = +90^\circ = +\pi/2$. By the **physics convention** (IEEE/engineering), a $+90^\circ$ phase lead of $E_y$ over $E_x$ produces a wave that rotates *clockwise when viewed from the source looking toward the receiver* (i.e., in the $+z$ direction, against the propagation). This is classified as **RHCP** in the engineering convention, or LHCP in the strict physics Stokes convention — students must explicitly state the convention used.

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Compute the axial ratio AR. For circular polarization, the ellipse is a circle, so $a = b$ and the axial ratio is:

$$
AR = \dfrac{a}{b} = 1 \quad\text{(or 0 dB)}
$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Final statement: The wave is **circularly polarized** with axial ratio = 1 (0 dB) and rotation sense is **right-handed** in the engineering (IEEE) convention.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Before Writing the Exam:**
> 1. **Sign convention for circular polarization sense:** KTU frequently awards 1 mark *specifically* for stating the convention used (IEEE/engineering vs. physics/Stokes). Failure to mention the convention can cost the mark even if the answer is otherwise correct. *Always say:* "In the engineering (IEEE) convention, the sense of rotation is ..."
> 2. **Confusing $\phi_y - \phi_x$ with $\phi_x - \phi_y$:** The sign of $\Delta\phi$ determines the rotation sense. Writing it backwards flips RHCP ↔ LHCP, costing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing linear with elliptical:** A common student error is to assume any wave with two components is "elliptical." If $\Delta\phi = 0$ or $\pi$, the ellipse degenerates to a **line**, not a circle. Always verify $\Delta\phi$ first.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in tilt-angle formulas:** The formula for $\psi$ involves $\tan 2\psi$, not $\tan\psi$. Computing $\arctan(E_{0y}/E_{0x})$ directly gives the wrong angle unless $\Delta\phi = 0$. This is a 2-mark trap in numericals.
> 5. **Mixing up Brewster angle and critical angle:** Brewster's angle exists for *both* total-internal-reflection-allowing and non-allowing media; the critical angle exists only when $n_1 > n_2$. Conflating the two is a frequent error.
> 6. **In phasor-form problems, dropping the $j$:** When the given field has a factor $j$ multiplying $\hat{y}$, students often forget it means a $90^\circ$ phase lead. Always expand to real time-domain before classifying.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following is a **high-density rapid-revision checklist** for KTU Module 2 — Polarization of Waves.

- **Polarization** = trajectory traced by the tip of the $\vec{E}$ vector in the transverse plane. Three shapes: line, circle, ellipse.
- **General wave** in $+z$: $\vec{E} = \hat{x}E_{0x}\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_x) + \hat{y}E_{0y}\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_y)$. Two degrees of freedom: amplitude ratio + phase difference $\Delta\phi$.
- **General polarization ellipse** (master equation):
  $\dfrac{E_x^2}{E_{0x}^2} + \dfrac{E_y^2}{E_{0y}^2} - \dfrac{2 E_x E_y \cos\Delta\phi}{E_{0x} E_{0y}} = \sin^2\Delta\phi$.
- **Linear condition:** $\Delta\phi = 0$ or $\pi$. **Circular condition:** $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$ **and** $\Delta\phi = \pm\pi/2$. **All else:** elliptical.
- **Tilt (azimuth) angle** $\psi$: $\tan 2\psi = \dfrac{2 E_{0x} E_{0y} \cos\Delta\phi}{E_{0x}^2 - E_{0y}^2}$.
- **Ellipticity angle** $\chi$: $\sin 2\chi = \dfrac{2 E_{0x} E_{0y} \sin\Delta\phi}{E_{0x}^2 + E_{0y}^2}$. For circular polarization, $\chi = \pm 45^\circ$.
- **RHCP vs. LHCP:** Always state the convention (IEEE engineering vs. physics Stokes). In engineering convention, RHCP has the $\vec{E}$-vector rotating **clockwise when viewed from behind the wave (looking in the direction of propagation).**
- **Axial ratio** AR $= a/b$ (major to minor axis). AR = 1 (0 dB) for circular; AR = $\infty$ (linear).
- **Brewster's angle:** $\theta_B = \arctan(n_2/n_1)$. At $\theta_B$, the reflected wave is **purely s-polarized** (TE), and the reflected + refracted rays make exactly $90^\circ$.
- **Unpolarized light:** DOP = 0; light from sun, tungsten bulb, candle, etc. Made polarized by reflection (Brewster), transmission through polaroid, scattering, or birefringence.
- **Engineering applications to recall:** antenna polarization matching, Brewster windows in lasers, 3D cinema glasses, LCD displays, weather radar (circular polarization to suppress rain clutter), polarization-maintaining optical fibers.
- **Common SI units to remember:** $\vec{E}$ in $\mathrm{V/m}$, $\vec{H}$ in $\mathrm{A/m}$, intensity $I$ in $\mathrm{W/m^2}$, intrinsic impedance $\eta_0 = 377\;\Omega$, phase in radians, angles in degrees/radians (specify in exam).
- **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark Part B numericals, always: (1) tabulate the given $E_{0x}$, $E_{0y}$, $\Delta\phi$; (2) classify polarization by the conditions table; (3) compute $\psi$ and $\chi$ *only* if the wave is elliptical; (4) state the convention used for rotation sense; (5) box the final answer with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflection and Refraction of Plane Electromagnetic Waves at Boundaries for Normal and Oblique Incidence (Parallel and Perpendicular Polarization)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reflection and Refraction of Plane Electromagnetic Waves at Boundaries for Normal and Oblique Incidence (Parallel and Perpendicular Polarization) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition When a plane electromagnetic wave traveling through one medium encounters a boundary with a second medium, **partial reflection** back into the first medium and **partial transmission (refraction)** into the second medium occur simultaneously. The boundary conditions on the tangential components of the electric and magnetic fields at the interface determine the exact amplitudes, phases, and directions of the reflected and refracted waves. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCECT501 — Module 3) syllabus, this behavior is studied for two cases: - **Normal Incidence:** The incident wave vector is perpendicular ($\theta_i = 0°$) to the boundary plane. - **Oblique Incidence:** The incident wave vector makes an arbitrary angle $\theta_i \ne 0°$ with the normal to the boundary. Under oblique incidence, two distinct linear polarizations are considered: - **Perpendicular Polarization (s-polarization / TE mode):** The electric field vector $\vec{E}$ is perpendicular to the plane of incidence (also called the **E-field is normal to the page**). - **Parallel Polarization (p-polarization / TM mode):** The electric field vector $\vec{E}$ lies in the plane of incidence (the magnetic field $\vec{H}$ is perpendicular to the plane of incidence). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Plane of Incidence** is the plane containing the incident wave vector $\vec{k_i}$, the surface normal $\hat{n}$, and hence also the reflected wave vector $\vec{k_r}$ and the refracted (transmitted) wave vector $\vec{k_t}$. All three wave vectors lie in this single plane by Snell's law. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Row Boat Crossing a River" Intuition Imagine you are pushing a long stick (representing a plane EM wave) at an angle toward the edge of a swimming pool (the boundary between air and water). - The part of the stick still in the air **bounces back partially** — this is **reflection**. - The part that enters the water **bends** because water is "heavier" (optically denser) and slows the wave down — this is **refraction**. - The angle at which it bends depends on how fast the wave travels in each medium (the **refractive index**). If you tilt the stick so it hits the water at exactly the **right angle (Brewster's angle)**, the reflected wave disappears — just like polarized sunglasses eliminate glare from a lake at a specific angle. If you push the stick from water toward air at a steep enough angle, the wave cannot escape and **bounces entirely back** — this is **total internal reflection**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Parameters - **Speed of light in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\epsilon_0} = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$ - **Refractive index of medium:** $n = \sqrt{\mu_r \epsilon_r}$ (for non-magnetic media, $\mu_r = 1$, so $n = \sqrt{\epsilon_r}$) - **Snell's law parameter:** The ratio of refractive indices governs refraction: $n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$ > [!NOTE] > In Module 3 of PCECT501, the derivations are typically restricted to **lossless, homogeneous, isotropic, non-magnetic dielectric media** with constants $\mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_0$ unless stated otherwise. The intrinsic impedance then reduces to $\eta = \eta_0 / n$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Boundary Conditions — The Heart of the Problem When an EM wave strikes the planar interface between medium 1 (parameters $\epsilon_1, \mu_1, \eta_1, k_1, n_1$) and medium 2 (parameters $\epsilon_2, \mu_2, \eta_2, k_2, n_2$), the fields on either side of the boundary are linked by **Maxwell's boundary conditions** (assuming the interface carries no free surface charge or surface current): - Tangential $\vec{E}$ is continuous: $E_{1t} = E_{2t}$ - Tangential $\vec{H}$ is continuous: $H_{1t} = H_{2t}$ - Normal $\vec{D}$ is continuous (no free charge): $D_{1n} = D_{2n}$ - Normal $\vec{B}$ is continuous: $B_{1n} = B_{2n}$ For plane wave analysis, only the **tangential continuity equations** are needed since the field components are decomposed into tangential and normal pieces. ## 2.2 Wave Vector Geometry (Phase Matching) Let the boundary be the $z = 0$ plane. All three waves (incident, reflected, transmitted) have the same angular frequency $\omega$. The phase of each wave at $z = 0$ must match, leading to: $$k_1 \sin\theta_i = k_1 \sin\theta_r = k_2 \sin\theta_t$$ This yields **two fundamental laws**: $$\boxed{\theta_i = \theta_r \quad \text{(Law of Reflection)}}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{\sin\theta_i}{\sin\theta_t} = \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \quad \text{(Snell's Law)}}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table completely. The Fresnel coefficients below are the **most-tested formulas** in KTU ESE Module 3. | Quantity | Normal Incidence | Perpendicular Polarization (s / TE) | Parallel Polarization (p / TM) | |---|---|---|---| | Reflection Coefficient (E-field) | $\Gamma = \dfrac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}$ | $\Gamma_\perp = \dfrac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i - \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}$ | $\Gamma_\parallel = \dfrac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t - \eta_1 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i}$ | | Transmission Coefficient (E-field) | $\tau = \dfrac{2\eta_2}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}$ | $\tau_\perp = \dfrac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}$ | $\tau_\parallel = \dfrac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i}$ | | Reflectance (Power) | $R = \vert\Gamma\vert^2$ | $R_\perp = \vert\Gamma_\perp\vert^2$ | $R_\parallel = \vert\Gamma_\parallel\vert^2$ | | Transmittance (Power) | $T = \dfrac{\eta_1}{\eta_2}\vert\tau\vert^2$ | $T_\perp = \dfrac{\eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}\vert\tau_\perp\vert^2$ | $T_\parallel = \dfrac{\eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}\vert\tau_\parallel\vert^2$ | | Energy Conservation | $R + T = 1$ | $R_\perp + T_\perp = 1$ | $R_\parallel + T_\parallel = 1$ | | Brewster's Angle | Not applicable | No zero (no Brewster's angle) | $\tan\theta_B = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | | Critical Angle | Not applicable | $\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ (when $n_1 > n_2$) | Same as $\perp$ | ## 2.4 Why & How — Physical Interpretation of Each Step - **Step 1 — Impedance mismatch drives reflection:** Even at normal incidence, if $\eta_1 \ne \eta_2$, a portion of the wave bounces back. A perfect impedance match ($\eta_1 = \eta_2$) eliminates reflection — this is the principle behind anti-reflection coatings and radome design. - **Step 2 — The angle of refraction depends on the medium properties:** Higher refractive index → wave slows down → wavefronts bend toward the normal. This is Huygens' principle in action. - **Step 3 — Polarization matters at oblique incidence:** At normal incidence, the two polarizations are identical. But at oblique incidence, $\Gamma_\perp \ne \Gamma_\parallel$, leading to the phenomenon that **unpolarized light becomes partially polarized upon reflection**. - **Step 4 — Brewster's angle (polarization angle):** When $\theta_i + \theta_t = 90°$, the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular. For p-polarization, this forces the reflected E-field to be zero — giving perfectly polarized reflected light. This is critical in **laser optics** and **glare-reducing lenses**. - **Step 5 — Critical angle and total internal reflection (TIR):** When going from a denser to a rarer medium ($n_1 > n_2$), if $\theta_i > \theta_c$, the refracted wave becomes **evanescent** (exponentially decaying, non-propagating). No real power crosses the boundary; all energy is reflected. Applications: **optical fibers**, **prisms in binoculars**, and **TIR fluorescence microscopy**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Field | Application | |---|---| | Optical Fiber Communication | TIR in glass fibers for long-distance signal transmission | | Radar Cross-Section Reduction | Impedance-matched coatings (Radar Absorbing Material) | | Solar Cells | Anti-reflection coatings tuned to specific polarization | | Laser Systems | Brewster-angle windows to achieve polarized output | | Microwave Engineering | Radome design, dielectric lens antennas | | Medical Imaging | Optical coherence tomography (OCT) uses partial reflection at tissue boundaries | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > All derivations below assume **lossless, non-magnetic, homogeneous, isotropic media** with $\mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_0$. This implies $n = \sqrt{\epsilon_r}$ and $\eta = \eta_0/n$. --- ## 3.1 Derivation I — Reflection and Transmission for Normal Incidence ### 3.1.1 Setup A plane wave traveling in $+z$ direction in medium 1 strikes the planar boundary at $z=0$ with medium 2. The incident, reflected, and transmitted electric fields are: $$\vec{E_i} = \hat{x}\, E_0\, e^{-j k_1 z}$$ $$\vec{E_r} = \hat{x}\, \Gamma E_0\, e^{+j k_1 z}$$ $$\vec{E_t} = \hat{x}\, \tau E_0\, e^{-j k_2 z}$$ The corresponding magnetic fields (using $\vec{H} = \hat{k} \times \vec{E}/\eta$): $$\vec{H_i} = \hat{y}\, \dfrac{E_0}{\eta_1}\, e^{-j k_1 z}$$ $$\vec{H_r} = -\hat{y}\, \dfrac{\Gamma E_0}{\eta_1}\, e^{+j k_1 z}$$ $$\vec{H_t} = \hat{y}\, \dfrac{\tau E_0}{\eta_2}\, e^{-j k_2 z}$$ ### 3.1.2 Apply Boundary Conditions at $z=0$ **Tangential E-field continuity:** $$E_0 + \Gamma E_0 = \tau E_0$$ This simplifies to: $$1 + \Gamma = \tau \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$ **Tangential H-field continuity:** $$\dfrac{E_0}{\eta_1} - \dfrac{\Gamma E_0}{\eta_1} = \dfrac{\tau E_0}{\eta_2}$$ This simplifies to: $$1 - \Gamma = \tau\, \dfrac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$ ### 3.1.3 Solve Simultaneously Divide Eq. 1 by Eq. 2: $$\dfrac{1 + \Gamma}{1 - \Gamma} = \dfrac{\eta_2}{\eta_1}$$ Cross-multiply: $$\eta_1(1 + \Gamma) = \eta_2(1 - \Gamma)$$ $$\eta_1 + \eta_1 \Gamma = \eta_2 - \eta_2 \Gamma$$ $$\Gamma(\eta_1 + \eta_2) = \eta_2 - \eta_1$$ $$\boxed{\Gamma = \dfrac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}}$$ Substitute back into Eq. 1: $$\tau = 1 + \Gamma = 1 + \dfrac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1} = \dfrac{\eta_2 + \eta_1 + \eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}$$ $$\boxed{\tau = \dfrac{2\eta_2}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}}$$ ### 3.1.4 Verification **Limit check 1:** If $\eta_2 = \eta_1$ (matched media) → $\Gamma = 0$, $\tau = 1$. ✓ No reflection, perfect transmission. **Limit check 2:** If $\eta_2 \to \infty$ (perfect conductor) → $\Gamma = 1$, $\tau = 2$. The transmitted E-field is twice the incident — the well-known doubling of E-field at a perfect conductor. ✓ **Limit check 3:** Energy conservation: $R = \Gamma^2 = \left(\dfrac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}\right)^2$ $$T = \dfrac{\eta_1}{\eta_2}\tau^2 = \dfrac{\eta_1}{\eta_2}\left(\dfrac{2\eta_2}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}\right)^2 = \dfrac{4\eta_1 \eta_2}{(\eta_2 + \eta_1)^2}$$ $$R + T = \dfrac{(\eta_2 - \eta_1)^2 + 4\eta_1\eta_2}{(\eta_2 + \eta_1)^2} = \dfrac{\eta_2^2 - 2\eta_1\eta_2 + \eta_1^2 + 4\eta_1\eta_2}{(\eta_2 + \eta_1)^2} = \dfrac{(\eta_2 + \eta_1)^2}{(\eta_2 + \eta_1)^2} = 1\ \checkmark$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Fresnel Equations for Perpendicular Polarization (s-polarization) ### 3.2.1 Geometry The plane of incidence is taken as the $xz$-plane. For **perpendicular polarization**, $\vec{E}$ is along the $y$-axis (perpendicular to the plane of incidence), and $\vec{H}$ lies in the $xz$-plane. The three waves have wave vectors in the $xz$-plane making angles $\theta_i, \theta_r, \theta_t$ with the $z$-axis (the boundary normal). The E-field components are: $$\vec{E_i} = \hat{y}\, E_0\, e^{-j k_1 (x \sin\theta_i + z \cos\theta_i)}$$ $$\vec{E_r} = \hat{y}\, \Gamma_\perp E_0\, e^{-j k_1 (x \sin\theta_i - z \cos\theta_i)}$$ $$\vec{E_t} = \hat{y}\, \tau_\perp E_0\, e^{-j k_2 (x \sin\theta_t + z \cos\theta_t)}$$ The corresponding H-field components are obtained from $\vec{H} = \dfrac{1}{\eta}\hat{k} \times \vec{E}$. For a wave propagating with $\hat{k} = \hat{x}\sin\theta + \hat{z}\cos\theta$ and $\vec{E} = \hat{y}E$: $$\vec{H} = \dfrac{E}{\eta}(\hat{x}\cos\theta - \hat{z}\sin\theta)$$ So the tangential H-component (along $x$) is: $$H_{ix} = \dfrac{E_0}{\eta_1}\cos\theta_i \cdot e^{-j k_1 (x \sin\theta_i + z \cos\theta_i)}$$ $$H_{rx} = -\dfrac{\Gamma_\perp E_0}{\eta_1}\cos\theta_i \cdot e^{-j k_1 (x \sin\theta_i - z \cos\theta_i)}$$ $$H_{tx} = \dfrac{\tau_\perp E_0}{\eta_2}\cos\theta_t \cdot e^{-j k_2 (x \sin\theta_t + z \cos\theta_t)}$$ ### 3.2.2 Apply Boundary Conditions at $z=0$ At $z=0$, the exponentials reduce using phase matching ($k_1 \sin\theta_i = k_2 \sin\theta_t$): **Tangential E-field (y-component):** $$1 + \Gamma_\perp = \tau_\perp \quad \text{...(Eq. 3)}$$ **Tangential H-field (x-component):** $$\dfrac{\cos\theta_i}{\eta_1}(1 - \Gamma_\perp) = \dfrac{\tau_\perp \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2} \quad \text{...(Eq. 4)}$$ ### 3.2.3 Solve From Eq. 3: $\tau_\perp = 1 + \Gamma_\perp$. Substitute into Eq. 4: $$\dfrac{\cos\theta_i}{\eta_1}(1 - \Gamma_\perp) = \dfrac{(1 + \Gamma_\perp)\cos\theta_t}{\eta_2}$$ Cross-multiply: $$\eta_2 \cos\theta_i(1 - \Gamma_\perp) = \eta_1 \cos\theta_t(1 + \Gamma_\perp)$$ $$\eta_2 \cos\theta_i - \eta_2 \cos\theta_i \Gamma_\perp = \eta_1 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t \Gamma_\perp$$ $$\Gamma_\perp(\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t) = \eta_2 \cos\theta_i - \eta_1 \cos\theta_t$$ $$\boxed{\Gamma_\perp = \dfrac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i - \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}}$$ And the transmission coefficient: $$\tau_\perp = 1 + \Gamma_\perp = \dfrac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_\perp = \dfrac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Fresnel Equations for Parallel Polarization (p-polarization) ### 3.3.1 Geometry For **parallel polarization**, $\vec{H}$ is along the $y$-axis (perpendicular to the plane of incidence), and $\vec{E}$ lies in the $xz$-plane. The H-fields are written first as: $$\vec{H_i} = \hat{y}\, \dfrac{E_0}{\eta_1}\, e^{-j k_1 (x \sin\theta_i + z \cos\theta_i)}$$ $$\vec{H_r} = -\hat{y}\, \dfrac{\Gamma_\parallel E_0}{\eta_1}\, e^{-j k_1 (x \sin\theta_i - z \cos\theta_i)}$$ $$\vec{H_t} = \hat{y}\, \dfrac{\tau_\parallel E_0}{\eta_2}\, e^{-j k_2 (x \sin\theta_t + z \cos\theta_t)}$$ The corresponding E-fields from $\vec{E} = \eta \vec{H} \times \hat{k}$ have tangential $x$-components: $$E_{ix} = -E_0 \cos\theta_i \cdot e^{-j k_1 (x \sin\theta_i + z \cos\theta_i)}$$ $$E_{rx} = \Gamma_\parallel E_0 \cos\theta_i \cdot e^{-j k_1 (x \sin\theta_i - z \cos\theta_i)}$$ $$E_{tx} = -\tau_\parallel E_0 \cos\theta_t \cdot e^{-j k_2 (x \sin\theta_t + z \cos\theta_t)}$$ ### 3.3.2 Apply Boundary Conditions at $z=0$ **Tangential E-field (x-component):** $$-\cos\theta_i(1 - \Gamma_\parallel) = -\tau_\parallel \cos\theta_t$$ $$\cos\theta_i(1 - \Gamma_\parallel) = \tau_\parallel \cos\theta_t \quad \text{...(Eq. 5)}$$ **Tangential H-field (y-component):** $$\dfrac{1}{\eta_1}(1 + \Gamma_\parallel) = \dfrac{\tau_\parallel}{\eta_2} \quad \text{...(Eq. 6)}$$ ### 3.3.3 Solve From Eq. 6: $\tau_\parallel = \dfrac{\eta_2}{\eta_1}(1 + \Gamma_\parallel)$. Substitute into Eq. 5: $$\cos\theta_i(1 - \Gamma_\parallel) = \dfrac{\eta_2}{\eta_1}(1 + \Gamma_\parallel) \cos\theta_t$$ $$\eta_1 \cos\theta_i(1 - \Gamma_\parallel) = \eta_2 \cos\theta_t(1 + \Gamma_\parallel)$$ $$\eta_1 \cos\theta_i - \eta_1 \cos\theta_i \Gamma_\parallel = \eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_2 \cos\theta_t \Gamma_\parallel$$ $$\Gamma_\parallel(\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i) = \eta_1 \cos\theta_i - \eta_2 \cos\theta_t$$ $$\boxed{\Gamma_\parallel = \dfrac{\eta_1 \cos\theta_i - \eta_2 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_1 \cos\theta_i + \eta_2 \cos\theta_t} = \dfrac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t - \eta_1 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i}}$$ And the transmission coefficient: $$\boxed{\tau_\parallel = \dfrac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_1 \cos\theta_i + \eta_2 \cos\theta_t}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Brewster's Angle for Parallel Polarization ### 3.4.1 Condition for Zero Reflection For p-polarization, set $\Gamma_\parallel = 0$. The numerator of $\Gamma_\parallel$ must vanish: $$\eta_2 \cos\theta_t - \eta_1 \cos\theta_i = 0$$ $$\eta_2 \cos\theta_t = \eta_1 \cos\theta_i$$ Using $\eta = \eta_0/n$: $$\dfrac{\cos\theta_t}{n_2} = \dfrac{\cos\theta_i}{n_1}$$ $$n_1 \cos\theta_t = n_2 \cos\theta_i$$ ### 3.4.2 Combine with Snell's Law Square both sides and use $\sin^2\theta_t = 1 - \cos^2\theta_t$: $$n_1^2 (1 - \sin^2\theta_t) = n_2^2 \cos^2\theta_i$$ $$n_1^2 - n_1^2 \sin^2\theta_t = n_2^2(1 - \sin^2\theta_i)$$ Apply Snell: $n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t \Rightarrow n_1^2 \sin^2\theta_i = n_2^2 \sin^2\theta_t$. Substitute $\sin^2\theta_t$: $$n_1^2 - n_1^2 \cdot \dfrac{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta_i}{n_2^2} = n_2^2 - n_2^2 \sin^2\theta_i$$ $$n_1^2 - \dfrac{n_1^4 \sin^2\theta_i}{n_2^2} = n_2^2(1 - \sin^2\theta_i)$$ Multiply through by $n_2^2$: $$n_1^2 n_2^2 - n_1^4 \sin^2\theta_i = n_2^4 - n_2^4 \sin^2\theta_i$$ $$\sin^2\theta_i (n_2^4 - n_1^4) = n_2^4 - n_1^2 n_2^2 = n_2^2(n_2^2 - n_1^2)$$ $$\sin^2\theta_i = \dfrac{n_2^2(n_2^2 - n_1^2)}{(n_2^2 - n_1^2)(n_2^2 + n_1^2)} = \dfrac{n_2^2}{n_2^2 + n_1^2}$$ $$\cos^2\theta_i = 1 - \sin^2\theta_i = \dfrac{n_1^2}{n_2^2 + n_1^2}$$ Therefore: $$\tan^2\theta_i = \dfrac{\sin^2\theta_i}{\cos^2\theta_i} = \dfrac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}$$ $$\boxed{\tan\theta_B = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}}$$ This is **Brewster's angle**. Note: For s-polarization, $\Gamma_\perp = 0$ requires $\eta_2 \cos\theta_i = \eta_1 \cos\theta_t$, which has no physical solution — **there is no Brewster's angle for perpendicular polarization**. --- ## 3.5 Derivation V — Critical Angle and Total Internal Reflection ### 3.5.1 Condition For TIR, the wave travels from a denser to a rarer medium ($n_1 > n_2$). By Snell's law: $$\sin\theta_t = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2} \sin\theta_i$$ Since $n_1/n_2 > 1$, $\sin\theta_t$ can exceed 1 — which is physically impossible for a propagating wave. The threshold is reached when $\sin\theta_t = 1$: $$\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$$ $$\boxed{\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)}$$ ### 3.5.2 Behavior Above Critical Angle For $\theta_i > \theta_c$, we define $\sin\theta_t > 1$, so $\cos\theta_t = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_t}$ becomes purely imaginary: $$\cos\theta_t = -j\sqrt{\sin^2\theta_t - 1} = -j\alpha$$ Substituting into the Fresnel coefficients makes $\Gamma_\perp$ and $\Gamma_\parallel$ complex numbers of unit magnitude: $\vert\Gamma\vert = 1$. Hence **all incident power is reflected** — confirming total internal reflection. The transmitted field becomes **evanescent** — it decays exponentially away from the boundary as $e^{-\alpha k_2 z}$, with no net power flow into medium 2. # 4. The notation s (German *senkrecht* = perpendicular) and p (German *parallel*) refer to the E-field's relationship with the plane of incidence. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define the plane of incidence. Distinguish between parallel and perpendicular polarization with neat diagrams.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **plane of incidence** is the plane containing the incident wave vector $\vec{k_i}$ and the surface normal $\hat{n}$ at the point of incidence. By Snell's law, the reflected and refracted wave vectors also lie in this plane. - **Perpendicular polarization (s / TE):** The electric field vector $\vec{E}$ oscillates **perpendicular** to the plane of incidence. The magnetic field $\vec{H}$ lies in the plane of incidence. - **Parallel polarization (p / TM):** The electric field vector $\vec{E}$ lies **in the plane of incidence**. The magnetic field $\vec{H}$ is perpendicular to the plane of incidence. **Diagram:** Draw the boundary as a horizontal line with normal pointing up. Mark $\vec{k_i}, \vec{k_r}, \vec{k_t}$ at angles $\theta_i, \theta_r, \theta_t$. Show $\vec{E}$ as a dot (out of page) for s-polarization and as a vertical in-plane arrow for p-polarization. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **State Snell's law and the law of reflection. What is the critical angle and when does total internal reflection occur?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Law of reflection:** $\theta_i = \theta_r$ — the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. - **Snell's law:** $\dfrac{\sin\theta_i}{\sin\theta_t} = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} = \dfrac{k_2}{k_1}$ — the ratio of sines equals the ratio of refractive indices (or wave numbers). - **Critical angle:** $\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ — valid only when $n_1 > n_2$ (wave goes from denser to rarer medium). - **Total internal reflection (TIR):** Occurs when $\theta_i \ge \theta_c$. Beyond this angle, no real refracted wave exists; the transmitted field becomes evanescent (exponentially decaying), and all incident power is reflected back into medium 1 ($\vert\Gamma\vert = 1$). --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **ESE Pattern: Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question] **(a)** Derive the Fresnel equations for reflection and transmission of a plane electromagnetic wave incident obliquely on a dielectric boundary, for the case of **perpendicular polarization**. Clearly state the boundary conditions used. (7 marks) **(b)** A plane wave in free space is incident at an angle of $30°$ on a glass medium with $\epsilon_r = 4$ and $\mu_r = 1$. For perpendicular polarization, compute the reflection and transmission coefficients. (7 marks) #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks** *Step 1 — Setup and boundary conditions (2 marks):* Consider a plane wave in medium 1 ($n_1, \eta_1$) incident at angle $\theta_i$ on a planar interface at $z = 0$ with medium 2 ($n_2, \eta_2$). The plane of incidence is the $xz$-plane. For s-polarization, $\vec{E}$ is along $\hat{y}$ (perpendicular to the plane of incidence). The wave vectors satisfy: - Law of reflection: $\theta_r = \theta_i$ - Snell's law: $n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$ Boundary conditions at $z=0$: - Tangential E continuous: $E_{1y} = E_{2y}$ - Tangential H continuous: $H_{1x} = H_{2x}$ *Step 2 — Write field expressions (2 marks):* The total E-field in medium 1 and the E-field in medium 2 at the boundary are: $$E_0(1 + \Gamma_\perp) = \tau_\perp E_0 \Rightarrow 1 + \Gamma_\perp = \tau_\perp \quad \text{...(i)}$$ For the H-field (using $\vec{H} = \dfrac{1}{\eta}\hat{k}\times\vec{E}$), the tangential component in medium 1 is: $$H_{1x} = \dfrac{E_0 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_1}(1 - \Gamma_\perp)$$ In medium 2: $H_{2x} = \dfrac{\tau_\perp E_0 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2}$ Setting them equal: $$\dfrac{\cos\theta_i}{\eta_1}(1 - \Gamma_\perp) = \dfrac{\tau_\perp \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ *Step 3 — Solve the system (3 marks):* Substitute (i) into (ii): $$\eta_2 \cos\theta_i(1 - \Gamma_\perp) = \eta_1 \cos\theta_t(1 + \Gamma_\perp)$$ Rearranging: $$\Gamma_\perp = \dfrac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i - \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}$$ $$\tau_\perp = \dfrac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}$$ **Part (b) — 7 marks** *Step 1 — Determine parameters (2 marks):* - Medium 1 (free space): $\eta_1 = \eta_0 = 120\pi\ \Omega$, $n_1 = 1$ - Medium 2 (glass): $\eta_2 = \eta_0/\sqrt{\epsilon_r} = 120\pi/2 = 60\pi\ \Omega$, $n_2 = \sqrt{4} = 2$ *Step 2 — Apply Snell's law (1 mark):* $\sin\theta_t = (n_1/n_2)\sin\theta_i = (1/2)\sin 30° = (1/2)(0.5) = 0.25$ $\theta_t = \sin^{-1}(0.25) = 14.48°$ $\cos\theta_t = \sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = 0.9682$ $\cos\theta_i = \cos 30° = 0.8660$ *Step 3 — Compute $\Gamma_\perp$ (2 marks):* $$\Gamma_\perp = \dfrac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i - \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t} = \dfrac{60\pi(0.8660) - 120\pi(0.9682)}{60\pi(0.8660) + 120\pi(0.9682)}$$ $$\Gamma_\perp = \dfrac{51.96 - 115.51}{51.96 + 115.51} = \dfrac{-63.55}{167.47} \approx -0.3794$$ *Step 4 — Compute $\tau_\perp$ (2 marks):* $$\tau_\perp = \dfrac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t} = \dfrac{2(60\pi)(0.8660)}{167.47} = \dfrac{326.73}{167.47} \approx 0.6206$$ **Final Answer:** $\Gamma_\perp \approx -0.379$ (negative sign indicates phase reversal), $\tau_\perp \approx 0.621$. Reflectance: $R_\perp = \Gamma_\perp^2 \approx 0.144$, Transmittance: $T_\perp = \dfrac{\eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}\tau_\perp^2 \approx 0.856$. Verify: $R_\perp + T_\perp = 1\ \checkmark$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question] **(a)** Derive the expression for Brewster's angle for parallel polarization. Show that no Brewster's angle exists for perpendicular polarization. (7 marks) **(b)** A plane wave in a medium with $n_1 = 1.5$ is incident on a boundary with a medium having $n_2 = 1.0$. Calculate (i) the critical angle, (ii) Brewster's angle, and (iii) the reflection coefficient at Brewster's angle for parallel polarization. (7 marks) #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks** *Step 1 — Brewster's condition (2 marks):* For p-polarization, the reflection coefficient is: $$\Gamma_\parallel = \dfrac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t - \eta_1 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i}$$ For $\Gamma_\parallel = 0$ (no reflection), the numerator must vanish: $$\eta_2 \cos\theta_t = \eta_1 \cos\theta_i$$ Substituting $\eta = \eta_0/n$ and squaring with Snell's law $n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$: *Step 2 — Derivation of Brewster's angle (3 marks):* After algebraic manipulation (as shown in Section 3.4): $$\tan\theta_B = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$$ *Step 3 — Why no Brewster's angle for s-polarization (2 marks):* For perpendicular polarization, setting $\Gamma_\perp = 0$ requires: $$\eta_2 \cos\theta_i = \eta_1 \cos\theta_t$$ Substituting Snell's law yields $n_1^2 = n_2^2$, i.e., identical media — a trivial case. For any physically distinct dielectric pair, no solution exists. Hence perpendicular polarization **never** has a Brewster's angle. **Part (b) — 7 marks** *Step 1 — Critical angle (2 marks):* $$\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.6667$$ $$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.6667) \approx 41.81°$$ *Step 2 — Brewster's angle (2 marks):* $$\tan\theta_B = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.6667$$ $$\theta_B = \tan^{-1}(0.6667) \approx 33.69°$$ *Step 3 — Verification and reflection coefficient at $\theta_B$ (3 marks):* At $\theta_B = 33.69°$, by definition of Brewster's angle for parallel polarization: $$\Gamma_\parallel(\theta_B) = 0$$ Therefore, the reflectance $R_\parallel = \vert\Gamma_\parallel\vert^2 = 0$ — no power is reflected for p-polarized light at Brewster's angle. All the incident p-polarized power is transmitted. Cross-check: At $\theta_B$, $\theta_i + \theta_t = 90°$ (verified: $\sin\theta_t = 1.5 \sin 33.69° = 1.5 \times 0.5547 = 0.8321$, so $\theta_t = 56.31°$, and $33.69° + 56.31° = 90°\ \checkmark$). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read This Carefully!** > > 1. **Mixing up $\Gamma_\perp$ and $\Gamma_\parallel$ formulas:** Students frequently swap the $\cos\theta_i$ and $\cos\theta_t$ positions. Memorize the *physical reason*: in the tangential H-boundary condition for p-polarization, $\eta$ goes with the $1/\eta$ side, so the terms rearrange differently than for s-polarization. > > 2. **Forgetting Snell's law before plugging angles:** If the problem gives $\theta_i$ and $n_1, n_2$, you **must** compute $\theta_t$ first using Snell's law. Many students substitute $\cos\theta_i$ and $\cos\theta_t$ without calculating $\theta_t$. > > 3. **Sign of $\Gamma$:** A negative $\Gamma$ means a **180° phase reversal** of the E-field upon reflection (occurs when going from low-$\eta$ to high-$\eta$ medium at normal incidence, or in s-polarization for many cases). Always report the sign. > > 4. **Energy conservation check:** If $R + T \ne 1$ in your answer, you have made an arithmetic or sign error. Always verify. > > 5. **Brewster's angle only for p-polarization:** A common wrong statement is that Brewster's angle exists for both polarizations. **It does NOT exist for s-polarization.** State this clearly. > > 6. **TIR requires $n_1 > n_2$:** Critical angle is only meaningful when the wave goes from a denser (higher $n$) to a rarer (lower $n$) medium. If $n_1 < n_2$, $\sin\theta_c > 1$ and TIR is impossible. > > 7. **Intrinsic impedance formula:** In non-magnetic media, $\eta = \eta_0/\sqrt{\epsilon_r} = \eta_0/n$. Don't write $\eta = \eta_0\sqrt{\epsilon_r}$ — that's the mistake students most commonly make. > > 8. **Transmittance includes the impedance ratio:** $T = (\eta_1/\eta_2)\tau^2$ for normal incidence, with extra $(\cos\theta_t/\cos\theta_i)$ factors for oblique. The transmitted *power* is **not** just $\tau^2$. --- ## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Plane of incidence:** Contains $\vec{k_i}, \hat{n}, \vec{k_r}, \vec{k_t}$. All wave vectors are coplanar. - [x] **Two fundamental laws:** $\theta_i = \theta_r$ and $n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$ (Snell's law). - [x] **Two polarizations:** s (perpendicular/TE) and p (parallel/TM). At normal incidence, both give identical results. - [x] **Fresnel equations:** $\Gamma_\perp, \tau_\perp$ and $\Gamma_\parallel, \tau_\parallel$ — must be memorized. - [x] **Normal incidence simplification:** $\Gamma = (\eta_2 - \eta_1)/(\eta_2 + \eta_1)$, $\tau = 2\eta_2/(\eta_2 + \eta_1)$. - [x] **Energy conservation:** $R + T = 1$ always (use as a verification check). - [x] **Brewster's angle:** $\tan\theta_B = n_2/n_1$, **only for p-polarization**. Reflected light is perfectly s-polarized. - [x] **Critical angle:** $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$ (valid only when $n_1 > n_2$). Beyond $\theta_c$: TIR. - [x] **TIR physics:** $|\Gamma| = 1$, $\Gamma$ is complex, transmitted field is evanescent (decays exponentially), no net power crosses boundary. - [x] **Boundary conditions used:** Only the two tangential continuity conditions (E and H) are needed for non-conducting media. - [x] **Impedance of non-magnetic medium:** $\eta = \eta_0/n$, where $n = \sqrt{\epsilon_r}$. - [x] **Matched media:** $\eta_1 = \eta_2 \Rightarrow$ no reflection, perfect transmission. Applied in anti-reflection coatings. - [x] **Applications to remember:** Optical fibers (TIR), Brewster windows (lasers), radar absorbers (impedance matching), anti-glare sunglasses (Brewster's angle polarization). --- ### End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Reflection and Refraction of Plane EM Waves

topics.title: Snell's Law of Refraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Snell's Law of Refraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Snell's Law of Refraction** (also known as the **Law of Refraction** or the **Descartes-Snell Law**) is a fundamental principle of geometrical optics and electromagnetics that quantitatively describes how an electromagnetic wave changes its direction of propagation when it travels from one isotropic medium into another isotropic medium with different refractive indices.

Mathematically, Snell's Law is stated as:

> **"The ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is a constant, equal to the ratio of the phase velocities of the wave in the two media, or equivalently, the ratio of the refractive indices of the two media."**

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

where:
- $n_1, n_2$ are the **refractive indices** of medium 1 and medium 2 respectively
- $\theta_1$ is the **angle of incidence** (measured from the normal)
- $\theta_2$ is the **angle of refraction** (measured from the normal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Snell's Law is treated as a direct consequence of the **continuity of the tangential component of the wave vector** at a planar dielectric interface. The law is independent of polarization (for isotropic media) and follows directly from Maxwell's boundary conditions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a shopping cart at an oblique angle across a smooth floor (medium 1) onto a rough carpet (medium 2). The wheel that hits the carpet first slows down, while the other wheel continues at its original speed on the smooth floor. This causes the cart to pivot and change direction at the boundary. The **smoother floor is the optically denser (faster) medium** in our analogy, and the **carpet is the optically rarer (slower) medium**.

In the **electromagnetic analogy**:
- The shopping cart = the light ray (wavefront)
- The floor = medium with refractive index $n_1$ (light travels faster)
- The carpet = medium with refractive index $n_2$ (light travels slower)
- The direction change at the boundary = **refraction**

Another intuitive picture: Think of marching soldiers in a diagonal line crossing a muddy field. The first row of soldiers hitting the mud slows down, while the rest are still on the dry ground. The line **bends** — this is exactly how a wavefront bends at a refractive interface.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s (exact by definition: 299,792,458 m/s)
- **Refractive index of vacuum:** $n_0 = 1.0000$
- **Refractive index of air (STP):** $n_{air} \approx 1.0003$
- **Refractive index of water:** $n_{water} \approx 1.333$
- **Refractive index of glass (typical crown):** $n_{glass} \approx 1.52$
- **Refractive index of diamond:** $n_{diamond} \approx 2.417$

> [!NOTE]
> The **refractive index** $n$ of a medium is defined as $n = \dfrac{c}{v}$, where $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum and $v$ is the phase velocity in the medium.

---

## 1.4 Why Snell's Law Matters in Electromagnetics

Snell's Law is the **cornerstone boundary condition** when studying:
1. **Optical fiber communication** (signal propagation in step-index and graded-index fibers)
2. **Antenna radome design** (EM wave transmission through protective shells)
3. **Radar cross-section analysis** (target detection in layered media)
4. **Photonic devices** (waveguides, beam splitters, prisms)
5. **Atmospheric propagation** (radio wave bending, mirage effects)

It directly arises from the requirement that the **tangential component of the wave vector $\vec{k}$** must be continuous across a dielectric boundary — a direct consequence of the **continuity of the tangential $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ fields** (Maxwell's boundary conditions).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Setup

Consider a plane electromagnetic wave incident on a flat interface separating two lossless, homogeneous, isotropic dielectrics:

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| $n_1$ | Refractive index of medium 1 (region of incidence) |
| $n_2$ | Refractive index of medium 2 (region of refraction) |
| $\theta_i$ | Angle of incidence (measured from surface normal) |
| $\theta_t$ | Angle of refraction/transmission (measured from surface normal) |
| $\hat{n}$ | Unit normal to the interface (pointing from medium 1 to medium 2) |
| $\vec{k}_i, \vec{k}_t$ | Wave vectors in medium 1 and medium 2 respectively |

The interface lies in the $xy$-plane at $z=0$, and the normal $\hat{n}$ is along $+z$.

---

## 2.2 The Logic Behind Snell's Law — Step by Step

The derivation hinges on **phase matching** at the interface. Any point on the interface is shared by both the incident and refracted waves; therefore, the phase accumulation along the interface must be equal for both waves.

**Step 1 — Form the incident wave:**
$$\vec{E}_i(\vec{r},t) = \vec{E}_{0i}\,e^{j(\vec{k}_i \cdot \vec{r} - \omega t)}$$

**Step 2 — Form the transmitted wave:**
$$\vec{E}_t(\vec{r},t) = \vec{E}_{0t}\,e^{j(\vec{k}_t \cdot \vec{r} - \omega t)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the boundary condition at $z=0$:**
For the tangential fields to be continuous, the phase factors along the interface must match for all points $\vec{r}_{\parallel}$ and all times $t$. This forces:
$$\vec{k}_i \cdot \vec{r}_{\parallel} = \vec{k}_t \cdot \vec{r}_{\parallel}$$
at the interface, which yields:
$$k_i \sin\theta_i = k_t \sin\theta_t$$

**Step 4 — Relate wave number to refractive index:**
$$k_i = \dfrac{\omega n_1}{c}, \quad k_t = \dfrac{\omega n_2}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and cancel $\omega/c$:**
$$\dfrac{\omega n_1}{c}\sin\theta_i = \dfrac{\omega n_2}{c}\sin\theta_t \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$$

This is **Snell's Law**.

---

## 2.3 Special Cases and Companion Phenomena

### (a) Normal Incidence
When $\theta_i = 0^\circ$, we get $\theta_t = 0^\circ$ — the ray passes straight through, but its **wavelength changes**: $\lambda_2 = \lambda_1 (n_1/n_2)$.

### (b) Critical Angle (Total Internal Reflection)
When $n_1 > n_2$ (light going from denser to rarer medium), there exists a **critical angle** $\theta_c$ at which $\theta_t = 90^\circ$:

$$\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$$

For $\theta_i > \theta_c$, **total internal reflection (TIR)** occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> During TIR, although no net energy crosses the boundary, an **evanescent wave** decays exponentially into the second medium over a characteristic **penetration depth** (typically of the order of a wavelength). This evanescent coupling is the operational principle of optical fiber sensors and near-field microscopy.

### (c) Brewster's Angle
For parallel-polarized light, the reflected ray vanishes at the **Brewster angle**:
$$\theta_B = \arctan\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$

This is a companion concept to Snell's Law because the refracted ray at this angle satisfies $\theta_t + \theta_B = 90^\circ$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $n = c / v$ | Definition of refractive index | dimensionless |
| 2 | $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ | **Snell's Law** (KTU core) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $k_i \sin\theta_i = k_t \sin\theta_t$ | Tangential wave-vector continuity | rad/m |
| 4 | $\sin\theta_c = n_2 / n_1$ | Critical angle for TIR ($n_1 > n_2$) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\theta_B = \arctan(n_2 / n_1)$ | Brewster's angle (no reflection for $\parallel$-pol) | degrees or radians |
| 6 | $\lambda_2 = \lambda_1 (n_1 / n_2)$ | Wavelength change across interface | meters (m) |
| 7 | $\delta = \lambda / (2\pi n_2 \sqrt{\sin^2\theta_i - (n_2/n_1)^2})$ | Penetration depth of evanescent wave | meters (m) |
| 8 | $\eta = \sqrt{\mu / \varepsilon}$ | Intrinsic impedance of medium | ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 9 | $v = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = c/n$ | Phase velocity in dielectric | m/s |
| 10 | $F = \tau / \sin\theta_c$ | Numerical aperture approximation factor | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> Note carefully: in **vertical pipes inside tables**, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid markdown-breaking vertical bars. Example: $\vert \sin\theta \vert$ instead of $\vert\sin\theta\vert$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Optical Fiber Communication:** Snell's Law governs the guided propagation in step-index fibers. The condition $\theta_i > \theta_c$ inside the core ensures total internal reflection. The **numerical aperture** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ is computed from the critical angle via Snell's Law.
2. **Anti-Reflection Coatings:** Multi-layer thin films (such as those on camera lenses) are designed using the wave impedance concept plus Snell's Law to minimize Fresnel reflections.
3. **Atmospheric Ducting & Mirages:** Radio waves and light bend in the atmosphere due to a gradient in $n$ (caused by temperature/humidity). The differential form of Snell's Law ($n(z)\sin\theta(z) = \text{const}$) is the basis of **radio horizon** and **tropospheric ducting** calculations.
4. **Prism Spectrometers:** The deviation angle of a prism depends on Snell's Law applied at both faces, enabling wavelength dispersion.
5. **LiDAR and Radar:** Range and angle calculations in layered atmospheres or through windows require Snell's Law for beam steering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Snell's Law from Phase Matching (Huygens-Fresnel Approach)

This is the **derivation most frequently requested in KTU ESE Module 3**.

### Setup
Consider a plane wave incident on a planar dielectric interface at $z=0$. The wave vector in medium 1 makes angle $\theta_i$ with the normal. The interface is the $xy$-plane. We need to relate $\theta_i$ to $\theta_t$.

### Step 1 — Express the incident wave
Let the incident electric field be a uniform plane wave in medium 1 with angular frequency $\omega$ and wave vector $\vec{k}_i$:

$$\vec{E}_i(\vec{r},t) = E_{0i}\,e^{j(\vec{k}_i\cdot\vec{r} - \omega t)}$$

with magnitude:
$$\vert \vec{k}_i \vert = k_1 = \dfrac{\omega n_1}{c} = \omega\sqrt{\mu_1\varepsilon_1}$$

### Step 2 — Decompose the wave vector into tangential and normal components
Choose the plane of incidence to be the $xz$-plane (without loss of generality). Then:

$$\vec{k}_i = k_1\sin\theta_i\,\hat{x} + k_1\cos\theta_i\,\hat{z}$$

Similarly, the transmitted wave vector in medium 2 is:
$$\vec{k}_t = k_2\sin\theta_t\,\hat{x} - k_2\cos\theta_t\,\hat{z}$$

(Negative $z$-component indicates propagation into medium 2.)

### Step 3 — Write the field expressions
$$\vec{E}_i = E_{0i}\,e^{j(k_1\sin\theta_i\,x + k_1\cos\theta_i\,z - \omega t)}$$
$$\vec{E}_t = E_{0t}\,e^{j(k_2\sin\theta_t\,x - k_2\cos\theta_t\,z - \omega t)}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition at $z=0$
At the interface $z=0$, Maxwell's boundary condition requires the **tangential component of $\vec{E}$** to be continuous. Equivalently, the **phase along the interface** must be identical for both waves at every point $(x,y)$ on the boundary and at every instant $t$:

$$k_1\sin\theta_i\,x - \omega t \;=\; k_2\sin\theta_t\,x - \omega t \quad \text{for all } x, t$$

The $\omega t$ terms cancel because **both waves must oscillate at the same frequency** in a linear, time-invariant medium (this is the **frequency condition**, not Snell's Law).

### Step 5 — Cancel common factors
Dividing both sides by $x$:

$$k_1\sin\theta_i = k_2\sin\theta_t$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the dispersion relations
Using $k_1 = \omega n_1 / c$ and $k_2 = \omega n_2 / c$:

$$\dfrac{\omega n_1}{c}\sin\theta_i = \dfrac{\omega n_2}{c}\sin\theta_t$$

The common factor $\omega/c$ cancels, yielding:

$$\boxed{\,n_1\sin\theta_i = n_2\sin\theta_t\,}$$

This is **Snell's Law** — a direct consequence of phase matching of the tangential field components at a dielectric boundary.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Snell's Law from Fermat's Principle of Least Time

Fermat's principle states: **light travels along the path that makes the optical path length stationary (usually minimum).**

### Setup
A ray travels from point $A$ in medium 1 to point $B$ in medium 2, crossing a flat interface. The interface is the $x$-axis. $A$ is at $(0, h_1)$ and $B$ is at $(d, -h_2)$ with $d > 0$. The ray crosses the interface at point $(x, 0)$ with $0 < x < d$.

### Step 1 — Compute optical path length
$$\text{OPL} = n_1 L_1 + n_2 L_2$$
where $L_1 = \sqrt{x^2 + h_1^2}$ and $L_2 = \sqrt{(d-x)^2 + h_2^2}$.

### Step 2 — Apply the stationary condition
$$\dfrac{d(\text{OPL})}{dx} = 0$$

Compute each derivative:

$$\dfrac{dL_1}{dx} = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + h_1^2}} = \sin\theta_1$$

$$\dfrac{dL_2}{dx} = \dfrac{-(d-x)}{\sqrt{(d-x)^2 + h_2^2}} = -\sin\theta_2$$

### Step 3 — Set the derivative to zero
$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 - n_2 \sin\theta_2 = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve

$$\boxed{\,n_1\sin\theta_1 = n_2\sin\theta_2\,}$$

Same result, derived from a completely different (variational) principle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Angle for Total Internal Reflection

### Step 1 — Start with Snell's Law
$$n_1\sin\theta_i = n_2\sin\theta_t$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange
$$\sin\theta_t = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2}\sin\theta_i$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the physical limit
For TIR to be possible, the wave must originate in the **denser** medium: $n_1 > n_2$. As $\theta_i$ increases, $\sin\theta_t$ also increases. The maximum physically meaningful value of $\sin\theta_t$ is $1$ (i.e., $\theta_t = 90^\circ$, the refracted ray runs along the interface).

### Step 4 — Set $\sin\theta_t = 1$ and solve for the critical angle
$$1 = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2}\sin\theta_c$$

$$\boxed{\,\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\,}$$

For all $\theta_i > \theta_c$, the wave undergoes total internal reflection.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Evanescent Wave Penetration Depth

When $\theta_i > \theta_c$, $\sin\theta_t$ formally exceeds 1, giving a complex $\cos\theta_t$:

$$\cos\theta_t = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_t} = \pm\,j\sqrt{\sin^2\theta_t - 1} = \pm\,j\alpha$$

where $\alpha = \sqrt{\sin^2\theta_i (n_1/n_2)^2 - 1}$ is real and positive.

The transmitted field becomes:
$$E_t \propto e^{j(k_2\sin\theta_t\,x)} \cdot e^{\mp k_2\alpha\,z}$$

The positive exponent in $z$ corresponds to the **growing** branch (non-physical, discarded), and the negative exponent gives the **decaying evanescent wave**:

$$E_t \propto e^{-k_2\alpha\,z}\,e^{j(k_2\sin\theta_t\,x - \omega t)}$$

The **penetration depth** $\delta$ is defined as the distance over which the amplitude decays by a factor of $e$:

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{k_2\alpha} = \dfrac{\lambda_2}{2\pi\sqrt{\sin^2\theta_i - (n_2/n_1)^2}}$$

For $\theta_i$ just above $\theta_c$, $\delta$ is of the order of a wavelength — this is why **near-field optical microscopy** (SNOM) can resolve features below the diffraction limit.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Style)

### Example 1: Basic Snell's Law
**Problem:** A light ray in air ($n_1 = 1.00$) strikes a glass surface ($n_2 = 1.50$) at $40^\circ$. Find the angle of refraction.

**Solution:**
$$\sin\theta_t = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2}\sin\theta_i = \dfrac{1.00}{1.50}\sin 40^\circ = \dfrac{0.6428}{1.50} = 0.4285$$
$$\theta_t = \arcsin(0.4285) = 25.37^\circ$$

### Example 2: Critical Angle
**Problem:** A ray in water ($n_1 = 1.33$) hits a water-air interface. Find the critical angle.

**Solution:**
$$\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} = \dfrac{1.00}{1.33} = 0.7519$$
$$\theta_c = \arcsin(0.7519) = 48.75^\circ$$

### Example 3: Apparent Depth (Mirage-type Geometry)
**Problem:** A coin lies at the bottom of a water pool of depth $d = 2.0$ m. A viewer at near-normal incidence sees it. By how much does its apparent depth differ from the actual depth?

**Solution:** At normal incidence, $\sin\theta_i = \sin\theta_t = 0$, so Snell's Law gives the same angles. The apparent depth is reduced by a factor of $n_2/n_1$:
$$d_{apparent} = d \cdot \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} = 2.0 \cdot \dfrac{1.00}{1.33} = 1.50 \text{ m}$$

So the coin appears to be at **1.50 m** instead of 2.0 m — a **0.5 m** shift.

---

## 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Problem Type | Governing Equation | Use This When... |
|--------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Find $\theta_t$ given $\theta_i$ and indices | $n_1\sin\theta_i = n_2\sin\theta_t$ | Direction change at interface |
| Find critical angle | $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$ | $n_1 > n_2$, ask "TIR possible?" |
| Find Brewster angle | $\theta_B = \arctan(n_2/n_1)$ | Polarization, no-reflection |
| Evanescent wave decay | $\delta = \lambda/(2\pi\sqrt{\sin^2\theta_i - (n_2/n_1)^2})$ | TIR field penetration |
| Numerical Aperture | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Optical fiber coupling |
| Apparent depth shift | $d_{app} = d(n_2/n_1)$ | Viewing through layered media |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Refraction Geometry at a Dielectric Interface

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional flow** of how an incident ray, refracted ray, and the corresponding angles are related at a dielectric boundary.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INCIDENT RAY (Medium 1, n1)"] --> B["INTERFACE: z = 0 plane"]
    B --> C["REFRACTED RAY (Medium 2, n2)"]
    B --> D["REFLECTED RAY (Medium 1, n1)"]
    N["SURFACE NORMAL (z-axis)"] -.-> B

    P1["ANGLE OF INCIDENCE: theta_i"] --> A
    P2["ANGLE OF REFRACTION: theta_t"] --> C
    P3["ANGLE OF REFLECTION: theta_r"] --> D

    Q1["Snell's Law: n1 sin(theta_i) = n2 sin(theta_t)"] --> C
    Q2["Law of Reflection: theta_i = theta_r"] --> D
    Q3["Frequency Continuity: omega_i = omega_t"] --> C
    Q4["Tangential k Continuity: k_i sin(theta_i) = k_t sin(theta_t)"] --> C

    C --> R{"Is theta_i greater than theta_c?"}
    R -- "NO" --> S["Ordinary refraction: ray bends toward or away from normal"]
    R -- "YES (n1 > n2)" --> T["TOTAL INTERNAL REFLECTION: evanescent wave in medium 2"]

    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB4B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style N fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style T fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style S fill:#CCFFCC,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Refraction Behavior Based on Indices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["START: Ray from Medium 1 to Medium 2"] --> S1{"Compare n1 and n2"}

    S1 -- "n1 less than n2 (denser rarer boundary)" --> S2["Ray bends TOWARD normal"]
    S2 --> S2a["theta_t less than theta_i"]
    S2a --> S2b["Wavelength shrinks: lambda2 = lambda1 (n1/n2)"]

    S1 -- "n1 greater than n2 (rarer to denser)" --> S3["Ray bends AWAY from normal"]
    S3 --> S3a["theta_t greater than theta_i"]
    S3a --> S3b["Critical angle: sin(theta_c) = n2/n1"]
    S3b --> S3c{"theta_i greater than theta_c?"}
    S3c -- "NO" --> S3d["Normal refraction occurs"]
    S3c -- "YES" --> S3e["TOTAL INTERNAL REFLECTION (TIR)"]
    S3e --> S3f["Evanescent wave: decay length delta"]

    S1 -- "n1 equals n2" --> S4["No bending: ray passes straight through"]
    S4 --> S4a["theta_t = theta_i, lambda unchanged"]

    style S0 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style S2 fill:#CCFFCC,stroke:#060
    style S3 fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#006
    style S3e fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#900
    style S3f fill:#FFD700,stroke:#A00
    style S4 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Optical Fiber (Snell's Law in Action)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["LIGHT SOURCE (laser/LED)"] --> COUPL["COUPLING LENS"]
    COUPL --> CORE["FIBER CORE (n1 = 1.48)"]
    CORE -- "Snell's Law at core-cladding boundary" --> CLAD["CLADDING (n2 = 1.46)"]
    CLAD -- "Total Internal Reflection" --> CORE
    CORE --> OUT["OUTPUT END FACE"]
    OUT --> DET["PHOTODETECTOR"]

    NA["NUMERICAL APERTURE: NA = sqrt(n1^2 - n2^2)"] -.-> COUPL
    TH["CRITICAL ANGLE: sin(theta_c) = n2/n1"] -.-> CORE
    EV["EVANESCENT FIELD in cladding"] -.-> CLAD

    style SRC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style CORE fill:#FFB4B4,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style CLAD fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#006,stroke-width:2px
    style EV fill:#FFCC99,stroke:#A00
    style NA fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#060
    style TH fill:#FFF0E0,stroke:#A60
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Refraction Event

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph MED1["MEDIUM 1 (n1)"]
        IR["Incident Ray"]:::ray
        RR["Reflected Ray"]:::ray
        NORM1["Normal (downward arrow)"]:::axis
    end

    subgraph IFACE["DIELECTRIC INTERFACE z=0"]
        SURF["--- interface plane ---"]:::surface
    end

    subgraph MED2["MEDIUM 2 (n2)"]
        TR["Transmitted (Refracted) Ray"]:::ray
        NORM2["Normal (continued)"]:::axis
    end

    IR --> SURF
    SURF --> RR
    SURF --> TR
    NORM1 --> SURF
    NORM2 --> SURF

    classDef ray fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef axis fill:#DDDDDD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef surface fill:#000000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
```
