<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define Poynting vector. State its physical significance.**
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The Poynting vector is defined as the cross product of the electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ and the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$:

$$\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It represents the **instantaneous power density** (power per unit area) flowing through a surface perpendicular to the direction of propagation.
- Its **direction** gives the direction of energy flow in the electromagnetic wave.
- Its **magnitude** gives the rate of energy crossing a unit area placed perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation.

---

#### **Q2. What is the average power density of a uniform plane wave?**
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
For a sinusoidal uniform plane wave with electric field phasor $\vec{E}_s$ and magnetic field phasor $\vec{H}_s$, the **time-average Poynting vector** is:

$$\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(\vec{E}_s \times \vec{H}_s^*) \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

In free space, in terms of the peak electric field $E_0$:

$$S_{avg} = \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0} = \frac{E_0^2}{754} \approx 1.327 \times 10^{-3} E_0^2 \text{ W/m}^2$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from Maxwell's curl equations, derive the expression for the Poynting vector and state the Poynting theorem.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A uniform plane wave propagating in free space has an electric field given by $\vec{E}(z,t) = \hat{a}_x 50 \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$ V/m. Calculate:
   (i) The corresponding magnetic field $\vec{H}(z,t)$.
   (ii) The instantaneous Poynting vector $\vec{S}(z,t)$.
   (iii) The time-average power density $S_{avg}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Poynting Vector:**

**Step 1:** Write Maxwell's two curl equations in a source-free region ($\vec{J}=0, \rho=0$):

$$\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{E} = -\mu \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{H} = \varepsilon \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2:** Dot-multiply (1) by $\vec{H}$ and (2) by $\vec{E}$:

$$\vec{H} \cdot (\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{E}) = -\mu \vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

$$\vec{E} \cdot (\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{H}) = \varepsilon \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract (4) from (3):

$$\vec{H} \cdot (\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{E}) - \vec{E} \cdot (\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{H}) = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\mu H^2 + \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2\right)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the vector identity:
$$\vec{\nabla} \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = \vec{H} \cdot (\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{E}) - \vec{E} \cdot (\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{H})$$

Therefore:

$$\vec{\nabla} \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = -\frac{\partial w}{\partial t}$$

where $w = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 + \frac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ is the total EM energy density.

**Step 5:** Define the **Poynting vector**:

$$\boxed{\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}}$$

The Poynting theorem is:

$$\boxed{\vec{\nabla} \cdot \vec{S} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = 0}$$

This expresses conservation of electromagnetic energy: the net outflow of power equals the rate of decrease of stored field energy.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $\vec{E}(z,t) = \hat{a}_x 50 \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$ V/m; medium is free space with $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ Ω.

**(i) Magnetic Field:**

Since $\vec{E}$ is along $\hat{a}_x$ and the wave propagates in $+z$ direction, $\vec{H}$ is along $\hat{a}_y$ (from right-hand rule: $\hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y$).

$$\vec{H}(z,t) = \hat{a}_y \frac{E_0}{\eta_0} \cos(\omega t - \beta z) = \hat{a}_y \frac{50}{120\pi} \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$$

$$\boxed{\vec{H}(z,t) = \hat{a}_y \, 0.1326 \cos(\omega t - \beta z) \text{ A/m}}$$

**(ii) Instantaneous Poynting Vector:**

$$\vec{S}(z,t) = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} = \hat{a}_x 50 \cos(\omega t - \beta z) \times \hat{a}_y \frac{50}{120\pi}\cos(\omega t - \beta z)$$

Using $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$:

$$\boxed{\vec{S}(z,t) = \hat{a}_z \frac{2500}{120\pi}\cos^2(\omega t - \beta z) = \hat{a}_z \, 6.631 \cos^2(\omega t - \beta z) \text{ W/m}^2}$$

**(iii) Time-Average Power Density:**

Using $\langle \cos^2 \rangle = 1/2$:

$$S_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{E_0^2}{\eta_0} = \frac{(50)^2}{2 \times 120\pi} = \frac{2500}{240\pi}$$

$$\boxed{S_{avg} \approx 3.316 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the time-average power density of a uniform plane wave in terms of the electric and magnetic field phasors. Why is the time-average power density practically more useful than the instantaneous one?

**(b) [7 Marks]** The magnetic field of a plane wave in free space is given by $\vec{H}(z,t) = \hat{a}_y 2 \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$ A/m. Find:
   (i) The wave impedance and direction of propagation.
   (ii) The electric field phasor $\vec{E}_s$.
   (iii) The total power crossing a square surface of side 1 m placed in the $xy$-plane.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Time-Average Power Density Derivation:**

For a sinusoidal uniform plane wave, the phasor fields are:
$$\vec{E}(z,t) = \text{Re}[\vec{E}_s e^{j\omega t}], \quad \vec{H}(z,t) = \text{Re}[\vec{H}_s e^{j\omega t}]$$

The instantaneous Poynting vector is:

$$\vec{S}(t) = [\text{Re}(\vec{E}_s e^{j\omega t})] \times [\text{Re}(\vec{H}_s e^{j\omega t})]$$

Using the identity $\text{Re}(A)\times\text{Re}(B) = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(AB^*) + \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(AB e^{j2\omega t})$:

$$\vec{S}(t) = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(\vec{E}_s \times \vec{H}_s^*) + \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(\vec{E}_s \times \vec{H}_s e^{j2\omega t})$$

Averaging over one full period $T = 2\pi/\omega$, the second (oscillating) term vanishes, leaving:

$$\boxed{\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(\vec{E}_s \times \vec{H}_s^*)}$$

**Why time-average is more useful in practice:**
- Real instruments (power meters, bolometers) cannot track the optical/microwave-frequency oscillation of $\vec{S}(t)$.
- The human eye and most sensors respond to average intensity, not instantaneous peaks.
- Safety standards (FCC, ICNIRP) are quoted in terms of **time-average** power density (W/m²).
- It directly gives the DC-equivalent power that would heat a resistor or charge a battery.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $\vec{H}(z,t) = \hat{a}_y 2 \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$ A/m in free space.

**(i) Wave Impedance and Direction:**
- Medium: free space → $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ Ω.
- The argument $(\omega t - \beta z)$ indicates propagation in the **$+z$ direction**.
- The intrinsic impedance of the medium equals the wave impedance for plane waves: $\eta = \eta_0 = 120\pi$ Ω.

**(ii) Electric Field Phasor:**

$$\vec{H}_s = \hat{a}_y 2 e^{-j\beta z} \text{ A/m}$$

For a plane wave in free space, $\vec{E}_s = \eta_0 \vec{H}_s \times \hat{a}_k$ where $\hat{a}_k = \hat{a}_z$ is the propagation direction. Using $\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x$:

Wait — let me redo this carefully. The correct relation is $\vec{E}_s = -\eta_0 \hat{a}_k \times \vec{H}_s$ for plane waves, which gives:

$$\vec{E}_s = -\eta_0 (\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_y 2 e^{-j\beta z}) = -\eta_0 (-\hat{a}_x) 2 e^{-j\beta z} = \hat{a}_x \cdot 240\pi \cdot e^{-j\beta z}$$

$$\boxed{\vec{E}_s = \hat{a}_x \, 240\pi \, e^{-j\beta z} \approx \hat{a}_x \, 753.98 \, e^{-j\beta z} \text{ V/m}}$$

**(iii) Total Power Crossing 1 m × 1 m Surface in $xy$-plane:**

$$P = \int \vec{S}_{avg} \cdot d\vec{A} = S_{avg} \cdot A = \frac{\vert E_s \vert^2}{2\eta_0} \cdot A$$

$$P = \frac{(240\pi)^2}{2 \times 120\pi} \cdot 1 = \frac{240\pi}{2} = 120\pi \approx 377 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 376.99 \text{ W} \approx 377 \text{ W}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $\vec{E}_s$ direction** — Always verify $\vec{E}_s \times \vec{H}_s$ points in the direction of propagation $\hat{a}_k$. Wrong sign → 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Confusing $\eta$ with $\eta_0$** — Free space $\eta_0 = 120\pi$ Ω; in a different medium, you MUST compute $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ first. Using $\eta_0$ everywhere loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in time-average** — The biggest trap! Instantaneous $S = E_0 H_0 \cos^2$, average $S = \frac{1}{2} E_0 H_0$. Marks are deducted heavily if you write $S_{avg} = E_0^2/\eta$ without the $\frac{1}{2}$.
> 4. **Mixing RMS and Peak values** — KTU problems almost always use peak (amplitude) values. If RMS is given, convert using $E_{rms} = E_0/\sqrt{2}$ before substitution.
> 5. **Wrong vector identity** — In the derivation, students often write $\vec{\nabla} \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = \vec{E} \cdot (\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{H}) - \vec{H} \cdot (\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{E})$ (sign reversed). This flips the entire sign of the Poynting theorem and costs 3–4 marks.
> 6. **Skipping units** — Always state the final answer with units (W/m² for power density, W for total power). Missing units = 0.5 mark penalty.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Poynting vector definition:** $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ — units W/m², direction given by right-hand rule.
- [x] **Physical meaning:** Instantaneous power density (power flow per unit area) of an EM wave.
- [x] **Poynting theorem:** $\vec{\nabla} \cdot \vec{S} + \partial w/\partial t = 0$ — energy conservation.
- [x] **Energy density:** $w = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 + \frac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ (J/m³); in a plane wave $w_e = w_m$.
- [x] **Time-average power density (sinusoidal):** $\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(\vec{E}_s \times \vec{H}_s^*)$ — units W/m².
- [x] **Free-space formula:** $S_{avg} = E_0^2/(2\eta_0) \approx E_0^2/754$ W/m².
- [x] **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ Ω.
- [x] **Intrinsic impedance of general medium:** $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ (for lossless, non-magnetic: $\eta = \eta_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$).
- [x] **Total power through a surface:** $P = \int_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{A}$ — only the component of $\vec{S}$ normal to the surface contributes.
- [x] **Direction of propagation:** Always along $\vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ (i.e., $\hat{a}_k$).
- [x] **Poynting vector vanishes inside a perfect conductor** (since $\vec{E}=0$ inside), confirming no energy penetrates a perfect conductor.
- [x] **In a plane wave:** $|\vec{E}| = \eta |\vec{H}|$ and $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \hat{a}_k$.
- [x] **KTU 2024 expectation:** Derive the Poynting vector (7 marks), apply the time-average formula to compute power density in a numerical problem (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poynting Vector Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Poynting Vector Theorem # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Poynting Vector Theorem** (also known as the *energy conservation theorem for electromagnetic fields*) is the electromagnetic field analogue of the work-energy theorem in mechanics. It is a statement of the conservation of energy in the presence of time-varying electric and magnetic fields and was independently formulated by **John Henry Poynting** (1884) and **Oliver Heaviside**. The theorem introduces a vector quantity called the **Poynting Vector**, denoted by $\vec{S}$, which physically represents the **instantaneous power density** (energy flowing per unit area per unit time) transported by an electromagnetic field across a given surface. Mathematically, the Poynting Vector is defined as the cross product of the electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ and the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$: $$\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} \;\;[\text{units: } \text{W/m}^2 \text{ or } \text{J/(s·m}^2\text{)}]$$ The associated **Poynting Theorem** (in differential form) is the governing energy-balance equation: $$\nabla \cdot \vec{S} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} = 0$$ where $w = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ is the **total electromagnetic energy density** (electric + magnetic), and $\vec{J}\cdot\vec{E}$ is the **ohmic power dissipated per unit volume**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** This topic appears in Module 3 of PCECT501 (Electromagnetics) and is universally tested in **Part B (14-mark)** questions. Students must be capable of stating the theorem, deriving it from Maxwell's curl equations, and applying it to canonical problems (coaxial cable, plane wave, current-carrying conductors). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a river of energy flowing through space. The **Poynting Vector** is the engineer’s way of measuring: - **How much energy** is flowing (magnitude of $\vec{S}$) - **In which direction** the energy is travelling (direction of $\vec{S}$, given by the right-hand rule for $\vec{E}\times\vec{H}$) **Real-world analogy — the water-hose analogy:** Think of an EM wave like water flowing through a pipe: - $\vec{E}$ acts like the pressure pushing water sideways - $\vec{H}$ acts like the swirling vortex around the pipe - Their cross product $\vec{E}\times\vec{H}$ points **along** the pipe, telling you how fast energy is streaming forward > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:** > - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** > - Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** > - Intrinsic impedance of free space: $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377$ **Ω** (exactly $120\pi$ Ω) ## 1.3 Why the Theorem Matters In any electromagnetic system (transmission lines, waveguides, antennas, transformers), engineers must account for: 1. **Energy stored** in the fields (capacitive / inductive) 2. **Energy dissipated** as heat in conductors ($I^2R$ losses) 3. **Energy transported** away from the system (radiation / guided wave) The Poynting Theorem is the *single bookkeeping equation* that balances all three — making it the most powerful diagnostic tool in electromagnetic energy analysis. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Building the Theorem Step by Step To understand the theorem, we must combine two fundamental physical ideas: - **Work done on charges** ($\vec{J}\cdot\vec{E}$) — power delivered to charges per unit volume - **Energy stored in fields** ($w$) — energy residing in $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ - **Energy flowing through surfaces** ($\vec{S}$) — power crossing a closed surface The three are linked by a *continuity equation*, structurally identical to charge conservation ($\nabla\cdot\vec{J} + \partial\rho/\partial t = 0$). ### Step-by-Step Conceptual Walkthrough - **Step 1 — Start with Power Delivered to Charges:** Power delivered per unit volume by the field to moving charges is $\vec{J}\cdot\vec{E}$. - **Step 2 — Express $\vec{J}$ via Ampère’s Law:** Use $\vec{J} = \nabla\times\vec{H} - \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ (Maxwell–Ampère equation). This is the key substitution that brings magnetic energy into the picture. - **Step 3 — Use Faraday’s Law for $\vec{E}$:** Apply $\nabla\times\vec{E} = -\dfrac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ to convert the curl terms into time derivatives. - **Step 4 — Identify Energy Density Term:** After algebraic rearrangement, the time derivatives combine to give $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2\right)$. - **Step 5 — Identify the Divergence of the Poynting Vector:** Using the vector identity $\nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) = \vec{H}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{E}) - \vec{E}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{H})$, the remaining curl terms reduce to $\nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H})$. - **Step 6 — Final Energy Balance:** Combining all the above yields the differential Poynting Theorem. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Name / Significance | SI Units | |---|---------|---------------------|----------| | 1 | $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ | Instantaneous Poynting Vector | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | 2 | $w_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$ | Electric energy density | $\text{J/m}^3$ | | 3 | $w_m = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ | Magnetic energy density | $\text{J/m}^3$ | | 4 | $w = w_e + w_m$ | Total EM energy density | $\text{J/m}^3$ | | 5 | $\nabla\cdot\vec{S} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t} + \vec{J}\cdot\vec{E} = 0$ | Poynting Theorem (differential form) | $\text{W/m}^3$ | | 6 | $\oint_S \vec{S}\cdot d\vec{s} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\int_V w\,dV - \int_V \vec{J}\cdot\vec{E}\,dV$ | Poynting Theorem (integral form) | $\text{W}$ | | 7 | $\vec{S}_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\{\vec{E}_s \times \vec{H}_s^*\}$ | Time-average Poynting Vector (phasor) | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | 8 | $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ | Intrinsic impedance of medium | $\Omega$ | | 9 | $P = \oint_S \vec{S}\cdot d\vec{s}$ | Total power crossing surface $S$ | $\text{W}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Note:** In **lossless** regions (free space, perfect dielectric), $\vec{J}=\vec{0}$, so the theorem reduces to $\nabla\cdot\vec{S} + \partial w/\partial t = 0$. In **steady-state** (time-harmonic), the time-average $\langle\vec{S}\rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{\vec{E}_s\times\vec{H}_s^*\}$. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Poynting Theorem is the foundational tool for analysing: - **Coaxial cables** — confirming that signal power flows in the dielectric between the conductors, *not inside the copper* - **Waveguides & optical fibres** — computing transmitted/reflected power at discontinuities - **Antennas** — deriving radiated power, directivity, and radiation resistance - **Transformers & inductors** — computing core losses and copper losses - **Microwave ovens, radar, satellite links** — power budget calculations - **Wireless charging & inductive heating** — quantifying transferred energy # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Poynting Theorem We begin with the power delivered per unit volume by an EM field to charges: $$p_v = \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute Maxwell–Ampère’s Law for $\vec{J}$:** We know (from Maxwell’s equation, with displacement current): $$\vec{J} = \nabla \times \vec{H} - \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$ Therefore: $$\vec{J}\cdot\vec{E} = \vec{E}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{H}) - \vec{E}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t}$$ **Step 2 — Use the Vector Identity for Divergence of a Cross Product:** Recall the fundamental vector identity: $$\nabla\cdot(\vec{A}\times\vec{B}) = \vec{B}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{A}) - \vec{A}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{B})$$ Applying this with $\vec{A}=\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}=\vec{H}$: $$\nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) = \vec{H}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{E}) - \vec{E}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{H})$$ Therefore: $$\vec{E}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{H}) = \vec{H}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{E}) - \nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H})$$ **Step 3 — Substitute back into the expression for $\vec{J}\cdot\vec{E}$:** $$\vec{J}\cdot\vec{E} = \vec{H}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{E}) - \nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) - \vec{E}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Faraday’s Law:** From Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction: $$\nabla\times\vec{E} = -\frac{\partial\vec{B}}{\partial t}$$ Substituting: $$\vec{J}\cdot\vec{E} = \vec{H}\cdot\left(-\frac{\partial\vec{B}}{\partial t}\right) - \nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) - \vec{E}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t}$$ $$\vec{J}\cdot\vec{E} = -\vec{H}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{B}}{\partial t} - \nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) - \vec{E}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t}$$ **Step 5 — Assume Linear, Isotropic Media:** For linear media: $\vec{D} = \varepsilon\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B} = \mu\vec{H}$. The time-derivative terms become: $$\vec{H}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{B}}{\partial t} = \mu\vec{H}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{H}}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\mu H^2\right)$$ $$\vec{E}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t} = \varepsilon\vec{E}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{E}}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2\right)$$ **Step 6 — Combine the Energy Density Terms:** Adding the two contributions: $$\vec{H}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{B}}{\partial t} + \vec{E}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\mu H^2 + \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2\right) = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t}$$ **Step 7 — Rearrange into the Final Form:** Bringing everything to one side: $$\nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) + \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + \vec{J}\cdot\vec{E} = 0$$ **Step 8 — Define the Poynting Vector and Final Result:** Setting $\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H}$, we obtain the **differential form of Poynting’s Theorem**: $$\boxed{\;\nabla\cdot\vec{S} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + \vec{J}\cdot\vec{E} = 0\;}$$ Integrating over a volume $V$ bounded by closed surface $S$, and applying the **Divergence Theorem** $\int_V (\nabla\cdot\vec{S})\,dV = \oint_S \vec{S}\cdot d\vec{s}$: $$\boxed{\;\oint_S \vec{S}\cdot d\vec{s} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_V w\,dV - \int_V \vec{J}\cdot\vec{E}\,dV\;}$$ This is the **integral form**, which has the direct physical interpretation: > *The net power flowing out through the surface equals the rate of decrease of stored energy inside plus the ohmic power dissipated inside.* ## 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Example: Power Flow in a Coaxial Cable **Problem:** A coaxial cable has inner conductor radius $a$, outer conductor radius $b$, carries current $I$ along $+z$ in the inner conductor and $-I$ in the outer. A voltage $V$ exists between them. Show that the power flowing down the cable equals $VI$, by computing the Poynting vector. **Step 1 — Find the Electric Field in the Dielectric:** By Gauss’s law, the radial electric field in the dielectric ($a \le r \le b$) is: $$\vec{E} = \frac{V}{r\ln(b/a)}\,\hat{a}_r \quad \text{(V/m)}$$ **Step 2 — Find the Magnetic Field in the Dielectric:** By Ampère’s law, encircling the inner conductor: $$\vec{H} = \frac{I}{2\pi r}\,\hat{a}_\phi \quad \text{(A/m)}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Poynting Vector:** $$\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H} = \frac{V}{r\ln(b/a)}\cdot\frac{I}{2\pi r}\,(\hat{a}_r\times\hat{a}_\phi) = \frac{VI}{2\pi r^2 \ln(b/a)}\,\hat{a}_z$$ Note that $\vec{S}$ points along $+\hat{a}_z$, i.e., the direction of wave propagation. **Step 4 — Integrate Over the Cross-Section:** Total power flowing through any cross-section $z = \text{const}$: $$P = \int_S \vec{S}\cdot d\vec{s} = \int_0^{2\pi}\!\int_a^b \frac{VI}{2\pi r^2 \ln(b/a)}\,r\,dr\,d\phi$$ $$P = \frac{VI}{2\pi\ln(b/a)}\cdot 2\pi\!\int_a^b \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{VI}{\ln(b/a)}\cdot\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = VI$$ Therefore, $P = VI$ — verifying that all power is transmitted through the **dielectric region**, not through the conductors. This is a classic result that students must commit to memory. ## 3.3 Phasor (Time-Harmonic) Form of Poynting Theorem For sinusoidal steady-state fields $\vec{E}(t) = \text{Re}\{\vec{E}_s e^{j\omega t}\}$ and $\vec{H}(t) = \text{Re}\{\vec{H}_s e^{j\omega t}\}$, the **time-average Poynting vector** is: $$\langle\vec{S}\rangle = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\{\vec{E}_s \times \vec{H}_s^*\}$$ The corresponding **time-average Poynting theorem** is: $$\nabla\cdot\langle\vec{S}\rangle = -\langle\vec{J}\cdot\vec{E}\rangle = -\frac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\{\vec{J}_s\cdot\vec{E}_s^*\}$$ > [!NOTE] > In free space (no charges, no currents): $\langle\vec{S}\rangle$ represents the average power radiated per unit area. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Energy-Flow Architecture (Poynting Vector Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional architecture of energy flow** in any electromagnetic system, mapped directly from the differential Poynting Theorem. Each block represents a distinct energy interaction, and the arrows correspond to the three terms in $\nabla\cdot\vec{S} + \partial w/\partial t + \vec{J}\cdot\vec{E} = 0$. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[EM Source
Time Varying E and H Fields] --> B[E Cross H
Poynting Vector S equals E times H] A --> C[Stored Electric Energy
0.5 epsilon E squared] A --> D[Stored Magnetic Energy
0.5 mu H squared] B --> E[Net Power Outflow
Surface Integral of S dot ds] C --> F[Total Energy Density W
W equals We plus Wm] D --> F F --> G[Rate of Change of W
Partial Derivative of W wrt t] G --> H[Ohmic Dissipation
J dot E Per Unit Volume] H --> I[Heat Loss In Conductors
I squared R Power] E --> J[Power Balance Equation
Net Outflow plus Storage plus Loss equals 0] G --> J H --> J J --> K[Energy Conservation
Satisfied At All Points In Space] ``` ## 4.2 Poynting Vector Direction (Right-Hand Rule) The direction of $\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H}$ follows the **right-hand rule** of vector cross product. For a plane wave travelling in the $+z$ direction: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[E Field
Along plus x axis] --> C[Poynting Vector S
Along plus z axis] B[H Field
Along plus y axis] --> C C --> D[Direction of Power Flow
Direction of Wave Propagation] ``` **Reading the diagram:** Sweep fingers from $\vec{E}$ ($\hat{x}$) towards $\vec{H}$ ($\hat{y}$); the thumb points along $\vec{S}$ ($\hat{z}$). For an EM wave in free space, $\vec{E}\perp\vec{H}\perp\vec{S}$ always — they form a mutually orthogonal right-handed triad. ## 4.3 Application Mapping: Where Poynting Theorem Is Used ```mermaid flowchart TD PT[Poynting Vector Theorem] --> A1[Coaxial Cable Analysis
Power Flow In Dielectric] PT --> A2[Antenna Radiation
Radiated Power and Gain] PT --> A3[Waveguide Engineering
Transmitted and Reflected Power] PT --> A4[Transformer Design
Core and Copper Losses] PT --> A5[Optical Fibre Communication
Power Budget per Unit Length] PT --> A6[Wireless Power Transfer
Coupled Coil Efficiency] PT --> A7[Microwave Engineering
S Parameter Network Analysis] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The block diagrams above are designed to render reliably in any Mermaid-compatible viewer (GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian). They use only alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters and contain no special formatting inside the labels, ensuring zero parse errors. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — Conceptual Definition **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > Define the Poynting Vector. State its SI unit and physical significance. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Poynting Vector** is defined as the cross product of the electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ and the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$: $$\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H}$$ **SI unit:** Watts per square metre ($\text{W/m}^2$). **Physical Significance:** It represents the **instantaneous power density** (power flowing per unit area per unit time) transported by the electromagnetic field at a point in space. The direction of $\vec{S}$ gives the direction of energy propagation. The total power crossing a surface is obtained by integrating $\vec{S}$ over that surface: $P = \int_S \vec{S}\cdot d\vec{s}$. --- ### Question A2 — Conceptual Application **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > The electric field in free space is $\vec{E} = E_0\cos(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_x$ V/m. Find the corresponding magnetic field and the instantaneous Poynting Vector. **Model Answer (3 marks):** For a uniform plane wave in free space, the magnetic field is perpendicular to $\vec{E}$ with magnitude $|\vec{H}| = |\vec{E}|/\eta_0$, where $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ Ω. $$\vec{H} = \frac{E_0}{\eta_0}\cos(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_y \;\; \text{A/m}$$ $$\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H} = \frac{E_0^2}{\eta_0}\cos^2(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_z \;\; \text{W/m}^2$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified for Module 3]** > **(a)** State and derive the Poynting Theorem in differential form, starting from Maxwell's equations. Explain the physical meaning of each term. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A coaxial cable with inner radius $a = 2$ mm and outer radius $b = 8$ mm carries a current of $I = 5$ A along the $+z$ direction in the inner conductor and $-I$ in the outer conductor. The voltage between the conductors is $V = 100$ V. Calculate the total power flowing through any cross-section of the cable using the Poynting Vector. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks **Statement of the Theorem (1 mark):** The Poynting Theorem states that the net power flowing out of a closed surface is equal to the rate of decrease of electromagnetic energy stored inside plus the ohmic power dissipated within the volume. **Derivation (5 marks):** Start with power delivered per unit volume: $$p_v = \vec{J}\cdot\vec{E}$$ Use Ampère–Maxwell law: $\vec{J} = \nabla\times\vec{H} - \dfrac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t}$ $$p_v = \vec{E}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{H}) - \vec{E}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t}$$ Apply the vector identity $\nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) = \vec{H}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{E}) - \vec{E}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{H})$: $$p_v = \vec{H}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{E}) - \nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) - \vec{E}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t}$$ Use Faraday's law: $\nabla\times\vec{E} = -\dfrac{\partial\vec{B}}{\partial t}$ For linear media ($\vec{D}=\varepsilon\vec{E}$, $\vec{B}=\mu\vec{H}$): $$p_v = -\mu\vec{H}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{H}}{\partial t} - \nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) - \varepsilon\vec{E}\cdot\frac{\partial\vec{E}}{\partial t}$$ $$p_v = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\mu H^2 + \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2\right) - \nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H})$$ Rearranging and defining $w = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$, $\vec{S}=\vec{E}\times\vec{H}$: $$\boxed{\;\nabla\cdot\vec{S} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + \vec{J}\cdot\vec{E} = 0\;}$$ **Physical meaning (1 mark):** | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | $\nabla\cdot\vec{S}$ | Net power flowing out per unit volume (W/m³) | | $\partial w/\partial t$ | Rate of change of EM energy stored per unit volume (W/m³) | | $\vec{J}\cdot\vec{E}$ | Ohmic power dissipated per unit volume (W/m³) | #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks **Step 1:** Electric field in the dielectric by Gauss's law (1 mark): $$\vec{E} = \frac{V}{r\ln(b/a)}\,\hat{a}_r = \frac{100}{r\ln(4)}\,\hat{a}_r = \frac{72.13}{r}\,\hat{a}_r \;\; \text{V/m}$$ **Step 2:** Magnetic field by Ampère's law (1 mark): $$\vec{H} = \frac{I}{2\pi r}\,\hat{a}_\phi = \frac{5}{2\pi r}\,\hat{a}_\phi = \frac{0.7958}{r}\,\hat{a}_\phi \;\; \text{A/m}$$ **Step 3:** Poynting Vector (1 mark): $$\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H} = \frac{72.13}{r}\cdot\frac{0.7958}{r}\,(\hat{a}_r\times\hat{a}_\phi) = \frac{57.39}{r^2}\,\hat{a}_z \;\; \text{W/m}^2$$ **Step 4:** Total power by surface integration (3 marks): $$P = \int_0^{2\pi}\!\int_a^b \frac{VI}{2\pi r^2\ln(b/a)}\,r\,dr\,d\phi = \frac{VI}{\ln(b/a)}\!\int_a^b\!\frac{dr}{r} = VI$$ $$P = 100 \times 5 = 500 \;\; \text{W}$$ **Step 5:** Verification — The result $P = VI = 500$ W confirms that all transmitted power flows through the dielectric, not through the conductors themselves (1 mark). --- ### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for Module 3]** > **(a)** Define the Poynting Vector. What is the physical significance of the Poynting Theorem? Distinguish between the instantaneous Poynting Vector and the time-average Poynting Vector for time-harmonic fields. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** In free space, the phasor electric field of a uniform plane wave is $\vec{E}_s = 10\,e^{-j\beta z}\,\hat{a}_x$ V/m, where $\beta = \pi/3$ rad/m. Compute (i) the magnetic field phasor $\vec{H}_s$, (ii) the instantaneous Poynting Vector $\vec{S}(t)$, and (iii) the time-average power crossing a surface of area $2\text{ m}^2$ perpendicular to $z$ at $z = 0$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks **Definition (2 marks):** The Poynting Vector is the vector cross product: $$\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H} \;\; [\text{W/m}^2]$$ It represents the instantaneous power density flowing at a point in space — magnitude gives the power per unit area, and direction gives the direction of energy propagation. **Physical significance of the theorem (3 marks):** Poynting's Theorem expresses **conservation of electromagnetic energy**: - The net power leaving a closed surface = Rate of decrease of stored EM energy + Rate of ohmic dissipation inside. This allows engineers to compute radiated power, dissipated power, and stored energy in any electromagnetic device from field quantities alone — without ever using circuit theory. **Instantaneous vs Time-Average Poynting Vector (2 marks):** | Quantity | Formula | Use | |----------|---------|-----| | Instantaneous | $\vec{S}(t) = \vec{E}(t)\times\vec{H}(t)$ | Transient analysis | | Time-average | $\langle\vec{S}\rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{\vec{E}_s\times\vec{H}_s^*\}$ | Sinusoidal steady-state | The time-average form uses **phasors** and removes the time-dependence, which is essential for high-frequency communication systems. #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks **Given:** $\vec{E}_s = 10\,e^{-j\beta z}\,\hat{a}_x$ V/m, $\beta = \pi/3$ rad/m, free space ($\eta_0 = 120\pi\;\Omega$). **(i) Magnetic Field Phasor (2 marks):** For a plane wave travelling in $+z$ with $\vec{E}$ along $\hat{a}_x$, $\vec{H}$ must be along $\hat{a}_y$ (orthogonal to $\vec{E}$ and to the direction of propagation), and related by $\eta_0$: $$\vec{H}_s = \frac{\vec{E}_s}{\eta_0}\;\hat{a}_y = \frac{10}{120\pi}\,e^{-j\beta z}\,\hat{a}_y = \frac{1}{12\pi}\,e^{-j\beta z}\,\hat{a}_y \;\; \text{A/m}$$ **(ii) Instantaneous Poynting Vector (2 marks):** Convert phasors to time domain by taking real parts (assuming $\omega t$ phase): $$\vec{E}(t) = 10\cos(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_x$$ $$\vec{H}(t) = \frac{1}{12\pi}\cos(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_y$$ $$\vec{S}(t) = \vec{E}\times\vec{H} = \frac{10}{12\pi}\cos^2(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_z = \frac{5}{6\pi}\cos^2(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_z \;\; \text{W/m}^2$$ **(iii) Time-Average Power Through $A = 2\;\text{m}^2$ at $z=0$ (3 marks):** $$\langle\vec{S}\rangle = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\{\vec{E}_s\times\vec{H}_s^*\}$$ $$\langle\vec{S}\rangle = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\!\left\{10\,e^{-j\beta z}\cdot\frac{1}{12\pi}\,e^{+j\beta z}\right\}\hat{a}_z = \frac{5}{12\pi}\,\hat{a}_z \;\; \text{W/m}^2$$ Numerical value: $\langle S_z\rangle = \dfrac{5}{12\pi} \approx 0.1326$ W/m². Total average power: $$P_{avg} = \langle\vec{S}\rangle \cdot A = 0.1326 \times 2 = 0.2652 \;\; \text{W}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Poynting Theorem Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing $\vec{E}\times\vec{H}$ with $\vec{H}\times\vec{E}$** — direction matters! Use the right-hand rule. Wrong sign = full mark deduction in 14-mark questions. > 2. **Missing the displacement current term** $\partial\vec{D}/\partial t$ in Ampère's law when deriving the theorem — this is the single most important step; omitting it means the *magnetic energy term* $w_m$ never appears, and the derivation collapses. > 3. **Forgetting to apply the vector identity** $\nabla\cdot(\vec{A}\times\vec{B}) = \vec{B}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{A}) - \vec{A}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{B})$ — most students skip showing this explicitly. KTU examiners award a separate mark for stating and applying this identity. > 4. **Mixing up energy density $w$ (J/m³) with energy $W$ (J)** — small symbols vs capital; check before integrating. > 5. **In time-harmonic problems, using $\vec{E}\times\vec{H}$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{\vec{E}_s\times\vec{H}_s^*\}$** — the factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ comes from $\langle\cos^2(\omega t)\rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}$; omitting it doubles the answer. > 6. **In coaxial cable problems, integrating over the wrong area** — students often integrate $\vec{S}$ over the conductor cross-section instead of the dielectric annular region between $r=a$ and $r=b$. The Poynting Vector is non-zero ONLY in the dielectric. > 7. **Unit errors** — $\vec{S}$ in W/m² but $P$ in W; forgetting to multiply by the area gives wrong final numerical value. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Poynting Vector Definition:** $\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H}$ — units $\text{W/m}^2$. - **Poynting Theorem (Differential Form):** $\nabla\cdot\vec{S} + \partial w/\partial t + \vec{J}\cdot\vec{E} = 0$. - **Poynting Theorem (Integral Form):** $\oint_S \vec{S}\cdot d\vec{s} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\int_V w\,dV - \int_V \vec{J}\cdot\vec{E}\,dV$. - **EM Energy Density:** $w = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ — units $\text{J/m}^3$. - **Time-Average Form:** $\langle\vec{S}\rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{\vec{E}_s \times \vec{H}_s^*\}$. - **Derivation requires three Maxwell equations:** Ampère–Maxwell, Faraday, and the constitutive relations $\vec{D}=\varepsilon\vec{E}$, $\vec{B}=\mu\vec{H}$. - **Vector identity (must state explicitly):** $\nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) = \vec{H}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{E}) - \vec{E}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{H})$. - **Coaxial cable result (high-yield):** Total power transmitted = $VI$, and all of it flows through the **dielectric**, not the conductors. - **Plane wave in free space:** $|\vec{S}_{avg}| = E_0^2/(2\eta_0)$, with $\eta_0 = 120\pi\;\Omega$. - **For lossless free space:** $\vec{J}=\vec{0}$, so the theorem reduces to $\nabla\cdot\vec{S} = -\partial w/\partial t$ — energy is conserved with no dissipation. - **Direction rule:** $\vec{E}$, $\vec{H}$, and $\vec{S}$ form a right-handed orthogonal triad; the Poynting Vector is always perpendicular to both fields. - **Always end a derivation with the boxed theorem** — KTU examiners allocate a separate mark for the final boxed statement. - **Numericals always use SI units** — convert mm to m before substituting; failing to do so is a frequent arithmetic error in board exams. > **Final Note:** Mastering the Poynting Theorem requires fluency with vector calculus (curl, divergence, vector identities) and the physical meaning of energy conservation. Practise at least 5 derivations and 3 numerical problems before the KTU End Semester Examination to secure full marks.

topics.title: Transmission Line as Circuit Elements (L and C)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmission Line as Circuit Elements (L and C)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Transmission Line as a Circuit Element?

In circuit theory, when the physical dimensions of components are negligibly small compared to the wavelength of the operating signal, we use **lumped element models**. However, at high frequencies (like RF, microwaves, and in high-speed digital circuits), the wavelength becomes comparable to the physical length of the conducting wires. In such cases, we must treat the line as a **distributed parameter system**, where voltage and current vary in both magnitude and phase along the length of the line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCECT501 - Module 4):**
> A transmission line is modeled as a **distributed parameter network** consisting of four per-unit-length parameters:
> - **R (Ω/m)** — Series resistance per unit length (conductor ohmic loss)
> - **L (H/m)** — Series inductance per unit length (magnetic field storage)
> - **G (S/m)** — Shunt conductance per unit length (dielectric loss)
> - **C (F/m)** — Shunt capacitance per unit length (electric field storage)

These four parameters together form the **RLGC model**, which is the foundation of Telegrapher's Equations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long garden hose used to water plants far away. As you push water in, there is a slight delay before water comes out the other end. The hose has:
- **Friction** along its walls → equivalent to series **R**
- **Inertia** of the water column → equivalent to series **L**
- **Leakage** through the porous hose material → equivalent to shunt **G**
- **Ability to store water** in the elastic walls → equivalent to shunt **C**

Similarly, a transmission line:
- The **magnetic field encircling the conductors** stores energy → this manifests as distributed **inductance L**
- The **electric field between the two conductors** separated by a dielectric stores energy → this manifests as distributed **capacitance C**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Required:**
> - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$ (permeability of free space)
> - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ (permittivity of free space)
> - For a non-magnetic, non-ferrous conductor: $\mu \approx \mu_0$, and $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$

---

### 1.3 Why Lumped Models Fail at High Frequencies

| Criterion | Lumped Element | Distributed (Transmission Line) |
|---|---|---|
| **Wavelength λ vs Length ℓ** | $\ell \ll \lambda$ | $\ell \geq \lambda/10$ |
| **Voltage/Current** | Spatially uniform | Vary with position *z* and time *t* |
| **Phase Variation** | Negligible | Significant |
| **Modeling Approach** | Single R, L, C, M | Per-unit-length R, L, G, C |

**Rule of Thumb:** When the line length exceeds approximately **1/10th of the wavelength**, the distributed model MUST be used.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Distributed Parameter Circuit Model

Consider a two-conductor transmission line of differential length $\Delta z$. The equivalent circuit of this infinitesimal section consists of:
- **Series elements** (along the line): $R\Delta z$ and $L\Delta z$
- **Shunt elements** (across the line): $G\Delta z$ and $C\Delta z$

When we take the limit $\Delta z \to 0$, we obtain the per-unit-length parameters:
- **R (Ω/m):** Accounts for finite conductivity of the metallic conductors
- **L (H/m):** Accounts for the magnetic field surrounding the conductors
- **G (S/m):** Accounts for the lossy nature of the dielectric between conductors
- **C (F/m):** Accounts for the electric field between the two conductors

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Two-Wire Line (radius *a*, sep. *D*) | Coaxial Line (inner *a*, outer *b*) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacitance $\vert C \vert$ | $\dfrac{\pi\varepsilon}{\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)} \approx \dfrac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$ | F/m |
| 2 | External Inductance $L_{ext}$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln(D/a)$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)$ | H/m |
| 3 | Total Inductance $\vert L \vert$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln(D/a) + \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a) + \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ | H/m |
| 4 | Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ (lossless) | $\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ (lossless) | Ω |
| 5 | Phase Velocity $v_p$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | m/s |
| 6 | LC Product Identity | $LC = \mu\varepsilon$ | $LC = \mu\varepsilon$ | (s/m)² |
| 7 | General Formula for Two-Wire | $C = \dfrac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln\left[\dfrac{D}{2a} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{D}{2a}\right)^2 - 1}\right]}$ | — | F/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Identity for TEM Transmission Lines in Homogeneous Medium:**
> $$LC = \mu\varepsilon$$
> This is a fundamental KTU result. It means once you compute C, you automatically have $L = \mu\varepsilon/C$. For air medium: $LC = \mu_0\varepsilon_0 = 1/c^2$.

---

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind the Formulas

**Why does a transmission line have inductance?**
When current flows in a conductor, it sets up a magnetic field. This magnetic field encircles the conductor and links with the circuit. By Faraday's law, any change in this linkage induces a voltage opposing the change — this is the physical manifestation of distributed inductance.

**Why does a transmission line have capacitance?**
The two conductors, separated by a dielectric, form a parallel-plate-like structure. Charge accumulation on one conductor (say +Q per unit length) and -Q on the other creates an electric field between them. This field stores energy — the manifestation of distributed capacitance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **Antenna Feed Lines:** Used in TV, radio, and cellular base station feeds. Standard 50 Ω and 75 Ω coaxial cables are designed using these formulas.
> - **High-Speed PCB Design:** Microstrip and stripline geometries are analyzed using per-unit-length L and C to predict signal integrity.
> - **Power Transmission:** Although low frequency, the same concepts apply to long overhead lines (modeled with distributed inductance and shunt capacitance to ground).
> - **Telecommunications:** Twisted-pair Ethernet cables, USB, HDMI, and high-speed serial links all rely on controlled characteristic impedance derived from L and C.

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions & Assumptions

1. **TEM Wave Assumption:** We assume Transverse ElectroMagnetic propagation (no E or H component along the direction of propagation).
2. **Quasi-Static Field:** For L and C derivation, fields are treated as static at each cross-section.
3. **Perfect Conductor Approximation (for L & C derivation):** Conductor resistivity is ignored for field calculation; only geometry matters.
4. **Homogeneous, Isotropic Dielectric:** $\mu$ and $\varepsilon$ are uniform between conductors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Capacitance Per Unit Length of a Two-Wire Transmission Line

**Geometry:** Two parallel cylindrical conductors, each of radius **a**, separated by center-to-center distance **D** ($D \gg a$).

**Step 1 — Set up the charge distribution:**
Assume a charge $+\lambda$ (C/m) on conductor 1 and $-\lambda$ on conductor 2.

**Step 2 — Electric potential at any point P in the plane of conductors:**
By superposition of potentials from two line charges:

$$
V(r_1, r_2) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)
$$

where $r_1$ is distance from center of conductor 1, $r_2$ is distance from center of conductor 2.

**Step 3 — Compute potential on the surface of conductor 1:**
On the surface of conductor 1, $r_1 = a$. The point diametrically opposite on conductor 2 has $r_2 = D - a$. Approximating $D - a \approx D$:

$$
V_1 = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute potential on the surface of conductor 2:**
By symmetry, $V_2 = -V_1$ (since the roles of $r_1$ and $r_2$ swap and charge is $-\lambda$).

$$
V_2 = -\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute potential difference:**

$$
V = V_1 - V_2 = \frac{2\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) = \frac{\lambda}{\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Capacitance per unit length:**

$$
C = \frac{\lambda}{V} = \frac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)} \quad \text{F/m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exact form (without $D \gg a$ approximation):**
> $$C = \frac{\pi\varepsilon}{\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)} = \frac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln\left[\dfrac{D}{2a} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{D}{2a}\right)^2 - 1}\right]}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Inductance Per Unit Length of a Two-Wire Transmission Line

**Step 1 — Current in conductor 1: $+I$ (return through conductor 2 as $-I$)**

**Step 2 — Internal flux linkage (inside conductor 1):**
For uniform current density, the internal magnetic flux per unit length inside conductor 1 is:

$$
\Phi_{int,1} = \frac{\mu I}{8\pi}
$$

**Step 3 — External flux linkage (between conductor surfaces):**
The magnetic field at distance $r$ from conductor 1's axis is $B = \dfrac{\mu I}{2\pi r}$ (for $a \leq r \leq D-a$).
External flux per unit length due to conductor 1:

$$
\Phi_{ext,1} = \int_a^{D-a} \frac{\mu I}{2\pi r}\, dr \approx \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — External flux linkage due to conductor 2 (return current $-I$):**
By symmetry, $\Phi_{ext,2} = \dfrac{\mu I}{2\pi}\ln\left(\dfrac{D}{a}\right)$ in the same direction.

**Step 5 — Total flux linkage per unit length (using Lenz's law sign convention):**
The two external contributions ADD (since they reinforce in the loop):

$$
\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{int,1} + \Phi_{int,2} + \Phi_{ext,1} + \Phi_{ext,2}
$$

$$
\Phi_{total} = \frac{\mu I}{8\pi} + \frac{\mu I}{8\pi} + 2 \cdot \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

$$
\Phi_{total} = \frac{\mu I}{4\pi} + \frac{\mu I}{\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Inductance per unit length:**

$$
L = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{I} = \frac{\mu}{4\pi} + \frac{\mu}{\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) \quad \text{H/m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Verification using LC Identity:**
> $$LC = \left[\frac{\mu}{\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) + \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\right] \cdot \frac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}$$
> For $D \gg a$, the dominant term gives $LC \approx \mu\varepsilon$. The small $\dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ correction corresponds to the internal inductance contribution which becomes negligible at high frequencies due to skin effect.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Capacitance and Inductance of a Coaxial Line

**Geometry:** Inner conductor of radius $a$ (carries $+I$), outer conductor of inner radius $b$ (carries $-I$), separated by dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$ and permeability $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Electric field in dielectric region ($a < r < b$):**
By Gauss's law on a cylindrical surface of radius $r$:

$$
E(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon r} \quad \text{(radially outward)}
$$

**Step 2 — Potential difference between conductors:**

$$
V = \int_a^b E(r)\, dr = \int_a^b \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon r}\, dr = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance per unit length:**

$$
C = \frac{\lambda}{V} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)} \quad \text{F/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Magnetic field in dielectric region ($a < r < b$):**
By Ampere's law (only inner conductor current enclosed):

$$
H(r) = \frac{I}{2\pi r} \implies B(r) = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi r}
$$

**Step 5 — External flux linkage per unit length (between a and b):**

$$
\Phi_{ext} = \int_a^b B(r)\, dr = \int_a^b \frac{\mu I}{2\pi r}\, dr = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Internal flux linkage (inside conductors):**
Each conductor contributes $\dfrac{\mu I}{8\pi}$ internally. With both inner and outer conductors:

$$
\Phi_{int,total} = \frac{\mu I}{8\pi} + \frac{\mu I}{8\pi} = \frac{\mu I}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 7 — Total inductance per unit length:**

$$
L = \frac{\Phi_{ext} + \Phi_{int,total}}{I} = \frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) + \frac{\mu}{4\pi} \quad \text{H/m}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Derived Quantities

| Line Type | L (H/m) | C (F/m) | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (lossless) | $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Two-Wire** | $\dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln\left(\dfrac{D}{a}\right) + \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ | $\dfrac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\ln\left(\dfrac{D}{a}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$ |
| **Coaxial** | $\dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right) + \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\ln\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase Velocity in Free Space:** $v_p = c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> **Characteristic Impedance of Free Space:** $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377$ Ω

### 4.4 Functional Block: From Geometry to Per-Unit-Length L and C

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conductor Geometry]:::start --> B[Apply Gauss Law for E field]:::proc
    B --> C[Compute Voltage V via line integral]:::proc
    C --> D[Capacitance C equals lambda over V]:::resultC
    A --> E[Apply Ampere Law for H field]:::proc
    E --> F[Compute Magnetic Flux Phi via surface integral]:::proc
    F --> G[Inductance L equals Phi over I]:::resultL
    D --> H[Characteristic Impedance Z0 equals sqrt L over C]:::output
    G --> H
    D --> I[Phase Velocity vp equals 1 over sqrt LC]:::output
    G --> I

    classDef start fill:#581c87,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    classDef resultC fill:#166534,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef resultL fill:#166534,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

> **Caption:** Functional block diagram summarizing the complete analytical pipeline from physical geometry to the per-unit-length parameters L and C, and the derived quantities $Z_0$ and $v_p$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam KTU 2024 Scheme]`
Define the distributed parameter model of a transmission line. Why is it necessary at high frequencies?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> A transmission line is characterized by **per-unit-length parameters** R (Ω/m), L (H/m), G (S/m), and C (F/m), collectively called the RLGC model. When the line length becomes comparable to the wavelength ($l \geq \lambda/10$), the voltage and current vary with both position and time, making lumped element analysis invalid. The distributed parameter model accounts for these spatial variations and accurately predicts wave propagation behavior.

**Marking Scheme:**
- Definition of R, L, G, C with units — **1 Mark**
- Explanation of distributed model — **1 Mark**
- Reason for high-frequency necessity — **1 Mark**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Series Exam 2024 Scheme]`
For a lossless transmission line, show that $LC = \mu\varepsilon$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> From Maxwell's equations for TEM waves in a source-free region:
> $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\mu \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{H}}{\partial t}$ and $\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{E}}{\partial t}$.
>
> These yield a wave equation with phase velocity $v_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$. For a TEM wave in homogeneous medium, the wave propagates at $v_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$. Equating the two:
> $$\boxed{LC = \mu\varepsilon}$$

**Marking Scheme:**
- Statement of wave equation or Maxwell's curl — **1 Mark**
- Equating phase velocity expressions — **1 Mark**
- Final boxed result — **1 Mark**

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU ESE Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the per-unit-length capacitance of a parallel two-wire transmission line, clearly stating all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A parallel two-wire transmission line, made of conductors of radius $5$ mm and separated by $50$ mm center-to-center, is suspended in air. Calculate the per-unit-length inductance and characteristic impedance at high frequencies.

---

**Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Step 1:** Assume two parallel cylindrical conductors of radius $a$, separated by distance $D$ ($D \gg a$).

**Step 2:** Place line charges $+\lambda$ and $-\lambda$ on the two conductors.

**Step 3:** Using superposition, potential at any point with distances $r_1$ and $r_2$ from the two conductors is:

$$
V = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** On surface of conductor 1: $r_1 = a$, $r_2 \approx D$.

$$
V_1 = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** On surface of conductor 2 (by symmetry): $V_2 = -V_1$.

**Step 6:** Voltage difference:

$$
V_{12} = V_1 - V_2 = \frac{\lambda}{\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 7:** Capacitance per unit length:

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{\lambda}{V_{12}} = \frac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)} \text{ F/m}}
$$

**Assumptions:** (i) $D \gg a$ (ii) perfect conductors (iii) homogeneous dielectric (iv) quasi-static fields.

---

**Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Given:**
- $a = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $D = 50$ mm $= 50 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Air medium: $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$

**Step 1:** Compute the ratio $D/a$:

$$
\frac{D}{a} = \frac{50}{5} = 10
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\ln(D/a)$:

$$
\ln(10) = 2.3026
$$

**Step 3:** Per-unit-length inductance (using formula):

$$
L = \frac{\mu}{\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) + \frac{\mu}{4\pi}
$$

$$
L = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{\pi}(2.3026) + \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{4\pi}
$$

$$
L = (4 \times 10^{-7})(2.3026) + 10^{-7} = 9.21 \times 10^{-7} + 10^{-7}
$$

$$
\boxed{L = 1.021 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H/m} \approx 1.021 \, \mu\text{H/m}}
$$

**Step 4:** Capacitance per unit length:

$$
C = \frac{\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/a)} = \frac{\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{2.3026}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 1.208 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m} \approx 12.08 \text{ pF/m}}
$$

**Step 5:** Characteristic impedance (lossless):

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.021 \times 10^{-6}}{1.208 \times 10^{-11}}} = \sqrt{8.453 \times 10^{4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_0 \approx 290.7 \, \Omega}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU ESE Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the per-unit-length inductance and capacitance of a coaxial cable with inner conductor radius $a$ and outer conductor inner radius $b$, separated by a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$ and permeability $\mu$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A coaxial cable has an inner conductor radius of $1$ mm and outer conductor inner radius of $4$ mm. The dielectric is polyethylene with $\varepsilon_r = 2.26$. Find the characteristic impedance and the velocity of wave propagation.

---

**Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Capacitance Derivation:**

By Gauss's law, for $a < r < b$:

$$
E(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon r}
$$

Voltage difference:

$$
V = \int_a^b E\, dr = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{\lambda}{V} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}}
$$

**Inductance Derivation:**

By Ampere's law, for $a < r < b$:

$$
B(r) = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi r}
$$

External flux per unit length:

$$
\Phi_{ext} = \int_a^b B\, dr = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

Internal flux (both inner and outer conductor): $\dfrac{\mu I}{4\pi}$

Total inductance:

$$
\boxed{L = \frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) + \frac{\mu}{4\pi}}
$$

---

**Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Given:**
- $a = 1$ mm $= 10^{-3}$ m
- $b = 4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\varepsilon_r = 2.26$

**Step 1:** Compute $b/a = 4$:

$$
\ln(4) = 1.3863
$$

**Step 2:** Compute capacitance:

$$
C = \frac{2\pi \times 2.26 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{1.3863}
$$

$$
C = \frac{1.257 \times 10^{-10}}{1.3863} = 9.069 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 90.69 \text{ pF/m}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute inductance (high-frequency approximation, neglecting internal term):

$$
L \approx \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{2\pi}(1.3863)
$$

$$
L = (2 \times 10^{-7})(1.3863) = 2.773 \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 277.3 \text{ nH/m}}
$$

**Step 4:** Characteristic impedance:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.773 \times 10^{-7}}{9.069 \times 10^{-11}}} = \sqrt{3058.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_0 \approx 55.30 \, \Omega}
$$

**Step 5:** Velocity of propagation:

$$
v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}
$$

$$
v_p = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.26}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5033}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_p \approx 1.996 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the internal inductance term:** At low frequencies, the term $\mu/(4\pi)$ must be included. At high frequencies (skin effect), this becomes negligible. The KTU may specifically ask for "low-frequency inductance" — read carefully.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Always convert mm to m before computation. A common error: using $D/a = 10$ instead of $D/a = 10$ (dimensionless) is fine, but mixing radii in different units gives wrong answers.
> 3. **Using $c = 3 \times 10^8$ incorrectly:** For a medium with $\varepsilon_r \neq 1$, use $v_p = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$, **NOT** $c$.
> 4. **Wrong sign in $V_1 - V_2$:** The potential difference must be magnitude (positive). Many students lose 1 mark on sign handling.
> 5. **Forgetting the assumption $D \gg a$:** The simpler formula $\ln(D/a)$ is only valid for $D/a \geq 10$. For small separations, use the exact $\cosh^{-1}$ form.
> 6. **Neglecting to state boundary conditions in derivation:** Examiners award marks for clearly stating "uniform charge distribution, perfect conductors, etc."

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RLGC Model:** The fundamental distributed parameter representation of any transmission line consists of R (Ω/m), L (H/m), G (S/m), and C (F/m).
- **Two-Wire Line:**
  - $C = \dfrac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}$ F/m
  - $L = \dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln(D/a) + \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ H/m
- **Coaxial Line:**
  - $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$ F/m
  - $L = \dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a) + \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ H/m
- **Universal Identity:** $LC = \mu\varepsilon$ (in homogeneous medium).
- **Characteristic Impedance (lossless):** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$.
- **Phase Velocity:** $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC} = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$.
- **Internal Inductance Term $\mu/(4\pi)$:** Comes from flux inside the conductors; becomes negligible at high frequencies due to skin effect.
- **External Inductance:** Dominant term that depends only on conductor geometry (separation and radius).
- **Assumption:** $D \gg a$ (or $b > 3a$ for coaxial) is required for the simplified logarithmic forms.
- **Energy Storage:** L stores energy in the magnetic field $\left(\frac{1}{2}LI^2\right)$; C stores energy in the electric field $\left(\frac{1}{2}CV^2\right)$.
- **Causality:** For lossless line, $v_p$ is independent of frequency (no dispersion).
- **KTU 2024 Module 4 Coverage:** Derivation of L and C from physical geometry (this topic), followed by Telegrapher's equations and wave solutions in subsequent topics.
- **Practical Design:** Coaxial cable ratio $b/a \approx 3.6$ gives $Z_0 = 75$ Ω (video); $b/a \approx 1.65$ gives $Z_0 = 30$ Ω; $b/a \approx 2.3$ gives $Z_0 = 50$ Ω (RF standard).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Line Equations and Characteristic Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmission Line Equations and Characteristic Impedance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Transmission Line** is a pair of conductors designed to transport electromagnetic energy (or electrical signals) from one point to another. Unlike a simple wire at low frequencies, a transmission line is treated as a *distributed parameter network* because its physical length is comparable to the wavelength of the signal propagating through it. The line is characterized by four distributed parameters (per unit length):

- **R** : Series resistance per unit length (Ω/m) — accounts for finite conductor conductivity.
- **L** : Series inductance per unit length (H/m) — due to the magnetic field around the conductors.
- **G** : Shunt conductance per unit length (S/m) — accounts for dielectric loss in the insulator.
- **C** : Shunt capacitance per unit length (F/m) — due to the electric field between the conductors.

The **Transmission Line Equations** are a coupled pair of first-order partial differential equations (Telegrapher's Equations) that govern the voltage $V(x,t)$ and current $I(x,t)$ variations along the line. The **Characteristic Impedance** $Z_0$ is the intrinsic impedance presented by an infinitely long, perfectly matched line, defined as the ratio of the forward-traveling voltage to the forward-traveling current at any point on the line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The student must be able to derive the transmission line equations, identify the propagation constant $\gamma$, the characteristic impedance $Z_0$, the phase velocity $v_p$, and the wavelength $\lambda$ for a general (lossy) and lossless line. Questions in the KTU 2024 scheme frequently ask for the *derivation* of these equations from the equivalent circuit model.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long, thin rubber hose (a transmission line) carrying water (the electrical signal). The water in the hose has two interesting properties:

1. **Inertia (Inductance, L):** The water has mass. If you push water in at one end, it takes time for the pressure to translate into motion at the far end because the water's mass resists sudden changes in flow. This is exactly analogous to **inductance** resisting changes in current.

2. **Elasticity (Capacitance, C):** The rubber walls of the hose stretch and store water (energy) under pressure, just as the electric field between the two conductors of a transmission line stores charge. This is **capacitance**.

3. **Friction (Resistance, R):** The inner surface of the hose offers friction to water flow, dissipating energy as heat. This is **conductor loss (R)**.

4. **Leakage (Conductance, G):** Small holes in the hose allow water to leak out, dissipating energy. This is **dielectric loss (G)**.

When the hose is infinitely long, the wave you send in never "sees" the end. The ratio of pressure (voltage) to flow rate (current) it sees is the **characteristic impedance $Z_0$** — an intrinsic property of the *hose geometry and fluid*, not of the source or the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Reminder:** The speed of light in free space is $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. The phase velocity in a lossless line is $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, which equals $c$ for an air-filled line (and $c/n$ for a dielectric-filled line, where $n$ is the refractive index).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Distributed Parameter Model

At low frequencies, we can treat a cable as a lumped element (a single R, L, or C). However, when the **physical length of the line is on the order of one-tenth of the wavelength ($\ell \geq 0.1 \lambda$)**, the time it takes for the signal to traverse the line becomes significant. In this regime, the voltage and current are functions of **both position $x$ and time $t$**.

We model the line as a cascade of infinitesimal sections of length $\Delta x$, each containing series impedance $Z \Delta x$ and shunt admittance $Y \Delta x$:

$$Z = R + j\omega L \quad [\Omega/\text{m}]$$

$$Y = G + j\omega C \quad [\text{S/m}]$$

## 2.2 Operating Logic — From Circuit to Wave

The Telegrapher's Equations emerge from a simple application of **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** and **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** applied to the infinitesimal lumped section:

1. **KVL** around the loop: The voltage drop across the series impedance plus the change in voltage across the section equals the input voltage.
2. **KCL** at the top node: The current entering the section equals the current leaving plus the current shunted through the parallel admittance.
3. As $\Delta x \to 0$, the difference equations convert into **partial differential equations**.
4. Assuming a **time-harmonic phasor solution** of the form $e^{j\omega t}$, the time derivatives convert to $j\omega$, yielding a pair of coupled first-order ODEs in $x$.
5. Differentiating one and substituting into the other produces the **one-dimensional Helmholtz (wave) equation** for $V$ and $I$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | General (Lossy) Line | Lossless Line ($R=0, G=0$) | Units |
|---|----------|---------------------|----------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Series Impedance per unit length | $Z = R + j\omega L$ | $Z = j\omega L$ | $\Omega$/m |
| 2 | Shunt Admittance per unit length | $Y = G + j\omega C$ | $Y = j\omega C$ | S/m |
| 3 | Propagation Constant | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{ZY} = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$ | $\gamma = j\beta = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$ | 1/m |
| 4 | Attenuation Constant | $\alpha = \text{Re}\{\sqrt{ZY}\}$ | $\alpha = 0$ | Np/m |
| 5 | Phase Constant | $\beta = \text{Im}\{\sqrt{ZY}\}$ | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ | rad/m |
| 6 | Characteristic Impedance | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{Z}{Y}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{R+j\omega L}{G+j\omega C}}$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Phase Velocity | $v_p = \dfrac{\omega}{\beta}$ | $v_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | m/s |
| 8 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi}{\beta}$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_p}{f}$ | m |
| 9 | Input Impedance | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma \ell)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma \ell)}$ | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta \ell)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta \ell)}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Table Use:** Notice that the propagation constant $\gamma$ is a **complex number**. Its real part $\alpha$ causes exponential amplitude decay, while its imaginary part $\beta$ causes sinusoidal phase rotation. For the lossless case, $\gamma$ is **purely imaginary**, meaning *no attenuation occurs*.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transmission line theory is the cornerstone of:

- **Telecommunications:** Coaxial cables feeding antennas, twisted-pair Ethernet (Cat-5/6), and telephone lines.
- **RF/Microwave Engineering:** Microstrip lines and striplines on PCBs operating at GHz frequencies where $\lambda$ is in centimeters.
- **Power Systems:** High-voltage transmission lines (typically treated as low-frequency/lossless at 50/60 Hz since $\ell \ll \lambda$).
- **Radar & Satellite:** Waveguide systems where signal integrity depends critically on matched impedances to prevent reflections that would corrupt the received echo.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Telegrapher's Equations (Transmission Line Equations)

**Step 1 — Set up the equivalent circuit.**
Consider a small section of length $\Delta x$ at position $x$ from the sending end. The series impedance of this section is $Z \Delta x = (R + j\omega L)\Delta x$. The shunt admittance is $Y \Delta x = (G + j\omega C)\Delta x$. The voltage across the line at position $x$ is $V(x)$, and at $x + \Delta x$ it is $V(x) + \Delta V$. Similarly, the current is $I(x)$ entering the section and $I(x) + \Delta I$ leaving it.

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL).**
Going around the series loop, the input voltage equals the voltage drop across the series impedance plus the voltage at the output:

$$V(x) = (R+j\omega L)\Delta x \cdot I(x) + V(x) + \Delta V$$

Rearranging:

$$\Delta V = -(R + j\omega L)\Delta x \cdot I(x)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL).**
At the top node of the section, the current entering equals the current leaving plus the current lost to the shunt admittance:

$$I(x) = (G + j\omega C)\Delta x \cdot V(x) + I(x) + \Delta I$$

Rearranging:

$$\Delta I = -(G + j\omega C)\Delta x \cdot V(x)$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $\Delta x \to 0$.**
Dividing both sides by $\Delta x$ and applying the limit:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -(R + j\omega L) I$$

$$\frac{dI}{dx} = -(G + j\omega C) V$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **General Transmission Line Equations** (also called the Telegrapher's Equations in phasor form). They are valid for any two-conductor TEM transmission line.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The One-Dimensional Wave Equation

**Step 1 — Differentiate the first equation with respect to $x$.**

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -(R + j\omega L) \frac{dI}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the second equation into the first.**

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -(R + j\omega L)\bigl[-(G + j\omega C) V\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify to obtain the wave equation for voltage.**

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C) V$$

**Step 4 — Define the propagation constant $\gamma$.**

Let us define $\gamma^2 = (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)$. Then:

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = \gamma^2 V$$

**Step 5 — Write the analogous wave equation for current.**

A parallel procedure yields:

$$\frac{d^2 I}{dx^2} = \gamma^2 I$$

> [!NOTE]
> The differential operator $\frac{d^2}{dx^2} = \gamma^2$ is the same one-dimensional Helmholtz operator found in the wave equation $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu\varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$ for uniform plane waves in free space — confirming that the line supports propagating waves.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Solution of the Wave Equation — Forward and Backward Waves

**Step 1 — Solve the second-order ODE.**
The general solution of $\dfrac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = \gamma^2 V$ is:

$$V(x) = V^+ e^{-\gamma x} + V^- e^{+\gamma x}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the physical meaning of each term.**
- $V^+ e^{-\gamma x}$ : the **forward (incident) wave**, traveling in the $+x$ direction (toward the load). Its amplitude decays as $e^{-\alpha x}$ and oscillates with phase $-\beta x$.
- $V^- e^{+\gamma x}$ : the **backward (reflected) wave**, traveling in the $-x$ direction (back toward the source). Its amplitude decays as $e^{-\alpha x}$ (after reflecting at the load) and oscillates with phase $+\beta x$.

**Step 3 — Find the corresponding current.**
Using the transmission line equation $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -(R+j\omega L) I$, differentiate the voltage solution:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -\gamma V^+ e^{-\gamma x} + \gamma V^- e^{+\gamma x}$$

Equating to $-(R+j\omega L) I$:

$$I(x) = \frac{\gamma}{R+j\omega L}\left(V^+ e^{-\gamma x} - V^- e^{+\gamma x}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Define the Characteristic Impedance.**

$$Z_0 \equiv \frac{R+j\omega L}{\gamma} = \frac{\gamma}{G+j\omega C} = \sqrt{\frac{R+j\omega L}{G+j\omega C}}$$

So the current becomes:

$$I(x) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma x} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The minus sign on the reflected current is *physically necessary*: the reflected wave carries current in the **opposite direction** to the reflected voltage, just as a person walking backward on a moving walkway has a velocity vector pointing opposite to their displacement.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Characteristic Impedance for Lossless Line

For a lossless line, $R = 0$ and $G = 0$. Substituting into the general expression:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega L}{j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

This is a **real, positive, frequency-independent** quantity. For example, for a standard RG-58/U coaxial cable, $L \approx 250$ nH/m and $C \approx 100$ pF/m, giving:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{250 \times 10^{-9}}{100 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\ \Omega$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Phase Velocity and Wavelength (Lossless Case)

From the wave solution $V(x,t) = V^+ e^{-\alpha x}\cos(\omega t - \beta x)$ for a lossless line ($\alpha = 0$):

**Step 1 — Find the phase velocity.**
The phase front satisfies $\omega t - \beta x = \text{const}$. Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\omega - \beta \frac{dx}{dt} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_p = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{\omega}{\beta}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$.**

$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\omega\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 3 — Find the wavelength.**
By definition, the wave advances by one wavelength $\lambda$ in the time of one period $T$:

$$\lambda = v_p T = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{2\pi}{\beta}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lumped-Element Equivalent Circuit of an Infinitesimal Line Section

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the per-unit-length model of a transmission line, where the series impedance $Z \Delta x$ and shunt admittance $Y \Delta x$ are applied across an elemental segment of length $\Delta x$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IN: V at x, I at x] --> B[Series Resistor R dx]
    B --> C[Series Inductor L dx]
    C --> D[Shunt Capacitor C dx to ground]
    C --> E[Shunt Conductor G dx to ground]
    D --> F[OUT: V at x plus dx, I at x plus dx]
    E --> F
```

**Functional interpretation of the diagram:**

- The series branch ($R\,\Delta x$ in series with $L\,\Delta x$) models the conductor losses and magnetic energy storage.
- The shunt branch ($G\,\Delta x$ in parallel with $C\,\Delta x$) models the dielectric losses and electric energy storage.
- The input and output voltages differ by the drop across the series elements, and the input and output currents differ by the current bled through the shunt elements.

## 4.2 Forward and Reflected Wave Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source VS, ZS] --> tl[Transmission Line Z0, length L]
    tl --> load[Load ZL]
    tl -- Forward Wave V plus e to the power minus gamma x --> load
    load -- Reflected Wave V minus e to the power plus gamma x --> tl
    load -- Reflection Coefficient Gamma = ZL minus Z0 over ZL plus Z0 --> load
```

**Functional interpretation:**

- The forward wave $V^+ e^{-\gamma x}$ travels from source to load.
- Upon hitting the load, a fraction given by the reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ is sent back as the reflected wave $V^- e^{+\gamma x}$.
- The ratio of the two is fixed entirely by the mismatch between $Z_L$ and $Z_0$.

## 4.3 Signal Flow Architecture for Transmission Line Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[KVL and KCL on dx] --> B[Telegrapher Equations]
    B --> C[Second order Wave Equation]
    C --> D[General Solution]
    D --> E[Forward wave V plus]
    D --> F[Reflected wave V minus]
    E --> G[Propagation Constant gamma]
    F --> G
    E --> H[Characteristic Impedance Z0]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Phase velocity vp]
    G --> J[Wavelength lambda]
    H --> K[Matching for zero reflection]
```

**Functional interpretation:**

- The top-level process starts with KVL/KCL on the infinitesimal section.
- This produces the Telegrapher's equations, which reduce to a second-order wave equation.
- The general solution has two parts: a forward wave and a reflected wave.
- Both wave components share the same propagation constant $\gamma$ and characteristic impedance $Z_0$.
- These two quantities lead to the design-level quantities ($v_p$, $\lambda$, and the matching condition).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define characteristic impedance of a transmission line. Write its expression for a lossless line and explain why it is independent of frequency.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of a transmission line is the ratio of the voltage to the current of a single traveling wave on the line, defined mathematically as:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R+j\omega L}{G+j\omega C}}$$

For a lossless line ($R=0, G=0$):

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \quad [\Omega]$$
