j1.0)} \\ &= \frac{0.5(2.5) + 0.5(-j1.0) + j1.0(2.5) + j1.0(-j1.0)}{2.5^2 + 1.0^2} \\ &= \frac{1.25 - j0.5 + j2.5 + 1.0}{6.25 + 1.0} \\ &= \frac{2.25 + j2.0}{7.25} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Separate real and imaginary parts.** $$\Gamma_L = 0.3103 + j0.2759$$ **Step 5 — Convert to polar form.** $$|\Gamma_L| = \sqrt{0.3103^2 + 0.2759^2} = \sqrt{0.0963 + 0.0761} = \sqrt{0.1724}$$ $$|\Gamma_L| = 0.4152$$ $$\theta_L = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.2759}{0.3103}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.8892) = 41.63^\circ$$ **Final answer (a):** $$\boxed{\Gamma_L = 0.4152 \,\angle\, 41.63^\circ}$$ ### (b) VSWR **Step 6 — Apply the VSWR formula.** $$VSWR = \frac{1 + |\Gamma_L|}{1 - |\Gamma_L|} = \frac{1 + 0.4152}{1 - 0.4152} = \frac{1.4152}{0.5848}$$ $$\boxed{VSWR = 2.420}$$ ### (c) Return Loss **Step 7 — Apply the return-loss formula.** $$RL = -20 \log_{10} |\Gamma_L| = -20 \log_{10}(0.4152)$$ $$RL = -20 \times (-0.3819) = 7.638 \text{ dB}$$ $$\boxed{RL \approx 7.64 \text{ dB}}$$ ### (d) Graphical Verification (Smith Chart Method) | Step | Action | Result | |---|---|---| | 1 | Locate $z_L = 1.5 + j1.0$ | Intersection of $r = 1.5$ circle and $x = +1.0$ arc | | 2 | Draw $|\Gamma|$ circle through it | VSWR circle | | 3 | Read right-axis intersection | $VSWR = 2.42$ ✓ | | 4 | Read $|\Gamma|$ from radial scale | $|\Gamma| = 0.415$ ✓ | | 5 | Read "Angle of Reflection Coefficient" | $\theta_L = 41.6^\circ$ ✓ | ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Finding Line Impedance at a Distance **Problem:** For the same line ($Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$, $Z_L = 75 + j50\ \Omega$, $\lambda = 2\text{ m}$), find the input impedance at a distance $d = 0.4\lambda$ from the load. ### Step 1 — Recall the input-impedance formula. $$Z_{in}(d) = Z_0 \frac{1 + \Gamma(d)}{1 - \Gamma(d)}, \quad \Gamma(d) = \Gamma_L e^{-j 2\beta d}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the phase rotation. $$\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}, \quad 2\beta d = \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} = 4\pi (0.4) = 1.6\pi \text{ rad} = 288^\circ$$ ### Step 3 — Rotate $\Gamma_L$ clockwise by $288^\circ$. $$\Gamma(d) = 0.4152 \,\angle\, (41.63^\circ - 288^\circ) = 0.4152 \,\angle\, (-246.37^\circ)$$ Normalize to $[-180^\circ, +180^\circ]$: $$\Gamma(d) = 0.4152 \,\angle\, (113.63^\circ)$$ ### Step 4 — Convert back to rectangular form. $$\Gamma_r = 0.4152 \cos(113.63^\circ) = 0.4152 \times (-0.4024) = -0.1671$$ $$\Gamma_i = 0.4152 \sin(113.63^\circ) = 0.4152 \times 0.9154 = +0.3801$$ ### Step 5 — Compute $Z_{in}$. $$Z_{in} = 50 \times \frac{1 + (-0.1671 + j0.3801)}{1 - (-0.1671 + j0.3801)} = 50 \times \frac{0.8329 + j0.3801}{1.1671 - j0.3801}$$ ### Step 6 — Rationalize the denominator. $$|1.1671 - j0.3801|^2 = 1.1671^2 + 0.3801^2 = 1.3621 + 0.1445 = 1.5066$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= (0.8329 + j0.3801)(1.1671 + j0.3801) \\ &= 0.8329 \times 1.1671 + j(0.8329 \times 0.3801 + 0.3801 \times 1.1671) + j^2 (0.3801)^2 \\ &= 0.9721 + j(0.3166 + 0.4436) - 0.1445 \\ &= (0.9721 - 0.1445) + j(0.7602) \\ &= 0.8276 + j0.7602 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 7 — Divide and scale. $$z_{in} = \frac{0.8276 + j0.7602}{1.5066} = 0.5493 + j0.5046$$ $$Z_{in} = z_{in} \times Z_0 = (0.5493 + j0.5046) \times 50$$ $$\boxed{Z_{in} = 27.47 + j25.23 \;\; \Omega}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Given $z_L$ | Computed $|\Gamma|$ | Computed VSWR | Computed $Z_{in}$ @ $0.4\lambda$ | |---|---|---|---| | $1.5 + j1.0$ | $0.415$ | $2.42$ | $27.47 + j25.23\ \Omega$ | > [!TIP] > **Graphical cross-check on the Smith Chart:** After plotting $z_L = 1.5 + j1.0$, move clockwise along the VSWR circle by $0.4$ WTG (wavelengths-toward-generator). The new point should yield $z_{in} \approx 0.55 + j0.50$, matching the analytical result. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Smith Chart Block Architecture (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: ZL and Z0] --> B[Normalize: zL = ZL / Z0] B --> C{Identify Type} C -->|Real RL| D[Plot on Real Axis] C -->|Complex ZL| E[Intersection of Constant-r Circle and Constant-x Arc] D --> F[Compute Reflection Coefficient] E --> F F --> G["Γ = (zL - 1) / (zL + 1)"] G --> H[Draw VSWR Circle through zL] H --> I[Read VSWR on Right Real Axis] I --> J[Find Input Impedance at distance d] J --> K[Rotate Clockwise by d/λ WTG] K --> L[Read New z at New Point] L --> M[Denormalize: Z = z x Z0] M --> N[Output: Zin, VSWR, RL] ``` ## 4.2 Smith Chart Decision Tree — Pure Resistive vs. Complex Loads ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Load Impedance ZL] --> Q1{ZL has imaginary part?} Q1 -->|No, ZL is purely real| PureR[Pure Resistive Path] Q1 -->|Yes| Complex[Complex Impedance Path] PureR --> R1{ZL greater than Z0?} R1 -->|Yes| R2[Point lies on RIGHT of real axis
VSWR = ZL / Z0] R1 -->|No| R3[Point lies on LEFT of real axis
VSWR = Z0 / ZL] R2 --> R4[Γ is real and positive] R3 --> R5[Γ is real and negative] Complex --> C1[Locate intersection of r circle and x arc] C1 --> C2[Γ is complex with magnitude and angle] C2 --> C3[VSWR read on real axis to the right] C2 --> C4[Phase angle read on outer scale] R4 --> Output[Final Outputs] R5 --> Output C3 --> Output C4 --> Output Output[VSWR, |Γ|, θ, RL, Zin at distance d] ``` ## 4.3 Constant-Resistance and Constant-Reactance Curve Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SC[Smith Chart Layout] direction LR CX[Chart Center: z=1] --- R0[Outer Circle: r=0 Pure Reactance] CX --- RInf[Center Point: r equals infinity] R1[Constant r=0.2 Circle] --- CX R2[Constant r=1 Circle] --- CX R3[Constant r=2 Circle] --- CX R4[Constant r=5 Circle] --- CX X1[Constant x=+1 Arc Upper Half Inductive] --- CX X2[Constant x=-1 Arc Lower Half Capacitive] --- CX end ``` ## 4.4 Voltage Standing Wave Pattern on the Line ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Generator Side: z=1 Matched Source] --> B[Transmission Line: |V| varies sinusoidally] B --> C[Voltage Maximum at dmax] C --> D[Voltage Minimum at dmin equals dmax plus lambda over 4] D --> E[Load ZL Mismatch Point] E --> F[Reflection: Gamma L causes standing wave] F --> G[Standing Wave Ratio S equals Vmax over Vmin] ``` ## 4.5 Input Impedance Rotation — Movement on Smith Chart ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Initial Point: zL] --> M1[Move CLOCKWISE on VSWR circle] M1 --> P1[At distance 0.125 lambda: shifted 90 degrees] P1 --> P2[At distance 0.25 lambda: crossed real axis Vmax or Vmin] P2 --> P3[At distance 0.5 lambda: returned to original zL] P3 --> Note[Half rotation equals half wavelength] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Set B]** **Define the Smith Chart. What is the significance of the center of the Smith Chart?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (2 marks):** The Smith Chart is a polar plot of the complex reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ on a normalized impedance plane, used for solving transmission-line problems graphically. It maps the entire right-half of the complex impedance plane into a unit circle. > > **Significance of the center (1 mark):** The center of the Smith Chart corresponds to $\Gamma = 0$, which represents a **perfectly matched load** ($Z_L = Z_0$, $z_L = 1 + j0$). The radial distance from the center to any plotted point equals $|\Gamma|$, making the center the reference for **zero reflection**. --- ### Question A.2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **A 50 Ω line is terminated by $Z_L = 100\ \Omega$. Find the VSWR.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Step 1:** Normalize: $z_L = 100 / 50 = 2.0$ (purely real). > > **Step 2:** For a real load $z_L > 1$: > $$\Gamma_L = \frac{z_L - 1}{z_L + 1} = \frac{2.0 - 1}{2.0 + 1} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333$$ > > **Step 3:** Compute VSWR: > $$VSWR = \frac{1 + 0.3333}{1 - 0.3333} = \frac{1.3333}{0.6667} = \boxed{2.0}$$ > > (Equivalently, for a real load with $z_L > 1$, $VSWR = z_L = 2.0$ directly — a one-line shortcut.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4, Q7]** > A lossless transmission line with $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ and electrical length $0.6\lambda$ is terminated by a load $Z_L = 25 - j75\ \Omega$. > > **(a) (7 marks)** Plot the normalized load impedance on the Smith Chart and find: > (i) the reflection coefficient $\Gamma_L$ in polar form, > (ii) the VSWR, and > (iii) the return loss in dB. > > **(b) (7 marks)** Using the Smith Chart, determine: > (i) the input impedance $Z_{in}$ at the generator end, > (ii) the distance of the first voltage minimum from the load, and > (iii) the distance of the first voltage maximum from the load. #### Solution to Part (a) **Step 1 — Normalize the load impedance.** $$z_L = \frac{Z_L}{Z_0} = \frac{25 - j75}{50} = 0.5 - j1.5$$ **Step 2 — Compute the reflection coefficient.** $$\Gamma_L = \frac{z_L - 1}{z_L + 1} = \frac{(0.5 - j1.5) - 1}{(0.5 - j1.5) + 1} = \frac{-0.5 - j1.5}{1.5 - j1.5}$$ Rationalize: $$\begin{aligned} \Gamma_L &= \frac{(-0.5 - j1.5)(1.5 + j1.5)}{(1.5 - j1.5)(1.5 + j1.5)} \\ &= \frac{-0.75 - j0.75 - j2.25 - j^2 2.25}{1.5^2 + 1.5^2} \\ &= \frac{(-0.75 + 2.25) + j(-0.75 - 2.25)}{2.25 + 2.25} \\ &= \frac{1.5 - j3.0}{4.5} \\ &= 0.3333 - j0.6667 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to polar form.** $$|\Gamma_L| = \sqrt{0.3333^2 + 0.6667^2} = \sqrt{0.1111 + 0.4445} = \sqrt{0.5556} = 0.7454$$ $$\theta_L = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-0.6667}{0.3333}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-2.0) = -63.43^\circ$$ $$\boxed{\Gamma_L = 0.7454 \,\angle\, -63.43^\circ}$$ **Step 4 — Compute VSWR.** $$VSWR = \frac{1 + 0.7454}{1 - 0.7454} = \frac{1.7454}{0.2546} = \boxed{6.856}$$ **Step 5 — Compute return loss.** $$RL = -20 \log_{10}(0.7454) = -20 \times (-0.1276) = \boxed{2.55 \text{ dB}}$$ #### Solution to Part (b) **Step 1 — Rotate on the Smith Chart by $0.6\lambda$ toward the generator (clockwise).** Since one full revolution = $0.5\lambda$, a $0.6\lambda$ rotation equals $0.6\lambda - 0.5\lambda = 0.1\lambda$ extra. So we move clockwise by $0.6\lambda$ on the WTG scale. Phase change: $$\Delta \theta = -\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} = -4\pi(0.6) = -2.4\pi = -432^\circ$$ Equivalent to $-432^\circ + 360^\circ = -72^\circ$. So the new angle of the reflection coefficient is: $$\theta_{in} = \theta_L + \Delta \theta = -63.43^\circ - 432^\circ = -495.43^\circ$$ Subtract $360^\circ$: $$\theta_{in} = -135.43^\circ$$ **Step 2 — Compute the new $\Gamma_{in}$ in rectangular form.** $$\Gamma_{in} = 0.7454 \cos(-135.43^\circ) + j\,0.7454 \sin(-135.43^\circ)$$ $$\Gamma_{in} = 0.7454 \times (-0.7075) + j \cdot 0.7454 \times (-0.7067) = -0.5274 - j0.5268$$ **Step 3 — Compute $Z_{in}$.** $$Z_{in} = Z_0 \frac{1 + \Gamma_{in}}{1 - \Gamma_{in}} = 50 \cdot \frac{(1 - 0.5274) - j0.5268}{(1 + 0.5274) + j0.5268}$$ $$Z_{in} = 50 \cdot \frac{0.4726 - j0.5268}{1.5274 + j0.5268}$$ **Step 4 — Rationalize the denominator.** $$|1.5274 + j0.5268|^2 = 1.5274^2 + 0.5268^2 = 2.3329 + 0.2775 = 2.6104$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= (0.4726 - j0.5268)(1.5274 - j0.5268) \\ &= 0.4726 \times 1.5274 - j0.4726 \times 0.5268 - j0.5268 \times 1.5274 + j^2 (0.5268)^2 \\ &= 0.7219 - j0.2490 - j0.8046 - 0.2775 \\ &= (0.7219 - 0.2775) + j(-0.2490 - 0.8046) \\ &= 0.4444 - j1.0536 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Divide and scale.** $$z_{in} = \frac{0.4444 - j1.0536}{2.6104} = 0.1702 - j0.4036$$ $$Z_{in} = 50 \times (0.1702 - j0.4036) = \boxed{8.51 - j20.18 \;\; \Omega}$$ **Step 6 — Distance of first voltage minimum from the load.** The voltage minimum lies on the **right side** of the real axis (positive-real $\Gamma$) on the VSWR circle. The reflection coefficient phase is $\theta_L = -63.43^\circ$. The angle of the voltage minimum on the WTG scale is read where the VSWR circle meets the positive real axis (which is $\theta = 0^\circ$). For voltage minimum: $$d_{min} = \frac{\theta_L}{4\pi} \lambda \quad \text{(in same direction convention as WTG scale)}$$ Since $\theta_L$ is **negative**, we move **clockwise** (toward generator) by: $$d_{min} = \frac{63.43^\circ}{720^\circ} \lambda = 0.0881 \lambda$$ $$\boxed{d_{min} = 0.0881 \lambda}$$ **Step 7 — Distance of first voltage maximum from the load.** Voltage maximum is $\lambda/4$ farther from the load than voltage minimum: $$d_{max} = d_{min} + \frac{\lambda}{4} = 0.0881\lambda + 0.25\lambda = 0.3381\lambda$$ $$\boxed{d_{max} = 0.3381 \lambda}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to normalize** the load impedance by $Z_0$ (loses 1–2 marks). > 2. **Sign error on the imaginary part** of $\Gamma_L$ — recall that $z_L$ in the lower half-plane gives $\Gamma_i < 0$. > 3. **Direction confusion:** clockwise on the Smith Chart = toward generator = **WTG increasing** on the outer scale. > 4. **Mixing up $d_{max}$ and $d_{min}$:** voltage **minimum** lies at the **right** of the real axis (where the chart is "stretched out"), and voltage **maximum** lies at the **left** (where the chart is "compressed"). This is because $|\Gamma| + |\Gamma_{min}|$ is small at the minimum. > 5. **Not de-normalizing** $z_{in}$ back to $Z_{in} = z_{in} \cdot Z_0$ at the end. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4, Q7 Alt]** > For a transmission line with $Z_0 = 75\ \Omega$ and load impedance $Z_L = 30 + j60\ \Omega$: > > **(a) (7 marks)** Determine: > (i) the normalized load admittance $y_L$, and > (ii) the VSWR. > > **(b) (7 marks)** If the line has electrical length $0.3\lambda$, find the input impedance at the generator. Also, locate the position of the first voltage maximum from the load. #### Solution to Part (a) **Step 1 — Normalize the load impedance.** $$z_L = \frac{Z_L}{Z_0} = \frac{30 + j60}{75} = 0.4 + j0.8$$ **Step 2 — Convert to normalized admittance.** On the Smith Chart, $y_L$ is found by **rotating $z_L$ by 180°** (entering through the back of the chart), i.e., $y_L = 1/z_L$ analytically. $$y_L = \frac{1}{z_L} = \frac{1}{0.4 + j0.8}$$ Rationalize: $$y_L = \frac{0.4 - j0.8}{0.4^2 + 0.8^2} = \frac{0.4 - j0.8}{0.16 + 0.64} = \frac{0.4 - j0.8}{0.80}$$ $$y_L = 0.5 - j1.0$$ **Step 3 — Compute $|\Gamma_L|$.** $$|\Gamma_L| = \frac{|z_L - 1|}{|z_L + 1|} = \frac{|(0.4 - 1) + j0.8|}{|(0.4 + 1) + j0.8|} = \frac{|-0.6 + j0.8|}{|1.4 + j0.8|}$$ $$|\Gamma_L| = \frac{\sqrt{0.36 + 0.64}}{\sqrt{1.96 + 0.64}} = \frac{\sqrt{1.0}}{\sqrt{2.6}} = \frac{1.0}{1.6125} = 0.6202$$ **Step 4 — Compute VSWR.** $$VSWR = \frac{1 + 0.6202}{1 - 0.6202} = \frac{1.6202}{0.3798} = \boxed{4.266}$$ #### Solution to Part (b) **Step 1 — Compute phase of $\Gamma_L$.** $$\theta_L = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.8}{-0.6}\right) = 180^\circ - 53.13^\circ = 126.87^\circ$$ (Quadrant II — negative real, positive imaginary.) **Step 2 — Rotate by $0.3\lambda$ toward the generator.** $$\Delta \theta = -4\pi(0.3) = -1.2\pi = -216^\circ$$ $$\theta_{in} = 126.87^\circ - 216^\circ = -89.13^\circ$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\Gamma_{in}$ in rectangular form.** $$\Gamma_{in} = 0.6202 \cos(-89.13^\circ) + j\,0.6202 \sin(-89.13^\circ)$$ $$\Gamma_{in} = 0.6202 \times 0.0152 + j \cdot 0.6202 \times (-0.9999) = 0.00943 - j0.6201$$ **Step 4 — Compute $Z_{in}$.** $$Z_{in} = 75 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.00943 - j0.6201}{1 - 0.00943 + j0.6201} = 75 \cdot \frac{1.00943 - j0.6201}{0.99057 + j0.6201}$$ **Step 5 — Rationalize the denominator.** $$|0.99057 + j0.6201|^2 = 0.99057^2 + 0.6201^2 = 0.9812 + 0.3845 = 1.3657$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= (1.00943 - j0.6201)(0.99057 - j0.6201) \\ &= 1.00943 \times 0.99057 - j1.00943 \times 0.6201 - j0.6201 \times 0.99057 + j^2 (0.6201)^2 \\ &= 0.9999 - j0.6259 - j0.6142 - 0.3845 \\ &= (0.9999 - 0.3845) - j(0.6259 + 0.6142) \\ &= 0.6154 - j1.2401 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Divide and scale.** $$z_{in} = \frac{0.6154 - j1.2401}{1.3657} = 0.4507 - j0.9080$$ $$Z_{in} = 75 \times (0.4507 - j0.9080) = \boxed{33.80 - j68.10 \;\; \Omega}$$ **Step 7 — Locate first voltage maximum.** Voltage maximum is on the **negative real axis** of the Smith Chart (left side), which corresponds to $\theta = 180^\circ$ in reflection coefficient angle. We need to find the distance from the load to where $\theta_{reflection} = 180^\circ$. Current $\theta_L = 126.87^\circ$. Need to reach $180^\circ$: $$\Delta\theta = 180^\circ - 126.87^\circ = 53.13^\circ \text{ counter-clockwise}$$ But moving toward generator is **clockwise**, so we need to reach $180^\circ$ from below: $$180^\circ + 360^\circ - 126.87^\circ = 413.13^\circ \text{ clockwise}$$ Convert to distance: $$d_{max} = \frac{413.13^\circ}{720^\circ}\lambda = 0.5738\lambda$$ Subtract full rotations ($n \cdot 0.5\lambda$): $$d_{max} = 0.5738\lambda - 0.5\lambda = 0.0738\lambda$$ $$\boxed{d_{max} = 0.0738 \lambda}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Alternative-question pitfall — Admittance trap:** Many students confuse normalized **impedance** $z_L$ with normalized **admittance** $y_L$. On the Smith Chart, $y_L$ is at the diametrically opposite point from $z_L$ (a $180°$ rotation). Using the wrong one yields input impedances that differ by a factor — losing 7 marks outright. **Rule of thumb:** if the problem mentions stub matching, Bode plots, or matching networks, you likely need **$y_L$**, not $z_L$. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Calculation of Line Impedance and VSWR Using Smith Chart** ### 🎯 The Five Must-Know Equations 1. **Normalization:** $z_L = Z_L / Z_0$ 2. **Reflection Coefficient:** $\Gamma = (z_L - 1)/(z_L + 1)$ 3. **VSWR:** $S = (1 + |\Gamma|)/(1 - |\Gamma|)$ 4. **Return Loss:** $RL = -20 \log_{10} |\Gamma|$ dB 5. **Rotation:** $\Gamma(d) = \Gamma_L e^{-j 4\pi d / \lambda}$ (clockwise = toward generator) ### 🎯 Top 10 KTU Board-Exam Triggers | # | Concept | What the Examiner Tests | |---|---|---| | 1 | Normalize $Z_L$ by $Z_0$ | First-step trap (lose 1 mark if forgotten) | | 2 | Identify upper/lower half | Inductive ($+jx$, top) vs. capacitive ($-jx$, bottom) | | 3 | Compute $\Gamma$ analytically | Both rectangular and polar forms expected | | 4 | Draw the VSWR circle | Centered at origin, passes through $z_L$ | | 5 | Read VSWR on right real axis | Numerical value of intersection | | 6 | Read $|\Gamma|$ on radial scale | Distance from chart center | | 7 | Read $\theta_\Gamma$ on outer scale | "Angle of Reflection Coefficient" | | 8 | Rotate clockwise for $z_{in}$ | Distance in WTG units | | 9 | De-normalize $z_{in}$ to $Z_{in}$ | Multiply by $Z_0$ | | 10 | Position of $V_{max}$ and $V_{min}$ | Real-axis crossings, $V_{min}$ on the right | ### 🎯 Memory Anchors (Mnemonics) - **"Up = In = Inductor"** → Upper half = $+jx$ = **inductive** region. - **"Clock to the Gen"** → Moving **clockwise** on Smith Chart = moving **toward the generator** (increasing WTG). - **"Half chart = Half wavelength"** → 360° rotation on Smith Chart = $\lambda/2$ physical distance. - **"Right axis = VSWR"** → The horizontal real axis to the **right of center** is the **VSWR scale**. - **"Min on the Right"** → $V_{min}$ is on the **right** real axis (closer to short-circuit region); $V_{max}$ is on the **left** (closer to open-circuit region). ### 🎯 Common Numerical Shortcuts (Save Time in Exam) - **Pure real load, $z_L > 1$:** $VSWR = z_L$, $\Gamma = (z_L - 1)/(z_L + 1)$ is positive real. - **Pure real load, $z_L < 1$:** $VSWR = 1/z_L$, $\Gamma$ is negative real. - **Pure imaginary load** ($r = 0$): $|\Gamma| = 1$, $VSWR = \infty$ (total reflection). - **Matched load** ($z_L = 1$): $|\Gamma| = 0$, $VSWR = 1$, $RL = \infty$ dB. - **Short circuit** ($z_L = 0$): $|\Gamma| = 1$, $VSWR = \infty$, $\Gamma = -1$. - **Open circuit** ($z_L = \infty$): $|\Gamma| = 1$, $VSWR = \infty$, $\Gamma = +1$. ### 🎯 Pitfalls — Where 80% of Marks Are Lost 1. **Forgetting normalization** — always the first step. 2. **Sign error in the imaginary part of $\Gamma$** — rationalize carefully. 3. **Confusing $d_{max}$ and $d_{min}$** — remember the right side is $V_{min}$. 4. **Wrong rotation direction** — clockwise is toward the generator. 5. **Not de-normalizing at the end** — examiners expect $Z_{in}$ in ohms, not $z_{in}$. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark problem, allocate ~2 marks for normalization, ~4 marks for $\Gamma$ computation, ~3 marks for VSWR/RL, and ~5 marks for $Z_{in}$ at the given distance. If the question has a sub-part on $V_{max}$/$V_{min}$ positions, dedicate the last 2 marks to the WTG-scale reading. **Show all your arithmetic** — KTU examiners award **step marks** liberally for clear working.

topics.title: The Hollow Rectangular Waveguide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The Hollow Rectangular Waveguide # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **Hollow Rectangular Waveguide** is a single-conductor, hollow metallic tube with a rectangular cross-section (dimensions **a × b**, where **a > b**) that guides electromagnetic energy from one point to another through the mechanism of total internal reflection of waves off its conducting walls. It supports the propagation of electromagnetic waves above a characteristic **cutoff frequency ($f_c$)** and is the standard transmission medium for microwave and millimetre-wave engineering. Unlike a transmission line, the rectangular waveguide: - Has **no inner conductor**. - Does **not support TEM (Transverse Electro-Magnetic) modes** because it cannot sustain a static electric field between conductors. - Supports only **TE (Transverse Electric)** or **TM (Transverse Magnetic)** modes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For PCECT501 (Module 4), students must master the derivation of TE/TM field components, the dominant mode TE₁₀, and the parameter trio — cutoff frequency, phase velocity, and group velocity. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a wide, rectangular hall with perfectly reflecting walls. If you stand at one end and shout, the sound waves reflect off the side walls and travel down the hall in **zig-zag paths** (just like light in an optical fibre). The waves that travel at the correct angle reinforce each other and form a stable pattern — this is analogous to a waveguide **mode**. If you try to shout at too low a pitch (long wavelength), the waves simply spread out and get absorbed — they do not "fit" into the hall. This is the concept of **cutoff**: only waves with frequency higher than $f_c$ can propagate. In a rectangular waveguide: - The **broad wall dimension `a`** sets the dominant cutoff. - The **narrow wall dimension `b`** controls higher-order modes. - The wave bounces between the conducting walls in a zig-zag pattern while travelling along the z-axis. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** A microwave oven uses a rectangular waveguide to channel **2.45 GHz** microwaves from the magnetron to the cooking cavity. The waveguide walls are made of polished metal for minimum loss, exactly as in communication systems. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants | Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $c$ | Speed of light in free space | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$ | | $\eta$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\mathbf{120\pi \approx 377 \ \Omega}$ | | $\varepsilon_0$ | Free-space permittivity | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}}$ | | $\mu_0$ | Free-space permeability | $\mathbf{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}}$ | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why a Hollow Pipe Guides Waves A two-wire transmission line fails at microwave frequencies because: 1. **Skin effect** makes conductors very lossy. 2. **Radiation losses** become severe as conductors approach antenna dimensions. 3. **Dimensional tolerances** become impossibly small. A **hollow closed metallic pipe** confines the field entirely inside, eliminating radiation, and the large surface area reduces current density, minimising resistive loss. --- ## 2.2 The Mode Concept (TE and TM) Because the waveguide has only **one conductor**, the static Laplace equation cannot yield a 2-D potential field with $\mathbf{E} \perp \mathbf{H}$. Hence **TEM modes are impossible**. The propagating modes are: ### TE Modes (Transverse Electric) - $E_z = 0$, $H_z \neq 0$ - Electric field lies entirely in the transverse (x-y) plane. - Denoted as **TE$_{mn}$**, where $m$ = number of half-wave variations along `a`, $n$ = number of half-wave variations along `b`. - **Allowed:** $m \geq 1$, $n \geq 0$. ### TM Modes (Transverse Magnetic) - $H_z = 0$, $E_z \neq 0$ - Magnetic field lies entirely in the transverse plane. - Denoted as **TM$_{mn}$**. - **Allowed:** $m \geq 1$, $n \geq 1$ (cannot have $n=0$ due to boundary conditions). --- ## 2.3 The Dominant Mode: TE₁₀ The mode with the **lowest cutoff frequency** is the **TE₁₀** mode. Its cutoff frequency is: $$f_c = \frac{c}{2a}$$ Because it offers the largest usable bandwidth before the next mode (TE₂₀) appears, it is the **workhorse mode** of all rectangular waveguide systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > The dominant mode is **TE₁₀**, not TE₁₁ or TE₂₀, because it gives the largest single-mode operating bandwidth. Always remember: in a rectangular waveguide, $\mathbf{a > b}$ ensures TE₁₀ is dominant. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Cutoff wavenumber | $k_c = \sqrt{(m\pi/a)^2 + (n\pi/b)^2}$ | rad/m | | Cutoff frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}$ | Hz | | Cutoff wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}}$ | m | | Phase constant | $\beta = k\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ | rad/m | | Phase velocity | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$ | m/s | | Group velocity | $v_g = c\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ | m/s | | Guide wavelength | $\lambda_g = \dfrac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$ | m | | Wave impedance (TE) | $Z_{TE} = \dfrac{\eta}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$ | $\Omega$ | | Wave impedance (TM) | $Z_{TM} = \eta\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ | $\Omega$ | | Power (TE₁₀) | $P = \dfrac{ab}{4}\vert E_0\vert^2 \dfrac{\beta}{\omega\mu}$ | W | | Product $v_p v_g$ | $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ | — | > [!NOTE] > **LaTeX Tip for Tables:** The absolute value $\vert E_0 \vert^2$ is rendered using `\vert` so as not to break the markdown pipe parser. KTU paper-setters love giving numerical problems on this exact triplet — $(f_c, v_p, v_g)$. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Rectangular waveguides are indispensable in: - **Radar systems** (X-band: 8.2–12.4 GHz; Ku-band: 12.4–18 GHz). - **Satellite communication feeds** and horn antennas. - **Particle accelerators** (SLAC, CERN) where extremely high-power microwaves are transmitted with minimal loss. - **Microwave ovens** (2.45 GHz, dominant TE₁₀ mode). - **Plasma heating** in fusion reactors (Electron Cyclotron Resonance Heating). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of TE$_{mn}$ Mode Fields in a Hollow Rectangular Waveguide ### Step 1 — Coordinate Setup and Maxwell's Curl Equations Consider a hollow rectangular waveguide with **Perfect Electric Conductor (PEC)** walls, broad dimension **a** along the x-axis, narrow dimension **b** along the y-axis, and propagation along the **z-axis**. Assuming time-harmonic fields $e^{j\omega t}$ and z-propagation $e^{-j\beta z}$, Maxwell's equations yield: $$\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -j\omega\mu\mathbf{H} \quad ; \quad \nabla \times \mathbf{H} = j\omega\varepsilon\mathbf{E}$$ ### Step 2 — Wave Equation for Longitudinal Field $H_z$ For TE modes, $E_z = 0$ and $H_z$ satisfies the **Helmholtz wave equation**: $$\nabla^2 H_z + k^2 H_z = 0$$ Expanding $\nabla^2 = \partial^2/\partial x^2 + \partial^2/\partial y^2 + \partial^2/\partial z^2$ and substituting $H_z(x,y,z) = H_0(x,y)e^{-j\beta z}$: $$\frac{\partial^2 H_0}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 H_0}{\partial y^2} + (k^2 - \beta^2)H_0 = 0$$ Let $k_c^2 = k^2 - \beta^2$ (the cutoff wavenumber squared): $$\frac{\partial^2 H_0}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 H_0}{\partial y^2} + k_c^2 H_0 = 0$$ ### Step 3 — Separation of Variables Assume $H_0(x,y) = X(x)Y(y)$. Substituting and dividing by $XY$: $$\frac{1}{X}\frac{d^2X}{dx^2} + \frac{1}{Y}\frac{d^2Y}{dy^2} + k_c^2 = 0$$ This separates into two ODEs with constants $k_x^2$ and $k_y^2$ such that $k_x^2 + k_y^2 = k_c^2$: $$\frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} + k_x^2 X = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{d^2 Y}{dy^2} + k_y^2 Y = 0$$ The general solution is: $$X(x) = A\cos(k_x x) + B\sin(k_x x)$$ $$Y(y) = C\cos(k_y y) + D\sin(k_y y)$$ ### Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions on PEC Walls The tangential electric field must vanish on all four walls: 1. At $x=0$ and $x=a$: $E_y = 0$ implies $\partial H_z/\partial x = 0$ 2. At $y=0$ and $y=b$: $E_x = 0$ implies $\partial H_z/\partial y = 0$ The condition $\partial H_z/\partial x = 0$ requires $B = 0$ in $X(x)$. The condition $\partial H_z/\partial y = 0$ requires $D = 0$ in $Y(y)$. So: $$X(x) = A\cos(k_x x) \quad ; \quad Y(y) = C\cos(k_y y)$$ Applying periodicity at $x=a$: $k_x a = m\pi \Rightarrow k_x = m\pi/a$ Applying periodicity at $y=b$: $k_y b = n\pi \Rightarrow k_y = n\pi/b$ Therefore: $$k_c^2 = \left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2$$ The longitudinal magnetic field becomes: $$H_z(x,y,z) = H_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta z}$$ ### Step 5 — Derive Transverse Components From the curl equation $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -j\omega\mu\mathbf{H}$, in component form: $$E_x = -\frac{j\omega\mu}{k_c^2}\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial y} \quad ; \quad E_y = \frac{j\omega\mu}{k_c^2}\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial x}$$ $$H_x = -\frac{j\beta}{k_c^2}\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial x} \quad ; \quad H_y = -\frac{j\beta}{k_c^2}\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial y}$$ Substituting $H_z$: $$\boxed{\; \begin{aligned} H_z &= H_0 \cos\!\left(\tfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta z} \\[4pt] H_x &= \frac{j\beta}{k_c^2}\!\left(\tfrac{m\pi}{a}\right) H_0 \sin\!\left(\tfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta z} \\[4pt] H_y &= \frac{j\beta}{k_c^2}\!\left(\tfrac{n\pi}{b}\right) H_0 \cos\!\left(\tfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\tfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta z} \\[4pt] E_x &= \frac{j\omega\mu}{k_c^2}\!\left(\tfrac{n\pi}{b}\right) H_0 \cos\!\left(\tfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\tfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta z} \\[4pt] E_y &= -\frac{j\omega\mu}{k_c^2}\!\left(\tfrac{m\pi}{a}\right) H_0 \sin\!\left(\tfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta z} \end{aligned}\;}$$ with $E_z = 0$, where $m = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ and $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ --- ## 3.2 Specialisation to Dominant TE₁₀ Mode Setting $m=1$, $n=0$, and $k_c = \pi/a$: $$H_z = H_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)e^{-j\beta z}$$ $$H_x = \frac{j\beta a}{\pi}H_0 \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)e^{-j\beta z}$$ $$E_y = -\frac{j\omega\mu a}{\pi}H_0 \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)e^{-j\beta z}$$ All other components ($H_y, E_x, E_z$) are zero. > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that the field expressions have **no y-dependence** — the dominant TE₁₀ field is uniform along the narrow dimension `b`. This is a key reason it is easy to excite and analyse. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Cutoff Frequency The dispersion relation in the waveguide is: $$k^2 = k_c^2 + \beta^2$$ In free space, $k = \omega\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0} = \omega/c$. For real propagation ($\beta$ real), we need $\omega^2\mu_0\varepsilon_0 \geq k_c^2$. The cutoff angular frequency $\omega_c$ is defined by: $$\omega_c = \frac{k_c}{\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}} = c \cdot k_c$$ Substituting $k_c = \sqrt{(m\pi/a)^2 + (n\pi/b)^2}$: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}$$ In free space: $$\boxed{\; f_c = \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2} \;}$$ For TE₁₀, this reduces to $f_c = c/(2a)$. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Phase and Group Velocities From $k^2 = k_c^2 + \beta^2$, with $k = \omega/c$: $$\beta = \frac{\omega}{c}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\omega_c}{\omega}\right)^2} = \frac{\omega}{c}\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$$ **Phase velocity** is the velocity of constant-phase fronts: $$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$$ **Group velocity** is the velocity of energy/information: $$v_g = \frac{d\omega}{d\beta} = c\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$$ Note the elegant product: $$v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation of Power Transmitted in TE₁₀ Mode The time-average Poynting vector is $\mathbf{S}_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}(\mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{H}^*)$. For TE₁₀, only $E_y$, $H_x$, $H_z$ are non-zero: $$(\mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{H}^*)_z = E_y(-H_x^*) = \left(\frac{\omega\mu a H_0}{\pi}\sin\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\left(\frac{\beta a H_0^*}{\pi}\sin\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\frac{1}{2}\text{ factor applied}$$ $$S_z = \frac{\omega\mu\beta a^2 \vert H_0\vert^2}{2\pi^2}\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)$$ Integrating over the cross-section $0 \leq x \leq a$, $0 \leq y \leq b$: $$P = \int_0^b \int_0^a S_z \, dx \, dy = \frac{\omega\mu\beta a^2 \vert H_0\vert^2}{2\pi^2} \cdot b \cdot \frac{a}{2}$$ $$\boxed{\; P = \frac{\omega\mu\beta a^3 b \vert H_0\vert^2}{4\pi^2} = \frac{ab}{4}\vert E_0\vert^2 \frac{\beta}{\omega\mu} \;}$$ where $E_0 = \omega\mu a H_0 / \pi$ is the peak electric field. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Waveguide Geometry and Coordinate Convention The following diagram defines the rectangular waveguide geometry and shows how a TE₁₀ wave "bounces" down the guide in a zig-zag path between the broad walls. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Source
Magnetron / Generator"] --> B["Waveguide Section
a x b cross section
PEC walls"] B --> C["TE10 Dominant Mode
Bounces between
broad walls"] C --> D["Matched Load
Antenna / Detector"] subgraph BC ["Inside the Waveguide"] direction TB X["Top Wall y = b"] --- Y["Zig-zag wave path
angle theta to z-axis"] Y --- Z["Bottom Wall y = 0"] Y2["Field maxima at x = a/2"] Y --- Y2 end ``` ## 4.2 Mode Propagation and Cutoff Filter Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD M["Maxwell Curl Equations"] --> H["Helmholtz Wave Equation
del-square Hz + k-square Hz = 0"] H --> S["Separation of Variables"] S --> BC["Apply PEC Boundary Conditions
Tangential E = 0 on walls"] BC --> MODES["TE_mn and TM_mn Modes
with kc-square = mpi/a square + npi/b square"] MODES --> DOM["TE10 Dominant Mode
lowest cutoff fc = c over 2a"] MODES --> FC["Cutoff Frequency
fc = c/2 times sqrt m/a square + n/b square"] FC --> VELS["Phase Velocity vp greater than c
Group Velocity vg less than c
Product vp vg = c square"] DOM --> POWER["Power Transmission
P = ab/4 times E0 square times beta over omega mu"] DOM --> WALL["Wall Currents Js = n cross H
Longitudinal on broad walls
Transverse on narrow walls"] WALL --> SLOT["Slot Antenna Design
Slot parallel to Js = no radiation
Slot perpendicular to Js = radiates"] ``` ## 4.3 Mode Chart (Conceptual — Mode Cutoff Spectrum) The mode chart shows the cutoff frequencies of all modes. In the operating band between $f_{c,\text{TE}_{10}}$ and $f_{c,\text{TE}_{20}}$, **only TE₁₀ propagates**. ```mermaid graph LR F["Frequency Axis f in GHz"] --> F1["fc TE10 = c over 2a"] F1 --> REGION["Single-Mode Region
TE10 ONLY"] REGION --> F2["fc TE20 = c over a"] F2 --> MULTI["Multi-Mode Region
TE10 plus TE20 plus others"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Aid:** The dominant mode TE₁₀ has field variations **only along x** (one half-cycle across the broad wall `a`) and **constant along y**. Hence the subscript is "1, 0" and not the other way round. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define a waveguide. Why is a rectangular waveguide preferred over a coaxial transmission line at microwave frequencies?** **Model Answer:** A waveguide is a hollow metallic structure that guides electromagnetic waves by successive reflections from its conducting walls. A rectangular waveguide is preferred over a coaxial line at microwave frequencies because: 1. **Lower loss** — large surface area reduces resistive heating. 2. **Higher power handling** — no inner conductor to break down. 3. **No radiation loss** — completely closed structure. 4. **Mechanical simplicity** — easier fabrication at millimetric dimensions. 5. Coaxial lines suffer from severe **skin effect** and **mode conversion** above ~3 GHz. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **What is the dominant mode in a rectangular waveguide? Why is it called dominant?** **Model Answer:** The dominant mode is **TE₁₀** — the mode with the **lowest cutoff frequency**, given by $f_c = c/(2a)$. It is called "dominant" because: 1. It has the **lowest cutoff frequency** of all possible modes. 2. It allows the **widest single-mode operating bandwidth** before higher-order modes (TE₂₀, TE₀₁) appear. 3. Its field structure is **simple, with no y-variation**, making excitation and analysis straightforward. 4. It supports efficient power transmission with well-defined wall currents. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students lose 2–3 marks by: > 1. Forgetting to write $E_z = 0$ explicitly in TE mode derivations. > 2. Using the wrong intrinsic impedance ($\eta$ vs $\eta_0$). > 3. Mixing up the conditions $m \geq 1, n \geq 0$ (TE) and $m \geq 1, n \geq 1$ (TM). > 4. Skipping the boundary condition justification — KTU expects 2–3 lines on *why* $B = 0$ or $D = 0$ in separation of variables. --- ### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — 14 Marks **Derive the field expressions for TE₁₀ mode in a hollow rectangular waveguide. Hence obtain expressions for the cutoff frequency and guide wavelength.** #### Part (a) — Field Derivation [7 Marks] Starting from Maxwell's equations, for TE modes $E_z = 0$ and $H_z$ satisfies: $$\nabla^2 H_z + k^2 H_z = 0$$ With separation of variables $H_z = X(x)Y(y)e^{-j\beta z}$: $$\frac{1}{X}\frac{d^2X}{dx^2} + \frac{1}{Y}\frac{d^2Y}{dy^2} + (k^2 - \beta^2) = 0$$ Setting $k_c^2 = k^2 - \beta^2$ and applying **PEC boundary conditions** ($E_y = 0$ at $x=0, a$ requires $\partial H_z/\partial x = 0$; $E_x = 0$ at $y=0, b$ requires $\partial H_z/\partial y = 0$): $$H_z = H_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)e^{-j\beta z} \quad \text{for TE}_{10}$$ Using the transverse field relations (from $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -j\omega\mu\mathbf{H}$): $$E_y = \frac{j\omega\mu}{k_c^2}\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial x}\bigg|_{m=1,n=0}$$ $$E_y = -\frac{j\omega\mu a}{\pi}H_0 \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)e^{-j\beta z}$$ $$H_x = -\frac{j\beta}{k_c^2}\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial x} = \frac{j\beta a}{\pi}H_0 \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)e^{-j\beta z}$$ $$H_y = 0, \quad E_x = 0, \quad E_z = 0$$ #### Part (b) — Cutoff Frequency and Guide Wavelength [7 Marks] For TE₁₀, $k_c = \pi/a$, so: $$f_c = \frac{k_c}{2\pi\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}} = \frac{c}{2a}$$ The phase constant: $$\beta = \frac{\omega}{c}\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\sqrt{1 - (\lambda/\lambda_c)^2}$$ The guide wavelength (distance for $2\pi$ phase change along z): $$\lambda_g = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam – June 2023] — 14 Marks **An air-filled rectangular waveguide with dimensions $a = 2.286$ cm and $b = 1.016$ cm operates at 10 GHz in the dominant TE₁₀ mode. Calculate:** **(a)** Cutoff frequency, guide wavelength, phase velocity, and group velocity. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Wave impedance and verify that $v_p v_g = c^2$. **[7 Marks]** #### Part (a) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks] Given: $a = 2.286 \times 10^{-2}$ m, $b = 1.016 \times 10^{-2}$ m, $f = 10 \times 10^9$ Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. **Step 1 — Cutoff frequency (TE₁₀):** $$f_c = \frac{c}{2a} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2.286 \times 10^{-2}} = 6.562 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz} \approx 6.56 \text{ GHz}$$ **Step 2 — Operating free-space wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10^{10}} = 0.03 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 3 — Ratio $f_c/f$:** $$\frac{f_c}{f} = \frac{6.562}{10} = 0.6562$$ $$\left(\frac{f_c}{f}\right)^2 = 0.4306$$ **Step 4 — Guide wavelength:** $$\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{0.03}{\sqrt{1 - 0.4306}} = \frac{0.03}{\sqrt{0.5694}} = \frac{0.03}{0.7546} = 0.03976 \text{ m} \approx 3.98 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 5 — Phase velocity:** $$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.7546} = 3.976 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 6 — Group velocity:** $$v_g = c\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2} = 3 \times 10^8 \times 0.7546 = 2.264 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$ #### Part (b) — Wave Impedance and Verification [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Wave impedance for TE mode:** $$Z_{TE} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{377}{0.7546} = 499.6 \ \Omega \approx 500 \ \Omega$$ **Step 2 — Verification of $v_p v_g = c^2$:** $$v_p \cdot v_g = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} \times c\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2} = c \cdot c = c^2$$ Numerically: $$v_p \cdot v_g = 3.976 \times 10^8 \times 2.264 \times 10^8 = 9.00 \times 10^{16} = (3 \times 10^8)^2 = c^2 \quad \checkmark$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** A hollow metallic tube guiding EM waves above cutoff — no inner conductor. - **No TEM mode:** Because there is only one conductor, $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = 0$ in 2-D cross-section has no solution. - **Mode notation:** TE$_{mn}$ or TM$_{mn}$ where $m, n$ are half-cycle variations along `a` and `b`. - **Dominant mode is TE₁₀** with $f_c = c/(2a)$ — largest single-mode bandwidth. - **Boundary conditions on PEC:** Tangential $\mathbf{E} = 0$ and normal $\mathbf{B} = 0$. - **Three velocity identities:** - $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ - $v_p \geq c \geq v_g$ (always) - $v_p$ exceeds $c$ but **carries no information** (constant-phase fronts, not energy). - **Wave impedance identities:** - $Z_{TE} \cdot Z_{TM} = \eta^2$ - $Z_{TE} > \eta$ and $Z_{TM} < \eta$ always. - **TE validity:** $m \geq 1, n \geq 0$ — note TE$_{m0}$ modes are valid but TM$_{m0}$ are not. - **Cutoff behaviour:** At $f = f_c$, $\beta = 0$ (no propagation); for $f < f_c$, $\beta$ becomes imaginary (evanescent). - **Wall currents ($J_s = \hat{n} \times \mathbf{H}$):** - Longitudinal ($J_z$) on the broad walls (top and bottom). - Transverse ($J_y$) on the narrow walls. - Slots cut **perpendicular** to $J_s$ radiate → forms slot antenna. - **Power flow:** Use $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\int (\mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{H}^*)\cdot d\mathbf{S}$. - **KTU standard dimensions (X-band):** $a = 2.286$ cm, $b = 1.016$ cm, $f_c = 6.56$ GHz. - **Memory trick:** *"One over a, zero over b"* → TE₁₀ = "1,0". The number that varies is the **first** index; the one that is constant is the **second**.

topics.title: TE and TM Wave-Dominant Mode, Group Velocity, and Phase Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT501 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** TE and TM Wave-Dominant Mode, Group Velocity, and Phase Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transverse Electric (TE) and Transverse Magnetic (TM) Waves

In a **hollow metallic waveguide** (rectangular or circular), electromagnetic waves cannot propagate in the pure TEM (Transverse ElectroMagnetic) mode because a single conductor cannot support TEM propagation. Instead, waves propagate as either:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TE Waves (Transverse Electric / H-Modes):** The electric field has **NO component along the direction of propagation** ($E_z = 0$), but the magnetic field **DOES** have a component along the direction of propagation ($H_z \neq 0$). Designated as $TE_{mn}$ or $H_{mn}$ modes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TM Waves (Transverse Magnetic / E-Modes):** The magnetic field has **NO component along the direction of propagation** ($H_z = 0$), but the electric field **DOES** have a component along the direction of propagation ($E_z \neq 0$). Designated as $TM_{mn}$ or $E_{mn}$ modes.

Here, the $z$-axis is the direction of wave propagation along the waveguide length.

## 1.2 The Dominant Mode

A waveguide can support an infinite number of TE and TM modes, each with a specific **cutoff frequency**. The mode that has the **lowest cutoff frequency** is called the **Dominant Mode**.

> [!NOTE]
> **For a rectangular waveguide of dimensions $a \times b$ (where $a > b$):** The **dominant mode is the $TE_{10}$ mode** (also called the $H_{10}$ mode). This is the most commonly used mode in practical microwave systems because it has the lowest attenuation and is easiest to excite.

The cutoff frequency for any $TE_{mn}$ or $TM_{mn}$ mode is given by:

$$f_{c,mn} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}} \sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}$$

For $TE_{10}$: $m=1, n=0$, so $f_{c,10} = \dfrac{1}{2a\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}} = \dfrac{c}{2a}$ (in free space).

## 1.3 Group Velocity and Phase Velocity — Intuitive Overview

**Conceptual Analogy — The Duck Analogy:**
Imagine a **train of ducks** swimming across a river, where the river current flows perpendicular to the line of ducks. Each duck represents a wave crest:
- The **speed of an individual duck** (relative to water) corresponds to the **phase velocity** — the rate at which a constant-phase point (a wave crest) moves.
- The **speed of the entire flock pattern** (the overall envelope of the group) corresponds to the **group velocity** — the rate at which the energy/information packet travels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase Velocity ($v_p$):** The velocity at which a point of constant phase (e.g., a wave crest) on a sinusoidal wave appears to move. In a waveguide, $v_p > c$ (faster than light!) — but **no energy or information travels at $v_p$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Group Velocity ($v_g$):** The velocity at which the **envelope** of a wave packet (or the energy/information) propagates. In a waveguide, $v_g < c$ — this is the **signal velocity**, consistent with Einstein's relativity.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| **$c$** | Speed of light in vacuum | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| **$\mu_0$** | Permeability of free space | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| **$\varepsilon_0$** | Permittivity of free space | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m |
| **$\eta_0$** | Intrinsic impedance of free space | $376.73 \approx 120\pi$ $\Omega$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Have Phase and Group Velocities in a Waveguide?

When an electromagnetic wave enters a waveguide, the **boundary conditions** at the conducting walls force the wave to bounce back and forth in a zig-zag pattern between the walls. This creates two simultaneous velocities:
- A velocity **along the axis** of the guide (the apparent phase velocity)
- A velocity **at the angle** of bouncing (the actual energy transport velocity, which equals the group velocity)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1: Solving the Wave Equation in a Rectangular Waveguide
The longitudinal field components ($E_z$ for TM, $H_z$ for TE) satisfy the **Helmholtz equation**:

$$\nabla^2 \psi + k_c^2 \psi = 0$$

where $k_c$ is the **cutoff wave number**.

### Step 2: Applying Boundary Conditions
For **TE modes** ($E_z = 0$), we solve for $H_z$ subject to $\partial H_z / \partial n = 0$ at the conducting walls. The general solution is:

$$H_z(x,y,z) = H_0 \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta_g z}$$

For **TM modes** ($H_z = 0$), we solve for $E_z$ subject to $E_z = 0$ at the conducting walls:

$$E_z(x,y,z) = E_0 \sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta_g z}$$

### Step 3: Computing the Propagation Constant
The propagation constant along the $z$-axis is:

$$\beta_g = \sqrt{k^2 - k_c^2} = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f_c}{f}\right)^2}$$

> **Why is this important?** When $f > f_c$, the wave propagates ($\beta_g$ is real). When $f < f_c$, the wave is evanescent ($\beta_g$ is imaginary, the wave decays exponentially).

### Step 4: Defining Phase Velocity
Phase velocity is the ratio of angular frequency to the phase constant:

$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta_g} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$$

Since the denominator is less than 1, **$v_p > c$** (superluminal phase velocity — does not violate relativity because no information travels at this speed).

### Step 5: Defining Group Velocity
Group velocity is the derivative of angular frequency with respect to the propagation constant:

$$v_g = \frac{d\omega}{d\beta_g} = c\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f_c}{f}\right)^2}$$

This is the velocity of energy transport and **$v_g < c$** (subluminal).

### Step 6: The Fundamental Relationship
Multiplying the two expressions:

$$v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$$

This is one of the most important relationships in waveguide theory and is a **favourite KTU question**.

### Step 7: Guide Wavelength
The wavelength as measured along the guide axis:

$$\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{v_p}{f}$$

> **Real-World Utility:** This relationship is critical in designing **radar systems, satellite communication, microwave ovens, and 5G base stations**. The dominant mode $TE_{10}$ is used in nearly all rectangular waveguide-based systems because of its predictable propagation characteristics.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---------|------------|------------------|
| Cutoff frequency | $f_{c,mn} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}$ | Lowest frequency a mode can propagate |
| Cutoff wave number | $k_{c,mn} = \sqrt{(m\pi/a)^2 + (n\pi/b)^2}$ | Spatial frequency of the standing wave pattern |
| Propagation constant | $\beta_g = \sqrt{k^2 - k_c^2}$ | Phase shift per unit length in guide |
| Phase velocity | $v_p = \omega / \beta_g = c / \sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}$ | Speed of constant-phase point |
| Group velocity | $v_g = d\omega/d\beta_g = c\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}$ | Speed of energy/information |
| Velocity relationship | $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ | Fundamental waveguide identity |
| Guide wavelength | $\lambda_g = \lambda / \sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}$ | Wavelength measured in guide |
| Dominant mode | $TE_{10}$ mode | Lowest cutoff, $f_c = c/(2a)$ |
| Phase constant (TE/TM) | $\beta_g = (2\pi/\lambda)\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}$ | Used for field calculations |
| Wave impedance (TE) | $Z_{TE} = \eta / \sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}$ | $> \eta$ (for TE modes) |
| Wave impedance (TM) | $Z_{TM} = \eta\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}$ | $< \eta$ (for TM modes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical exam tip:** In all these formulas, $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ is the speed of light in the dielectric **inside** the guide (usually air or vacuum), and $f$ is the **operating frequency** of the signal. Always verify that $f > f_c$ before applying the propagation formulas.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cutoff Frequency for $TE_{mn}$ and $TM_{mn}$ Modes

### Starting Point
For a rectangular waveguide with broad wall $a$ and narrow wall $b$ (with $a > b$), we solve the Helmholtz equation for the longitudinal field component. For **TE modes** ($E_z = 0$):

$$\frac{\partial^2 H_z}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 H_z}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 H_z}{\partial z^2} + k^2 H_z = 0$$

### Step 1: Assume a Separable Solution
We assume $H_z = X(x)Y(y)Z(z)$, which gives $X(x)Y(y)Z(z)$ form. Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{X}\frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} + \frac{1}{Y}\frac{d^2 Y}{dy^2} + \frac{1}{Z}\frac{d^2 Z}{dz^2} + k^2 = 0$$

For wave propagation along $z$, $Z(z) = e^{-j\beta_g z}$, giving:

$$\frac{1}{X}\frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} + \frac{1}{Y}\frac{d^2 Y}{dy^2} + k^2 - \beta_g^2 = 0$$

### Step 2: Apply Separation Constants
Let $k_x^2$ and $k_y^2$ be the separation constants such that $k_x^2 + k_y^2 = k^2 - \beta_g^2 = k_c^2$:

$$\frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} + k_x^2 X = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{d^2 Y}{dy^2} + k_y^2 Y = 0$$

The general solutions are sinusoidal:

$$X(x) = A\cos(k_x x) + B\sin(k_x x)$$

$$Y(y) = C\cos(k_y y) + D\sin(k_y y)$$

### Step 3: Apply Boundary Conditions for TE Modes
For TE modes, the boundary condition on the conducting walls at $x = 0, a$ and $y = 0, b$ is:

$$\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0,a} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial H_z}{\partial y}\bigg|_{y=0,b} = 0$$

This forces $B = 0$, $D = 0$, $k_x = m\pi/a$, and $k_y = n\pi/b$. Therefore:

$$H_z = H_0 \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta_g z}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Cutoff Wave Number and Frequency
The cutoff wave number is:

$$k_{c,mn}^2 = k_x^2 + k_y^2 = \left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2$$

The cutoff frequency (where $\beta_g = 0$ and $k = k_c$) is:

$$f_{c,mn} = \frac{k_c}{2\pi\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}$$

In free space, this simplifies to $f_{c,mn} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Group Velocity and Phase Velocity Relationship

### Starting Point
The propagation constant in a waveguide is given by:

$$\beta_g^2 = k^2 - k_c^2 = \omega^2 \mu\varepsilon - k_c^2$$

### Step 1: Solve for $\omega$ as a Function of $\beta_g$
Rearranging the dispersion relation:

$$\omega^2 \mu\varepsilon = \beta_g^2 + k_c^2$$

$$\omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\beta_g^2 + k_c^2}$$

### Step 2: Differentiate to Get Group Velocity
Differentiating both sides with respect to $\beta_g$:

$$\frac{d\omega}{d\beta_g} = \frac{\beta_g}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{\beta_g^2 + k_c^2}} = \frac{\beta_g}{\omega\mu\varepsilon} = \frac{\beta_g c^2}{\omega}$$

But $v_g = d\omega/d\beta_g$ and $v_p = \omega/\beta_g$, so:

$$v_g = \frac{c^2}{v_p}$$

### Step 3: Cross-Verification Using $v_p$
Starting from $v_p = \omega/\beta_g$:

$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta_g} = \frac{\omega}{\sqrt{\omega^2\mu\varepsilon - k_c^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - (k_c/\omega)^2}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$$

Similarly:

$$v_g = \frac{d\omega}{d\beta_g} = c\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$$

### Step 4: Final Verification
Multiplying the two:

$$v_p \cdot v_g = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}} \cdot c\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2} = c^2$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A rectangular waveguide has dimensions $a = 4$ cm, $b = 2$ cm. It operates at $f = 7.5$ GHz with the dominant mode. Find: (i) cutoff frequency, (ii) phase velocity, (iii) group velocity, (iv) guide wavelength.

**Solution:**

### Part (i): Cutoff Frequency of Dominant Mode ($TE_{10}$)
For $TE_{10}$: $m = 1$, $n = 0$:

$$f_{c,10} = \frac{c}{2a} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.04} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.08} = 3.75 \text{ GHz}$$

### Part (ii): Phase Velocity
First compute the ratio $f_c/f$:

$$\frac{f_c}{f} = \frac{3.75}{7.5} = 0.5$$

$$\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.25} = \sqrt{0.75} = 0.866$$

$$v_p = \frac{c}{0.866} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.866} = 3.464 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

### Part (iii): Group Velocity
$$v_g = c \times 0.866 = 3 \times 10^8 \times 0.866 = 2.598 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Verification:** $v_p \cdot v_g = 3.464 \times 2.598 \times 10^{16} = 9 \times 10^{16} = c^2$ ✓

### Part (iv): Guide Wavelength
Free-space wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{7.5 \times 10^9} = 0.04 \text{ m} = 4 \text{ cm}$$

Guide wavelength:

$$\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{0.04}{0.866} = 0.0462 \text{ m} = 4.62 \text{ cm}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of TE vs TM Dominant Behaviour

| Parameter | TE Mode (e.g., $TE_{10}$) | TM Mode (e.g., $TM_{11}$) |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Longitudinal E-field | $E_z = 0$ | $E_z \neq 0$ |
| Longitudinal H-field | $H_z \neq 0$ | $H_z = 0$ |
| Can have $n=0$ or $m=0$? | **Yes** (e.g., $TE_{10}, TE_{01}$) | **No** ($m,n \geq 1$) |
| Lowest cutoff mode | $TE_{10}$ (dominant) | $TM_{11}$ (lowest TM) |
| Cutoff frequency | $f_c = c/(2a)$ | $f_c = (c/2)\sqrt{(1/a)^2 + (1/b)^2}$ |
| Wave impedance | $Z_{TE} > \eta$ | $Z_{TM} < \eta$ |
| Practical use | Most waveguides | Rare, used in special filters |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mode Classification and Velocity Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EM Wave in Rectangular Waveguide] --> B{Is E_z zero?}
    B -- Yes --> C[TE Mode - H_mn]
    B -- No --> D[TM Mode - E_mn]
    C --> E{Dominant Mode Test}
    D --> E
    E -- Lowest f_c --> F[TE10 Dominant Mode]
    E -- Higher f_c --> G[Higher Order Modes]
    F --> H[Phase Velocity v_p greater than c]
    F --> I[Group Velocity v_g less than c]
    H --> J[Energy travels at v_g]
    I --> J
    J --> K[v_p times v_g equals c squared]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Field Component Architecture for TE10 Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TE10 Mode Setup] --> B[Broad Wall a]
    A --> C[Narrow Wall b]
    B --> D[m equals 1 Half Wave Variation]
    C --> E[n equals 0 No Variation]
    D --> F[Longitudinal H_z Exists]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Transverse E_y Maximum at Center]
    F --> H[Transverse H_x Maximum at Walls]
    G --> I[Wave Propagates along z with beta_g]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Phase Velocity v_p along z]
    J --> K[Group Velocity v_g transports Energy]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Velocity Relationship Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operating Frequency f] --> B{Compare with f_c}
    B -- f less than f_c --> C[Evanescent Mode]
    B -- f greater than f_c --> D[Propagation Mode]
    C --> E[Wave Decays Exponentially]
    D --> F[Compute beta_g]
    F --> G[Phase Velocity v_p equals omega by beta_g]
    F --> H[Group Velocity v_g equals d_omega by d_beta_g]
    G --> I[v_p greater than c but no info travels]
    H --> J[v_g less than c signal velocity]
    I --> K[v_p times v_g equals c squared]
    J --> K
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Energy Transport via Zig-Zag Bouncing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident Ray on Wall] --> B[Reflected Ray]
    B --> C[Zig Zag Path between Walls]
    C --> D[Apparent Axial Speed v_p]
    C --> E[Actual Beam Speed equals c]
    E --> F[Group Velocity v_g equals c cos theta]
    D --> G[v_p equals c by cos theta]
    F --> H[v_p times v_g equals c squared]
    G --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.4:** The zig-zag bouncing model gives a beautiful physical picture: $v_p = c/\cos\theta$ and $v_g = c\cos\theta$ (where $\theta$ is the angle of incidence of the wavefront on the waveguide wall). Their product is $c^2$ — a result that emerges naturally from the geometry of the bouncing wave.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the dominant mode in a rectangular waveguide. Why is $TE_{10}$ called the dominant mode?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The dominant mode in a rectangular waveguide is the mode that has the **lowest cutoff frequency** among all possible TE and TM modes. For a rectangular waveguide with broad wall $a$ and narrow wall $b$ (where $a > b$), the cutoff frequency is given by:
$f_{c,mn} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}$.

The $TE_{10}$ mode has $m = 1$, $n = 0$, giving the smallest cutoff frequency $f_{c,10} = c/(2a)$. This is because the other modes ($TE_{01}$, $TE_{11}$, $TM_{11}$, etc.) all have either $m \geq 1$ AND $n \geq 1$ (for TM) or have higher $m$ values. Therefore, $TE_{10}$ propagates at the lowest frequency, has the **simplest field configuration**, the **lowest attenuation**, and is the **easiest to excite** — making it the dominant mode. **(3 marks)**

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between phase velocity and group velocity? Why is $v_p > c$ acceptable while $v_g > c$ is not?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Phase velocity ($v_p$):** The velocity at which a point of constant phase (a wave crest) travels. In a waveguide, $v_p = c / \sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2} > c$.
- **Group velocity ($v_g$):** The velocity at which the **envelope** of a wave packet (the energy/information) propagates. In a waveguide, $v_g = c\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2} < c$.

$v_p > c$ is acceptable because no actual energy, mass, or information is carried at this speed — only the apparent motion of a phase front. Einstein's relativity forbids $v_g > c$ because the group velocity represents **signal velocity**, and information cannot travel faster than light. The fundamental relation $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ ensures that as $v_p$ becomes very large near cutoff, $v_g$ becomes very small — the energy crawls forward while the phase fronts move rapidly. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **phase velocity** and **group velocity** in a rectangular waveguide. Show that $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Wave Equation in the Waveguide**
For wave propagation along $z$, the longitudinal field $H_z$ (for TE) satisfies:
$$\frac{\partial^2 H_z}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 H_z}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 H_z}{\partial z^2} + k^2 H_z = 0$$

**Step 2: Applying Separation of Variables**
Assuming $H_z = X(x)Y(y)Z(z)$ with $Z = e^{-j\beta_g z}$, and using separation constants $k_x^2 + k_y^2 = k^2 - \beta_g^2 = k_c^2$:
$$\frac{d^2X}{dx^2} + k_x^2 X = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{d^2Y}{dy^2} + k_y^2 Y = 0$$

**Step 3: Applying Boundary Conditions**
With $\partial H_z/\partial n = 0$ at the walls, we get $k_x = m\pi/a$ and $k_y = n\pi/b$. Therefore:
$$k_c^2 = \left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2$$

**Step 4: Phase Velocity**
From the dispersion relation $\beta_g^2 = k^2 - k_c^2$:
$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta_g} = \frac{\omega}{\sqrt{\omega^2\mu\varepsilon - k_c^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - (k_c/k)^2}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$$

**Step 5: Group Velocity**
$v_g = d\omega/d\beta_g$. Since $\omega^2\mu\varepsilon = \beta_g^2 + k_c^2$, differentiating with respect to $\beta_g$:
$$2\omega\mu\varepsilon \frac{d\omega}{d\beta_g} = 2\beta_g$$
$$v_g = \frac{\beta_g}{\omega\mu\varepsilon} = \frac{c^2\beta_g}{\omega} = \frac{c^2}{v_p}$$

Equivalently: $v_g = c\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$

**Step 6: Multiplying Both**
$$v_p \cdot v_g = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}} \cdot c\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2} = c^2$$

Therefore, $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$. **(7 marks — full marks distribution: 2 for setup, 2 for $v_p$, 2 for $v_g$, 1 for the product)**

---

**(b)** An air-filled rectangular waveguide has dimensions $a = 2.286$ cm, $b = 1.016$ cm. It operates at $f = 10$ GHz in the dominant mode. Calculate: (i) cutoff frequency, (ii) phase velocity, (iii) group velocity, (iv) guide wavelength, and (v) wave impedance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $a = 2.286$ cm $= 0.02286$ m, $b = 1.016$ cm $= 0.01016$ m, $f = 10$ GHz $= 10 \times 10^9$ Hz, dominant mode is $TE_{10}$.

**(i) Cutoff Frequency:**
$$f_{c,10} = \frac{c}{2a} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.02286} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.04572} = 6.562 \text{ GHz}$$

**(ii) Ratio $f_c/f$:**
$$\frac{f_c}{f} = \frac{6.562}{10} = 0.6562$$

$$1 - (f_c/f)^2 = 1 - 0.4306 = 0.5694$$

$$\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2} = 0.7546$$

**Phase Velocity:**
$$v_p = \frac{c}{0.7546} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.7546} = 3.976 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Group Velocity:**
$$v_g = c \times 0.7546 = 3 \times 10^8 \times 0.7546 = 2.264 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Verification:** $v_p \cdot v_g = 3.976 \times 2.264 \times 10^{16} \approx 9.00 \times 10^{16} = c^2$ ✓

**(iv) Guide Wavelength:**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm}$$
$$\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda}{0.7546} = \frac{0.03}{0.7546} = 0.03976 \text{ m} = 3.976 \text{ cm}$$

**(v) Wave Impedance (TE Mode):**
$$Z_{TE} = \frac{\eta_0}{\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{377}{0.7546} = 499.6 \text{ }\Omega$$

**(7 marks — full marks distribution: 1 for each of (i) and (ii), 2 for (iii)+(iv), 2 for (v), 1 for verification)**

---

### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain **TE and TM modes** in a rectangular waveguide with proper mathematical expressions for the longitudinal field components. Why is $TE_{10}$ the dominant mode? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of TE and TM Modes:**
A rectangular waveguide supports wave propagation only via TE (Transverse Electric) and TM (Transverse Magnetic) modes because the single-conductor structure cannot sustain TEM waves. The waves are classified based on whether the **electric** or **magnetic** field has a longitudinal (z-directed) component.

**TE Mode ($E_z = 0$):**
For TE modes, $E_z = 0$ and $H_z$ is obtained by solving the Helmholtz equation with the Neumann boundary condition $\partial H_z/\partial n = 0$ at all four walls ($x = 0, a$ and $y = 0, b$):

$$H_z(x,y,z) = H_0 \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta_g z}$$

The transverse field components $E_x, E_y, H_x, H_y$ are derived from $H_z$ using Maxwell's equations.

**TM Mode ($H_z = 0$):**
For TM modes, $H_z = 0$ and $E_z$ is obtained by solving the Helmholtz equation with the Dirichlet boundary condition $E_z = 0$ at all four walls:

$$E_z(x,y,z) = E_0 \sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)e^{-j\beta_g z}$$

**Why $TE_{10}$ is the Dominant Mode:**

The cutoff frequency for any mode is:
$$f_{c,mn} = \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}$$

- For $TE_{10}$: $f_{c,10} = c/(2a)$ — this is the **lowest** value (since $a > b$).
- For $TE_{01}$: $f_{c,01} = c/(2b) > c/(2a)$ because $b < a$.
- For $TE_{11}$ or $TM_{11}$: $f_{c,11} = (c/2)\sqrt{(1/a)^2 + (1/b)^2}$ — higher than $f_{c,10}$.
- TM modes require $m \geq 1$ AND $n \geq 1$ (since $\sin(0) = 0$ would make $E_z = 0$ everywhere, which is trivial). So $TM_{11}$ is the lowest TM mode and is still higher than $TE_{10}$.

Therefore, $TE_{10}$ has the **lowest cutoff frequency** and is the **dominant mode** — it propagates first as frequency is increased from zero, has the simplest field structure, and is the easiest to excite. **(7 marks — 2 for TE definition, 2 for TM definition, 2 for cutoff comparison, 1 for conclusion)**

---

**(b)** Starting from the dispersion relation $\omega^2\mu\varepsilon = \beta_g^2 + k_c^2$, **derive the relationship** $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ in a rectangular waveguide. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Write the Dispersion Relation**
For any waveguide, the propagation constant $\beta_g$ and angular frequency $\omega$ are related by:
$$\beta_g^2 = \omega^2\mu\varepsilon - k_c^2$$

where $k_c$ is the cutoff wave number (constant for a given mode and waveguide geometry).

**Step 2: Express Phase Velocity**
By definition, $v_p = \omega/\beta_g$. Therefore:
$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\sqrt{\omega^2\mu\varepsilon - k_c^2}}$$

**Step 3: Differentiate the Dispersion Relation to Find Group Velocity**
Differentiating $\omega^2\mu\varepsilon = \beta_g^2 + k_c^2$ with respect to $\beta_g$:
$$2\omega\mu\varepsilon \frac{d\omega}{d\beta_g} = 2\beta_g$$

$$\frac{d\omega}{d\beta_g} = \frac{\beta_g}{\omega\mu\varepsilon} = \frac{\beta_g \cdot c^2}{\omega} \quad \text{(since } c = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\text{)}$$

By definition, $v_g = d\omega/d\beta_g$, so:
$$v_g = \frac{\beta_g c^2}{\omega}$$

**Step 4: Multiply $v_p$ and $v_g$**
$$v_p \cdot v_g = \left(\frac{\omega}{\beta_g}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\beta_g c^2}{\omega}\right)$$

The $\omega$ and $\beta_g$ terms cancel out:
$$v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$$

**Step 5: Physical Interpretation**
- As $f \to f_c$ (cutoff): $\beta_g \to 0$, so $v_p \to \infty$ and $v_g \to 0$. The wavefronts race along the guide but the energy crawls.
- As $f \to \infty$: $\beta_g \to k$, so $v_p \to c$ and $v_g \to c$. The waveguide behaviour approaches free-space behaviour at very high frequencies.

**(7 marks — 1 for step 1, 2 for step 3, 2 for step 4, 2 for physical interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Convert $a$ and $b$ from cm to m, and $f$ from GHz to Hz before substituting. This is the **#1 mark-deduction reason** in KTU papers.
> 2. **Confusing TE and TM indices** — TM modes cannot have $m = 0$ OR $n = 0$. If a student writes $TM_{10}$, that mode **does not exist** — the examiner will deduct marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the verification step** — Always verify $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ in numerical problems. Examiners often give **1 extra mark** for the verification.
> 4. **Writing $v_p > c$ without context** — Always clarify that $v_p$ is the apparent phase velocity, **not the signal velocity**, to avoid losing the conceptual understanding marks.
> 5. **Using $c = 3 \times 10^8$ for non-vacuum media** — If the guide is filled with dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, use $c = 3 \times 10^8 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ instead.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — TE/TM Modes, Group & Phase Velocity**

- ✅ **TE mode:** $E_z = 0$, $H_z \neq 0$. **TM mode:** $H_z = 0$, $E_z \neq 0$. These are the **only** two mode types in hollow waveguides (no TEM!).
- ✅ **Dominant mode in a rectangular waveguide** (with $a > b$) is the **$TE_{10}$ mode** with $f_{c,10} = c/(2a)$.
- ✅ **TM modes require $m \geq 1$ AND $n \geq 1$** (otherwise $E_z = 0$ trivially). The lowest TM mode is $TM_{11}$.
- ✅ **Cutoff frequency formula:** $f_{c,mn} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}$ — must be in consistent SI units.
- ✅ **Phase velocity** $v_p = \omega/\beta_g = c/\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2} > c$ — this is the speed of wave crests, not energy.
- ✅ **Group velocity** $v_g = d\omega/d\beta_g = c\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2} < c$ — this is the **signal/energy velocity**.
- ✅ **Fundamental identity:** $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ — verified by both algebra and the zig-zag bouncing model.
- ✅ **Guide wavelength:** $\lambda_g = \lambda/\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2} = v_p/f > \lambda$.
- ✅ **At cutoff** ($f = f_c$): $v_p \to \infty$, $v_g \to 0$, $\lambda_g \to \infty$ — wave becomes standing, not propagating.
- ✅ **TE wave impedance:** $Z_{TE} = \eta_0/\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2} > 377 \text{ }\Omega$.
- ✅ **TM wave impedance:** $Z_{TM} = \eta_0\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2} < 377 \text{ }\Omega$.
- ✅ **Always check:** $f > f_c$ for propagation. If $f < f_c$, the wave is evanescent and decays as $e^{-\alpha z}$.
- ✅ **Mode designation** for $TE_{10}$: $m = 1$ means **one half-wave variation** in the broad dimension $x$, and $n = 0$ means **no variation** in the narrow dimension $y$.
- ✅ **Real-world use:** $TE_{10}$ mode is used in nearly all **microwave communication, radar, satellite, and 5G systems** using rectangular waveguides (e.g., WR-90, WR-62, WR-42, WR-28 waveguide sizes).
